### chocolate — 2012-05-07T20:49:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-high-fiber-diet-does-not-protect-against-asymptomatic-diverticulosis.1307/post-21985>

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/134/2/479.full](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/134/2/479.full)

Microbial Degradation Products Influence Colon Cancer Risk: the Butyrate Controversy1,2

All dietary fiber, by definition, escapes digestion in the small intestine and thus arrives relatively intact in the large intestine. Its fate in the large intestine depends upon the type of fiber and the colonic microflora. Highly fermentable fibers result in short chain fatty acids including butyrate, which is thought by some to be protective against colon cancer. However, not all studies support a chemopreventive effect for butyrate and the lack of agreement (particularly between in vivo and in vitro studies) on butyrate and colon cancer has been termed the “butyrate paradox.” There are a number of reasons for this discrepant effect including differences between the in vitro and in vivo environments, the timing of butyrate administration, the amount of butyrate administered, the source of butyrate (usually dietary fiber) as a potential confounder, and an interaction with dietary fat. Collectively, the studies suggest that the chemopreventive benefits of butyrate depend in part on amount, time of exposure with respect to the tumorigenic process, and the type of fat in the diet.

colon

butyrate

dietary fiber

omega 3 fatty acids

A recent National Academy of Sciences report defines dietary fiber as nondigestible carbohydrates and lignin that are intrinsic and intact in plants (1). This means that by definition, nearly 100% of dietary fiber reaches the colon. The fate of fiber in the colon depends primarily on the colonic microflora and the physio-chemical characteristics of the fiber itself. Fiber sources such as oat bran, pectin, and guar are highly fermented, whereas others such as cellulose and wheat bran may be poorly fermented (2–4). In general, foods rich in hemicelluloses and pectins, such as fruits and vegetables, contain fiber that is more completely fermented than foods rich in celluloses, such as cereals (2–4). The products of fiber fermentation include CO2, methane, other gases and short chain fatty acids (SCFA), the major anions in colonic contents. Butyrate, an SCFA fiber fermentation product, is thought to be chemopreventive by some, but not all studies show this beneficial effect against colon cancer development. This paper deals with potential reasons for the seemingly paradoxical effects of butyrate on colon physiology and colon carcinogenesis.

Previous SectionNext Section

Fermentation of fiber in the colon and its significance to colon cancer

There is some debate in the literature as to whether the fermentability of a fiber plays a role in its protection/ promotion of colon carcinogenesis (5,6). The poorly fermented fibers are good in vivo dilutors and may decrease the concentration of carcinogens, pro-carcinogens and tumor promoters in the fecal stream, thus reducing access of these substances to the colonic mucosa (7). Cummings Evidence (pro and con) that butyrate (a fiber fermentation product) may protect against colon cancer

Butyrate has been documented by Roediger to be the primary aerobic fuel for colon cells (11). Butyrate has long been documented to decrease the growth of most human colon cancer cell lines by inhibiting cell proliferation and enhancing differentiation and apoptosis (12,13). Although some consider butyrate production to be a potential mechanism by which dietary fiber may protect against colon carcinogenesis, this hypothesis remains the subject of debate, due in part to apparent inconsistencies in the literature (5,6). For example, the first animal study to test the effects of butyrate against the development of experimentally-induced colon cancer found that administration of butyrate in the drinking water of rats actually increased the percentage of rats with colon tumors (85% of the rats consuming 1% butyrate had tumors compared to 50% of the rats with no added butyrate in the drinking water) (14). In a study designed to test two fibers with very different degrees of fermentability (i.e., oat bran that is highly fermentable compared to poorly fermented wheat bran) with respect to their protection/promotion of experimentally-induced colon cancer, we found that oat bran resulted in significantly higher (P < 0.001) levels of butyrate in both the proximal and distal colon compared to wheat bran (15). However oat bran also resulted in a greater number of colon tumors than did wheat bran (52% of rats fed oat bran had tumors compared to 27% of rats fed wheat bran; P < 0.021) (15). In an effort to separate out the effects of butyrate itself from the fiber substrate for butyrate production, Deschner et al. provided tributyrin in the diet of mice and found no protective effect of butyrate administration against colon tumors (16).

Timing with respect to tumor development.

The effect of butyrate on colon carcinogenesis may also depend upon the timing of butyrate administration in relationship to the stage of cancer development. For example, butyrate is a known inhibtor of histone deacetylase (21,22), which means that in its presence DNA would be in a more open or “vulnerable” form. This might be optimal if DNA damage had occurred and repair enzymes were necessary to approach the damaged DNA. Alternatively, in the presence of a carcinogen, a more open configuration of DNA would not be optimal. It is interesting to note the reports from the major prospective cohort studies on whether or not fiber is protective against colon carcinogenesis. Two of the major prospective studies, such as the Nurses’ Health Study and the Health Professionals Followup Study, initially reported a protective effect of fiber against adenoma development (23,24), but later showed no such protection against colon cancer (25,26). Also, the three major clinical intervention trials, all of which used polyp recurrence as the endpoint, failed to show a protective effect against this surrogate marker for colon cancer (27–29). **Some have suggested that fiber may be protective against the early stages of polyp formation, but not at the stage of transition of a polyp to a carcinoma. All of the individuals in the three clinical intervention trials already had adenomatous polyps.**

> Thanks for the information. Could night sweats also be due to low leptin (.8 non fasted)? I have not had night sweats for three years since I started eating low carb and they are mysteriously back since starting the Leptin Reset now for two weeks.

Night sweats could be hormonal as Dr. [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) mentioned about

"Night sweats are a sign of poor ocular melatonin formation and LR due to IL 6 and nfr2 activation"​

I'm sure you are aware of the hormonal impacts of menopause.

[https://www.bloodandmilk.com/natural-remedies-for-night-sweats/](https://www.bloodandmilk.com/natural-remedies-for-night-sweats/)
[https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/320130](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/320130)​

However, there are metabolic reasons for night sweats.

[https://www.livestrong.com/article/557671-carbohydrate-ingestion-night-sweats/](https://www.livestrong.com/article/557671-carbohydrate-ingestion-night-sweats/)
​

These are typically resolved with a solid seafood keto diet as Dr. [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) recommends

[https://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx](https://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/?print=print)
[https://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-5-paradigm-drifts-paradigm-shifts-epi-paleo/](https://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-5-paradigm-drifts-paradigm-shifts-epi-paleo/) ​

### Saichi — 2022-02-08T20:24:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats.1311/post-309757>

I remember when I switched my CT from 52 deg to 47 deg last year I went through a week where I was too hot at night sleeping. But that went away and I've been happy with 47 ever since. 52's too easy for me.

My personal recent records are one hour in 47 deg and 30 min in 38 deg.

# Thread: Jack kicked off cruise???

### janagram — 2012-05-08T08:16:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22151>

Jack's in the news....kicked off cruise....wonder what set that off? Someone who is irritated with him?

### Birdy — 2012-05-08T10:15:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22160>

This has been going on for quite some time. There are plenty of people on places like MDA or paleohacks who disagree with Jack and feel that he is ruining "their" paleo. Someone started a twitter account, @\*\*\*\*krusesays. At first it was a few of Jack's quotes taken out of context, with many of the paleo luminaries chiming in, poking fun at Jack and his "followers". Then subtle changes were made to the quotes, and some, in the right frame of mind (like at a party, with friends), were pretty funny. However, with the wrong frame of mind, and with some other participants chiming in, it got pretty weird. Now, people here are used to seeing the bomb video, and all the reset stuff, change your genetics with a thought, etc., but that sort of stuff can sound weird to outsiders, like the FBI. Apparently, they thought @\*\*\*\*krusesays was Jack's twitter account, and someone also tipped off the cruise line that Jack was planning some sort of attack on the cruise ship. So that means the people who signed up for the cruise to hear the Kruse are sol for now.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-08T14:13:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22168>

[http://www.newschannel5.com/story/18169740/nashville-surgeon-target-of-cyber-attack-kicked-off-cruise-ship](http://http://www.newschannel5.com/story/18169740/nashville-surgeon-target-of-cyber-attack-kicked-off-cruise-ship)

Nevertheless, context is everything. Stupid as the tweet is, I don't know if its writer foresaw such a reaction. I don't imagine how with the "sh\*t" in front of the account Dr. Kruse became the first suspect either. I would like the penal system including ice immersion sentences.

I understand the captain decision, but I think it was a little disproportionate. He should have talked to Jimmy Moore at least, since it's the fifth year already. Fifteen minutes would have suffice to correct the misunderstanding.

Sorry Doc. I hope you only have to deal with this childish kind of _resistance_, though.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-08T17:25:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22217>

Someone just made a threat to a Paleo fx leader via FB that the Letter from "Carnival is a fake".......Here is the funny thing.......I have the email from Carnival in my personal account and 350 people on the cruise got that letter last night at dinner from the Captain. I wonder why someone would make a claim like this when it is obvious how serious this situation has become. This is a federal matter now and I think it is far out of my hands. The news media actually read the email in my house last night from the Carnival spokes person and I also received an apology from the head of security at Carnival yesterday. I think Carnival has handled this situation as best they can. An agent has been collecting data off many blogs and many websites that have motive and cause. If you cooperate you'll likely be dealt with differently than if they find you.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-08T19:26:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22282>

> **Cavemam;22339 wrote:** This does not smack of sophistication. Seems like the fake/mock tweet account may be a separate issue from the clearly "going off the rails" individual that did the real damage - the phone call. Can't wait to see how this unfolds. Now we are getting national attention, I wish I hadn't used my real photo and info on all my posts!!!!

If you drop your avatar, I think it will change immediately on your posts, but don't quote me on that!

I think whoever posted the tweet, whether in jest or not, did huge damage. It's like being at the airport and having a conversation about terrorism. Even if you're not making a threat and you're just commenting on some other situation, the authorities will take you aside and investigate. You just can't do that. I think whoever placed the phone call was right on; they had no way of knowing whether it was a joke or a potentially real threat (why they didn't call 911 instead of Carnival makes me suspicious). If you saw a tweet where someone was "joking" about bringing weapons to a school, you'd call it in right away. Same thing here IMO. You can't afford to not take something like this seriously.

### David — 2012-05-08T19:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22285>

> **Cavemam;22360 wrote:** Keep us posted on what is going on with everything! Retype the letter !

May 7, 2012

Greetings from the bridge,

I want to take this opportunity to personally welcome you on board. I also want to share some information regarding Dr. John Kruse, a guest who was supposed to sail as a part of your group.

Yesterday afternoon, we were notified of a Twitter message, under the handle of Dr. Kruse, alleging he intended to cause harm to the ship. Galveston police, the FBI, the Department of Homeland Security and the U.S. Coast Guard were informed immediately and, shortly thereafter, authorithies boarded the vessel to investigate the matter. Dr. Kruse was questioned, and since the investigation was ongoing, he was asked to disembark the vessel just prior sailing.

The FBI has been able to confirm today that Dr. Kruse was in no way responsible for threatening Twitter message.

I know Dr. Kruse was to speak with your group this week and I sincerely apologize he will be unable to join you as previously scheduled. Since the safety and well-being of my guests and crew is my number one priority, every security threat is taken seriously and fully investigated. It is for this reason that I felt it was in the best interest of all guests to err on the side of caution and not allow him to sail as planned.

Thank you for your understanding. Please enjoy your time on board.

Sincerely,

Captain Giovanni Cutugno

### Evo Mama — 2012-05-09T03:35:54+0300

My guess is part of it is a subconscious jealousy of his blog's popularity and of his innovative ideas. The fact that his ideas can be marginalized as being "wacky" is, IMHO, a red herring. The animosity comes not from their urge to "save Paleo from such lunacy" (imagined quote), but rather to preserve their own fragile egos. I would venture to guess that these individuals aren't even necessarily aware of this subconscious motivation within themselves.

Whenever a person has such distaste for another as to be moved to do even the slightest of harm, that is the precise moment one must look inward. In that delicate instance, a person can find the self awareness needed to let go of anger and hate, and discover a path to the betterment one's own existence.

### chocolate — 2012-05-09T04:14:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22454>

Anyone that comments on this as trivial makes the whole Paleo movement look stupid. It reminds me of the saying that silence equals permission. Paleo needs to raise hell. Carnival had a tough call and nothing is perfect. I worked for the airlines and discretion is a tough call. It could be that they reserved the behavior of denying boarding so in the future, if they truly felt threatened, they couldn't be sued for discrimination. It gets very kinky trying to cover everyone's rear. You should see the wheelchair hoops ....Christmas time you can throw a paying passenger off a plane if a wheelchair passenger wants the chair on board versus, cargo. It is considered part of their body.

Not militant and outrageously demanding.... or it was ten years ago. Never mind trying to rebook the wheelchair passenger for a later flight. A fat buxom bleach blonde complained they were chosen by male coworkers to be searched by me, during the window before TSA/ post 9/11, saying my coworker picked on her because she was attractive. I was called in to be asked if I had to search any attractive women on a certain flight. Nicely reported that, "no, I just searched a fat, sweaty (she was, not just an opinion) woman, the kind the guys go to baggage claim post flight and laugh at when thongs pop up from their jeans as they retrieve their luggage." The fact is, if my co-worker had the choice of who to search , I would have kicked his ass for making me lay hands on the her. Once, a TSA told me to search the next young lady, I thought he meant the next young lady. I didn't search the middle aged lady next in line and she got insulted. This is why discretion loses all of its merit. Then we had the option of refusing to search the opposite sex if no one trained was available. There was no way I would let someone miss their flight because I knew that I could behave professionally, it was also their option to be searched, and men don't overreact to uncomfortable situations. There was actually a shortage of metal detectors and we had to use our bare hands. I would never expect a guy searcher to trust a female in that situation. Sucks, but I understand the separation. You just have to have blanket policies to CYA. Dr Kruse IMO was removed so a real perceived threat wouldn't sue in the future. It goes to show you how piss poor the leadership is that doesn't speak out on this sort of thing if it does turn out to be Paleo establishment backlash. There is nothing not "Paleo" about this blog, its just circadian and evolutionary. Isn't that Paleo?

### Birdy — 2012-05-09T05:17:08+0300

At first it was funny to read some of Jack's quotes, and then there were more slightly skewered quotes mixed in. Recently the tone seemed much darker, crazier, and participants just piled on Jack and his followers and it looked like most of the luminaries were less willing to participate, despite being tweeted by @sks. ### Souldanzer — 2012-05-09T16:36:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22531>

> For some reason there seems to be more personal vitriol directed at Jack rather than professional disagreement.

That's the part that messes with me. It points out that what is happening really doesn't have much to do with what he's saying/writing or not. This is much more of a psychological/mind issue. I'm thinking it might have to do with people not wanting their paleo world rocked.

It's a known phenomenon that when you have a certain concept in your mind - the longer you've believed it the harder it is to give it up. People will ignore information to the opposite so they can stick to their known belief. It is unbelievable how long this can go on... look at some of the elderly who will keep doing things that are entirely outdated despite overwhelming information that it's useless.

The next stage would be cognitive dissonance - the moment part of you realizes you're wrong ("directionally incorrect" in this case... ) you start to feel crazy in your mind. Your established belief system goes into chaos b/c of the new information. That can cause severe anger/rage in my experience. It really can make you nuts.

While change appears difficult for most people, it is actually a concept the mind can learn to welcome. We have both evolutionary concepts in our brains - we like the known b/c it spells security, but we also have curiosity which humans have used extensively in the past to advance to the point we are now. Yes, there is risk involved with that... but also huge reward.

Curiosity is something that is neat to see in children... as they haven't been taught yet to be afraid of change. I love to watch my dog's curiosity

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-09T16:59:25+0300

You are right; this feels like a silly elementary school bully squad. I have seen only once the main 4 or 5 against Jack critique his ideas. Every other time they speak, it's about his style, or a misunderstanding of what he said because they don't get his style/lack of it (hard to blame them for the latter, IF they eventually realize it's actually a misunderstanding instead of continuing to beat a very dead straw man). Discourse. Reason. Is it so hard to stick to the facts?

# Thread: CT and Leptin RX Weight Gain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-05-08T22:01:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-22334>

I know Dr. K said weight gain can occur when hormones are out of balance. Heres my history:

Started Leptin Rx last summer, lost weight, was feeling good, but did not have signs of LS. Lost track, tried to do Post Leptin RX for a few months. Went nowhere. I have also been trying to treat my hormones and adrenal fatigue. Continued gaining weight, so decided to recommit to Leptin Rx and doing CT.

I have been Leptin Rx protocol for three weeks. CTing since end of Feb, early March. I have now gained 9 pounds since Nov. Two and a half pounds since the Leptin Rx. I am eating less than I was prior to LRx, and have completely removed dairy. Why am I still gaining?

I continue to have fatigue despite my supplements, and I have slow but constant detox from the CT. I have had no positive improvements from CT or Leptin Rx, which makes it very hard to stay on the straight and narrow. At this point though I am afraid of stopping because I don't know where it leaves me!

My thyroid is low, but I am told it is due to hormones and adrenal fatigue. So I am treating (or attempting to treat) those, but in the meantime I am getting worse. At what point does one consider treating thyroid?

Is it possible that CT is screwing around with my hormones to the point of causing weight gain where I had lost before? Anyone with insight feel free to jump in here!

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-08T22:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-22347>

> **tweety;22413 wrote:**
>
> Is it possible that CT is screwing around with my hormones to the point of causing weight gain where I had lost before? Anyone with insight feel free to jump in here!

THIS! the hormonal craziness is the result of releasing excess estrogen stored in your fat cells... so far I've hit three different estrogen dumps...  I expect more to come...

keep on w/the LR and the CT. it will work itself out... just remember you don't want to test while your detoxing...

### tweety — 2012-05-08T22:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-22353>

I know your story well! I just didn't want my estrogen dump and detox to include weight gain! I have low estrogen, but relative E dominance. But I did order a liver detox supp to see if that helps me. It did occur to me that in the two months of CT I have been doing I have not had increased need to urinate, so I am wondering if I am not clearing these detox symptoms. I just want to purge my body of this crap right now!

I started the progesterone cream at the same time as the weight gain began, so I am thinking I am on too low a dose to do anything but anger my estrogen.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-15T03:54:52+0300

> **kjgraffin;23950 wrote:** I have been reading all about how the estrogen dump from your fat cells will contribute to weight gain. I use one of those hand held body fat monitors and it hasn't budged, but yet the scaling is inching up? What am I missing or forgetting about?

body comp in both sexes is all about Testosterone and IGF1 levels.....not estrogen

with pcos, the big problem with body comp and the like, is the insulin factor....insulin mucks everything up - i can see it blocking any good side effects of all the testosterone in your body. insulin is what makes people store fat in a sense....think of diabetics who get fat patches where they inject insulin (so they have to move around their injection sites).

### angieonthegogo — 2012-05-25T02:51:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-27219>

I've gained nearly 10 lbs over the last few months. I've not really changed anything except adding CT and tweaking some supplements. I feel great except for the ocassional slow digestion/follow through and some bloating but the biggest hinderance is the pesky pounds. I've been racking my brain trying to think what I'm doing differently and the only thing I can come up with is BP coffee. I would drink coffee a few times a week with just HWC and stevia w/ my BAB but I started doing BP coffee and was having it almost every day. I'm wondering if all of the added fat is the issue or it is the increase in coffee. I seem to feel better with the added fat since it can help along the BAB in the morning but not sure what the culprit is. Then again, it is probable that CT inducde detox and hormonal chaos is to blame for perhaps part or all of it. Who knows. Sometimes I'm sick of thinking of these things after hitting the same brick wall repeatedly.

So today is day one of my experiment: Operation Sans Coffee.

### tweety — 2012-05-25T02:56:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-27223>

tell me about it. I have steadily been gaining 1.5lbs a month, which doesn't seem like much. But, given that its been six months of this... I didn't notice or worry until a few months ago. Now I weigh more than I have since I have known my husband. And yes I changed my diet. But I reintrodiced the Leptin RX and can tell you I am eating less than I was previously and still gaining. Its all hormones. I say this because I weighed myself on saturday and was down a pound and a half. I felt good, continued on. Got on the scale today and was three pounds up! I did nothing between these days that would warrant that, so it has to be hormones.

If you're gaining I would look at CT- its changing your hormones around for sure.

### hazyjane — 2012-05-25T20:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-27400>

Re: progesterone cream- I've heard of women gaining weight from progesterone cream. It can be converted to estrogen and it can also build up in the fatty tissues.

I did a little search on it and I found a message board with loads of women reporting weight gain from progesterone cream and BHRT:

[http://www.progesteronetherapy.com/progesterone-weight-gain.html#axzz1vu2o3NEH](http://http://www.progesteronetherapy.com/progesterone-weight-gain.html#axzz1vu2o3NEH)

My best friend was on the cream during her pregnancy and she gained a ridiculous amount of weight considering her diet (seriously- her main food craving was raw spinach!!) I saw what she was eating and it wasn't the kind of food that should lead to that kind of weight gain.

I found this quote that I thought was interesting (and I wonder if it applies to me, since progesterone cream has never been helpful, but very low dose (5 mgs) oral natural progesterone liquid (Apex Energetics) sublingually def. had a nice diuretic affect on me).

"There is another possible reason for weight gain with progesterone. According to Dr. Uzzi Reiss, a GYN in Beverly Hills, there are a small percentage of women who can tolerate only a minimum amount of progesterone. For these women, a normal dose is like an overdose. And one of the side effects is an increased appetite and weight gain (2).

For women who have a low tolerance, Dr. Reiss recommends using a low-dose 3% cream, applied vaginally and to the breasts. He said that this minimum amount is usually enough to give the benefits that would require a much higher dosage in other women."

Hi Tweety, I just read this post from March this year. How have things gone for you ? Have you managed to lose any of the weight. ?

### tweety — 2012-08-17T23:10:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-leptin-rx-weight-gain.1351/post-45426>

> **somewhatchubby;45597 wrote:** Hi Tweety, I just read this post from March this year. How have things gone for you ? Have you managed to lose any of the weight. ?
>

>
> Do you put on weight around your hips and backside ? I have a flat backside and no weight around the thighs but have a large bust and tend to be top heavy.I dont know if that has any meaning at all but a lot of women tend to load fat around the hips and thighs.

Well, unfortunately I am still sorting out issues. I have lost 1 or 2 lbs, but only by skipping progesterone. I skipped one month, lost 2lbs, used P again the next month, gained 2lbs. I am skipping it this month again, and have deflated a tiny bit. But, my irritability is huge this month. My dr has agreed to take me off of progesterone for now while we sort out the adrenal issues and thyroid trouble. I also purchased Dzugans' book, The Migraine Cure, and plan on studying up for what I should be working toward. I do not think I should skip progesterone next month based on my emotional symptoms.

I will say that based on testimony here and what I am reading, that I am on too low a dose. 3% cream from day 15 - period isn't cutting it. I think it needs to 5% or more, and on the other days as well. But we'll see.

And I am built similarly to you- gain all weight on my torso, never on my legs or butt. I am trying to redirect it to my bum, but its not working! But I do not have a large chest. I'm not curvy really at all!

I'd love to get more info on this test too. My doctor isn't familiar with it, but I'm hoping I can get him to order it. I emailed the lab that does the testing twice now to ask if there are any practitioners near me that have ordered it, but I have yet to receive a response.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-13T20:51:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exatest.1353/post-23718>

[http://www.exatest.com](http://http://www.exatest.com)

Date: Fri, 11 May 2012 17:31:23 -0700

From: [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

To:

Subject: INTRACELLULAR EXA TEST Re: Price of Exatest?

We don't provide the cost on the web site because we accept assignment. That is we accept what the insurance allows and it depends on your insurance contract.

Below is our standard informaton:

Thank you for contacting IntraCellular Diagnostics; we appreciate your interest in EXA Test

Below you will find

General information, cost, insurance issues, and a clinical description that may provide answers to questions patients generally ask:

Upon receipt of an order the specimen collection kit is sent priority mail to the healthcare provider. The test results are faxed and mailed to the provider one to two days from the moment we receive the specimen at the Intracellular Diagnostics laboratory.

EXA Test provides results for six elements: Magnesium, Calcium, Phosphorus, Potassium, Sodium, and Chloride.

We will review the specimen collection procedure with the healthcare provider and we also provide support to the physician for interpretation and protocol: 541-245-3212

Patients need a clean mouth for the test and must avoid all dairy, soy or any sticky foods, sodium, chewable tablets, Tums, chewable vitamins and minerals etc. before the test. It is best to fast overnight. When food is necessary, PT can eat water soluble light foods. Contact IntraCellular if you have any questions on this or any other matter: 541-245-3212

We do not distribute the specimen collection kit to patients. It is important that patients consult with their physician and plan any treatment with his or her advice.

We do make an exception if the patient lives in a rural area or has difficulty getting to a physician's office; but we require a prescription. It can be faxed to: 541-245-3215.

The kit will be sent wherever the healthcare provider requests.

For providers not familiar with the EXA test, we have scientific information, peer reviewed research and publications including quality of life studies.

ICD is a CLIA licensed laboratory and is HIPPA compliant.

Billing information and payment options

We are a Medicare contracted laboratory and we bill everyone below the Medicare allowed fees. Allowed fee is the maximum fee the insurance company will pay. We accept assignment, which means that we accept what the insurance company allows.

Medicare always pays 80% of the EXA Test if the patient's contract is in order and has not expired. Secondary insurance pays the balance. If the patient does not have secondary insurance we accept the Medicare payment. We do not accept any prepayments from Medicare patients.

Private insurance pays according to patient contract, please see if you have out of network benefits or deductibles; a prepayment of $250.00 and a copy of your insurance information is required(see below.) Any overpayments that may occur will be refunded to you after the insurance pays.

Below is additional billing information that may or may not apply to you. And, don't miss the letter to your doctor at the bottom of this email:

We do not accept Medicaid as primary insurance though we accept it as secondary insurance.

r />
If the patient's contract does not provide for out of network service, or the carrier is an HMO, the patient needs to obtain a pre-authorization for CPT Code 88348. The insurance company will also need an ICD9 Code or the physician's reason for the test. You may call our office for assistance.

For a Pre-authorization, the insurance company needs to know that Intracellular Diagnostics is an Independent Clinical Laboratory and the only Specialty Reference Laboratory that processes this specific test

Patients not certain that their insurance will pay for the EXA test may call our office 541-245-3212, Patient Services, for additional information.

The healthcare provider may send the specimen to Intracellular Diagnostics with a prepayment of $250.00 (with the PT's check or credit card) and the patient's insurance information so that we may process the EXA Test, and bill the insurance and try to get payment on the patient's behalf.

If the claim is denied or the patient is not covered, is out of network or not insured, IntraCellular accepts the prepayment of $250.00 as settlement. If there is an overpayment a refund will be sent to the patient.

.

Some insurance companies consider IntraCellular Diagnostics out of network and may send the check to the patient. The patient needs to send that check to IntraCellular Diagnostics. We bill everyone $570.00 which is below the Medicare allowed fee. Private insurance may or may not pay the full amount allowed. But if the insurance pays the claim IntraCellular will refund the patient any overpayments.

Other literature is on the Internet at [http://www.exatest.com](http://http://www.exatest.com) and published research is under Research and Reference [http://WWW.exatest.Com/Research.htm](http://http://WWW.exatest.Com/Research.htm)

Below is a clinical description and indications for the physician..

Sincerely,

Patient Services

IntraCellular Diagnostics, Inc.

945 Town Centre Dr. Ste. A

Medford, Oregon 97504

[www.exatest.com](http://http://www.exatest.com)

541-245-3212

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-13T20:51:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exatest.1353/post-23719>

For Physicians:

RE: EXA TEST, Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis using Analytical Scanning Electron Microscopy; Testing for intracellular tissue status of Magnesium, Calcium, Potassium, Sodium, Chloride, and Phosphorus.

The EXA test is most helpful when used with patients who: experience atrial fibrillation, are taking blood pressure medications or any medication that impact mineral electrolytes, have implanted devices/ICDs, or have other CAD-(Coronary Artery Disease), for pregnant women to diagnose and prevent eclampsia, diabetics, those who have chronic pain, or have difficult to diagnose pathological complaints like chronic fatigue syndrome, those who routinely supplement with calcium, athletes who deplete electrolytes in sports, and for patient with conditions where mineral electrolyte balance is compromised.

The EXA method for intracellular tissue analysis was developed within a traditional scientific environment by a medical scientist and professor of medicine, Burton Silver, Ph.D. EXA Test has seen extensive research by major institutions. It is evidence based and publications are in peer reviewed journals. The EXA Test is a noninvasive tissue analysis that also addresses the concerns of integrative medicine practitioners.

Although EXA was originally developed for cardiology, the sublingual epithelial cells as used by IntraCellular Diagnostics correlate with cells that are prevalent throughout the body. Essential minerals and electrolytes are so crucial throughout the body that EXA can be used as a diagnostic assay in a variety of conditions.

Sublingual cells have a high cytoplasm to nucleus structure facilitating mineral analysis. Epithelial buccal cells are aerobic and contain full metabolic biochemical pathways. They offer a rapidly renewing, homogeneous and easily accessible population which reflects current total body intracellular mineral status.

The EXA Test is a direct measurement of mineral electrolyte content in epithelial cells. Once collected and fixed, there is no manipulation of the cells and the specimen a long life span for diagnostic purposes.

Throughout his career, IntraCellular Diagnostics's founder, Dr. Burton Silver, has worked closely with many cardiologists including those at Baylor, John Hopkins, Bethesda Army Hospital, NASA, Cedars Sinai, and the NIH.

The test was validated by clinical work and research with the institutions above mentioned and others. The double blind studies were published with Johns Hopkins in Circulation-the Journal of the American Heart Association. More studies are published in other peer review journals. One of our more recent quality of life studies with Cedars Sinai and UCLA was published in the American Journal of Cardiology and is on our web site [http://WWW.exatest.Com/PDF%20Files/42003Silver%20Shechter%20Quality%20of%20Life%20in%20CAD%20PT%20AJC42003.pdf](http://http://WWW.exatest.Com/PDF%20Files/42003Silver%20Shechter%20Quality%20of%20Life%20in%20CAD%20PT%20AJC42003.pdf)

We also have publications online from the Hartford Hospital in Connecticut that references quality of life for patients with ICD devices and antiarrhythmic medications, and one that references the differences between serum and EXA tissue magnesium:

[http://www.exatest.Com/PDF%20Files/AdMagHTNStudy\_AnnPharmacother2009;43;online.pdf](http://http://www.exatest.Com/PDF%20Files/AdMagHTNStudy_AnnPharmacother2009;43;online.pdf) .

The various publications in Circulation, The Journal of the American Heart Association, focused on correlation of the sublingual epithelial tissue with heart, musculoskeletal, and deep organ tissue. The EXA Test results do not correlate with blood but with tissue:

[http://www.exatest.com/PDF%20Files/1.%20Correlation%20of%20%20Tissue%20Magnesium%20with%20Cardiac%20Levels%20Using%20a%20Non-invasive%20Testing%20Method%20(full%20paper)..PDF](http://http://www.exatest.com/PDF%20Files/1.%20Correlation%20of%20%20Tissue%20Magnesium%20with%20Cardiac%20Levels%20Using%20a%20Non-invasive%20Testing%20Method%20\(full%20paper\)..PDF)

Another Johns Hopkins research showed the magnesium and electrolytes of sublingual cells correlate with soft and connective tissue in the body. Magnesium studies do not correlate with blood; only 1% of magnesium is in the blood; 99% of Mg (and Potassium) is in the soft tissue.

Problems with metabolism of these essential elements are common. And there are a variety of conditions that EXA results can assist in identifying; When a baseline is established with EXA a first easy step is taken to resolve a complex problem. With EXA it is easy to track a patient's progress because dynamic changes show in the tissue first.

EXA Test is an early detector of a variety of conditions related to magnesium deficiency, calcification of tissue and overall mineral electrolyte imbalance. EXA results can assist in an early diagnosis of Hyperparathyroidism, osteoporosis not yet detected through bone scan, medication related metabolic changes, and the impact of some medications that either suppress or retain some elements creating symptoms not identifiable through blood tests.

For some patients dietary intake can complicate metabolism of essential elements. An example of excess dietary calcium intake are patients who have ingested for years large amounts of calcium to prevent osteoporosis without a balanced electrolyte protocol to regulate or modulate the excess. This practice is common and yet can lead to destructive high levels of calcium in the tissues. Early detection can assist in the prevention of complex diseases.

Altogether, EXA is a very useful diagnostic assay that assists the physicians in following their patients closely. EXA encourages treatment and patient compliance. ICD's EXA can assist you and your patients with solutions to short as well as long term problems and in obtaining desirable results that contribute to quality of life.

ICD is CLIA licensed and HIPPA compliant. IntraCellular Diagnostics is contracted with Medicare. EXA is covered by Medicare and most insurance. We do not accept Medicaid as primary insurance. We are a "Specialty Reference Lab" so out of network coverage for private insurance is available with pre-authorization and depending on patient contract.

Feel free to contact ICD if you have additional questions.

Sincerely,

Physician Services

IntraCellular Diagnostics, Inc.

945 Town Centre Drive

Suite A

Medford, Oregon 97504

[www.exatest.com](http://http://www.exatest.com)

541-245-3212

.............

>

> From the Exatest Interpretation Guide:
>
>
>
> **HIGH CALCIUM
>
> Elevated tissue calcium may be a sign of mobilization of bone calcium into soft
>
> tissues signaling early signs of developing osteoporosis. High intracellular calcium
>
> interferes with ATP formation, muscle contraction, relaxation, enzyme activity, and
>
> neuromuscular transmission.
>
> Increased cellular calcium predisposes to spasm of peripheral arterioles leading to
>
> increased blood pressure. Calcium may also be a factor in plaque formation,
>
> angina, hypertension and athero-arteriosclerosis. Calcium channel blockers as well
>
> as magnesium affects movement of calcium into the soft tissues and heart muscle.
>
> Parathyroid hormone, which regulates calcium, is increased when magnesium is
>
> low. PTH is lowered when magnesium stores are high.** Hypomagnesia occurs
>
> commonly in hyperthyroidism.
>
> Hypercalcemia is seen in malignant neoplasms (with or without bone involvement),
>
> primary and tertiary hyperparathyroidism, sarcoidosis, vitamin D intoxication,
>
> Paget's disease of bone, thyrotoxicosis, acromegaly, and the diuretic phase of renal
>
> acute tubular necrosis.
>
> In summary: Elevated intracellular calcium might indicate loss of calcium from
>
> skeletal tissues and might possibly indicate that the adequate intake of daily
>
> calcium be maintained or increased, depending on the judgment and diagnosis of
>
> the physician.**

Those would be the parts that worry me.... I'm not taking calcium so the calcium is coming from somewhere else than my nutritional intake..... my bones? I'm trying to HEAL my bones!! Now, it could just be the other option, which is that I'm low on mg and PTH is increased due to that, increasing Calcium. How to find out?

How is your vitamin D level? Also, are you taking vitamin K2? Dr. K says a healthy vitamin D level causes calcium to be absorbed and that vitamin K2 is what causes it to be absorbed into the bone.

As you already stated, the reason you are having problems with your Mg level is that high BG causes Mg to be sequestered into the cells of the body. Can't remember if it was Dr. K or someone else, but I have read that to decrease BG you need a high protein diet with low glycemic carbs.

You are correct, it just takes time to undo the deficiency. Just keep on trucking and before you know it your BG will come down and your Mg levels will improve.

Isn't it aggravating that we just can't flip a switch and fix all of this stuff.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T21:52:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exatest.1353/post-35526>

[http://chriskresser.com/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders](http://http://chriskresser.com/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders)

> My current approach with these patients is to do a cautious trial of raising their serum levels to a range of 60-70 ng/mL. If their symptoms improve at this level, I will then switch them to a maintenance dose while watching for clinical signs of vitamin D toxicity. These include kidney stones (also a sign of vitamin K2 deficiency), low appetite, nausea, vomiting, thirst, excessive urination, weakness and nervousness. I will also monitor serum calcium levels, because **elevated calcium in the blood is a sign of vitamin D toxicity** and a significant risk factor for cardiovascular disease (especially in the presence of vitamin K2 deficiency). Calcium levels above 11-12 mg/dL (or 2.8-3 mmol/L) are indicative of vitamin D toxicity.

Free T4 1.0 (.8-1.8)

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-10T16:34:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-up-for-hacking.1376/post-22816>

Do you know where you're at in your cycle via temps or CM? that might help.

your DHEA is low, your E is low (btw was this hsEstradiol or just the regular one?)

your P is low as well.

T is ok, I think you want in more in the middle ranges (but in general all your hormones are in the toilet!)

You're missing Pregnenolone, alderstrone - not sure if they were run - but if they were they some times are the last set of labs to come in.

Your also missing some thyroid test: Free T3, rT3, - hard to determine where you are at w/o a full set of labs.

initial guess - is you've probably got some pregnenolone steal going on, as a result of your adrenal fatigue - in addition to your Hashis...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-11T00:40:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-up-for-hacking.1376/post-23034>

Agree w/ almost all that has been said here! Although I think you may need more Testosterone as I thought optimal was around 60. Don't quote me on that but I had that in my notes although not sure who said that so the source could be bad.

If I were you, I would go to a natural dessicated thyroid (NDT) for the Hashis unless you have a rT3 ratio that's too low. My ratio was low so in March I switched from NDT to T3 only to help clear the rT3. I plan on going back on the NDT if everything goes to plan. However, if you have low cortisol/adrenal function than T3 may be a good choice! I have done just about everything there is to do w/Hashis/Hypo to help it, except T4 only drugs (which I won't take). I have taken almost every NDT out there and now have my favorite brand of what works best for me.

Progesterone affects thyroid and low progesterone can lead to low thyroid hormones. So make sure your progesterone is optimal but still balanced with the estrogen. The pregnenolone will also affect progesterone. I'm not a sex hormone expert but all those things are interconnected. Also I beleive the doc said that any DHEA under 160 is deficient.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-31T20:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-up-for-hacking.1376/post-28667>

Agree about the thyroid. Definitely need testing of FT3 and rT3 to check that conversion. Also my guess is that you will have a low FT3 based on the symptoms you have reported and you will need to add in some meds. It took me three years and four docs and no one would write a script with confirmed Hashis antibodies and a TSH over 4, just like yours. I self-treated until I finally found a wonderful BHRT doc. Be proactive when you go see this endo. I personally have had the worst time with endos so hopefully your experience will be better. Also even though your basic iron looks good your ferritin is very low and your iron sat % should be around 35%. Definitely need that offal! Good luck!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-04T23:49:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-up-for-hacking.1376/post-29424>

Just had super fun appt w endo -NOT. Geez. He basically said that he doesn't know why I'm not making hormones and has no advice for me. Then he gave me a hard time about seeing the func med dr. Which I don't really know how he knew about that. I did bring my lab work with me but he didn't look at at it end the end of appt. I did see the med student first and he looked at them. Idk. The med student told me my cholesterol was a little high. I said I wasn't worried and didn't you make hormones from cholesterol? He said yes. I said I'd take the extra cholesterol for now. The endo first said the pituitary cyst was not big enough to be causing my problems but then he looked at the MRI again and sort of changed his mind. But I think it's an incidental finding. I told him I stopped eating gluten and he asked me why. I said gluten and Hashimotos could be related. He said they are. So why are you asking why I quit gluten? You should be telling me to quit! Plus he gave me a hard time about switching to natural thyroid. He said "you want pig?" "who knows what's in that." he'd rather have chemicals. Whatever. Then he asked what the func med doc recommended and when I said elimination diet he pretty much rolled his eyes. I guess he doesn't think food matters. Frustrating. He suggested I go on back to the gyn or go on a birth control pill if I want to cycle. Umm, that's not the freaking point! He sent me away for 6 months w lab work before. He's going to check TSH, free T 4, prolactin and morning cortisol. Seems like he should check the cortisol now but whatever. And repeat MRI in 9 months. I will keep up w func med doc and new PMD in Aug and idk maybe I should step up to a gun who specializes in hormones. Or maybe that's overkill? Endo def seemed annoyed that "someone else" was measuring my thyroid antibodies. Drs are so territorial! Then he wonders why I pay out of pocket - umm to be treated like a whole person! I told him I've gains 15#s. do you think he cares? No but it's a big freaking deal to me! Ugh. Sorry rant over.

# Thread: Confused and Pondering Next Step
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 54
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rosekelly — 2012-05-10T20:10:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22930>

So I love CT. I was doing ice baths daily, gradually lowering the temp and immersing more of my body. I've also done three oceans swims in the last two weeks, temp 54. But, as I've mentioned here before, I have surgery scheduled later this month for endometrial cancer. I did think CT and abdominal icing would help me prepare for surgery, as well as healing my body in other ways that would help me deal with this and also prevent future cancer.

But every time I ice, my belly swells, and I get some cramping. Ice is not supposed to cause inflammation. But there you have it.

I don't know if I should ice or not. I'm baffled.

This type of cancer is estrogen-sensitive. I started taking DIM and Pro-gest natural cream last week. Less swelling, but I was also doing less icing. Now I'm icing again. Swelling and cramps again.

What is causing this swelling?

I don't want to ask Jack on the ask Jack thread, because it seems inappropriate to ask him questions directly related to my personal case of illness. However, I have no one else to ask. When I last saw the oncologist, I asked her what this fluid was all about, and she shrugged. "Just the disease," she said, as if it was immaterial. Well, it's not immaterial to me!

What I'm trying to figure out is if icing is safe in this situation, and post-surgery as well. Is it a separate issue from CT causing toxins to be released, including excess estrogen? Will it ever be safe for me to CT if I'm prone to cancer? Is weight loss even safe, since that releases toxins and estrogen too? Does this swelling even have anything to do with CT detoxing? Or is it something else entirely? You see my dilemma.

I'm not looking for professional medical guidance with this question, because I understand that's not the purpose of this forum. I'm exploring ideas, hoping to come up with a better understanding of what's happening in my body. I don't expect anyone out there to actually know the exact answer, but some help pondering the situation would help. Thanks!

### ealachan — 2012-05-10T20:21:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22938>

So you only get the swelling and cramping when you ice directly, but not when you do submersion CT?

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-10T20:26:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22941>

Ah, good question, thanks. I was getting it daily when I was doing submersion in ice baths, which I've stopped a week ago, around the same time I added DIM and Pro-Gest. Now I've begun spot icing, and I'm getting it again.

Interestingly, after my last ocean dunk five days ago, I didn't get it at all. I can't remember if I did after the earlier two. Hmm!

### vkiernan — 2012-05-10T20:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22958>

Yes on the estrogen dump and if you are not getting rid of it (elimination) it stays circulating in your system from what I've read. I agree with MJ, you need to ask Jack. If he can't answer you directly, he won't. I remember Quelson and Dr. K talking about dumping a ton of estrogen during CT but don't know about the swelling. I can tell you since CT'ing, my gut is working and I don't have the bloat/swelling like I did. I dumped a lot of estrogen and it was a bxxxx in the process.

Ask him for sure but do your own n=1 with ice vs. cold water. Maybe try the water at different temps too. I feel for you. Hope all goes well.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-10T21:02:09+0300

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-10T21:09:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22965>

If you really didn't get it during your ocean dunks, that makes me think about Earthing. The ocean or any natural body of water is grounded to the earths energy. When we connect with this water or stand barefoot on the ground the electrical currents in our body become negatively charged which has a very strong anti-inflammatory effect.

Your belly swelling does seem contrary to what we'd all expect to hear. I hope Dr. K will offer some insight for you.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-10T21:28:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22968>

I posted a question about the swelling on Ask Jack. Also, I googled endometrial cancer symptoms, and one is abdominal swelling. Don't know how I missed that. So I'm going to assume for a moment that this has nothing to do with estrogen and is only related to the cancer. So why does ice cause more of it? This is fascinating.

PaleoMom, I'm intrigued by the ocean affect. Since starting these swims, I'm over-the-head in love with the ocean and am sure it's good for me in many ways. I only wish I could go more often, and perhaps I will soon. I've always been an ardent barefooter, walk in my yard barefoot all summer, walk my dog barefoot (I'm the neighborhood hippie), but maybe I need bigger doses of earthing, and the ocean is the way to go.

And no, I really didn't get it after my last (third) ocean dunk. I remember feeling happy about that. The earlier two, it's hard to tell, because I was taking ice baths every day and also spot icing. But at the time of this last ocean dunk, I'd quit the other for a few days. It was a singular event. And no swelling.

This may be the key? Also, the ocean has so many important minerals. Another key? Okay, now I want to get over to the ocean again pronto!

### AKMan — 2012-05-10T22:28:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22991>

If I had cancer of any type I would:

a) Daily CT for 30-60 minutes as cold as I could get the water, (below 45 degress for sure)

b) Eat a fully ketogenic diet full of nutritious vegetables and loaded with fish

c) Fast for 48 hours once a week

That's just me.

Have you read Jacks blog about cancer? [http://jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with-new-cancer-diagnosis/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with-new-cancer-diagnosis/)

The comments section of that blog might be a good place to ask your question if you phrase it in general terms.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-10T22:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-22994>

Okay, Dr. Jack answered:

"not too surprising.....someone with cancer has increase inflammation and toxins in lymph and fat. CT will liberate more of them and cause more surface changes. Improve the diet and look at the supplements and read this blog [http://jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with...cer-diagnosis/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with...cer-diagnosis/)

"Using CT detox diet and supplements should be considered by you and your oncologist. Discuss all options with them before implementation. I think the ocean swims are best too."

I have read his cancer post. I do supplement as advised. I'm fully keto and have been for several weeks, my diet consisting of only grass-fed meat, wild fish, coconut oil, cruciferous veggies, homemade sauerkraut, and occasional mushrooms. Green tea. Water.

Akman, so you would go with the ice baths even though they aggravate the swelling? I'm game, just unsure still.

Why fast? Or is that on Jack's cancer blog. I'll go re-read it now. Thanks!

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-11T00:06:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23030>

I read Jack's cancer blog again. I'm going to assume any estrogen-dump concern I have is small potatoes compared to the benefits of CT, and I'll hope that the supps I'm taking will blunt any estrogen dump anyway. So back to daily CT baths and icing with some ocean swims mixed in.

The swelling and cramps were rather uncomfortable, at times causing me to take pain meds. This is so weird. Ice causing swelling and pain! But I am going to assume this is the best course to take.

45 degrees, Akman? Yikes. That will take a humongous amount of ice. And iron will.

### AKMan — 2012-05-11T04:07:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23076>

45 degrees. Unless you can get your water colder! 32 degrees if possible. But, really, I would go for as cold as I could get.

As to the fasting, I think that fasting is a pretty well-studied factor in beating cancer. Read this: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2815756/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2815756/)

It says, in part: "Previous human studies have shown that alternate day dietary restriction and short-term fasting (5 days) are well tolerated and safe \[10-12\]. In fact, children ranging from 6 months to 15 years of age were able to complete 14 to 40 hours of fasting in a clinical study carried out at the Children's hospital of Philadelphia \[13\]. Furthermore, alternate day calorie restriction caused clinical improvements and reduced markers of inflammation and oxidative stress in obese asthmatic patients \[12,14\]. Here, we report 10 cases of patients diagnosed with various types of cancer, who have voluntarily fasted prior to and following chemotherapy. The results presented here, which are based on self-assessed health outcomes (Table ​(Table1)1) and laboratory reports, suggest that fasting is safe and raise the possibility that it can reduce chemotherapy-associated side effects. However, only a randomized controlled clinical trial can establish its efficacy. "

And this: [http://www.worldhealth.net/news/every\_other\_day\_fasting\_may\_reduce\_cance/](http://http://www.worldhealth.net/news/every_other_day_fasting_may_reduce_cance/)

And, Mark Sisson at Mark's Daily Apple did a great series on fasting recently: [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/fasting-cancer/#axzz1uWFrh4VY](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/fasting-cancer/#axzz1uWFrh4VY)

Also, I have done 32 degree baths - Its harder to get the nerve to get in, but the joy is its actually easier!

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-11T07:46:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23111>

I agree with AKman's suggestions and would add homebrew kombucha to that mix. That would be important to me in your situation. I would also look into earthing more and stick with the ocean CT until you stop having the swelling from other CT. Do you live close to the ocean? If so I would really try to do that daily and for as long as your body can tolerate. Get lots of vitamin D from the sun too, so important to help your body fight this off.

Hugs to you. I think if you do the things suggested in this thread you'll do great and probably blow your docs away.

### Inger — 2012-05-11T09:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23119>

Rosekelly,

the sister of my brothers wife had ovarian cancer and fasted, 30 days on vegetable juices and herb-teas only (Breuss-fast, you can google it). She had no money to go to the doctor (she lives in Serbia, Europe) and my brother who lives in Schweden, sent her the juices/fasting-packages (you can buy the whole package for the Breuss-fast here) per post. She was really really weak and bleeded a LOT. She still fasted, and got really thin.. but she got a lot better and the bleeding stopped! After the fast she went to an acupuncturist, who gave her foot-massage. He pushed on a special point under her feet and it hurted quite a lot. After the treatment she got really bad pain in her stomach and went to the bathroom. There she realesed a big lump.. probably the ovarian tumor..? After that, she was 100% well, and it is a few years ago and she still ist healthy. I know this sounds crazy but it is true.

Also her father had prostate cancer and did the same long fast, got healed.

They eat a normal diet otherwise, not perfect so to say.. but still they remain healed. Amazing.

I do think fasting can help to heal cancer. Maybe the vegetable juices or herb teas helped too? I do not know..

Rosekelly,

try to eat some wild edibles/herbs/berries every day! They are really healing! If you do not know what kind are edible I can send you some pics of the ones I eat regularely. They grow everywhere! Wild food are absolutely the most healing ever! Try to eat mostly wild if you can..

And sun.. yes. Try to get sun ALL over your body! You need to be totally naked, cause certain parts that never get sun really are the places that needs it a lot, it helps the hormones, and it does the body so much good. You just have to find a place where no one can see you.. and stay in the sun as much as you can. I tan naked as much as possible, I am happy to have a yard where no neighbours can see me.

Inger

### kathylu — 2012-05-11T15:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23149>

Hi Roskelly,

I think CT can only help your situation, but it seems ocean is best if you can do it. You'll get lots of trace minerals and a lot of magnesium that way. I also think you could use a LOT more progesterone than you can get from Pro-Gest cream. Progesterone prevents endometrial hyperplasia, opposes the action of estrogen (very important if you are dumping estrogen from the CT) and is a natural diuretic. Read books by Uzzi Reiss, MD or Sergey Dzugan, MD or Thierry Hertoghe, MD. All have excellent explanations of estrogen/progesterone balancing and the reasons it works well. Then get a prescription from your doctor for bioidentical progesterone cream. I use 1 ml of 100mg/ml progesterone per day (no bloating since I started). Your other steroid hormones may need balancing as well, but I'm sure that has to wait until after your surgery. Are you having a hysterectomy/oophorechtomy? You will need to become an expert on the hormone replacement issue, because you cannot depend on your doctors knowing the latest information. Sad, but true.

Also, follow Jack's advice about cancer. Don't eat any sugar, ever (cancer LOVES sugar). IF may be useful too. Tons of green veggies.

Let us know how you are progressing...

### kathylu — 2012-05-11T15:47:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23151>

I'm sure each situation is different, but my sister-in-law had a complete hysterectomy in January due to endometrial cancer. Her doctor has her on BHRT (estriol/progesterone/testosterone/dhea/pregnenolone) post surgery, to deal with the hormonal fall-out. So, there ARE doctors who get it out there...

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-11T18:40:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23191>

Thank you all for your help. It's great to get all of your wisdom and experience. Now I have to sort it out and choose.

Ocean swims vs. deep CT with ice baths. I'm thinking I'll start driving over to the ocean every morning. Even though it's a 45 min drive, it's still less onerous than creating daily baths cold enough to qualify as deep CT. I'm waking early anyway. I can eat my BAB, drive over, swim for 30 min, and get back by 10. I make my own hours, so I can still fit my work schedule in. I start today, although later, because I have a mammogram this morning. Blowing off work entirely today. First things first.

Fasting? I can do one day a week. Not juice fasting though, due to sugars. My job, as I understand it, is to keep insulin as low as possible. I believe your SIL healed from it, Inger, but these things are personal, and I don't have a good feeling about juice fasting, so I don't think it will work for me. Is this foolish of me? I don't know! Nude sunbathing I can do though.

Kombucha is also out, for same reason, sugars. I was making it but decided to err on the side of caution, and stopped. I eat homemade saeurkraut every morning.

As for getting a prescription for bio-identical progesterone, I'm doubtful either of my docs will give me one, but I'll try today. My oncologist is the one who wants to remove my ovaries because "You don't need them at your age." !!! (I respectfully disagreed, and she has agreed to leave them unless she sees they are obviously diseased.) My other wonderful paleo doc who I saw for a consult says she isn't comfortable prescribing BHRT hormones to me without my oncologist's consent. She was talking post-op. Maybe pre-op, she might. Time for phone calls.

I think I covered everything as of now. You all are marvelous!

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-11T19:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23197>

Good luck Rose. Maybe lowcarb vegetable juice is a good idea. I would try fasting too and make sure you drink plenty of water. Enjoy the ocean!

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-11T19:11:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23198>

Called Paleo doc's office. Nurse told me the doc is generally against prescribing anything at all until the cancer itself is dealt with. Would never just prescribe progesterone alone anyway. Will have the doc call me to discuss further.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-11T19:13:14+0300

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-11T19:25:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23205>

> **Rosekelly;23281 wrote:** I do have a juicer because I was once into juicing. So maybe I could juice cruciferous veggies only. Sounds icky. Broccoli juice? But I'll consider it.

I think all leafy greens are lowcarb and high in nutrients? celery, cilantro, parsley? Just whatever you want and can eat, drink a glass of it!  From what I read, when we fast and lose weight, our body utilizes the bad/sick cells as energy - just like when winter comes the older leaves turn yellow and fall first - our body knows how to prioritize when it has to use some cells as energy to survive - the healthy and most important are saved while the ugly and broken are used. I don't know who "scientific" this idea is, but it makes very good sense to me. Besides, you may just starve the cancer cells to death!

### Birdy — 2012-05-11T19:52:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-23210>

I am not an expert by any means, but if I was in your situation, I would try to get the estrogen dump before the surgery instead of after. Listen to Robb Wolf's May 8 podcast about pms. For me, the problem was estrogen clearance, and until I have that licked, I am not moving forward with any HRT since I don't have a knowledgeable doc who can work with me on this. I needed to make sure that my insulin sensitivity was rock solid before adding in any other type of hormone. I started CT gradually and didn't get the high estrogen dump issues, but I was also working to manage the insulin with a more pristine lowcarb diet. (Think Bernstein/Rosedale over Wolf). I wouldn't do vegetable juices, but would drink a kale/celery shake in smaller quantities. Now that I know more about CT, I am trying to use that to solve the imbalance issues instead of even considering HRT. But, off to read more about estrogen clearance...

### Birdy — 2012-05-11T19:56:29+0300

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-15T18:22:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-24246>

Update: I'm no longer confused. Yay! I'm ocean swimming as much as I possibly can, which was twice this weekend. I'll be going the next three, maybe four days too. I want to get in five days a week. These cold ocean waters are intense! I love it.

I also did a 48-hour juice fast with cruciferous veggies. Felt great, and it was easy, no hunger. I'm planning to fast every 5 days for 48 hours.

Birdy, I'm eating homemade sauerkraut every day. And the question of when to dump toxins, before or after surgery, was something I couldn't figure out, but a friend of mine had a consult with Dr. K. on Saturday, she was gracious enough to ask that question on my behalf. He said, now, now, now. Just do it. Get rid of the crap. Jump in the ocean as often as possible. So now I'm comfortable with my path.

### Rosekelly — 2012-06-02T09:30:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29026>

Update: I had my surgery Tuesday. It went perfectly. The doc thinks she got all the cancer, says it looks like a stage one with no infiltration,and will know more with the lab report. And I kept my ovaries!

My recovery is going easily. Not much pain at all. The anesthesia really knocked me loopy more than anything else, but I'm feeling normal again. The incisions are tiny, it was all done laparascopically. The whole thing was amazingly easy with the best possible outcome.

I'm glad I did all that CT in the ocean beforehand. I can't tell how it affected the cancer directly, but obviously it did no harm. I felt really, really healthy and vigorous going into the surgery, and now, four days later, I'm almost back to normal. I have to wait two weeks until I can submerge myself in a bath (or ocean!), the doc says, but I can spot ice, and I have been, even did it at the hospital post-surgery. They thought it was funny but didn't stop me.

I'm sold on ice forever. Onward to balancing out all my hormones, with ovaries intact! Thank you, Dr. Kruse, for all your info here, which alerted me to the importance of my ovaries. God, I love my ovaries. I never gave them a second thought my entire life, and now I want to buy them flowers and sing to them.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-02T16:22:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29039>

Congratulations on the surgery going well! fingers crossed that there was no spreading and your labs are boring as heck!

### Rosekelly — 2012-06-03T21:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29246>

Yeah, Jan, the whole thing was amazingly easy. Thanks, love. Let's go to the ocean in a week and a half!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-04T01:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29265>

> **Rosekelly;29345 wrote:** Dr. Kruse, this is the kind of thought that changes my DNA. I appreciate you expressing it to me, because it's absolutely true and exactly the right words to spur action toward optimal health and life.

+1 Rosekelly. I'm also post-cancer (just under 2 years ago, surgery cleared it) and am totally embracing my 'clean slate.' Inflammation is always part of my focus... so in addition to the keto/seasonal paleo with CT and light cycles, I take turmeric in large doses (boiled for 10 minutes to increase bioavailability) daily and do breathing/meditation too. I get out for at least one (preferably two) walks every day, too. Those ocean swims you're doing are perfect! Enjoy your entire life as much as you're enjoying your ovaries right now. Cancer - any health crisis really - can be as much the 'invited teacher' as any other experience... as long as we learn! Great to hear your good news and updates!

### Rosekelly — 2012-06-04T23:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29419>

Thanks for chiming in with your history, KiwiLauren. Yay for clean slates! How do you ingest the turmeric? What is the large dose? I've been taking circumin as a supplement, but it would probably be cheaper to take turmeric every day in my food.

I walk daily too, and can't wait to return to the ocean soon. I meditate off and on, but for some reason keep forgetting to. I'm going to do go it right now.

(I didn't know turmeric should be boiled. Good tip.)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-05T00:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29428>

> **Rosekelly;29519 wrote:** Thanks for chiming in with your history, KiwiLauren. Yay for clean slates! How do you ingest the turmeric? What is the large dose? I've been taking circumin as a supplement, but it would probably be cheaper to take turmeric every day in my food.
>
>
>
> I walk daily too, and can't wait to return to the ocean soon. I meditate off and on, but for some reason keep forgetting to. I'm going to do go it right now.
>
>
>
> (I didn't know turmeric should be boiled. Good tip.)

On one of the liver threads, CoolingWeb posted a published bit of research showing the increased efficacy of curcurmin when it has been boiled for 10 minutes. My supplement budget is really limited so I use turmeric and use this research to make it more effective. Basically, I take about a heaping tablespoon per day. The way I make it is pour about 5 T into a pan with water (after it's boiled, it ends up being about an inch of water in a glass per day). I then boil it for 10 minutes, then pour it into a glass jar and put it in the fridge. Each morning for the next 5 days I pour 1/5 of the jar into a glass, add pepper (which also helps increase it's availability/activity) and drink it down with my morning meal in a few big glugs. It's not tasty, but it's not bad and only takes a few seconds. I like that it's cheap and I get the curcurmin from food, not a supplement. You can also squeeze fresh ginger into it, as well as cinnamon.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-05T06:01:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29488>

So glad to hear it went well, RoseKelly!

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-05T06:14:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-and-pondering-next-step.1384/post-29492>

You can also squeeze fresh ginger into it, as well as cinnamon.

Well now I know how to get more turmeric in me!! Thanks for the tip!

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-05T06:14:34+0300
