### DarleenMB — 2012-05-14T18:32:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23917>

How old are you? My first thought is ... MENOPAUSE.

Or maybe perimenopause.

Anytime a woman starts talking about sleep issues, the very first thing their doc should consider is the above. Sleep disturbances and depression are hallmarks of menopause.

Ask me how I know. Go ahead. Ask me.

I have recommended these books before and I'll keep doing it until every woman I know has read them. TS Wiley's **Sex, lies & menopause and any of the late Dr. John R. Lee's books which includes What your doctor may not tell you about menopause**.

Going to bed earlier won't help. Getting your hormones back to where they were when you were in your 20's, will. Again. Ask me how I know.

Menopause is NOT a dirty word. It is nothing to shy away from. It doesn't mean you are shriveled and dried up and worthless. It is a natural course your body takes. We cannot stay young forever but we CAN stay youthful which is more about attitude than about birthdays. I mean, picture this. I'm zooming down the road in my minivan rockin' out to Lady Gaga. Now COME ON! I intend to rock it right straight to the grave. And I do hope it's a long ways off but I refuse to get OLD.

My unasked for advice? Get those hormone levels checked.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-14T20:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23959>

LOL. I am on the Wiley Protocol. Which means I use estradiol and progesterone cream applied in the same rhythm that my body USED to make them. The dose varies depending on which day of my "cycle" I'm on. I say it that way 'cause I lost my parts about 15 years ago. so I'm on a lunar cycle.

and that's it for the hormones. It's taken nearly 10 months but my libido appears to have woken up and slipped her leash. Huh. Interesting, eh? Last time it was checked my testosterone was zero so I figured that's was it for Lady L. Then I found myself checking out some long, tall gorgeous hunk in the checkout line. Um. hello? Where did THAT come from.

I wander.

I cannot recommend the Wiley protocol highly enough. I feel fabulous and my poor old brain is sorta working again. No more brain fog, really GREAT sleep (thanks to the addition of my blu-blockers at night). I may not be 20 anymore but I sure feel good for the shape I'm in.

### Birdy — 2012-08-25T19:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47044>

I have been doing very well with using a sleep mask for the first part of the night. When I put it on, all distractions seem to disappear. I will be sewing more sleep masks. Recently I went on a trip, and forgot my mask, and had a tough couple of nights depending on where I stayed. A couple of nights I was treated to a nice dark air-conditioned room and had wonderful sleep. But at other times, I was stuck with the hotel lights, the flashing green smoke detector, the red letters on the alarm clock, and some really annoying blue led light coming out of the bathroom. These lights are impossible to turn off, and I found myself like a kid, making a tent around my sleeping area to block it all out, 'course then it was hot.

I am becoming more and more picky about the bedroom. I have cleaned much of the clutter out of it so if I have to stumble out of bed at night I don't trip over anything. I make sure not to store anything dusty or in orange crates (they are coated with pesticides). I make sure I have enough water by the late afternoon. I have a stash of books and magazines, which along with music, I enjoy before bed. I even have special knitting projects. I have to pick out all the colors during the daytime, and this is also the time to figure out patterns, do the gage, and start the tricky parts. Eventually I hope to create a small area of the bedroom devoted to spinning, since it is such a wonderful craft that can be done pretty well with no light. But right now, I need to be considerate of the allergy issue. The other parts of my home are still occupied by modern peoples, and right now if I want to spin, I have to venture into the land of the fluorescents.

### Pamela — 2012-08-25T19:17:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47048>

> **Birdy;47468 wrote:** I will be sewing more sleep masks.

Whenever I've tried masks they are tight or feel bad on my head. Will you be offering them for sale?

### Birdy — 2012-08-25T19:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47059>

Oh, I don't think I would be that good. My current sleep mask is stretched out and so it is comfy. It doesn't block out all of the light, though. I think for me, putting the mask on is just like calming things down. When I wear it, I just want to keep my eyes closed even though I am wide awake. It also helps keep my eyes from drying out. After I have had a long day outside, I like to put drops in my eyes and then put on the mask.

For the ones that I am planning to make, they will be made out of cotton, and I might use just a bit of batting in it to keep it soft and flexible, but unfortunately the batting doesn't really block light as well. I don't really have that much light, just street lights and a hallway light when it is too hot to close the door. What I really need is something to encourage me to keep my eyes closed, and my eyelids will block the rest of the light. My plan is to make the mask part the same as the airline masks, and then crochet an over-the-head strap system similar to a headlamp. That way it will stay in place better without the need for tightness.

Try overstretching the elastic on your masks and see if that doesn't help. My fave mask has no stretch left at all. I have tied the excess elastic into a knot to get the perfect size. I put one band underneath my hair, and the other band around the upper part of my head.

# Thread: Big Picture: Infradian Rhythm Applied to Vit-D/Carbs/CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T18:51:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23921>

I've read almost everything I could on Leptin RX, Paleo Diet, CT and a lot of the comments (there are a ton!) but I haven't really found a complete guide that unifies all the theories and puts them in the context of **Infradian rhythms** as a complete annual system... Especially with regards to Vitamin D and CT.

There's a lot of discussion of circadian rhythms through out the site, but once you've mastered the basics and optimized yourself on the 24-hour cycle I think it's natural to turn our attention to the annual cycle...

1) Jack has already mentioned that he cycles carbs from low (zero) in the winter months to slightly higher during the summer (Jack's FB Yamsagna), but I'm still not sure what (Jack's) "higher carb" generally means... I would imagine it's something along Paul Jaminet's "Perfect Health Diet" with about 100-150 grams of carbs from "safe starches", however, I don't think Jack has gone into detail about the summer diet yet, has he? I mean, you could easily put away 200+ grams of carbs from that yamsagna alone (haha)... Did I just miss it?

2) It seems like we should also abide by an infradian rhythm of Vit-D as well, just like we do with cabs... Higher levels of Vit-D (supplemental/natural sun exposure) during the summer while consuming higher levels of carbs, lower levels of Vitamin D (No supplementation?) during the winter with zero carbs? In fact, according to an interesting article on Vit-D by [Dr. Grominak](http://http://drgominak.com/vitamin-d), the blood levels should vary with the seasons and diet; summer being the building and growing season and winter being the slow living on stores sleeping season... If we supplement Vit-D during the winter (low carb, CT) are we actually doing harm?

3) My final question involves infradian rhythms with CT... my impression from reading CT 1-12 is that you do this year round, but shouldn't we stop this during the summer, when our carb intake and Vit-D levels are higher in nature? Wouldn't this create a mis-match?

Thanks in advance for helping me clarify the "Big Picture", maybe Jack will chime in on a few of these ;-)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-05-14T20:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23957>

I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:

1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.

2) If your vitamin D deficient, supplement all year and get the extra sun in the summer. If you're not deficient, just cycle with the season. (In my experience, the vast majority are deficient, however.)

3) If you're obese and sickly, you need CT year round to reverse disease and calm inflammation. If you're completely healthy, maybe you can get by with less CT in the summer.

Nice "horizontal" thinking JJtitus.

Recently, I just started asking myself the following: what am I doing in my life currently that's not in balance with my genetic, hormonal, and hypothalamic biology and then I try to rectify the mismatches.

### NickieG — 2012-05-14T21:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23968>

> **Dan in Utah;24039 wrote:** I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:
>
>
>
> 1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.

Dan....would you say more about carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage? I don't understand what that means.

Thanks

Nickie

### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T21:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23977>

> **NickieG;24050 wrote:** Dan....would you say more about carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage? I don't understand what that means.
>
> Thanks
>
> Nickie

I think what Dan means by eating from a palette based on "genetic heritage" is to choose carb/starch sources that your ancestors traditionally ate... for instance, if you were of Irish heritage you might use potatoes preferentially, or if you were of Asian descent you might choose white rice. I think taking this approach with your diet in general is probably a good basis... That being said, I wouldn't exclude other foods entirely just because it isn't part of your particular ancestor's traditional diet.

### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T22:23:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23985>

> **Dan in Utah;24039 wrote:** I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:
>
>
>
> 1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.
>
>
>
> 2) If your vitamin D deficient, supplement all year and get the extra sun in the summer. If you're not deficient, just cycle with the season. (In my experience, the vast majority are deficient, however.)
>
>
>
> 3) If you're obese and sickly, you need CT year round to reverse disease and calm inflammation. If you're completely healthy, maybe you can get by with less CT in the summer.

Dan, that's similar to the question I've been asking myself lately and that led to my 3 infradian rhythm discussions... the article in #2 actually was the catalyst for all three, but I have been contemplating this issue for a while...

1) That's along the lines of what I thought too... although initially Jack made it seem like VLC + CT was the optimal state (for increasing autophagy, lengthening telomeres), it seems like that's shifting to a more seasonal diet.... but I've also heard that every gram of sugar your body uses for energy instead of fat will make you age faster (shorten telomeres). So, which is it? Does the seasonal increase in blood Vit-D protect you from the damaging effects of a higher carbohydrate (sugar) diet? Or, do we just take a bit of a hit on telomere length over the summer and it's no big deal? I feel like I'm still missing something here...

2) I completely agree that you should supplement AFTER getting your blood levels checked, aiming for the 60-80 ng/ml range... but I still think there might be something to having lower levels of Vit-D during the winter than summer, or maybe just higher (80 ish ng/ml) levels during the summer while you're consuming more carbs. From the article it seems like declining levels of Vit-D (shorter days, lower sunlight intensity) along with elevated carb intake during the Fall could facilitate putting on fat stores for the winter.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-05-14T22:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23998>

> 1) That's along the lines of what I thought too... although initially Jack made it seem like VLC + CT was the optimal state (for increasing autophagy, lengthening telomeres), it seems like that's shifting to a more seasonal diet.... but I've also heard that every gram of sugar your body uses for energy instead of fat will make you age faster (shorten telomeres). So, which is it? Does the seasonal increase in blood Vit-D protect you from the damaging effects of a higher carbohydrate (sugar) diet? Or, do we just take a bit of a hit on telomere length over the summer and it's no big deal? I feel like I'm still missing something here...

I think these are essentially unanswerable at this juncture. Doubtless, Vitamin D is protective, but to what degree? Doubtless that advanced glycation endproducts are problematic. Until enough people have "before and after" telomeres, however, I feel a bit in the dark here, too. But if we take evolution as a guide, ketosis in winter, and near ketosis in the summer, is a reasonable target to shoot for. AGEs will bit your telomeres in the shorts, but so will ALEs. To me, it's a big battle against entropy. Eventually entropy is going to win out. Life is like a little air-pocket in a sea of entropy.

The best anyone can do is slow entropy down a bit by living in an optimal way. The essentials for me seem to be: night/day congruent sleep, low stress, paleo-style eating, cold exposure, sprinting and lifting and walking, with each component adapted to individual disease states and genetics.

>
>
> This sounds like it's in line with what we've discussed... higher levels of carbs (even up to 400g/day) during the height of summer if you are lean and fit, less if you still have weight to lose... my question would still be, even if you are lean and fit and CAN eat 400g of carbs a day, should you? That leads me to Dan's post:
>
>
>
> Dan in Utah;24080 wrote:** I think these are essentially unanswerable at this juncture. Doubtless, Vitamin D is protective, but to what degree? Doubtless that advanced glycation endproducts are problematic. Until enough people have "before and after" telomeres, however, I feel a bit in the dark here, too. But if we take evolution as a guide, ketosis in winter, and near ketosis in the summer, is a reasonable target to shoot for. AGEs will bit your telomeres in the shorts, but so will ALEs. To me, it's a big battle against entropy. Eventually entropy is going to win out. Life is like a little air-pocket in a sea of entropy.

My question would be; for a given amount of calories, which metabolic pathway (Glycolysis, Ketosis) produces the fewest oxidative end products (AGEs and ALEs, respectively)? I'm guessing it's ketosis (in general), but I'm getting the impression this changes with the seasons and it seems like Vit-D might play an important part...

# Thread: Lower freezing point with salt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-05-15T14:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24157>

If I want to lower the freezing point of tap water, how much salt would I need to add?

For instance; lowering 150litres from 0c to -2c.

### Onum — 2012-05-15T20:57:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24324>

> **primat;24239 wrote:** If I want to lower the freezing point of tap water, how much salt would I need to add?
>
>
>
> For instance; lowering 150litres from 0c to -2c.

Seawater which on average (according to Wikipedia) is 3.5% dissolved solids has a typical fp of -2 deg.C - although this isn't just Sodium Chloride of course -should be a good starting off point.

That would make it 5.25kg Salt in your 150l If you wanted to be really swish there is a product sold for marine aquariums called "instant ocean" or some such which AFAIK is just salt from evaporated sea water and is thus a more "natural" combination of salts, but I'm guessing won't be as economical.

### Onum — 2012-05-15T22:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24343>

Is your next question going to be "How the heck do I cool 150l of salt water to below freezing??"

I'm genuinely curious about this, I see you're in Norway but the weather I see isn't conducive to passive chilling to those temps.

If using ice you're going to need a shed-load\* (and of course that's water so it will dilute the salt and raise the fp) - you could use ice made from the same concentration solution to avoid a dilution effect)

\*By my calcs\*\* you would need over 20kg of -20Â°C ice to cool 150l of nominal 10Â°C water to around -2Â°C and it would have all melted by this point.

Of course you may have a cunning plan...

\*\*Not to be trusted

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-15T22:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24349>

This is what I was curious about. It is one thing to lower the freezing point. Completely another thing to get it to that new temp.

### primat — 2012-05-16T09:11:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24545>

> **Onum;24425 wrote:** How the heck do I cool 150l of salt water to below freezing??

Hehe - well, since ice is not a viable product here (you can get 2kg bags at around $8 at 7-eleven) I had to get ice somewhere else. The local fishmarket has a supplier that sells crushed ice at an affordable price pr kilo. I'm picking up 40kg today, just for a test. I thought I needed around 50kg? Anyways it's all going in

I havent got hold of salt yet, so I'll do this with just ice. It's probably going to be cold enough for a first deep ct-dip

### primat — 2012-05-16T09:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24547>

> **August;24433 wrote:** I wonder if adding salt or epson salts to the CT water would affect the rate at which heat is transferred from your body . . (I think sodium and magnesium are both technically considered metals) . . . I would guess not enough to make it worthwhile . . but it might be worth finding out for sure. .

Hmm, If it also would lower freezing temp I wouldn't mind trying it. Seems our local farm supplier has [bittersalt](http://http://www.felleskjopet.no/landbruk/Documents/Interne/Kraftfor_Plantekultur/Gj%C3%B8dsel/Produktdatablad/MAGNESIUM%20SULPHATE%207-HYDRATE%20FCC%202010-11-16%20MA2415.pdf) for bulk sale. Have to stop by and check price and availability...

Update: ordered 2x25kg bags of MgSO4Â·7H2O for a total of $75. Getting 50kg from our friendly neighbourhood store would set me back 100x500g bags @$17.5 = $1750  Ok, the stuff is more finely grained...

### Onum — 2012-05-16T13:29:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24554>

> **primat;24627 wrote:** Hehe - well, since ice is not a viable product here (you can get 2kg bags at around $8 at 7-eleven) I had to get ice somewhere else. The local fishmarket has a supplier that sells crushed ice at an affordable price pr kilo. I'm picking up 40kg today, just for a test. I thought I needed around 50kg? Anyways it's all going in
>
>
>
> I havent got hold of salt yet, so I'll do this with just ice. It's probably going to be cold enough for a first deep ct-dip

Well, it really depends on the temperature of the water you're trying to cool, 40kg of ice should be sufficient to cool 160l of 20Â°C water to about 0Â°. If the inititial temp of the water is about 10Â°C you may have to wait for half of it to melt to get down to your desired temp - it's the phase change of the ice from solid to liquid that will cool the water, any excess ice will act as a buffer to maintain the temp whilst your body tries to warm it up.

Good luck!

### primat — 2012-05-16T15:33:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24563>

Got to 0.9c after stirring and waiting a bit - I could have done with less ice too.

It felt less cold than the 8-10c tap water, that was a bit surprising. After 14 min I started shivering, and I got out at 18min. Warmed up faster than ever, maybe because duration was shorter than my usual 45 min @10c? 37.3c core temp before, 37.2c core temp after

# Thread: Ridin the wave. . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-05-15T16:16:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24198>

My microwave broke a couple of months ago. We've not replaced it. I wanted to see if we could go without - but to be honest, I haven't looked into the matter much. Some of our paleo/hippie type friends refuse to use them, but their reasons seem vague and silly sometimes (unlike sitting in ice, that's just plain cool  heh)

Aside from the superficial convention for "keeping up appearances" for professional reasons, most would think we've gone full hippie. No cosmetics, only homemade concoctionss. Outdoors earthing and attempting some sun with no sunscreen. Not letting our poor child have cake at parties. You, I think you know what I'm talkin about.

So is wave free a reasonable step? Ok in moderation? Never without aluminum foil hat?

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-15T16:28:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24202>

Never w/o a tinfoil hat, especially if you're watching ancient aliens....

seriously though - It depends on what works best for your family imo. We use a toaster oven to heat lots of stuff up...but sometimes its just easier/more convenient to use the microwave (especially when the sudden feed me now mommy monsters show up (patients not included in said monsters) also b/c both DH & I work full time... its hard to always have a fresh cooked meal w/in a reasonable time frame (dinner before 7pm) when we get home from work btwn 6-630!. I can prep meals on the weekend - or a day or two before hand...but then there's the whole reheat process... microwave makes dinner a little easier - probably not optimal - but it works for now...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-15T19:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24268>

We have a functioning microwave but I don't use it except to heat up water to put the environmentally safe "Oxy-Clean" in for the laundry! LOL I never use the micro for food any more. It's been over a year. We use the toaster oven as well when heating up food or even the stove top. Plus I eat food cold although the BF won't.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-15T19:31:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24272>

I store pots in my microwave and use it to melt coconut oil b/c it's warm in there.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-05-16T00:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24400>

No judgements on how bad the microwave is, but.... I pack all my husband's work lunches which he reheats in the microwave..... and I make sure they're always in glass, no plastic.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-16T01:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24429>

From what I've read I'd totally avoid eating anything microwaved. And once you go without for 6 months or more, you won't miss it (I'm on year 12 without one!).

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-16T03:15:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24459>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### freesia — 2012-05-16T04:39:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24495>

I haven't used ours in 2 years, and only rarely in the year or two before that after we adopted a Weston Price WOE. I have no idea why we still keep it around.

...Wait, it does have a nifty clock and timer we still rely on, that must be it...

I just use a stove element more often for reheating things, where as before I might have used the m.wave.

# Thread: Kruse Tracker
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-tracker.1473/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JC2K — 2012-05-15T17:03:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-tracker.1473/post-24219>

This is a bit on the geeky side, but a lot of the MMO games have Developer Trackers that will list any posts by the game developers with links to the actual post in the thread they responded to.

Example:

[http://www.swtor.com/community/devtracker.php](http://http://www.swtor.com/community/devtracker.php)

It would make me feel a bit stalkerlike, but can we get something like that for Jack? We can do something almost the same with the search functionality, but you can't see the new responses, and if the response is buried in a thread, you have to go through the whole thread to find the post to see if it is even something that you want to read (might not be pertinent to your interests).

### MJ* — 2012-05-15T19:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-tracker.1473/post-24276>

>
>
>
> It would make me feel a bit stalkerlike, but can we get something like that for Jack? We can do something almost the same with the search functionality, but you can't see the new responses, and if the response is buried in a thread, you have to go through the whole thread to find the post to see if it is even something that you want to read (might not be pertinent to your interests).

While you wait for this to develop...you can click on his user name (hover your mouse over it) and then click on see forum posts and you get all his posts.

2) Would icing the thyroid area be beneficial?

3) if my thyroid levels are normal, should I still be on Armour?

4) I am on 15 mg of Armour. I read that too low dose of thyroid can actually feed the disease and make it worse...is this true? Is 15 mg a really low dose?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-16T05:30:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hashimotos-experts.1475/post-24501>

Bren, I'm sorry but your thyroid numbers are not optimal. Your FT3 should be in the top of the range although the FT4 looks OK but could climb a tad. This means you could be taking a little more of the Armour. Make sure you shew it or crunch it. The latest version of Armour has some fillers that need some breaking apart so your body can get to the good stuff. I usually take NP Thyroid by Acella (generic version of Armour) and it just dissolves almost immediately and works great! Try going to [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) for good ideas on how to raise your dose. I followed those instructions and I felt wonderful. Also watch your thyroid numbers as you raise your dose. If you FT3 continues to be sluggish and FT4 gets higher you may be a poor converter (of T4 to FT3).

I guess it's time to start icing the carotid. I'm currently icing the jaw to prep for a minor surgery so might as well add the carotid now too! New task for all us Hashis folks!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-16T07:18:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hashimotos-experts.1475/post-24525>

This was from Dr K's Leaky Gut Rx blog post... thought it may be helpful to you, Bren. Lauren

The Hashimoto’s thing……it makes up 95% of all hypothyroid human cases today and it treated by medicine with CW. Most who have it have leaky guts and low levels of intracellular glutathione. Glutathione is a very important antioxidant that plays an important role in maintaining the correct oxidation-reduction potential inside cells. It scavenges oxidising free radicals, detoxifies heavy metals, pesticides, tylenol and leukotrienes. It stores and transports amino acids, regulates the cell cycle, protein synthesis and gene expression, and protects thyroid cells from self-generated hydrogen peroxide. Glutathione is quite important for patients with Hashimoto’s. Glutathione protects the body in an extraordinary number of ways, but the fact that it protects the thyroid against hydrogen peroxide is particularly significant for the thyroid. In untreated Hashimoto’s, the body’s levels of T4 (thyroxine) begin to fall, and TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone) levels generally start to climb. In order to stimulate the thyroid to make more T4, the TSH activates the thyroid cells to make hydrogen peroxide. If the TSH levels remain high, the thyroid cells continue to produce more and more hydrogen peroxide, and this can lead to increased inflammation, scarring, and ultimately the destruction of thyroid cells. If the hydrogen peroxide manages to gain entry to the cell, the damage becomes far worse.

Hydrogen peroxide is thought to play a significant role in the development and progression of Hashimoto’s disease, despite the fact that the thyroid gland actually requires hydrogen peroxide for thyroid hormone formation. Hydrogen peroxide is normally produced during the oxidation of the iodine ions. It is an essential part of the thyroid function. But the critical factor is the protection of the thyroid cells (glutathione level), and the place in which the iodine ions are oxidized. If oxidation occurs inside the cells, the hydrogen peroxide is produced inside the cells, and the cells sustain damage.

A study found that if hydrogen peroxide is allowed to enter thyroid cells, it attacks and cleaves thyroglobulin (protein within the thyroid), producing fragments that are able to diffuse into other cells – and these fragments were recognized by autoantibodies taken from people with Hashimoto’s disease. This suggests that hydrogen peroxide entry into thyroid cells may actually be the cause of Hashimoto’s disease!!!! SO if you have Hashimoto’s your glutathione levels maybe a proxy of how bad your disease really is. It can be a measure of really how leaky your gut is too.

The predominant infiltrating cells in autoimmune diseases such as Hashimoto’s thyroiditis are phagocytic macrophages, neutrophils and various T-cells; the macrophages and neutrophils damage the tissues by releasing inflammatory cytokines and proteins, including hydrogen peroxide making things worse because this further depletes glutathione inside the cells. This is complicated by co-morbid B12 and folate deficiencies in the diet because of the leaky gut. Glutathione requires both to be on hand to work well!

The body’s stores of glutathione can become depleted through any stressor. For example, alcoholism, HIV infection, cirrhosis, diabetes, surgical trauma, fasting, environmental toxins, overuse of acetaminophen, chronic stress, inadequate diet, elevated adrenaline release, extensive strenuous exercise, or infection. It is also associated with cigarette smoking, as smoking increases the rate at which the body uses glutathione.

Glutathione is not easily absorbed through oral supplementation, but is synthesized in the body from amino acids, and body levels can be increased through oral administration of glutathione precursors such as N-acetylcysteine (NAC), S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAMe) and L-gluatmine. Getting it into cells is even tougher and requires IV infusions using lipid soluble mediums. For serious autoimmune diseases it is often a great treatment. Oral administration of NAC of doses up to 8,000 mg/day was not found to cause clinically significant adverse reactions, although doses of 600mg/day were enough to significantly improve symptoms of chronic lung disease like COPD. I have used over 3000 mgs a day when my HS-CRP was around 3.0. When I have falls in Vitamin D levels I also reach for NAC or sometimesWHEY protein. Whey also breaks down into glutathione and reduces inflammation. This is why Whey use can augment weight loss in people with high CRP’s

Intake of NAC does increase zinc loss through urine, so zinc supplementation would be advisable for anyone supplementing NAC over an extended period. This can deplete your sex steroid hormones if you do not eat a lot of foods with zinc. Me personally when I take a ton I always make my oysters rockerfeller recipe.

Glutathione can also be up-regulated by vitamins C and E, and alpha lipoic acid, PQQ, spinach, offal, yeast extract, and broccoli. N-acetylcysteine is considered to be the most effective, fastest route to raising levels of glutathione. It also increases levels of glutathione only when there is an actual need, and it seems to concentrate only in tissues where glutathione is required. This need corresponds to high MMP9 levels in cells.

The fact that NAC is able to restore glutathione levels and thereby protect the thyroid is particularly interesting given its ability to crack open bacteria such as chlamydia pneumonia, which hide inside the body’s own cells during one part of their life cycle, using the host cell’s machinery to replicate. Chlamydia pneumoniae has been implicated in autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis, and N-acetylcysteine is considered an important part of treatment. I do not consider these diseases as part of AI’s. I consider finding titers of these in patients to be patho-mneumonic that glutathione levels are very very low in these people.

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-05-21T19:14:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hashimotos-experts.1475/post-26017>

> **Jennifer;24895 wrote:** I'm no expert, but in my experience the short fuse is either an adrenal thing (low blood sugar) or a no estrogen thing. I don't think DHEA would make you more aggressive. Someone else chime in if I"m off base here...

ha, and get used to doc's telling you your thyroid numbers look great when in fact they dont. unfortunately you have to educate yourself and get help elsewhere (i'd find a new doc if it were me)

i agree with this...i sometimes feel worse in the afternoon bc my adrenals can't keep up with the thyroid medication. I'm not on a super low dose of hydrocortisone (15mg)...i was on adaptogens before that and they may be enough to work for you. Apex Energetics makes something called "adaptocrin" that is pretty good. Licorice root tea can help keep cortisol in your system longer. The stop the thyroid website is a great resource and actually mentions this in their dosing instructions - how not being on enough or ramping up quickly enough can cause rebound affects.

I agree you need more thyroid meds. your FT3 is esp in the toilet. Be sure to ask for RT3 as you continue to test bc you want to ensure your T4 is converting to T3 and not RT3. If you have RT3, take milk thistle extract to help support your liver in clearing it.

you may be able to get your antibodies to "disappear" on the tests but unfortunately that does not mean you'll be cured (plus sometimes you are still producing anitbodies but they just dont measure them below a certain point...mine only shows >30, so if you are scoring 29, you'll look like you arent producing any which is incorrect). Once your body produces anitbodies - it never stops. You are lucky to catch this early though bc you can def minimize the effects and hopefully not have it be an all out battle!

other tips

i take NAC to help my gut and thyroid, and i also started LDN (low dose naltrexone - an immune system modulator)

avoid inflammatory foods - nightshades, grains, eggs or at least the ones that seem to affect you

# Thread: Personal Care products that fit the optimal (non hormonedisrupting chemicals) bill.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 79
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-05-16T03:55:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24478>

I have been experimenting with making my own personal care products, and it is so cool. I have given out lots of samples and people flock back wanting more and more. I'm perfecting formulas. Perhaps you will see them at Whole Foods soon!

My "base" for almost everything to go ON the body is this.

Coconut Oil

Macadamia Nut Oil

Cocoa Butter

Then when I want something to smell good, I add my handmade vanilla (made with organic beans and kettle one).

Products so far:

AU NATURAL - just the base, whipped into an easy to spread formula. I use this to remove eye makeup, wash my face in the shower (spread on and wash with just the oil and warm water) and spread on any delicate parts that want moisturizing.

PERFECT PITS - base + baking soda + lots of vanilla. Teh awesomest. Everyone that uses it comes back for more. Can also be used for rashes and itches.

HEAVY WHIP ME CREAM - base + vanilla whipped into a froth. It's edible too! I added to coffee once just to see.

Go You!

I use CO and baking soda (aluminum free version) under my arms but your base recipe looks like it would make a much easier to use cream. You've inspired me to UPGRADE!

Thx for the inspiration, and the pics

### Glamorama — 2012-05-16T05:51:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24506>

I use an organic salt crystal as deodorant, and sweet almond oil and/or macadamia oil with some drops of rose essence for lotion, eye make-up remover, night cream etc.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-16T06:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24514>

Do you have any ideas for natural shampoo to wash my hair with after swimming in the ocean? I do dunk my head under, so the hair gets plenty wet. Normally I wash minimally, every four or five days, but that's impossible with all the ocean swimming I'm doing. There's a lot of stuff in the ocean. Good for me, but not so good for soft, shiny hair.

Any ideas? Conditioner too.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-16T06:21:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24518>

Other than deo (CO & baking soda, and I do agree that mixing cocoa butter would probably make it easier to use ... it doesn't go bad?) & occasional soap on pits, I don't use anything on my body.

Nothing.

I don't even use makeup remover on the rare occasion I wear makeup. Because I never remove my body's oils intentionally anymore, makeup just comes off by itself after a few hours ... one of the few downsides!

I don't have any problems with the ocean and my hair, which I don't shampoo at all, ever. I do have BIG problems with chlorine. It makes the oils in my hair go nuts, like they go I'M BEING ATTACKED!!!!!!! and overproduce and seize up and it feels like I have a giant blob of wax on my head. I try to rinse in cool water before and after swimming in poisoned pools.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-16T13:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24552>

It makes the oils in my hair go nuts, like they go I'M BEING ATTACKED!!!!!!! and overproduce and seize up and it feels like I have a giant blob of wax on my head. I try to rinse in cool water before and after swimming in poisoned pools.

I use barely anything, either. My bottle of Dr Bronner's soap I purchased years ago is still half full and I even use it for other things like washing the floors.

I haven't quite figured out what to do with my hair. No poo doesn't appear to work for me. It starts to get greasy no matter how long I skip the shampoo. I had really short hair for years and it was okay then... just rinsed it every day. Now that my hair has grown to mid-back length I can't rinse everyday - it takes way too long to dry and blow-drying isn't something I want to get into the habit of. My hair actually grew long being no-poo, so it wasn't an issue of not giving it enough time.

I'd really like to skip the shampoo...

### Guest — 2012-05-16T15:44:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24568>

> **MamaGrok;24600 wrote:** Other than deo (CO & baking soda, and I do agree that mixing cocoa butter would probably make it easier to use ... it doesn't go bad?) & occasional soap on pits, I don't use anything on my body.
>

>

> I don't have any problems with the ocean and my hair, which I don't shampoo at all, ever. I do have BIG problems with chlorine. It makes the oils in my hair go nuts, like they go I'M BEING ATTACKED!!!!!!! and overproduce and seize up and it feels like I have a giant blob of wax on my head. I try to rinse in cool water before and after swimming in poisoned pools.

+1 You rock MamaGrok!! No soap is dope. Friends thought I was weird for not using soap on my face, but I never had pimples or blackheads until I did experiment with washing my face for a time. I am 57 with little to no wrinkling on my face anywhere. Never wore much make-up because it made me look clownish, so nothing of significance to take off.

I also have not used any type of deodorant in over 15 years. A few years back I had a breast exam and the doc said he never felt breast with, absolutely, no lumps at all. I know it's because I have have not suppressed the toxins. I don't have a problem today with stinky underarms.

I try not to miss any of your posts because I know there is always wisdom in them.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-16T16:10:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24576>

Well, different things for everyone, and I love the idea of mixing a thick butter into the deo I do use (because my body has not yet stopped stinking from the toxins coming out). Everyone has a different situation and should do what's best for that situation and not necessarily what I do! And I LOVE the idea of cavemam's products being in Whole Foods, helping lots of people (b/c face it,not everyone is as crazy as I am) to get rid of the toxins on their bodies. But I do enjoy the ease of a mostly product-free life. It's SOOOOOO easy to camp!!!

I mean, I already typically only shower once/week, and that's water-only. A washcloth and stream plus my little container of CO&BS will take care of my pits. I bring a tiny vial of tooth powder if I'm going to camp a long time, but (confession) I don't brush my teeth every day at home, so it's easy not to while camping. My general rule for the whole body is that if it's not showing or smelling, I don't do anything to it.

Eliminating the shampoo definitely takes some working with it. It took my husband only a few days to get through the over-production transition stage, but it took me nearly six weeks. It was summer, so I just wore my hair up every day. My hair is very different now. The pros are it dries more quickly (used to take 2hrs to air-dry, now only about 45m), has tons of volume (never any thought of needing a styling product), has a natural all-over wave (was always board straight until my third child, when it developed a hideous solitary s wave in just one section in the back!), tangles far less, is fairly water repellent in a light rain, and is super easy. The cons are I can't get it board straight without blow drying now (which I won't do more than once a year because that, too, takes 30m - I have thick hair!), it's not Breck-girl shiny anymore, and requires a short daily preening (much less time than hair care used to take) to keep the oils moving so they don't pile up at the scalp and leave me looking greasy. Another negative is that toxins flowing into or out of my body seem to cause a sebum (grease) response. Another positive is that when I get crap in my hair, like a night at a smoky bar or a wedding with ridiculous quantities of unnecessary stylist-applied hair products ...they're gone by morning. Just like a good sheep, my hair repels stuff.

I recently had to go through the long transition again because I had started using baking soda followed by apple cider vinegar as a wash about once a month. Then I liked the middle ground between Breck sheen & manageability & volume it offered me, and started doing it a little more often and BAM suddenly I needed to do it more than once a week or would be greasy and I was not happy about going there again, so I just cut out the ACV/BS cold turkey and re-transitioned into fully sebum'd hair. I will still use the ACV/BS for special events when I want to do something different with my hair, but not fall into doing it too often again.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-16T18:54:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24609>

> **Rosekelly;24596 wrote:** Do you have any ideas for natural shampoo to wash my hair with after swimming in the ocean? I do dunk my head under, so the hair gets plenty wet. Normally I wash minimally, every four or five days, but that's impossible with all the ocean swimming I'm doing. There's a lot of stuff in the ocean. Good for me, but not so good for soft, shiny hair.
>
>
>
> Any ideas? Conditioner too.

You might try just putting a little CO in your hair before you go in the ocean as a protectant. The salt is pretty harsh on the hair if you are in the ocean every day. Also you could put your hair up and not get it in the water (Yeah, I know that was obvious). I try to keep my hair out of the water if possible when I go in the ocean.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-16T19:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24619>

@Mamagrok: I was no-poo for about 10 years while having short hair, and it still didn't work for my long hair even though it grew in as no-poo. What do you mean with "preening"?

I would love to be no-poo again.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-16T19:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24621>

> **Suhenna;24650 wrote:** +1 You rock MamaGrok!! No soap is dope. Friends thought I was weird for not using soap on my face, but I never had pimples or blackheads until I did experiment with washing my face for a time. I am 57 with little to no wrinkling on my face anywhere. Never wore much make-up because it made me look clownish, so nothing of significance to take off.

I've pretty much stopped washing my face, too. I stopped using soap/cleansers on it years ago and recently I stopped using anything but a splash of cold water and every once in a while a little face brush. I do like the changes I am seeing... less dry, more even tone, just nicer skin. Never again will warm/hot water touch my face!!

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-16T19:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24623>

> **Souldanzer;24701 wrote:** @Mamagrok: I was no-poo for about 10 years while having short hair, and it still didn't work for my long hair even though it grew in as no-poo. What do you mean with "preening"?

A gentle scalp massage for a minute or two, especially in the most sebum-y areas, then gently moving those oils down the strands toward the ends. This was especially necessary when I was in the transition stage, or any time I ate stuff my body didn't like.

I can't say exactly what will work for everyone, but it was definitely a combination of time and preening and patience. Also, the realization that my hair is nothing like it used to be - better in some ways and more annoying in others. Overall, I believe the benefits (like volume, lack of chemicals, and free time!) outweigh the negatives (like never being a Breck girl (with the completely limp, hang-in-your-face no-volume hair that goes with it) again).

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-16T19:47:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24628>

lol, that would be me on the third day after washing. So then I give in on the fourth day. I'm about to cut it off again b/c I'm tired of dealing with it.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-16T22:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-24679>

Also you could put your hair up and not get it in the water (Yeah, I know that was obvious). I try to keep my hair out of the water if possible when I go in the ocean.

Obvious it may be, it didn't occur to me until after I posted this question. Swimming cap! Genius. Also, I like the CO idea. I'm seeing that hair-washing after every ocean swim might be in the cards, unless I can manage to keep it unwet.

Thanks!

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-18T19:33:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25236>

> **Shijin13;24661 wrote:** ok for the base what's the ratios? are you using straight cocoa butter? (I've got a brick of it TPITA to use b/c its so solid - I can't freaking cut it! I have to shred it to use in cooking) do you heat up all the oils then whip until frothy???? detailed directions pls!!!

Ok, so I still need to experiment and write down quantities. I've just been going by the feel-it method and haven't measured. BUT my best guess is:

1.5 cup of CO

1/8 cup coconut oil (because I get tired of trying to dig it out of the jar it's so hard!)

1/4 cup macadamia

I do melt everything, then babysit it while it cools and whip it every 20 mins or so, so it stays mixed up while cooling.

For the Perfect pits, add just about the same amount of baking soda as you have of goop.

If you have never made Vanilla - MUSTDO. Once you go real, you will never go back.

Order the beans from ebay. it's like $20 for 3/4 a lbd. Get the best rated organic ones. I like both tahitian and madagascar. My unscientific analysis is tahitian is more fragrant and madagascar more taste. . so use one for cooking thing, the other for smelling.

Get a big assed bottle of Kettle One Vodka. Split beans, shove them in vodka. Shake every couple of days for a few weeks, then let sit for a minimum of 6 months while shaking when you think of it.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-18T19:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25237>

Here is my vanilla operation. The little white jars are vanilla sugar - gift for clients I don't like. Heh j/k

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-18T19:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25238>

I have really REALLY tried to go poo free. Perhaps when I retire. I never could get that fresh clean look - I always looked a little grungy.

I have hope that now I think I'm looking less bald, that mebby I can swing a severe "all pulled back" kind of look with a ponytail. Right now it looks a little too old lady with too much scalp at the part.

No soap on body for over a year. People don't need soap.

So now, I use a little bit of shampoo on my part. Honestly for me, I keep thinking the small dollop that I rinse fairly quickly probably isn't that bad. So I haven't bothered with the baking soda head stuff.

Need to find something tho - any ideas are welcome!

I've got some good armpit stuff called funk butter... I also have the tropical traditions deoderant - what sucks is I can't use either after I've shaved/waxed my under arms - I have to wait 2days at least b/c the baking soda irritates me - so for those 2 days I use tea tree oil, lavendar and witch hazel - it keeps stink away for about 1/2 the day no where as good as the funk butter.

as for no poo - I use wen - I tired no poo - using morrocan method clay "shampoos" & "conditioners" - and my hair went to pieces... I use wen 2-3x/week - now that my hair is getting longer - I don't have to style it as much - so hoping that I can stretch to 2x/week max (w/exception of the days we go to the pool this summer) I was dd's hair 1x/week after swim lessons w/wen and her hair is so pretty...

### ealachan — 2012-05-18T20:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25247>

I did conditioner-only washing on my hair (using baking soda and ACV once a week or so, cheapo Suave coconut conditioner every other day to scrub my scalp) for almost a year and it worked well, but then I got a really short hair-cut and when the stylist did it, she not only used a bunch of SLS shampoo before she cut it, but then she loaded it up with all kinds of silicone-based styling gunk when she was done. I was actually pretty pissed about it, and I tried to go back to no-poo (after a good wash to get all the styling gunk out) after that, but the combination of going through the greasy stage again on top of having SUPER-short hair for the first time in like 20 years just sent my self-concious meter into the red and I went back to "normal" shampoo'ing. Still can't quite bring myself to start all over again. Maybe some day when I can take two weeks off so no one has to see me in the grease-ball stage...

The only time I ever use actual soap / bodywash is if I'm REALLY stinky for some reason, which is not often. I do still use traditional deodorant, but I'd like to try the stuff y'all have been talking about and see if it works for me instead!

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-18T20:47:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25270>

> **Shijin13;25328 wrote:** ok so its 1/5c of CO and then 1/8c of cocoa butter???

Ok, sorry for the lack of attention to detail!

1.5 cups of CO

1/8 cup cocoa butter

So, after you melt - and it cools, if it's too liquidy add more CO and Cocoa butter or if it's too stiff (doesn't spread) add more macadamia.

My next batch should be in about 2 weeks - I'll make sure to document amounts then!

Coconut oil and tea tree oil, and gse. For thrush on the bum I use the following: Desitin, lotramin, polysporin, and hydrocortisone - works like a charm - (ped gave us that recipe use 1oz of each - make a big batch and keep on hand!)

### kathylu — 2012-05-21T16:12:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25930>

I really don't get what's wrong with soap. It's just esterified (saponified) fat. I've been making "superfatted" coconut oil soap for years. The lather is sinfully luxurious and it leaves your skin moisturized, soft and smooth. I think the key is to use as little as possible (I shower every other day) and make sure there are no funky unpronounceable ingredients in it.

I also make my own skin lotion from coconut oil, sweet almond oil, cocoa and shea butter. I usually throw in a bit of vitamin E and C and scent it with organic essential oils...my fave is vanilla oil and orange oil. I smell like a orange creamsicle! You have to keep it in the frig though...no preservatives.

I'm about to try a shampoo recipe...I'll let you know how it goes. I would like to get away from using shampoo (wash my hair every 4th day).

I haven't used deodorant since I was in my 20's. After a few weeks it seemed like I didn't need it anymore. The odor just went away.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-21T17:04:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25950>

A - most soap isn't soap these days. It's detergent.

B - it's overused. Constantly removing the body's natural oils & flora has consequences, including the inability to make vitamin D from the sun, making girly parts more susceptible to bacterial imbalance (leading to infection), and much more.

I limit soap use to when I'm showing or smelling, and don't hesitate to use it (real soap) when needed.

### johnnyb — 2012-05-21T17:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-25952>

I had a case of athletes foot once and 4 weeks of lotrimin didn't do it. I ended up seeing the Dr who proscribed Lamisil which in now OTC. It kicked the crap out of the fungus in a week. I think the information said to use it for one week but expect the rash to take 2 weeks to heal.

not sure if this is considered natural but I've been using Gold Bond powder as deodorant/antiperspirant and it works really really well. No sweat and no stink. I powder all over after showering and powder the potential stinky/sweaty bits a bit more and it seems to be my new favourite...

here are the ingredients:

ACTIVE INGREDIENTS / PURPOSE:

Menthol 0.8% / Anti-Itch

Zinc Oxide 5.0% / Skin Protectant

Exactly my experience, although I often have to repeat the application a second time if the day is really hot and/or active.

I've also experienced a dramatic decrease in stink, the more I get stuff out of my body that it doesn't care for. It stinks while bad stuff goes in, stinks while it goes out, and stinks far less after it's gone.

Can't remember if I said it on this thread, but I've heard many a tale of farmers working hard in the sun all day with an appropriate (not overly burdensome) amount of sweat, but no stink at all... back in the day.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-31T17:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-28602>

I'm talking \*back in the day\* - literally not reeking. It makes sense to me - I see a considerable change in my personal odor based on whether bad crap is coming in and out of me or not. It makes sense that we wouldn't stink inherently - other animals don't. And I do admit that there is a big difference between the earthy natural smell of a sweating human and the reek of BO. The latter seems common to most everyone today without deodorants & frequent soapy washing. I don't think it was meant to be so.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-05T16:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/personal-care-products-that-fit-the-optimal-non-hormonedisrupting-chemicals-bill.1490/post-35619>

got this one from [http://www.eat-real-food-paleodietitian.com/](http://http://www.eat-real-food-paleodietitian.com/)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/35782=311-deo.jpg)

my natural deodorant experience!

mix baking soda with coconut oil until you get a paste. add essential lavender oil if desired. put in an old deodorant applicator and leave in the fridge (it is in 32C/90F).

apply as needed and smell good without any chemicals/aluminium! it is also a lot cheaper than buying commercial deodorants! PLUS, I love that I could eat what I put on my skin.

# Thread: Fasting + CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### youthfuliz — 2012-05-16T16:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-24583>

Has anyone tried fasting while doing CT? Dr. Kruse? I think I read somewhere AKman fasts once a week? And did a deep CT on a fasting day? Did anyone fast longer than 1-2 days while CT-ing? Just thought that's closer to hibernation... although we have our 2 hours hibernation every night... still fasting for a few days while doing CT may be beneficial?

Of course you need to have some extra body fat before going into a fast so your body has the correct energy source... and you also need to be already a keto adapted efficient fat burner. Anyone thinks this is interesting?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-16T17:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-24602>

I've been doing it. Mostly because i had been doing IF for several years before know about CT.

I can't say I notice any difference on IF days or not.

### vwaggs — 2012-05-16T17:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-24603>

I know when I CT if I have an empty stomach, I am shivering like crazy. So if I were to implement fasting, I would do it from dinner to dinner and CT right after my big protein/fat dinner and not eat until the next dinner for a 24 hr fast.

ha, you're using power law - dynamic equilibrium, not static. I like your approach. thanks for sharing.

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-27T04:28:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27791>

I take back everything I said about CT being easy while fasting. Not this time! I started a 48 hour fast Thursday a.m., and then went to the beach for my usual swim. The water felt colder than usual, and by 30 min, I started shivering and got out. I was cold for hours! I finally took a hot shower, and when I got out, I started shivering again!

I don't know why the first time it was easy and this time it was hard.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-27T04:57:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27793>

read the prtocol on the easy start menu .....the first two things too do.........your answer is there.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-27T18:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27854>

I tried a couple 24 hour fast lately and feel completely fine... I don't feel hungry, as if skipping a meal is normal. I did one CT on empty stomach, didn't feel anything different from my usual CTs. I have to admit that it's probably because I'm very much used to 56F cold bath now - that's the tap water temperature lately. I've been taking cold bath at this temperature for several weeks, don't shiver any more, and hardly even change skin color... just a few goosebumps on my arms. My tummy always gets pink in cold bath and that's still happening. I take a cold shower afterward and lately I enjoy it a lot since that's when I really feel a bit coldness and it's just wonderful to feel the water falling on my face, back, butt, legs... feel so much more invigorating than a quiet (not cold enough) soak.

I think I'm leptin sensitive... sleeping very well, hardly hungry anymore, often feel full before I can finish 2/3 of my BAB or dinner. I have 20 pounds of fat to lose, and I am a very efficient fat burner now, so I guess skipping a meal (24 hour fasting) here and there is not a bad thing for me.

Of course I don't suggest anyone who just started Leptin reset or CT to try fasting.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-27T18:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27855>

> **Rosekelly;27889 wrote:** I take back everything I said about CT being easy while fasting. Not this time! I started a 48 hour fast Thursday a.m., and then went to the beach for my usual swim. The water felt colder than usual, and by 30 min, I started shivering and got out. I was cold for hours! I finally took a hot shower, and when I got out, I started shivering again!
>
>
>
> I don't know why the first time it was easy and this time it was hard.

Rose, I guess the longer is your fast the colder you'll feel when you CT? So if you fast 48 hours maybe you should only do CT in the first day? How are you feeling now?

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-27T19:21:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27870>

That _was_ the first day, youthfuliz. I didn't even attempt CT on the second day.

On the third day, when I ended the fast, I ate my BAB and then drove to the ocean for my usual CT. It was still difficult. I only made it maybe 20 minutes before starting to shiver. I was quite uncomfortable until I ate some food and took a long walk, which made me feel much better. But I also had carb cravings all day, which I haven't had in quite a while!

None of this happened the first time I did CT while fasting. Go figure. And yeah, like you, I tolerated the fasting itself quite well.

BTW, I started CT 6 weeks ago and do an ocean swim in 54 degree water 5X/week. I've rarely shivered at all, and I always enjoy the cold water immensely, so this was surprising and unpleasant!

Today I'm all recovered and feeling fine.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-27T19:39:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27873>

Rose, was the air temperature lower in the last few days? I guess that influence your tolerance of coldness of the water. Also your sleep the night before may influence your tolerance of coldness. From what I read here, quite a few people mentioned that it's not a linear progress, there're easy days and sometimes harder days. Glad you're feeling well now.

On the carb cravings: I had a week or so when I felt more hungry and had cravings for carbs... that's about two months into CT? I asked others whether they felt the same way (CT induced hunger: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1248-CT-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1248-CT-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass) ) and was glad to find others who felt the same way for a short period of time. Now my own carb cravings have passed and I acutally get the full signal about 15 minutes into my breakfast/dinner... I have often stopped eating within a few minutes of getting that full signal. So hope your cravings will be easy to handle and pass soon.

### Onum — 2012-05-28T01:24:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27932>

> **youthfuliz;27972 wrote:** Rose, was the air temperature lower in the last few days? I guess that influence your tolerance of coldness of the water.

That definitely seems to be the case, I started CT'ing 6 weeks ago and the air temp has generally been around 50F sometimes less/slightly more - I've also been IF'ing the last few weeks (in the 1 meal/day sense) - CT'ing on an empty stomach has always been harder, but post CT warm-up has always been a bit miserable.

This last week Summer arrived (at last) and typical air-temps have climbed to over 70F and CT'ing (particularly post CT warm-up), even on an empty stomach has been "delightful"

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-28T03:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-ct.1500/post-27958>

haha it's nice to hear about delightful post ct warmup  I find it's easy to warm up these days too.
