# Thread: Is shrimp really optimal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-05-22T16:12:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26313>

Anyone see this report about imported shrimp? I don't trust shrimp from the Gulf these days and I've always been told farmed shrimp are heavily contaminated as well. What are our options?!?!

[http://abcnews.go.com/WNT/video/imported-shrimp-tested-chemicals-16382541](http://http://abcnews.go.com/WNT/video/imported-shrimp-tested-chemicals-16382541)

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-22T17:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26330>

I have felt exactly the same about Gulf & farmed shrimp, and will be interested to hear any informed opinions on the matter!

### MJ* — 2012-05-22T18:40:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26368>

> **BJK77;26404 wrote: Anyone see this report about imported shrimp? I don't trust shrimp from the Gulf these days and I've always been told farmed shrimp are heavily contaminated as well. What are our options?!?!
>
>
>
> [http://abcnews.go.com/WNT/video/imported-shrimp-tested-chemicals-16382541](http://http://abcnews.go.com/WNT/video/imported-shrimp-tested-chemicals-16382541)**

**
Wow I love shrimp this is gross...but they recommend domestic shrimp and that's not looking like a smart option either, [check this out](http://http://thinkprogress.org/climate/2012/04/18/466660/legacy-of-bp-oil-spill-eyeless-shrimp-and-fish-with-lesions/?mobile=nc) from the Gulf spill:

"The Deepwater Horizon oil rig exploded nearly two years ago to the day, beginning an oil spill that lasted three months and released some two hundred million gallons of oil into the Gulf of Mexico. BP may have declared their mission accomplished, but the results of the spill are still trickling out.

The latest? Shrimp with no eyes, fish with lesions, and clawless crabs.**

Scientists believe that shrimp, fish, and crabs in the gulf have been deformed by the chemical released to disperse oil during the spill. Fishers in the area say that they’ve been noticing deformities on their catches since. Al Jazeera reports:"

This can't be good for us either!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T18:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26369>

I eat lots of shrimp.......

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-22T19:09:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26389>

I learned long ago that the efforts to clean up the oil spills usually do a lot more damage than the spill itself.

Better living through chemistry, eh?

### MJ* — 2012-05-22T20:18:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26413>

> **Jack Kruse;26460 wrote:** I eat lots of shrimp.......

Do they have eyes?

### MJ* — 2012-05-22T20:20:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26414>

> **MamaGrok;26480 wrote:** I learned long ago that the efforts to clean up the oil spills usually do a lot more damage than the spill itself.
>
>
>
> Better living through chemistry, eh?

This would sure prove that...I am all for independence of the US on foreign oil, but there has to be an alternative, what of the epigenetics of these fish from this? How will that impact our epigenetics?

### hazyjane — 2012-05-22T23:36:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26489>

I'm really freaked out by Gulf shrimp! I keep up with Kindra Arneson on facebook, a fisherman's wife in the Gulf who has become the spokesperson for people affected by the spill (esp. health-wise from the Corexit) and she's been posting all kinds of pics of deformed fish and shrimp and reporting on the chemicals in water samples taken.

It's not looking good... from her reports, it's a lot worse than they're letting us know. There is still oil leaking and Corexit is still being sprayed, according to fishermen. A LOT of people in the region are sick all the time now

Shrimp might be ok if you can find the wild caught Oregon shrimp (though I wonder about the reports of radioactive cesium accumulating on the West Coast...)

### Glamorama — 2012-05-23T01:43:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26517>

I've stopped eating shrimp because I mostly had access to Thai prawns, and they are of poor quality.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-23T05:31:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-26594>

I have gone back to farmed, Since there only seems to be gulf shrimp. We need a list of seafoods from coldest to hottest. When I see things that have location name (like Norwegian salmon) - I am afraid they are tricking me with marketing.

### TimB — 2014-06-26T04:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-132299>

I realize that this thread is 2 years old and the corexit incident has been shoved down the memory hole along with any valid data about the resulting negative effects on the seafood in the Gulf.....but I'm considering moving to the Florida panhandle and would like to eat the seafood down there knowing that it's actually supporting optimal health. Has anyone run into any up to date info on the actual state of things there?

I ask this fully recognizing that "Modern healthcare, modern research and paleo leadership have a common tie: when your income depends on denial of reality, apparently reality often loses,"....a great quote from Jack which undoubtedly also applies to this deliberate Gulf poisoning, and the resulting news blackout on the negatively affected people, the sea life and the seafood there. Just wondering if anyone has run into any recent data that is reliable concerning the Gulf food chain contamination.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-26T15:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-132329>

I live down here and have visited NOAA and they are writing and filing reports that the gulf water is essentially fine post spill. None of the toxins in the clean up have showed up in their tests. If they would show up the would be in the crawfish and oyster fields first and they have been spectacularly clear.........so I just keep eating them.

### TimB — 2014-06-26T17:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-shrimp-really-optimal.1588/post-132339>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=132329)
>
> I live down here and have visited NOAA and they are writing and filing reports that the gulf water is essentially fine post spill. None of the toxins in the clean up have showed up in their tests. If they would show up the would be in the crawfish and oyster fields first and they have been spectacularly clear.........so I just keep eating them.

Thanks Jack. We'll be taking a trip down there soon. It would be great to share some oysters with you sometime.
Bon appetit!

# Thread: Genetics vs Epigenetics smackdown!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-05-22T20:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26427>

I would love to see some of the geeks on here go to town on genetics and epigenetics. I think my understanding is a little off.

What is your understanding of it. Feel free to expand on those thoughts. And, how about - if you drop a link to something, give your summary too?

### AKMan — 2012-05-23T03:20:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26540>

I don't think you are being ignored, this question made my head explode. I've done so much critical thinking the last few weeks, I can't take on another, today. First I have to solve the evolution vs God conundrum, then get a handle on Factor X (I think I know what it is), then I have to define 'cold adapted'. After that I will answer your question...

You guys are awesome!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-23T03:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26547>

Simple.......think identical twins. They have identical DNA and a genome. Why do they not have an identical life as they live it out? Their DNA does not change but the way it is expressed however does because of the envirnoment they are in.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-23T05:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26595>

> **Jack Kruse;26639 wrote:** Simple.......think identical twins. They have identical DNA and a genome. Why do they not have an identical life as they live it out? Their DNA does not change but the way it is expressed however does because of the envirnoment they are in.

Beautiful explanation!

My husband is an identical twin and I've always wondered about their differences.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-23T05:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26596>

> **Jack Kruse;26639 wrote:** Simple.......think identical twins. They have identical DNA and a genome. Why do they not have an identical life as they live it out? Their DNA does not change but the way it is expressed however does because of the envirnoment they are in.

Ok, Chocolate and Akman, time to drop some twin study research on me!

You may call me bossy.

### MJ* — 2012-05-23T07:34:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26619>

> **AKMan;26632 wrote:** I don't think you are being ignored, this question made my head explode. I've done so much critical thinking the last few weeks, I can't take on another, today. First I have to solve the evolution vs God conundrum, then get a handle on Factor X (I think I know what it is), then I have to define 'cold adapted'. After that I will answer your question...
>
> You guys are awesome!

LOL all good problems to have...

### AKMan — 2012-05-24T00:52:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26854>

I finally gave this a little thought, and here's what I think: The regulation of gene expression plays a pivotal role in complex phenotypes, and epigenetic mechanisms such as DNA methylation are essential to this process. The availability of next-generation sequencing technologies allows us to study epigenetic variation at an unprecedented level of resolution. Even so, our understanding of the underlying sources of epigenetic variability remains limited. Twin studies have played an essential role in estimating phenotypic heritability, and these now offer an opportunity to study epigenetic variation as a dynamic quantitative trait. High monozygotic twin discordance rates for common diseases suggest that unexplained environmental or epigenetic factors could be involved. Recent genome-wide epigenetic studies in disease-discordant monozygotic twins emphasize the power of this design to successfully identify epigenetic changes associated with complex traits. We describe how large-scale epigenetic studies of twins can improve our understanding of how genetic, environmental and stochastic factors impact upon epigenetics, and how such studies can provide a comprehensive understanding of how epigenetic variation affects complex traits.

Just kidding--that is the abstract of this study: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3063335/?tool=pmcentrez](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3063335/?tool=pmcentrez)

This is truly a mind-blowing field involving lots of chemistry. I know arguments about evolution usually include "how come if I cut my leg off, my baby isn't born with one leg missing?" Sounds stupid, but if the mother does something to harm her fetus, like smoke in the first trimester, the baby could be born with a missing or deformed limb and that child could go on to pass that trait to it's offspring.

The underlying theme in epigenetics is DNA methylation--whatever the hell that means. This is truly heady stuff, but here's a study for you to look at!

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-24T01:00:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26855>

>

>

Thanks. DNA methylation is something I need to brush up on. Interestingly enough, I never seem to need it in real estate. My conversations with my working peers go in a very different direction than this. I'm a looking for twin studies. They say there is a high rate of discordant disease, but I was going on the assumption that there was a high rate of similarity. I am looking into this.

I have a girlfriend just diagnosed with monozygotic twins! I wonder if she will let me influence one seperatly from the other so I can study what happens. It's probably good I didn't go into biology, I don't think I would pass the ethical muster.

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T01:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26871>

My husband had identical twins sisters. Freckled red haired beauties with heart shaped faces. One succombed to ms. One was diagnosed bipolar and overdosed on otc sleeping pills. They had a red haired even more beautiful grandma that died of breast cancer under thirty years of age. I think there was some psychosis involved with all three. The redhaired even more beautiful grandma's dad had a ferry boat business and they were camped on a cold but not frozen lake baby making and running the business. He died of drowning. After his ferry boat sank, he was found off the end of the pier with rocks in his pocket. It took twenty years and a good drunk for that to come out. They were the bottle feeding type. I think they used goats milk and hired help. A lot gets lost in history sometimes. The family historian glossed over a lot. I think it was just becoming fashionable to use bottles. I fear my husband is going to turn up neanderthal. He has the blood type and the personality. Anything wrong with our genes are my hillbilly butt's fault though, according to his mother. The twin studies, though have similarities that Dr. Kruse identified and have me landed squarely here. Things that have a common thread, ms, mental illness, scoliosis, melanin, dopamine problems, all of them have me here. The circadian really got me. The red haired grandma married a telegrapher with the rail lines and they were everywhere, but ate like kings (not circadian) and someone was on the machine all the time. My husband's mom could still tap out morse code after she hadn't thought about it in years, and she was less than twelve when her mom died. These were very intelligent people. And skilled artists. So here I am, this is the first place to help connect the dots.

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T01:56:49+0300

Its the neanderthal.... the smartest, most creative, loftiest thinking and the fieriest and most forgiving. That dopamine receptor issue swings both ways. Red hair rocks. I haven't got the slightest touch. Both my kids have strands and they look so nice in the sun.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-24T02:08:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26879>

> **Cavemam;26947 wrote:** Thanks. DNA methylation is something I need to brush up on. Interestingly enough, I never seem to need it in real estate. My conversations with my working peers go in a very different direction than this. I'm a looking for twin studies. They say there is a high rate of discordant disease, but I was going on the assumption that there was a high rate of similarity. I am looking into this.
>
>
>
> I have a girlfriend just diagnosed with monozygotic twins! I wonder if she will let me influence one seperatly from the other so I can study what happens. It's probably good I didn't go into biology, I don't think I would pass the ethical muster.

All methylation comes from the diet.....B12, B1, B5, B6, etc.....the other factor is acetylation......guess where that comes from Acetyl-CoA from fat metabolism.......This affect chromatin which binds DNA up into clumps. This dictates which part of the DNA is translated and expressed.

### Jim — 2012-05-24T02:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26898>

For a great non-technical introduction to epigenetics check out [The Biology of Belief](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-Biology-Belief-Unleashing-Consciousness/dp/1401923127/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1337815937&sr=1-1) by [Bruce Lipton](http://http://www.brucelipton.com/). He's got his own website and another book called [Spontaneous Evolution](http://http://www.brucelipton.com/spontaneous-evolution-overview/) which I will obviously have to read.

Here is an excerpt from the book over view.

> Spontaneous Evolution reveals how changing our understanding of biology and human history will help us navigate these turbulent times, and invites readers to reconsider:
>
>
>
> The unquestioned pillars of biology including random evolution, survival of the fittest, and the role of DNA;
>
> the blueprint for our sustainable, life-affirming future that is literally inside you – encoded in each of the trillions of cells comprising your body
>
> how our beliefs about nature and human nature shape our politics, culture, and individual lives; and
>
> how each of us can become planetary “stem cells” supporting the health and growth of our world and every individual in it.
>
>
>
> By releasing the old beliefs that keep the status quo in place, and by building our lives and world on this heartening new story, we can trigger the spontaneous evolution of our species.
>
>
>
> Infused with intelligence and wit, Spontaneous Evolution is an insightful, playful, and ultimately hopeful look at the unfolding destiny of our species-and how you can play an active role in birthing the evolution of humanity.

### AKMan — 2012-05-24T02:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26902>

I guess the real questions for me concerning epigenetics are:

\- How much is in my immediate control, ie. I eat right=I don't get cancer

\- How much is out of my control, ie. Mom ate wrong=I get cancer

\- Since I'm not planning on producing any new progeny, are my concerns the same as someone who wants to start a family?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-24T02:48:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-vs-epigenetics-smackdown.1594/post-26905>

My belief is you have about 90% control......10% is due to transgenerational effects that your mom and dad have to fix for you 0-6......most do not.

I love my ice vest. They are awesome for working in the yard. Only problem the more you ct the faster they warm up. They only seem to last an hour or so now.

I was buying $100 worth of ice a week. So last month I bought a small ice machine for about 800 bucks. It only has storage for 25 pounds of ice. I also bought a coleman 7 day cooler and 4- 4.5 gallon trash cans. So as soon as the bin fills I put it in a trash can and store it in the cooler. This produces about 60 pounds a day and the cooler allows my to do the ct on my schedule.

We had a bunch of family in and it was nice having extra ice around. Expensive yes but it should pay for itself rather quickly.

### Tizawee — 2012-05-23T16:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-26665>

I use a combination of frozen 1 liter bottles + ice. I found that 9 1-liter bottles will lower the water temp about 10 deg. Also I get my ice from [www.**icehouseamerica**.com/](http://http://www.icehouseamerica.com/). They are national, sell ice out of little stand-alone structures in strip mall parking lots, 20 pounds for $1.50.

I am fortunate that we have a large fridge on the back porch that we use for overflow. It has about 80 pounds of ice in the freezer on a regular basis. I've been thinking that, if my ice comsumption goes up much further, I'd invest in a chest freezer.

Elin

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-27T08:41:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-27811>

> **AKMan;26839 wrote:** I feel sorry for you guys in the south. Sure is nice to get free CT in the winter up here.

I know much of CT is uncharted territory, but I'd like opinions on this.... I am going into winter where I live (southern New Zealand). We've got temps down to freezing and below every evening; daytime highs are in the 40's. My house is uninsulated and I do not turn on any heaters, day or night. I have not been wearing a jacket (though I do wear a layer of merino wool most days) and I do sleep with covers on. By night, you can see your breath inside! Here is my question I'd like opinions on. Do you think that exposing myself to cold in this way, day in - day out, for at least 4 months is a good form of CT?

I am pretty cold most days but try to wear enough that I don't shiver all day long. However, because I'm cold most of the time, I have avoided doing the ice baths which I used to do 4x/week because I'm already quite cold and I doubt I'd ever warm up. I know that air is less effective than water, but on the other side, I am exposing myself to a pretty constant dose of winter. AKMan, what did you do in Alaska during the winter? I remember you saying you walked outside in bare feet (I have NOT attempted that, though I take 2 walks a day outside without adding layers). What do you all think?

### Kiera2 — 2012-05-27T09:30:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-27812>

> **KiwiLauren;27910 wrote:** ...... Here is my question I'd like opinions on. Do you think that exposing myself to cold in this way, day in - day out, for at least 4 months is a good form of CT?
>
>
>
> I am pretty cold most days but try to wear enough that I don't shiver all day long...........What do you all think?

I don't know Lauren, but I've lived in a very old, uninsulated, drafty house for almost 20 years. We only had a gas heater in one room, and if it was windy, the carpet would rise up off the floorboards in there! Because of the cost to run electric fan heaters, I would only let the kids turn on a little blow heater in the kitchen at breakfast if they could see their breath.

I could always handle a lot colder temperatures than 'normal' people; I wear less clothes, and feel uncomfortable in centrally heated homes. But I certainly wasn't cold adapted, and neither are my kids. My older daughter especially is always bundled up against the cold, and keeps a wood fire burning in her home, even though she grew up with no heating in a frosty house.

So, if living like that were the only efforts you were making towards being cold adapted, I would say no, that's not enough. And, if you were cold adapted already from your prior CTing, I don't think you should be feeling cold all day. That just doesn't seem right; do you mean cold as opposed to a warm summer's breeze, or are you miserable with achy feet and on the verge of shivering? (I know that feeling LOL) I used to have to keep warm with lots of housework and gardening.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-06-13T13:14:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-30867>

> **Raiken3712;26690 wrote:** Would wearing the vest be enough to cool skin temp to 50-55...it should work right?

Well, I am in the _do it yourself camp now, since the shipping cost of the official_ ice vest was prohibitive:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I bought 4 flexible ice panels (little more than 5â‚¬ each), attach in pairs with packing tape and used the packing tape to make a pair of braces in between the two pieces. I haven't got an skin thermometer, but I am quite sure I get the cherry pink effect.

I began with the inner shirt, an outer shirt over the ice-vest, a belt over the two pieces, and a jacket over all of it (actually I am wearing those right now). After two days doing two hours each (it was quite comfortable and I didn't shiver), I began to start each session with a few minutes (first day 5, second 10, third 15, fourth 20, today 30) without the inner shirt. I know Dr. K said to disregard the compression shirt once you are able to wear the ice packs for one hour, but since I haven't used a compression shirt I am going slowly .

hmmmm, I am seriously thinking of buying a pack of those plastic popsicles. They are pretty cheap, and will last for the season. Now all I need is a vest to put them in.

### Birdy — 2012-06-14T17:29:41+0300

How DO you get used to sleeping cold? I can't fall asleep, but once I do sleep for awhile, I am able to throw off all the covers and I am fine sleeping like that for a little while.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-15T00:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-31257>

> **Jack Kruse;31162 wrote:** I think Lauren you are getting a benefit.......but in winter I sleep with my bedroom windows open naked on top of the covers. It is tough to get used to but when you sleep you sleep like a rock.

Thanks! I'm going baby steps toward the sleeping cold. I'm still wearing a t-shirt and under covers, but this is a serious step in the right direction... last winter was layers of thermals and layers of down comforters! And a heater

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-19T20:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-59206>

> **KiwiLauren;31361 wrote:** Thanks! I'm going baby steps toward the sleeping cold. I'm still wearing a t-shirt and under covers, but this is a serious step in the right direction... last winter was layers of thermals and layers of down comforters! And a heater

I'd love to hear more about how you fared over the winter. I keep the window open at night (have always loved a cold room), and try to use the least amount of covers. However, it's pretty pathetic, when I think about JK sleeping in the 40s with nothing! Can we really acclimate to sleeping in the cold, even when starting with less than optimal hormone levels? How does one acclimate to this? Just go cold turkey and feel really cold for a few nights? But then sleep quality is affected (at least temporarily), which is a negative. I would love to get started on this.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-20T17:57:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effective-efficient-ct.1603/post-59344>

I know this is little late in the discussion, but just wanted to update with what I've found works for making sufficient amounts of ice while making rent and bills, too:

30 of these suckers: 1 qt freezer storage containers. Come in packs of three, ordered 10 packs for a total of 30 costing $56. My roommate bought a pack for other purposes that are now moot, so I end up using 33 of these. 1 quart is approximately 2 pounds of ice, so these contribute 66 lbs of ice. This is enough to bring 78 degree tap water down to 48 degrees. It takes 3 days to freeze them all from regular filling with faucet tap, 2 days if you refill them from your still-cold CT bath water (this is good for 2-3 rounds before the water gets nasty and you want to start afresh).

I also throw in any other incident ice from ice containers that came with the fridge, which adds another 2 lbs, as well as the cold packs from the Cool Fat Burner. With the cooling of the groundwater (finally!) and 68 lbs of ice plus the cold packs, I was able to get the water down to 42 degrees.

$62 dollars for re-usable 66 lbs of ice beats the heck out buying ice every time you CT.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-23T20:30:14+0300

### admin — 2012-05-24T01:16:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sending-info-before-consult.1609/post-26866>

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T05:30:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-26951>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selkie](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selkie)

Selkies (also known as silkies or selchies) are mythological creatures found in Faroese, Icelandic, Irish\[1\], and Scottish folklore. The word derives from earlier Scots selich, (from Old English seolh meaning seal).\[2\] **Selkies are said to live as seals in the sea but shed their skin to become human on land. The legend apparently originated on the Orkney and Shetland Islands.**

### AmyK — 2012-05-25T04:55:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-27245>

I find this sooooooo fascinating and a particular thought keeps rolling around in my head so I think I just need to put it out thereâ€¦.if I am totally off base, please "nope" me so I can move on!

I had never heard of the aquatic ape theory before someone mentioned it on the Factor-X thread. I haven't had time yet to read up on it, but it sounds like many people dismiss it because there still are no "supporting fossils". I understand epigenetics have sped up since KT and there would be FEWER fossils, but it still seems like there SHOULD be an explanation for the complete lack of them.

Is it possible, because we know our ancestors could survive in hibernation or suspended animation for an extended period of time, they could have experienced and survived a flood that froze or been caught in a frozen marine environment for an extended period of time? I keep thinking of all the environmental signals going to each fetus that would have been in utero. Mom may have been mostly or partly a terrestrial being before being forced into this situation, but because that fetus is getting information exclusively about the marine environment, it seems like that baby would come out looking significantly different than momâ€¦thus, no missing fossil.

### AmyK — 2012-05-25T05:41:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-27259>

Or maybe things wouldn't have had to freeze... it could have been cold enough to slow down the bodily processes, extending the gestational period, and exponentially speeding up epigenetics.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-25T13:10:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-27282>

> **AmyK;27343 wrote:** I find this sooooooo fascinating and a particular thought keeps rolling around in my head so I think I just need to put it out thereâ€¦.if I am totally off base, please "nope" me so I can move on!
>
>
>
> I had never heard of the aquatic ape theory before someone mentioned it on the Factor-X thread. I haven't had time yet to read up on it, but it sounds like many people dismiss it because there still are no "supporting fossils". I understand epigenetics have sped up since KT and there would be FEWER fossils, but it still seems like there SHOULD be an explanation for the complete lack of them.
>
>
>
> Is it possible, because we know our ancestors could survive in hibernation or suspended animation for an extended period of time, they could have experienced and survived a flood that froze or been caught in a frozen marine environment for an extended period of time? I keep thinking of all the environmental signals going to each fetus that would have been in utero. Mom may have been mostly or partly a terrestrial being before being forced into this situation, but because that fetus is getting information exclusively about the marine environment, it seems like that baby would come out looking significantly different than momâ€¦thus, no missing fossil.

I put the AA theory up on the factor X thread because I was thinking about how we differed from other primates, and what selective pressures would lead to those specific adaptations.

At each subsequent ice age since KT there would have been selective pressures put on all mammals. A series of epigenetically survivable bottlenecks.

The most recent and probable one for the AA to emerge, would have been MIS6, when less that a thousand of us primates were at the tip of Africa, diving and cooking their food. (see the scientific American article "how the sea saved humanity")

A small population would likely inherit epigenetic traits faster....

How could we tell pre/post MIS6 H.Sapiens apart if the bones of the aquatic ape look the same as H.Sapien?

I think we likely are the aquatic ape, we didn't come from them, we became them, so no missing link if most of the changes are superficial from a fossil record.

Fascinating implications, knowing that the DNA for all of these traits was available for selection.

### curves64 — 2012-05-25T18:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-27371>

**Woo Woo Alert!** At the risk of sounding way too out there I'm having some unusual experiences swimming in the ocean. Although i have been in the water half a dozen times in the last few weeks, the unusual experiences didn't start until I submerged my face in the water. I don't know what shifted exactly but I started having what I can only call distant recollections of being in the water. The more I did the crawl the more I "remembered" playing in the ocean. Mind you, I had never been swimming in the ocean prior to these last few weeks (always swam in pools or lakes), and had these experiences BEFORE hearing about the AA theory. In other words, my experience preceded my intellectual curiosity. Maybe this is how a fetus feels in the womb, I'm not sure. What I do know is how natural I feel swimming in the ocean all by myself.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-28T06:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-27982>

[http://www.arkansasalligatorfarm.com/services.html](http://http://www.arkansasalligatorfarm.com/services.html)

The town I grew up in had a tourist alligator farm. If you were a kid there you knew all about the merman. I remember it was well discussed after trips - and it was generally accepted w the hillbilly thinkers "my family  " that this was a transitionary animal before apes.

Funny, it was seeing the skeleton on the animal planet show that jogged my memory - it was a lightbulb - i had seen a real one before!

All of us non tourist knew they had put a fake head on it, but the body was an actual creature - or so the story goes.

I googled it, cant believe it was still there! I got to see and touch it countless times as a youth. Seemed real to moi. The thing that freaked me out tonight was it was identical to the animal planet one!

> All of us non tourist knew they had put a fake head on it, but the body was an actual creature - or so the story goes.
>
>
>
>
>

### PrimalPam — 2012-10-28T16:28:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mermaids-are-the-missing-link.1617/post-60554>

Almost started a new thread due to the spam....arrrrgh....in the last few posts.....so please ignore them...

But decided to revitalize this thread because there are so many terrific comments already.

I just happened to catch part of the "Mermaids" show on Discovery last night. All I can say is....wow.

I just kept thinking, wow, Dr. K. is RIGHT...we DID walk up out of the water. The evidence is staggering. And it all makes so much more sense now, reading all the comments on this thread about loving the water so much despite not having ever been a great swimmer before, or that shorter-distance swimmers prefer a layer of fat for buoyancy.

My experience is that, it took me months to gather the courage do do CT. I kept reading, and thinking, well, that's crazy, so that's where I'll stop with this Leptin thing. I was looking for a diet, wanted to lose a few lbs. Submerging my body in 50-degree water? Are you freaking KIDDING me????

But I finally decided, after "peer pressure" here (lol), to try it. The very first time I did it, I dumped my entire freezer ice into the tub, stuck a thermometer in, and did the little-toe first thing. Within 20 minutes, I was fully submerged and it felt great. I could not believe how...."natural"....it felt. And I remember reading Dr. K., saying, do it 2-5 x/week. I figured, I'll get in there a couple of times, and call it a day. Um, no. I did it 5 x/week for 4 months straight. On my days "off", I wanted to write Dr. K. and ask if 6-7 times a week was ok, but I was trying to follow the protocol, so I "limited" myself to 5 x/week.

So in the past 4 weeks, I've been traveling a ton (5 trips back-to-back-to-back) and figured I could do without CT for a little bit. But I am missing it now SO much I plan to get back to my 5 x/week immediately.

So the question is: WHY do I like CT so much? Spending close to an hour in frigid water makes me feel....good?

The other question is: I am 50 years old. I NEVER ate seafood (except canned tuna...now yuk). But I eat it every day now. I order it in restaurants, voluntarily. Automatically. Like, I automatically scan the menu for the seafood options. I don't even look at anything else. My family now looks at me like I have 3 heads, because the KNOW I hate seafood. And my shopping cart is always full of it now, and I'm constantly scanning for seafood recipes.

This show was SO good, and I'm going to find it and DVR it for re-watch.

Point is, Dr. K. is RIGHT...we DID swim up out of the oceans. I came here this morning to re-read the blogs where he spoke so specifically of it, because I just kept thinking....O.M.G., I read that already!

I have almost never used my heater in my house. People have always thought I was nuts, and even though I live in Houston, it DOES get cold. This morning, when I woke up, my house thermostat read 55. I HATE going to people's houses, stores, offices, where I feel the heater. No one has ever understood this about me, nor did I...I just liked the way it felt. NOW I get it.

# Thread: Aching buttocks and thigh muscles?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### 2bls — 2012-05-24T17:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27024>

9 days into _clean_ LR and even though I'm taking large amounts of magnesium, potassium, Vit D3 and K2 as well as Vit A, C, and a host of other supplements, I have very strange aching pain in my buttocks and thigh muscles- deep in the muscle, not pain like post-excercise pain but deeper. It almost feels like sciatic nerve pain but it only appeared after a couple of days LR...I can't really sleep with this pain and I'm feeling exhausted.

Will LR generally resolve this? I've been drinking water, very little coffee and trying to get enough of the proper minerals that may be washing out with any release of retained water.

Is this just another symptom of the carb-flu? Otherwise I feel fine, no cravings from day one, not hungry between meals,other than this pain-in-the-butt pain, LR seems to be quite smooth for me so far..

any thoughts?

### nonchalant — 2012-05-24T18:18:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27042>

Hi 2BLS, welcome! Does the pain feel better if you get up and move around? Stretch your hips/thighs a bit?

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T19:44:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27066>

If you look up pelvic torsion stretches on you tube and si joint stretches, you might find relief. Its just little things like crossing one leg in front of the other and reaching for your toes or elevating them on a stool and bearing your weight on just one leg. After a couple of days you alternate legs. Just when you think about. Not with any great discipline.

### 2bls — 2012-05-24T20:02:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27073>

thanks for your comments. I'm doing all the stretches that my chiropractor has suggested and it does feel better when walking around or when stretching but as soon as I lie down to sleep, the pain starts and keeps me semi-awake and tossing to find some relief all night.

Something that does give a tiny bit of relief is laying on a couple of tennis balls at certain points on my buttocks(one TB on each side) not sure why that helps but as soon as I move, the pain returns

.....just from some quick reading it might be close to piriformis syndrome. I have overpronating feet with functional flat arches which apparently is one of the causes of this. Lunge stretches can be a way to relieve this...or using a kneelsit chair at work(good luck getting the company to pay for one of those!) Lunge stretches sound like something I could do...I just have to learn what they are and how to do them....

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T20:29:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27092>

My husband sleeps with a pillow between his knees.

### nonchalant — 2012-05-24T23:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27171>

2BLS, you might want to try Krill Oil, or up the dosage. I had this pretty bad right after I first went low carb, about a year and a half ago. Not sure why. I tried potassium, which gave me some relief, but then I had to increase the dosage, and increase it again. Then I tried magnesium, which also gave relief, but later I had to increase the dosage also. I still take 1000 mg of magnesium. I found increasing the krill oil that I was already taking to 1000 mg really helped.

I still get some pain in the area, but nothing like the first couple months after starting the diet. I have found recently, after going nuts over some fruit (what a slippery slope it is!), that inflammation really aggravates it.

I know what you are talking about googling and finding piriformis syndrome. I found a youtube video of some guy demonstrating exercises in a hotel room. Those exercises exactly pinpointed the painful area, and they did help quite a bit. I haven't done them in many months, but the video helped me confirm what the problem is.

CT also helps. I haven't been spot icing the area, since it doesn't bother me much any more, but that would almost certainly help.

Hope you get some relief, it is quite painful, and I know about it keeping you awake. I would get up just to walk around for some quick relief. Ibuprophen helps, but who wants the leaky gut and injured kidneys!

### chocolate — 2012-05-25T00:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27183>

I was thinking about that, too, Nonchalant. I had to add fat to make the minerals work.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-25T02:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27196>

This happened to me last month, but it was in conjunction with illness. I don't know if it was viral or bacterial. I had diarrhea, followed by a head and chest cold with a mouth infection. Iodide/iodine, potassium, magnesium, sea salt and probiotics and lactoferrin finally cleared it. Although I still have lingering gum issues. My problem is I'm not consistent with the supplements.

I sleep with one eye open griping my pilllow tight...........

### lonergirl — 2012-05-25T02:55:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27221>

> **Jack Kruse;27307 wrote:** I sleep with one eye open griping my pilllow tight...........

Metallica: Enter Sandman

### 2bls — 2012-05-25T20:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27403>

[http://www.theshoppingchannel.com/Orthaheel/Sandals/Orthaheel-Womens-Orthotic-Layla-Slide-Sandal/pages/productdetails?nav=R:433964](http://http://www.theshoppingchannel.com/Orthaheel/Sandals/Orthaheel-Womens-Orthotic-Layla-Slide-Sandal/pages/productdetails?nav=R:433964)

I was so desperate that I bought some orthotic sandals from Orthaheel a few days ago. They are supposed to help with the overpronation of feet and knees. So, while I was typing my post yesterday, I had been wearing the new shoes for about 2 hours....I wore them the rest of the day...felt like I was walking uphill on a sand dune even though I was on flat ground. I never realized how hard it can be just to walk when your feet feel weird. Anyway, last night I went to bed and interestingly, the pain in hips and legs was noticeably lessened!

I still didn't get much sleep because I had sinus, ear and chest congestion so bad I coughed most of the night, but the pain was definitely less! I'm embarassed to be wearing orthotics but hey, if they can even help with the pain, I'll wear them. I'll see how tonight goes with the sleeping, I have been taking large amounts of the minerals mentioned as well as 1000mg of Krill OIL just before bed too. If its a structural issue, why haven't I felt this kind of pain before? Why now, starting the LR?

I thank everybody for their helpful suggestions and comments, I am noting them all for future use.

### nonchalant — 2012-05-25T22:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-27428>

> **2BLS;27501 wrote:**
>
>
>
> I have been taking large amounts of the minerals mentioned as well as 1000mg of Krill OIL just before bed too. If its a structural issue, why haven't I felt this kind of pain before? Why now, starting the LR?
>
>
>
> I thank everybody for their helpful suggestions and comments, I am noting them all for future use.

That's a good question. I had that pain years before, however, but only a few times per year. For a few days in a row. I used to dose myself pretty good with aspirin, advil, whatever. Now that I'm avoiding such crutches, I sure notice the pain more, when I do get it.

### 2bls — 2012-05-31T22:27:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-28726>

so the aching buttocks and thighs have gone since I've been wearing the Orthtic sandals. Still puzzled about the timing of the pain at same time as starting LRx.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T01:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aching-buttocks-and-thigh-muscles.1625/post-30619>

2BLS, I have started seeing a chiropractor. He found some curvature in my lower spine, off to my right side. It's obvious even to me, looking at the xray. I think it may be the root cause of the pain in my right hip. No idea about why the hip pain started up when I went low carb, though. I did injure my tailbone when I was about 5-6 years old. He also found issues in two other areas of my back.

Anyway, after the adjustment sessions, I've noticed that I don't have to lean slightly to the right to keep the hip from hurting while I walk. I hope this becomes the permanent solution to that strange pain. I've also noticed that my tailbone is no longer off to my right side! Wow, that was fast and painless!

Anyway, since the sandals helped your pain, perhaps you may have a similar issue?

# Thread: seafood adaptation!...

**Subforum:** Factor X

### janagram — 2012-05-24T18:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27041>

OK, how much? Everyday? We need to explore the safest and best sources of seafood! I don't know that much about the subject. My mother was from No. Dakota....we didn't eat a lot of fish. (Hi, Paleocowgirl!)

But. I have ( in recent years) begun eating delicious wild salmon and halibut. I enjoy those when at a good restaurant, and I cook them well myself, but often beef is more tempting. If I had grown up on the gulf coast I would just looove shrimp, but I don't. How to learn to? Or are scallops really good? (I had a slightly yucky dish of scallops once, so don't order those.) tentacles are out, no calamari. I amy not be the only one with this issue. I am also very motivated to seafood adapt! So I am literally opening this thread to cultivate the factor x implications of diet!!!

### AKMan — 2012-05-24T18:30:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27043>

My attempt since last winter to eat a ketogenic seafood rich diet included eating wild-caught salmon once or twice a week, shrimp once or twice a week, and other seafood like mussels and scallops. However, I often felt I was falling short of my goal.

My solution was to fill my bottom drawer at work with sardines! Mostly the skinless, boneless variety packed in water or olive oil. Kipper snacks, too. My breakfast now consists of 1 or 2 cans of sardines. If I eat lunch it is a green salad or steamed veggies and sardines or cooked fish if I can get some. My goal is to eat only fish during the day and dinner is usually beef or venison (moose). I raise chickens, so I eat eggs on the weekend, but I also pig out on grilled salmon on weekends.

My HDL went from 39 to 61 since January! Triglycerides are 51, from 60.

### MJ* — 2012-05-24T18:31:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27044>

I love your thread, will try and add some things when I have time!

They seem possible in an ideal world.....whereas, mussels, wellllll, (never say never , my hub loves them)....and about those sardines, Akman, that's a stretch....but again, maybe it could be a special taste....I once had a ...hm, what to call it? a kind of pesto made with sardines and olives and capers that was pretty good on a cracker...oh wait, I said cracker! Sorry!

### Huck — 2012-05-24T19:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27057>

A good way to start with fish and seafood is to see them as a vehicle for butter and garlic. I love shrimp, scallops, and cod cooked in garlic butter. Crawfish tails are good but expensive, unless you can catch your own. Saute some celery, onion, green pepper, and garlic and throw in some crawfish.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-24T19:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27058>

Sea scallops (not bay) are delicious grilled or sauteed simply in a good fat, sprinkled with salt and pepper. I look at most seafood like most veggies - pretty meh alone, but a nice vehicle for fat & salt & yeah, garlic.

But the quality issue keep s me from eating more. Where is safe shrimp? I know to buy salmon only wild, but what about the other types? I know they shouldn't be farmed, but how do I find out? where do I get them?

I know small fish, like herring, are supposed to be safer, I guess because they don't get farmed? (yes, and the theoretical mercury issue, although I also read that bigger fish also concentrate selenium (?) more, and that chelates the mercury or something.

Very confusing.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-24T19:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27060>

> **janagram;27152 wrote:** I still hold out hopes for scallops being good

So one easy way that always gets rave reviews at my house is to take some bay scallops and poach them in butter (or fat of your choice). Get a pan that is just big enough to hold your scallops. Melt butter (or CO or combo) and put the scallops in. If they aren't fully submerged you will have to turn them over once. Cook them slowly - just enough heat so the fat is trembling until they are opaque. Have some steamed veg ready to go (fennel bulb and celeriac root are a good match). Grab a bowl, toss in your steamed veg, top with scallops, drizzle some of the fat from cooking the scallop over the top of the dish and sprinkle with salt. If you are serving this for a larger crowd - grill a filet of salmon to go with it.

Scallops are like a good steak - over cook them and they turn tough and yucky.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-24T19:39:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27061>

3-5 meals a week......the worse off you are the more it should be 5-6 a week.

### AKMan — 2012-05-24T20:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27081>

You guys ever hear of Ceviche'? You take raw fish (salmon, trout, halibut, scallops work best IMO), squeeze lime and/or lemon juice over it until it is cover, refrigerate for 4-8 hours. The acidic juice cooks the fish. While it's soaking, add chopped onions, cilantro, garlic, capers, green onion tops/chives.

I eat it right out of the bowl, but also good as a side or a topping to grilled fish. Or corn tortillas if you do those.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-24T20:16:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27083>

At what point does the benefit of any kind of seafood outweigh the negatives of modern seafood?

\- If you can only get farmed fish, should you eat it, anyway?

\- If you can only get clawless crabs from the Gulf, should you eat it, anyway?

\- If you can only get three-headed shrimp from the coast of Japan, should you eat it, anyway?

I just don't know where the balance is. I may just stick to canned herring & sockeye salmon until I can figure it out. (Are all canned oysters safe?)

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T21:14:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27119>

> **janagram;27152 wrote:** I still hold out hopes for scallops being good....so if they are...how to prepare, or what to order in a restaurant. And also shrimp. They seem possible in an ideal world.....whereas, mussels, wellllll, (never say never , my hub loves them)....and about those sardines, Akman, that's a stretch....but again, maybe it could be a special taste....I once had a ...hm, what to call it? a kind of pesto made with sardines and olives and capers that was pretty good on a cracker...oh wait, I said cracker! Sorry!

Scallops are wonderful if done well. If done badly, they get chewy like erasers... You basically just want to get a good hot pan with a little butter in it and sear the scallops. They're better when they're not "done" all the way through, at least to me.

Mussels are wonderful steamed in white wine, and also in a coconut curry broth - that's one of my all-time favorites.

Sardines, I've only just tried recently, and I was actually shocked at how much I liked them. They tasted like somewhat stronger tuna to me. I was a little skeeved out by the crunch bones, but not so much so that I didn't polish off the whole can.

### Optimalbound — 2012-05-24T21:18:16+0300

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T21:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27123>

> **MJ\*;27210 wrote:** ceviche? what does that word mean MamaGrok?

ceviche is a South American method of preparing seafood. You use citrus juices to soak the seafood. The acid denatures the proteins in the seafood, essentially "cooking" it, but without heat. You can add all kinds of other stuff to the marinade as well.

[Linky](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceviche)

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T21:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27124>

> **MJ\*;27218 wrote:** I've never cooked muscles, how do you know when they are done?

They're in shells. The shells open up when they're done. If you get ones where the shell is already open and don't close up when you tap them before you start cooking, throw those ones away immediately. Any that don't open after you've cooked them...throw those away, too. They're not good.

Easy-peasy.

>
>
>
> [Linky](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceviche)

It's just like anything else - it's all about kitchen cleanliness and proper prep work. If you're careful about cleaning and not cross-contaminating stuff, you're at a pretty low risk of getting food poisoning from anything, let alone ceviche.

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T21:34:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27132>

> **MJ\*;27224 wrote:** Cool that is easy...even I can handle that!!!

Yeah! It takes like 5-7 minutes, tops. You want to be careful to remove the beards and scrub the mussels well before cooking, and you really only need a little bit of liquid to cook them in. The best part is actually the juice that the mussels release into the cooking liquid, so you want to make sure you slurp that all up too.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-25T19:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27376>

> **MJ\*;27223 wrote:**
>
> That's another question, I gave up all grains, is there a way to prepare a sushi like dish without rice that will keep everything together?

Have you ever had a hand roll at a sushi joint? They basically roll up the sushi contents in a larger nori square (basically the whole square that they make the rolls out of). It makes a cone shape. For rice use cauli rice. To make the nori a little more "biteable" just toast the square lightly over a flame.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-25T19:21:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27380>

> **ealachan;27274 wrote:** Cocktail sauce is just ketchup mixed with horseradish. Mark's Daily Apple has an old post with a primal Ketchup recipe somewhere, but I've never tried it myself.

MDAs primal ketchup recipe is fabulous - if you can do tomatoes. Hubster loves it. If you can't do tomatoes try subbing dried prunes. Dice 'em, poach to soften, blend it up a bit and then follow the primal recipe. I know prunes sound kinda gross but it is good and fairly close to the flavor profile you would expect. I use green peppercorns as a sub for anything that calls for spicy red peppers.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-25T23:16:23+0300

> **janagram;27138 wrote:** OK, how much? Everyday? We need to explore the safest and best sources of seafood! I don't know that much about the subject. My mother was from No. Dakota....we didn't eat a lot of fish. (Hi, Paleocowgirl!)
>
> But. I have ( in recent years) begun eating delicious wild salmon and halibut. I enjoy those when at a good restaurant, and I cook them well myself, but often beef is more tempting. If I had grown up on the gulf coast I would just looove shrimp, but I don't. How to learn to? Or are scallops really good? (I had a slightly yucky dish of scallops once, so don't order those.) tentacles are out, no calamari. I amy not be the only one with this issue. I am also very motivated to seafood adapt! So I am literally opening this thread to cultivate the factor x implications of diet!!!

Love the shout out! The Dakota rawwk! I'm like you and did NOT grow up eating fish. As Catholics, we had our obligatory fish on Fridays during Lent, but it was always breaded shrimp, fish sticks or tuna noodle casserole. OBVIOUSLY, these don't cut it...but I'm learning to like real fish now. I've gotten pretty adventurous, trying all kinds when I travel. I'm addicted to sashimi now! Yum! I like to add shrimp to my steak kabobs, too. (Gotta keep the beef to keep it Midwestern ha!)

### MJ* — 2012-05-26T18:16:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-27654>

> **Anna K.;27745 wrote:** Yes, me too! Is crawfish good for you?
>
>
>
> Is crawfish seafood?
>
>
>
> this is what I found:
>
> No, they are not seafood! They are commonly mistaken as seafood but in reality they are a type of freshwater shellfish that are raised in freshwater ponds and can even be found in ditches that retain water, hence one of their nicknames of "mud bugs".

ok wow I always thought shellfish and seafood were synonymous terms...like all shellfish are seafood, but not all seafood is shellfish...bugs hug?

### PaleoDentist — 2012-05-30T03:51:31+0300

### JC2K — 2012-06-05T00:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29441>

No. It's pretty nasty stuff. I cut my shrimp eating after I researched it more. Think factory farms for cattle, but a thousand times worse. You want wild caught, but good luck finding clean sources. I wouldn't trust Gulf shrimp right now for example.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-05T03:29:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29464>

This may shock you.....but bad seafood is better than great grass fed meat or organ meat.......the nutrient density is that much better.

### JoeBranca — 2012-06-05T03:49:34+0300

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-05T05:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29481>

I recently bought flounder roe at a local fish shack on L.I. Really cheap $2.00 a pound. It's long pouches of dense fish eggs that I sliced 1/2" thick and curry/turmeric/ butter/CO black pepper slow fry in the pan -1 billion fish eggs delicious!

### JoeBranca — 2012-06-05T06:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29501>

fresh uni is mind blowing anyway, but if a place serves it right out of the shell, you'll enter a new world of subtle but layered amazingness!!!

there was a place down here in MV that served it that way but they've since changed ownership and I haven't been back to check. I'm guessing you'll have to do some investigations of your own.

Another option is to take a field trip to the Redondo Pier where all the fresh off the boat seafood is sold. I've seen the black spikey urchins there before. You'll just need a scissors and some crafty hands to cut the bottoms out!

I still ponder an idea of someday doing a coastal road trip where at some point we stop in SB and I bring a bucket or a net and jump in the drink to grab my own urchins! ; ) Not sure how far out they are though, will need to research.

### JoeBranca — 2012-06-05T07:15:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29505>

Krusing, also do you like snails? Some sushi places might carry them sometimes (our local one does, thanks to my dad's influence on the owner ; ) but also there's a traditional Italian grocer/deli in Tustin that carries canned scungili (conch). It's a bit pricey around $6/can but they taste amazing, especially if you throw them in some sort of marinara. I've added them to take-out Thai coconut seafood soup. If you have a Peppino's anywhere near you, they have scungili too which is surprising.

Interesting that Dr K says bad fish is better than grass fed beef. Thanks for the tip on cilantro for mercury. I try to eat fish for lunch and/or dinner ever day. ### ealachan — 2012-06-05T20:37:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29576>

> **August;29676 wrote:** When I hear "bad seafood" . . I just think iffy seafood . . like past it's prime. . I don't do iffy seafood . . . I've had unfortunate experiences in the past that have turned my off of seafood for a long time . . . and now that I'm back to eating it again . . I don't want to risk my future relationship with seafood by eating something that might turn me off of it for good. . .
>
>
>
> I actually find some of the farmed stuff to vile to eat too . . . I got a hold of some stinky, nasty farmed Tilapia . . no more Tilapia and if I can help it no more farmed fish. . . actually sometimes the farmed salmon is okay as far as taste . . but I think I can taste the difference b/t wild caught and farmed!

Any tilapia you get will likely be farmed. Fish farmers actually use hormones to treat the tilapia to change their sex to mostly male, because if there are too many females the fish will breed too much and lead to way too many small fish and not enough fish that are big enough to process. Farmed tilapia has also been found to have as high as an 11:1 O6:O3 ratio.

In other words...yuck.

### JoeBranca — 2012-06-05T23:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-29612>

yes... my dad's side of the family is Sicilian and growing up in Gloucester, MA they would pick those off the rocks down at the harbor, haul them back home in buckets, get the scissors and get to work. It's labor intensive for the amount of roe (or actually gonad) you get per urchin, but the unique taste more than makes up for it. It has sort of a nutty, sweet flavor.

It's usually not available live out of the shell in sushi bars, but if you are ever in a sushi bar in California and they offer uni as sushi, make sure it's a somewhat vivid yellow or deep orange and \*not\* pale, texture is firm and \*not\* slimy, and that it has no smell to it. The freshness factor makes the difference between an amazing experience and a miserable experience. Seafood sections in Japanese markets will carry it too (as sashimi grade uni).

I'm not sure on it's nutritional profile. The Japanese have known it to be an aphrodesiac food. With some googling I found out it is rich in anandamide, which I guess activates the dopamine pathway in the brain. Well chocolate has anandamide as well, so I can't know what the implications are for including more urchin in one's diet. Maybe Dr Kruse has some input.

### MartiD — 2012-06-08T18:54:34+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-06-08T20:08:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-30171>

CO (or ev olive oil) work as about SPF 2-4. That means that if you can go out on the longest day of the year at noon and get burned in 30m, CO will get you between 1 and 2hrs before you burn. Not bad, esp. since commercial sunscreen is not ony full of chemicals, but it doesn't protect you from skin cancer, since

\- It's only effective against the rays that cause the warning sign (sunburn). Commercial SPF \*only\* refers to these rays.

\- Even though they've tried to make it protective against UVA rays, they've failed. Every one of them breaks down in ... sunlight. Even physical blocks like zinc/titanium oxide are problems these days b/c most of them contain micronized particles with unknown long-term effects.

Putting on sunscreen allows you to stay out in the sun far past your individual "safe" point (which will depend on time of year, time of day, cloud cover, base tan, genetics, omega 3:6 ratio (so far as I can tell) & other dietary factors I have't yet identified), vit D status, etc.), which is why skin cancer continues to increase the more sunscreen people use. It removes the warning system without removing the danger of excess exposure.

I use CO, and when I've reached my personal max, I cover up. I've found that starting 3 years ago, my body tells me exactly when I've had enough. I watch my children, and theirs does the same - they seek shade naturally when they're on the edge.

I normally have to be careful first 3 or 4 times in sun until I get a base but this year I have just tanned darker than ever before with no burn. I think Dr. K has said he uses CO instead of sunscreen too. It's weird I know!

I am fair skinned and have always burned. I haven't done anything stupid like spend 10 hrs in blazing sun, but I have been working and playing for many hours at a time in full sun with no shirt or hat and since going paleo I do not burn anymore and I have not used any sunblock since 2010. I tan for the first time in my life. WTF? One more amazing benefit.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-16T20:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-31577>

I was in Las Vegas this week for some R & R, and decided to get some Vitamin D with about 15 mins of pool time. I forgot to pack my sunscreen and didn't want to pay an exorbitant amount that they charge in the hotel. I ended up staying poolside for 2 hours, playing in the water, drying off in the sun, repeat.

I'm very fair-skinned and burn extremely easily, even with sunscreen. But this week, in 105 degree cloudless Las Vegas, I am amazed as anyone that I did not even get slightly pink. Must be the CO, the CT, the extra seafood (from zero consumption to once daily), to full-on Paleo.....) Funny, I didn't connect the dots until reading this thread.

### MartiD — 2012-06-26T19:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-33710>

I had the same experience in Vegas last week. Several days at pool, very hot, no clouds, high index. I applied CO each morning before going out and never got red, just a really deep tan. Everyone around me keep putting on 30 spf and still burned. I also enjoyed the cold pool (not sure how hotel managed that with temps 108) but no one would get in pool because it was to cold. I jumped right in and loved it! My only issue now is my skin is very dry even after being home for 5 days. I have doubled up my internal and external CO usage to try to help but I can put CO on my lower legs and in 30 minutes I look flaky and dry again. Hopefully the hot humid weather here in Mid West will get me back to normal soon! I had a hard time in that dry heat!

### vlynnb — 2012-06-27T22:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-34024>

So interesting. I don't sunburn or peel even though I have osteoporosis and leaky gut. I have medium coloring. But what is interesting to me is that as a children growing up in the Rocky Mountains (we moved from the valley floor to the mountains each summer) we played outside all day long. Even at the beginning of the summer when we came up from the valley and very little sun exposure in the winter to immediately playing outside in the sun all day we did not burn. I suppose we went in and out of sun and shade as we played but never did so intentionally. We did eat a lot of grass fed meat but we also ate a lot of breads. Don't know if the fact that it was in the 1950's-60's and wheat in the bread may have been different made a difference. Of course we probably were cold adapted too with no central heat in the winter, oil stove in the living room only, playing outside in the cold winter at least an hour a day, and cold evenings and mornings even in the summer. We also always rinsed our face in cold water after a wash and rinsed our hair in cold water after washing. We were all skinny and healthy until our lifestyle changed after we grew up.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T23:44:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-34047>

Oh, you remind me, I wanted to share this mini cold adaptation. I now wash my hair in the sink in only cold water and a bit of shampoo. I prefer it greatly to the hot shampoos I used to take. Plus it may do my brain some good.

After reading the last blog I have some questions (not able to get my login to work) so I thought I would ask here. I love shellfish, althought can't really afford it everyday, have become somewhat addicted to salmon, enjoy tilapia, and cod. From what I gathered in blog, the shellfish is great even if it isn't fresh but preferred raw. I'm not sure about eating frozen crab legs or shrimp raw because I don't necessary trust the preparation needed to freeze and ship seafood. Does salmon, if wildcaught, benefit us as much? I also love sushi but most of it here is done with imitation crab so I assume that is a bad idea...not to mention the rice.

### [email redacted] — 2012-07-20T04:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-39572>

CEVICHE!!!! TIS SO AWESOME.

[http://mexican.food.com/recipe/citrus-ceviche-with-shrimp-and-scallops-305111](http://http://mexican.food.com/recipe/citrus-ceviche-with-shrimp-and-scallops-305111)

You can use a variety of seafoods, I had with shrimp, lobster, scallops, and some white fish I didn't recognize. They usually serve it with a chip or toast point - but if it's summer, I would suggest a toasted plantain.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-21T06:05:58+0300

> You can use a variety of seafoods, I had with shrimp, lobster, scallops, and some white fish I didn't recognize. They usually serve it with a chip or toast point - but if it's summer, I would suggest a toasted plantain.

I asked my favorite seafood restaurant if they would start serving ceviche, and they said they already do. The "Chilled Seafood Salad" I love so much IS ceviche! Yes, it is AWESOME. I thought the seafood in that dish was cooked conventionally.

So tonight I squeezed a dozen limes over some frozen scallops. I'll let it sit in the fridge overnight and see what I get. I have some frozen shrimp as well, but I'll thaw it overnight and put it in later, since they are already cooked. The recipes I've seen say to add the cilantro just before serving. This is fun.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-21T06:51:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-39935>

My relationship with fruit and veggies sure have changed. Instead of the emphasis I used to place on them, now they are garnish, to be nibbled on after all the meat is eaten. When I see cucumbers, I think of future pickles. When I see cabbage, I think of sauerkraut. Now when I see citrus, I think of a cooking device for my seafood.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-22T02:40:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-adaptation.1626/post-40136>

Oh hey, everyone! Get some limes, some scallops, and some cilantro. That was heaven!

I made a mistake adding cooked shrimp, though. They got slightly tough. All the other stuff, celery, red cabbage, onion, carrots, tomato, & avocado were good too. But really it was the limes, cilantro, and raw seafood. Gonna have to do that once/week.

### BJK77 — 2012-05-24T22:58:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/browned-veggies.1630/post-27165>

Can anyone explain why I can tolerate zucchini, cabbage, broccoli, etc just fine UNLESS it's browned, even just slightly? It seems to incite sugar cravings, which I really haven't had in 2 years now! Do you think it's just because browning the veggies brings out their sugar more?!?! I absolutely love zucchini chips and roasted cabbage, broccoli, etc but every time I have it I want more, more, more!

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T23:03:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/browned-veggies.1630/post-27167>

It seems to incite sugar cravings, which I really haven't had in 2 years now! Do you think it's just because browning the veggies brings out their sugar more?!?! I absolutely love zucchini chips and roasted cabbage, broccoli, etc but every time I have it I want more, more, more!

Browning is caramelization, which at its core is chemical changes in sugars...so maybe?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/browned-veggies.1630/post-27168>
