### Chrysanthemama — 2013-03-14T04:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-80473>

_Vitex agnus-castus_, commonly known as Chasteberry, is known to increase progesterone in the medical herbalism world. It is commonly prescribed to help alleviate PMS, but that is not actually a good use for the herb, because most women probably don't do labs and have no idea what their PG:E2 ratio is. It can have serious side effects for women who self-prescribe, mostly if they are very estrogen deficient, but also if their PG is elevated. It is suggested that Vitex stimulates dopamine in the hypothalamus which, in turn, suppresses prolactin and favors progesterone.

I think that for women who are not PG dominant and not severely deficient in estrogen, it can be a useful way to help support BHRT, and assuming they are testing, they will be able to monitor their PG:E2 ratio and be able to guide their use of the herb along with their BHRT.

For women who are severely estrogen deficient, however, it should also be used with caution, especially if they already experience PMS depression symptoms.

This blurb from Medical Herbalism is interesting: [http://medherb.com/Materia\_Medica/Vitex\_and\_suicidal\_depression.htm](http://medherb.com/Materia_Medica/Vitex_and_suicidal_depression.htm)

I had been down to my smallest weight in 5 years (123#, 5/8") since becoming hypo and then the only change was adding that darn E2. I did also have about 6 strawberries since they are in season where I live but otherwise no diet or supp changes. It's so weird and we are all different!

I've been taking the Biest twice a day at .5ml (I think) but I don't have the bottle in front of me. That's half the dose the doc gave me. I only now do that twice a week. The Pg I do every day. Because I'm so close to being done with cycles both my doc and Dr K recommended taking Pg every day. Dr. K wanted me to increase the Pg (100mg oral bio) but my doc wouldn't sign on for an increase.

I was on the Viville dot a few years ago. I really liked it except for the adhesive. I get little mini rashes if I keep a bandaid on too long and the same thing happened with the dot. I kept moving the spot but after a month I'd have a bunch of little rashes. I'd be interested in trying it again though. I never noticed it was stimulating but back then I was sleeping good.

### freshveggies — 2013-03-14T22:25:29+0200

I kept moving the spot but after a month I'd have a bunch of little rashes. I'd be interested in trying it again though. I never noticed it was stimulating but back then I was sleeping good.

THis last month, I had a cycle that was 55 days long because I took progesterone all month long. I really didn't feel normal doing that. At this point, I was still thinking of mimicking a normal cycle and doing E for the first half then adding in P the last half. Sounds like you will be doing a static dose. I kind of still want to cycle. But I am open to other options too as things change.

### Joann — 2013-03-15T00:17:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-80575>

> [curves64 said:](/goto/post?id=80503)
>
> Joann, fascinating. At the risk of over-sharing, when I started to do sprints on the beach before my swims, I had . . . how do I say this . . . some leakiness. I had to stop. I never connected "thinning, color, urinary continence) to E. My sex hormones were pretty low when I tested last October but E was still above P.

Yup wish I knew then what I know now...hindsight now. Using a little estriol vaginally will help strengthen tissues....bladder, urethra, and be in the "Pink"  estriol does not cause thickening lining in uterus.

### Joann — 2013-03-15T00:37:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-80583>

> [Patty Cakes said:](/goto/post?id=80533)
>
> Joann, you sound liek you know a lot about hormones. E2 was high but now is low as in <6 on the lab report. Pg was mid range which was good with so little estrogen. T was in the low end. No libido, no urinary continence or seemingly other issues that I can tell "down there". Hot flashes at that E2 level were 6-8 times a night so sleep was disrupted and they were starting to happen during the day which was completely unbearable since my office is too hot anyway. When I took T before it did absolutely nothing for my libido (but did help my grow facial hair which was not a bonus) and Dr. K mentioned in the hormone webinar I believe that you start with Pg/E2 ratio and get that optimal first then add T.
>
> I am taking the 100mg oral bio Pg and that minimal amt of Biest. I just added some Pg gel to my regime to get more and hope it helps. I jsut want the hot flashes to go away and not have the bloaty looking pooch!

I hear ya' When you tested hormones, was it serum or saliva? For me doing saliva back then screwed up my decisions since it looked like I had high E, T, and P was off the charts. serum showed otherwise.
Using P orally shows up on serum testing but I've found the creams and gels do not. what that means idk. I have thoughts about it. also E topically does not show up on my serum tests.
I think if you use P all month ....when you stop you may get a bleed. Have you tried Triest? a little estrone in the mix might help. 80% estriol, 10 Estradiol, 10 estrone.

> [Patty Cakes said:](/goto/post?id=80474)

> This is interesting. I have tested total estrogens each time and my estrone is practically nil. I'm "for all intense and purposes menopausal" (words of JK) although every few months my body decides to have a period! So I have no estrone, tons of E2 and a belly. I had been down to my smallest weight in 5 years (123#, 5/8") since becoming hypo and then the only change was adding that darn E2. I did also have about 6 strawberries since they are in season where I live but otherwise no diet or supp changes. It's so weird and we are all different!

I reread above and noticed your hypo.....ok, I would keep a look on SHBG since when using E causes it to go up which may bind up thyroid. when E goes up then SHBG goes up.

### BJK77 — 2013-03-15T18:44:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-80687>

Ok ladies, I need some input here! So many of you seem to have such a good handle on hormone issues and I just can't seem to wrap my head around them. I feel like my situation is constantly changing and every time I think I begin to figure out what's going on with me things end up doing a 180 and I'm lost all over again!

I doubled my oral Pg a few weeks ago because I was having trouble sleeping at the beginning and end of my cycles. I now take 200 mg before bed and I do this throughout my entire cycle. It solved the problem of not sleeping well at the beginning of my cycle, but now I'm having difficulty sleeping during ovulation. I wake up in the middle of the night feeling wide awake and super HOT. I'm also finding myself extremely depressed, weepy and emotional and colder during ovulation, which I don't get since this used to be more of a PMS thing for me. After getting my thyroid somewhat under control I had finally started feeling warmer without ice cold hands and feet all the time, but that's now returning too.

We cut wifi and the breakers for the bedrooms at night (and that did seem to help my sleep initially). I am super strict about minding artificial light at night. It's a bit more difficult in the morning with DST because I now have to wake and be out the door when it's still dark out. Point is I don't thing there's any other contributing factors here. These issues seem to definitely be related to ovulation ?? Any thoughts on what I should try to fix this? I need some sleep!!

### curves64 — 2013-03-21T19:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81344>

My biggest "issue" still remaining was the diminishment of my orgasms. I have an amazing new bf and we have a great sexual relationship. But prior to meeting him, my ability to have great orgasms slipped from a 12 to a 4. Since I was always a super horny woman, this seemed like death to me. I felt like a part of me just disappeared. After one application of the progesterone gel, I had an orgasm reminiscent of my former ones. Although it's only been a few days, i can feel that side of myself coming back to life and I love it!!!!

I intend to add DHEA to the mix but the DHEA I bought was shipped to the wrong address. The error will give me time to see how the progesterone works by itself a little longer so no big deal.

I remember Jack saying in a QA that we shouldn't worry about too much Progesterone so I'm going to enjoy playing around with my dosage and my orgasms

### freshveggies — 2013-03-25T19:32:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81737>

HI Ladies, for those who are still cycling, how are you increasing your progesterone without messing up your cycle? For those of you who are not estrogen dominant also how are you getting more progesterone without over-riding the estrogen?

### SeaJane — 2013-03-25T22:12:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81770>

> [freshveggies said:](/goto/post?id=81737)
>
> HI Ladies, for those who are still cycling, how are you increasing your progesterone without messing up your cycle? For those of you who are not estrogen dominant also how are you getting more progesterone without over-riding the estrogen?

I am curious about this as well. I tried the Wiley protocol as given in the book since I was low e2 and low pg although estrogen dominant (ratio was 30). The second month was the best (my P/E2 ratio was over 100 compared to 30 when I started) and I felt the difference. The third month I felt worse again and my P/E2 ratio was back to 30. I also had extreme cold and raynard's symptoms mid-month when estrogen was especially high (didn't have this before BHRT) and mid-month bleeding and then light periods that were arriving on day 26 even though I was on a 28 day cycle. So this month, I am going on intuition. I am supplementing progesterone all month although the first two weeks is lower than the second two weeks. I started at 200mg/day and then between day 11 and day 14 gradually increased to 500mg/day where I am at here at day 16. Estradiol I dosed at 4-5mgs day 1 - day 9, upped to 6mg Day 10 - day 12, I then dropped to 2 mg day 13 and 14, and then went to 4 mg day 15-16. So far, I feel the best of any cycle so far--no bleeding, no extreme cold, only 1 instance of raynard's. I am still trying to decide whether I will increase my progesterone a lot more or keep it steady where it is at.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-26T19:42:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81872>

Just a report on taking my compounded Pg (w/probiotics) vaginally. After about 10 days now I have decided to go back to oral. There are a couple of reasons: 1) My sleep has turned into not complete 8 hours or even 6 hours, very disruptive 2) In the morning, I think I am "loosing" a bunch of it that drips out.

Mostly it's the sleep issue so I'm back to 100 mg orally and supping with 34 gms of Pg gel. Still looking for Pg drops (sublingual) if anyone knows of a brand or good ones that actually raise the Pg level.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-26T19:57:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81876>

I take the oral at night and the gel in the morning. I think I may need to add more gel at night but I'm waiting a few more weeks before I make that decision. It shouldn't be that big of a deal since I know my Pg/E2 ratio needs some help.

I am one of those who do this every day. When I started Pg supping several years ago, I did it every day. I continued to have regular periods for a long time after (years) so I just kept it up. Then stopped taking Pg (idiocy!!!) for a few years and have started back up again in the past couple of years. I have always loved my Pg. I thought of cycling it but I'm 54 and have a period every few months still so I've been told since my body likes it so much as evidenced by better sleep, less bloating, helps maintain proper weight, etc that I can do it every day. My current doc told me if I want a period every month I should cycle it. Are there any reasons why this would be a better path to take?

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-26T20:03:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81878>

I thought of cycling it but I'm 54 and have a period every few months still so I've been told since my body likes it so much as evidenced by better sleep, less bloating, helps maintain proper weight, etc that I can do it every day. My current doc told me if I want a period every month I should cycle it. Are there any reasons why this would be a better path to take?

I can't answer if cycling is better or not... Dzugan does cycle, but at lower doses in the beginning of a cycle. me I've discovered that I do better on a steady state of the same qty of PG across the cycle. this past cycle was 28days! complete FIRST EVER! yeah. I'm taking 20% Pg gel 1ml am and 1ml pm + .2ml of 5% sublingual in the am and .3ml of 5% sublingual in the pm.

I think I need more in the first 5 days and last 5days of my cycle - given how much my sleep goes to crap during those two weeks... follow your gut!

### freshveggies — 2013-03-26T20:06:36+0200

I thought of cycling it but I'm 54 and have a period every few months still so I've been told since my body likes it so much as evidenced by better sleep, less bloating, helps maintain proper weight, etc that I can do it every day. My current doc told me if I want a period every month I should cycle it. Are there any reasons why this would be a better path to take?

I would say if it isn't broke don't change it for now. Sleep might trump cycling. I had a doctor who told me my PG was too high based on a saliva test so I quit it in 2007 and 6 weeks later got insomnia. Ill informed doctor. I would like to be able to take it like you do with success. I don't see a problem with how you do this. Dr. Uzzie has his menopausal patients take it same dose all month long and I really like Dr. Phuli Cohan's book and she does this too. If I took it same all month long, I would quit TOM and I don't know if I should do that. I started to get a very down feeling when I did that,. Once I quit the P and just did E first half, I felt much better. Not sure yet what my body wants.

my dh uses T w/p cream...cant remember why but he "borrowed" some of my P cream and had the worst sleep, waking up all night. He never did that again.

### BJK77 — 2013-03-26T22:08:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81887>

> [Patty Cakes said:](/goto/post?id=81876)
>
> I take the oral at night and the gel in the morning. I think I may need to add more gel at night but I'm waiting a few more weeks before I make that decision. It shouldn't be that big of a deal since I know my Pg/E2 ratio needs some help.
>
> I am one of those who do this every day. When I started Pg supping several years ago, I did it every day. I continued to have regular periods for a long time after (years) so I just kept it up. Then stopped taking Pg (idiocy!!!) for a few years and have started back up again in the past couple of years. I have always loved my Pg. I thought of cycling it but I'm 54 and have a period every few months still so I've been told since my body likes it so much as evidenced by better sleep, less bloating, helps maintain proper weight, etc that I can do it every day. My current doc told me if I want a period every month I should cycle it. Are there any reasons why this would be a better path to take?

I've been taking it straight through my cycle for the last month or so even though my current doc advises against it . . . Jack suggested I give it a try to improve my sleep since it always suffered at the start of my cycle when I was cutting the Pg.

I'm currently taking 200 mg orally at night and 100 mg Prometrium vaginally. Like Patty, I find it definitely helps with bloating, sleep and just a general overall positive feeling, but I am really struggling with my weight and everything points to the Pg being the culprit. I have hips, thighs and love handles that have never existed on my body before! I don't want to sacrifice my newfound positive outlook on life and overall happiness for something as vain as my appearance, but at the same time the weight is totally stressing me out. My clothes aren't even fitting . . . I'm used to being very slim. I suddenly have curves (excessively so) and I can't say I like them

### tweety — 2013-03-26T22:35:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81890>

I have excess weight and am taking a break. But, when I feel like its time I will switch to the oral progesterone. The biggest positive result for me with the P cream was stable mood and no more outbursts! And that was huge because I've dealt with terrible moodiness and anger since I was a teen! This month is my first full month off, and while I haven't lost any of my excess weight yet (boo), I am still fairly calm. A few outbursts, but pretty minor. I think my body still needs the P, but clearly the way I was supplying it wasn't the right answer.

What I have been told is that the topical cream has a greater tendency to cause weight gain, and the vaginal dosing acts much like the cream. So, if weight gain or other problems are surfacing, has anyone tried just switching to the oral and seeing a reduction in the negative side effects?

### Patty Cakes — 2013-03-27T04:32:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81934>

> my dh uses T w/p cream...cant remember why but he "borrowed" some of my P cream and had the worst sleep, waking up all night. He never did that again.

Right now my doc will only script me the 100mg oral Pg so I had to find another source for the extra my body wants. I need to bring my ratio to a better number. I need to do labs again here soon to see if the Pg gel has helped at all. Plus the little E I've been taking should show a higher level there as well. My doc is usually pretty good about this kind of stuff but she's definitely not as pro Pg as I am!

This month is my first full month off, and while I haven't lost any of my excess weight yet (boo), I am still fairly calm. A few outbursts, but pretty minor. I think my body still needs the P, but clearly the way I was supplying it wasn't the right answer.
>
> What I have been told is that the topical cream has a greater tendency to cause weight gain, and the vaginal dosing acts much like the cream. So, if weight gain or other problems are surfacing, has anyone tried just switching to the oral and seeing a reduction in the negative side effects?

With both Prometrium I used years ago and now the compounded, I have never had any negative side effects with the oral. Just my n=1, and my body loves it. I haven't hit those high doses some of you have take so maybe there is a sweet spot that was passed with the high doses.

### tweety — 2013-03-27T23:45:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-81996>

> [Patty Cakes said:](/goto/post?id=81935)
>
> With both Prometrium I used years ago and now the compounded, I have never had any negative side effects with the oral. Just my n=1, and my body loves it. I haven't hit those high doses some of you have take so maybe there is a sweet spot that was passed with the high doses.

Yes, while I think my initial dosing was too small, somewhere along the way I went too high. I know my levels are still strong because I just completed my first month without it. The last few months using the cream I experienced depression right before my period started. Also, swelling in my feet. This month, I had a few little anger moments, but recovered very quickly and calmly... and no swelling and no depression! Yay! I don't know if my body picked up the slack and started producing its own (doubtful) or if I just surpassed what I needed, but so far I am hesitantly happy.

Now, if I could just lose this weight on my middle...

Funniest thing I have noticed- before P if my weight was up in any way I was immediately depressed and hyper focused. Now, I am aware I am unhappy with my shape but when I don't think about it I don't worry or obsess. In other words, my physical image doesn't line up with my mental perception of myself. That's a first in my life. I'll take it.

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-03-29T22:33:11+0200

Quick question for you ladies in the know

I think I read somewhere that an ideal progesterone to estradial ratio is like 300:100 or somewhere along there.

I have been perscribed progesterone but recently backed off of it due to adverse side effects

I'm wondering if its perhaps bc they did not also give me estrogen?

My numbers are:

Progesterone: 10.6 ng/ml
Estradial: 120 pg/ml
testosterone: 24 ng/dL
Pregnelone: 89 ng/dL

These were drawn on the 21st day of my cycle - thoughts? am i considered estrogen dominant? estrogen deficient?

### tweety — 2013-03-30T00:16:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-82214>

I've been listening to Sara Gottfried too. I like her.

As far as D Ribose- I took it for a few days and realized that it was making it hard for me to settle at night and then I woke up extremely groggy and couldn't get through the morning without a nap. I took it out and I am fine. That was only 5g, so I am thinking in a week or two I may try a smaller dose and see...

I also wanted to say- I was on a high dose of NDT for the winter (180mg). I started walking outside barefoot in the snow when taking my dog out (so only for a minute at a time). Within a few days my thyroid meds were too strong, so I dropped down to 150. Too much, down to 120, too much, down to 90. The days are getting longer and we're getting more light, but I think the barefoot grounding is really helping. I was suprised when I read it needs to be 30-40 minutes at a time for it to really work, because I am feeling better with just a little. I can't wait until I can spend more time out there barefoot!

Ugh, got back from the Drs last weekend. Saw a NP bc my Doc wasnt available and wow - what a mistake. She said I was actually progesterone dominant (!) - when i brought up the estrogen dominance - but didnt really offer any help. She is also the one - who refused to give me metformin bc she said I was hypoglycemic as a result of adrenal thyroid issues. She said it would make me worse. Showed a complete lack of understanding that irritated me to no end. I told her I was frustrated with my weight not really moving and she said it was adrenal thyroid but I was like - yeah we've been working on this for 2 years now and I'm still having issues. I left that appt practically in tears from frustration with my situation. Im on cortisol (although attempting to wean off due to the negative effects on Blood Glucose) and on thyroid - even when I get my levels to where you think they would be good, I'm still not losing weight. I have to do something like HCG to move anything at all but I really dont want to have to do that 1-2 x a year for the rest of my life.

I'm not sure what to do with the P. I just seem to react badly too it. I got super large painful breasts from it, and I was gaining some weight plus any more and it would put me to sleep and/or give me REALLY dry eyes.

My cycles have been over 35 days and my periods are light-to med. Not the heavy you'd expect with hashis.

Not sure what my next steps are. Dzugan doesnt seem to know what to do with me.

Guess I'm hoping the metformin might help me re-set the insulin/glucose stuff that seems to wreak so much havoc on my other hormones in general. So far I do - after a few days of side effects - notice a differnce. I know longer have weird intense cravins so its easier to stick to the protocols. Guess overseas perscriptions it is!

Megan - saw this last night... I realize your frustrations with the lack of understanding your health providers have. lets take a step back and take a deep breath, and understand that while they may have the degree - they don't know your body, nor do they understand your particular situation.

looking at your Pg:E2 ratio which is 76.5 - you need more Pg - you are E-dominate based upon the ratio. One things for sure you definitely need more Pregnenolone. Here's the thing with your thyroid/adrenal issues - they're 100% related to your Brain-Gut axis and EMF. I'd wager your still not leptin sensitive at the liver b/c you're still having the reactive hypoglycemia - but its not b/c of your adrenals - its b/c your Liver isn't functioning well - its operating on a status quo - doing just what it needs to get buy. coming from my own struggles of trying to loose weight - its not going to happen until you bring the liver up to speed, and fully address the thyroid - how are your thryoid levels looking ? you've got hashi's - so based upon the EMF series - that is the result of the disconnection from the Schumann resonance. you may be doing a good job of limiting your EMF - but you're still using your iPad - which is giving off boku EMF and WIFI. Go to a hard wired internet cable - connect a keyboard and use a stylus if you're going to use the ipad. turn your breakers off in your bedroom at night. And get out side into nature and spend as much time as you can there. The fact that HCG works for you - tells me you have a BIG EMF problem that you don't know about. you need to start checking - I highly recommend considering the EMF boot Camp - b/c I've learned a lot from it... you might not realize but you could have something pushing out EMF that you didn't know about - say a new flat screen TV, your Printer etc.

As for Pg giving your tender breasts - it could be your estrogen battling the amt of Pg you've got. while your ratio says your E-dominate - you could be like Vikieran - who actually needs more E. it might be the case that you need more Pg and more E. - I would start updating Dzugan every 5 days - take detailed notes of how you feel. I would talk to your Dzugan adviser and express your frustration - I did that - and wound up having an hour & 1/2 telephone consult w/Dzugan - I expressed my frustration and he's more open to my inputs now. The more detail you provide him the more he can help you.

metformin is just one thing you can do to help w/the insulin resistance & the reactive hypoglycemia - the other is go back to the BAB - however focus on it being high fat, moderate protein this time around. also make sure you eat even just a little bit of a small lunch to keep you smooth and steady. focus on eating a ketogenic diet - until your labs start showing you're getting to the the heart of the problem. given you have adrenal issues - I'd say you're very sensitive to EMF. you might want to consider using cycloset first -go back and lobby your doc to use that - cycloset will help you reset your dopamine and cortisol patterns, it will also help with your insulin resistance -but the key is going back and following all the principles of the Leptin Reset eating an epi paleo template...

I'm not sure if any of this helped - but please pick my brain if you need more help!

Wow, Gretchen - this IS REALLY HELPFUL. It at the very least reminds me that I am not crazy and that there are still ways to address things even though at times I feel I have tried EVERYTHING. Sometimes I just get really tired of it all and feel so hopeless. To the point where I am honestly searching for a good therapist to help me get through this while I address the health issues. I've always have a hard time being patient.

This past weekend my husbands family dealt with a death in the family (grandma) so his immediate family flew up to help. I was pretty busy from Sat-Wed. Hosting, making runs to the airport, etc. It was great to see everyone even during sad circumstances, but I was really struck at how HEALTHY i ate compare to what they were doing. They still use fake butter fake sugar eat gluten etc. Literally his sister eats nothing but processed foods. I adore here and she is lucky bc she has no health issues but but she asically eats raisin toast every morning, chik-fil-a for lunch and cinnamon toast crunch for dinner (!!!!!). And here I am, eating paleo/grain free/dairy free/sugar free...taking supps, exercising (biking)....and yet? I need to get over it but as I tried to answer questions -why they should get away from skim milk and if they are drinking milk at all it should be whole, grassfed/hormone free preferably raw...I kinda had to throw in a "but take what I say with a grain of salt bc I'm the one with health issues". Trying to be light about it. I have learned so much since taking charge of my health but sometimes i feel as if I have nothing to show for it. Even my husband says "maybe you are doing to much and should just go back to square one before you were on all these supps and elimations diets and whatnot." Dont get me wrong, he's EXTREMELY supportive but I can see where he's coming from and know he feels frustrated bc he cant really help.

I will continue to look at EMF. I have been dealing with a non-stop busy season from Sept until now, but its finally slowing down. Besides finding a new job (bc honestly I dont believe that kind of stress did anything for my health), it should give me a chance to address some things and do some more research. I've fallen behind on blog posts and just tryed to get the high level stuff from these boards.

I really appreciate your insight bc you seem to grasp what Dr K says and are able to put his information into easy to understand sentences which is a godsend around here!

I am acutally testing my thyroid levels today. They were wacky in Dec actually showed a little HIGH even though I was sure they were low based on symptons. I'm curious what they will show now.

I have been eating BAB but will try what you said - maybe more moderate on the protein side, higher on the fat.

I dont think I can go completely wi-fi free in my home yet - but will look at at least turning it off when we aren't using it and making the bedroom an EMF zone. The breaker part is an especially good idea.

I really wanted to install flux on my ipad but cant figure out how to jailbreak it. So I've been using those orange glasses at night for TV watching and computer using. Not exactly great for the sex life though, I'll say that!

My husband and I are now actively planning on moving from the city. It will probably take another year or a year in a half to get our ducks in a row but I think moving to a less stressful environment that is easier on the pocket book will be great. Between the income tax, the sales tax and my property tax, my husband and i feel a squeeze when we shouldnt considering our income(my healthcare costs/supp costs dont help!).

I'm also feeling pressure bc its definitely baby making time, but obv that cant/wont happen until these health issues are addressed.

I'm suspecting that I need E to balance out the P. My periods are lighter and farther apart - usually more than 35 days. I am hoping they up the pregneolone at the very least. I know they aren't a good idea, but when I think back on it, I felt HEALTHIER when I was on the pill - a combo of fake E and fake P. I'm not going back to that, but I sometimes wonder if having BOTH of those hormones in my body was part of the reason my weight was steadier and I had less issues and felt better. I got off them around the time I suspected thyroid issues (sure that that pill didnt help that!) but long before I was actually diganosed. I will talk to the D consultant and maybe push for my own consult with the big man himself. I def should update them more but I think part of me isnt trusting that they will be able to help me. I need to get over that.

Apologies for the novel! You have given me some hope and more to think about. Thank you!

I just thought I would post here a couple of things I have done wrong lately hopefully you guys are smarter than I was!

I was using a Progesterone vaginal pessary for 30 days ...I was also taking oral Pg and told to take it 2 hours before bed ...so I also did the pessary \[bullet shape\] at the same time - I laid down for at least 30 minutes if not longer but would notice some leakage - so I just thought I wasn't absorbing it very well by that route. It turns out that you need to insert the pessary before bed and then don't get up - this from a pharmacist.

The next stupid thing - I am also using Pg cream and putting it on my tummy or thighs. It turns out if you use essential oils they could inhibit absorption. I have just discovered essential oils and love them and have been putting them in the bath when I do C/T - almost every day!

So - I just got the results from my last ASI and needless to say - my Pg didn't come up all that much - very disappointing.
All this stuff costs so much money and I really want to get the best results possible. I suppose the pharmacist should of told me or I should have asked...... when you know better - you do better.....

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-04-10T23:40:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-83671>

> [tweety said:](/goto/post?id=83644)
>
> nuttmegs- how did your body resist the thyroid medication? In what way do you mean?

Well it has since settled down ...it was over a year ago, but when I was first diagnosed it just seemed like my body would swell up once it hit my system. Not typical bloat like PMS but something I had never experienced before. I'd have stomach issues, it would give me edema esp in my stomach of all places and I'd become SO FATIGUED and tired. Its like it did the opposite of what it should have. It was so bad 2 summers ago that I ended up sitting out a lot of my family time. I coudnt walk around with people downtome bc I was in pain (stomach hurting, swelling, uncomfortable in my clothes). And I was tired.

We backed up and went extremely slow. At somepoint I got to my current dose and i have been ok since. As my husband pointed out though - I've never been where I need to with it. I think bc I was just happy to not be in pain I got lazy about staying on top of it. I am certainly not optimal though. I was keeping a daily journal at the time just to keep up with symptoms. I should probably go and re-read that thing.

In any event, it wasnt as simple as "oh hey, your thyroid is low, take this armour and you'll feel all better!" its like i had to ease into it. We eventually added cytomel as well but again slowly. Adding HC seemed to help some, but now i suspect a bit of weight gain from the HC so I most be on too much. I'm weaning off of it - on about 5 mg in the morning. Still waiting to level off before backing off further.

One time I increased my dose and the stars aligned. I felt like my old self, my body wasnt swollen or fatigued but I mentioned yawning at one point so the NP said "oh yeah, up it" and once I did that, It sent me over the edge and I had to back off again.

I just isn't/wasn't an easy adjustment. I had side effects that showed up from the estrogen as well.

My hope is that if a thyroid dose is needed, that I've healed my adrenals, etc enough over the last coulpe of months to tolerate it.

### tweety — 2013-04-11T15:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-83739>

Nuttmegs- I have been on thyroid for almost a year now. It did help with energy and my mood this winter was fantastic! First ever winter without depression. However, I continued to gain weight, esp when adding in Progesterone cream. And the way I gained it was so unusual. Eventually I noticed edema in my feet and ankles. With winter I was always wearing socks and pants, so I never really noticed until the end. I just stopped my thyroid dose altogether two days ago. My thinking was this: one of two things happened- I was a high dose of armour and didn't realize it was too high for a little while. heart palps, extreme hunger, irritability- maybe two weeks of this? this coincided with spring light and me going barefoot (grounding) in the snow. Everytime I dropped the symptoms were alleviated for a few days, then they started up again. This went on until I was on a very low dose. Combined with your thoughts that you were reacting the opposite of what you should have (ie hormone resistance) I decided to do a little n=1 for myself.
I will be testing again soon I am sure, so am not concerned. I feel like my body needs a reset. The symptoms your mentioned I definitely experienced- upset stomach, bloating and pain. I am hesitant to add anything else to my protocol until I settle down a bit. And yes, journaling is key! I started a food journal but quickly realized it should be food, activities, time schedules, and supplements. Alot of info in one little book!

Long story short, hang in there. You are not alone in your adverse reactions.

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-04-11T20:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-83774>

> [tweety said:](/goto/post?id=83739)
>
> Nuttmegs- I have been on thyroid for almost a year now. It did help with energy and my mood this winter was fantastic! First ever winter without depression. However, I continued to gain weight, esp when adding in Progesterone cream. And the way I gained it was so unusual. Eventually I noticed edema in my feet and ankles. With winter I was always wearing socks and pants, so I never really noticed until the end. I just stopped my thyroid dose altogether two days ago. My thinking was this: one of two things happened- I was a high dose of armour and didn't realize it was too high for a little while. heart palps, extreme hunger, irritability- maybe two weeks of this? this coincided with spring light and me going barefoot (grounding) in the snow. Everytime I dropped the symptoms were alleviated for a few days, then they started up again. This went on until I was on a very low dose. Combined with your thoughts that you were reacting the opposite of what you should have (ie hormone resistance) I decided to do a little n=1 for myself.
> I will be testing again soon I am sure, so am not concerned. I feel like my body needs a reset. The symptoms your mentioned I definitely experienced- upset stomach, bloating and pain. I am hesitant to add anything else to my protocol until I settle down a bit. And yes, journaling is key! I started a food journal but quickly realized it should be food, activities, time schedules, and supplements. Alot of info in one little book!
>
> Long story short, hang in there. You are not alone in your adverse reactions.

Thank you for sharing that - its so frustrating!! Yes, our experience makes it a bit more complicated. Most docs are just confused. When I had graves about 8 years ago, I had the opposite reaction to that as well - I gained a good 30 pounds virtually overnight. It was always frustrated talking with nurses when I checked in...theyd say "HYPER? No you obviously have HYOPthyroidism" Ugh. That was kicked into remission so now I'm dealing with this...too bad I didnt have an opposite reaction to hashis - would have been nice to be too skinny, ha

So yes, in short, being on too much meds can make you feel worse and even cause weight gain. I always tell my friends if it was so simple to lose weight on thyroid meds, I'd be a millionaire bc I'd be selling it in the street. Unfortunately, as we all know it is much more complicated than that.

i'm on the edge of my seat waiting for my thyroid lab work to be sent over. I think it should have come in by now....

I think I might just try maca and chasteberry (through a brand called fertility blend)...supposedly these remedites work at the HPA axis and help your body produce these hormones on its own. THAT would be nice. If not, I'll add in the P and E

Better, tell Dzugan what I'm doing...off to do another update!

### tweety — 2013-04-11T21:56:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-83785>

Just a thought nuttmegs- Have you considered another underlying cause that could be driving your strange responses?

For example, I went through my old lab work just to reassess my situation.I came across the fact that I did the western blot test for lyme and had two bands come back positive. At the time I was told I did not have what was needed for a definitive diagnosis, so therefore I didn't have lyme. However, a year later, I researched lyme again and found I have some other unexplained and random weirdness going on that others with lyme did as well. Lyme can contribute to hormone imbalance, as well as just about everything else! I am now going to pursue homeopathic options in treating this as I believe I can't resolve my other problems while this is still an issue. Perhaps there is something going on for you that is wreaking havoc on your progress?

T3: 174 (71 - 180)
RT3: 10.2 (9.2 - 25.1)

i just came from a naturopath who does his own testing, so it's all covered in his consult fee. He does not necessarily use antibiotics, but was recommending looking into a RIFE machine. So I am going to research what exactly that is. I actually won't be going back for other reasons, but it gave me another avenue to pursue.

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-05-10T23:29:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-85809>

> [colleencoble said:](/goto/post?id=28841)
>
> It was six years ago last week since I started on the Dzugan protocol. It's been life-changing for me. I was one of his first patients after he opened his migraine clinic. **Not sure if all of you know how he got involved with hormones but he was doing clinical trials with cancer patients and hormones to see if strengthening their immune system by restoring their hormones would help them fight their cancers. That was successful** but he kept having patients come to him and say, "Dr, I don't know what you did but my migraines are gone." He's very up on natural remedies too. I love the guy. He's a real genius with hormones. He knows exactly how to tweak them based on your symptoms. It's almost scary. LOL

Have been re-reading this thread and found this part interesting....I think i read somewhere that BHRT is considered treatment for lyme in some circles because it strengthens the immune system. I wish my system would stop fighting new things as this could be a key for me! keep on keeping on.

Also, perhaps you smarties can help me understand something.

I have noticed - just second hand -that birth control pills seem to be more easily tolerated in general than BHRT (not depo - two of my friends gained massive amounts of weight on that which fell off once they discontinued). I was on them for years and all it did was good things: no PMS, no cramps, regular light cycle, etc. i also never struggled with my weight at that time.

Of course, I understand now that pills are too quickly adminstered by docs who would rather COVER UP problmes (like PCOS...so many stories of girls who only discovered they had PCOS AFTER they stopped the pills bc it hid the symptons for so long) rather than get to the bottom of hormone dysregulation. I also believe that being on BCP for so long no doubt speed up me getting hashi's - It just did more harm than good in the long run and Im glad to be off of them. However...

Why do you think they are gernally more easily tolerated than BHRT? It seems it takes a while to get the magic BHRT dose for everyone. BCP are so much less flexible - its a set dose in a pack, in one form , no tweaking possible. Is it because BCP are set and not cyclical? Bc to my understanding , most are consistent E/P, low levels with a one week placebo. Does this make it more easily tolerated? Perhaps I should try something similar except with BHRT instead of cycling.....

Its just odd bc my girlfirends will swich up their BC method seemingly from month to month: maybe they try the ring, then the patch, then this pill then that, and very rarely do I hear them complain off massive weight gain or painful breasts. Generally they stay consistent.

Just thinking out loud here.

BCP are so much less flexible - its a set dose in a pack, in one form , no tweaking possible. Is it because BCP are set and not cyclical? Bc to my understanding , most are consistent E/P, low levels with a one week placebo. Does this make it more easily tolerated? Perhaps I should try something similar except with BHRT instead of cycling.....

I never tolerated BCPs - when I take them - I bleed constantly. TALK. ABOUT. SUCK. Mirena - helped me w/my migraines - progestin (mimics progesterone which was what I needed and alleviated cysts), Noreplant - worked at first, then became increasingly horrible - so bad that I had them take it out early... and went back to BCPs and constant bleeding... until I baddgered my ob for mirena.

Why do women accept BCPs - b/c its the only thing we know about for preventing pregnancy. we're told it will help our symptoms of X, Y & Z. we trust our doctors - but how much of what they know is what't they've studied on their own, and how much is what they've been sold by the drug mfrs?

every woman is different, every woman has a different reaction... n = 1 is key but we've lost that experience in the BIG Pharma Medical care we live in... so our symptoms we're told are just side effects everyone gets them etc... when the reality is it may or may not be true... the reason they keep switching up is b/c the side effects suck..and they're trying to find something that works... which is why I eventually ended up using the Fertility Awareness MEthod....

### JanSz — 2013-06-07T05:52:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-87674>

> [endless said:](/goto/post?id=87668)
>
> Could someone please help me figure out what my DHEA level is in whatever the 'US' standard units is? I have tried but am having real trouble....my value is given in umol/L. What unit should I be converting it to??

desirable values=

DHEAs,serum----
Women (275-400) μg/dL
Men (350-490) μg/dL

///////
umol/L=μmol/L
///////////////
to convert from the conventional unit to the SI unit, multiply by the conversion factor;
to convert from the SI unit to the conventional unit, divide by the conversion factor.

Estradiol pg/mL 3.671 pmol/L(30pg/mL \* 3.671)=110.13pmol/L
Estriol ng/mL 3.467 nmol/L
Estrone ng/dL 37 pmoI/L
Testosterone ng/dL 0.0347 nmol/L(700ng/dL \* 0.0347)=24.9nmol/L
DHEA ng/dL 0.0347 nmol/L
**DHEA Sulfate μg/dL 0.0271 μmol/L**
DHEAs 100 mcg/dL=100 Âµg/dL= 2.71Âµmol/L= 2.71umol/L
DHEAs 500mcg/dL=500Âµg/dL=13.55Âµmol/L=13.55umol/L
DHEAs 640mcg/dL=17.34Âµmol/L=17.34umol/L

Pregnanediol (urine) mg/24h 3.12 ÂµmoI/d
Pregnanetriol (urine) mg/24 h 2.97 Âµmol/d
Progesterone ng/mL 3.18 nmol/L
1 nmol/L of Progesterone converts to 0.31 ng/mL

Nutricology/ Allergy Research Group Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix
Pregnenolone 150mg is micronized and sustained released in a lipid matrix

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B003PWJFJW/ref=oh\_details\_o00\_s00\_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B003PWJFJW/ref=oh_details_o00_s00_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

When you are missing progesterone
very likely your pregnenolone and cortisol may be low

pregnenolone should fix all three
or even four (aldosterone)

plus
it all will work much more naturally under control of your own enzymes

It works for me, I am using it already over 2 years

I am using one of those pills at wakeup next pill 10-11PM

Eventually (and relatively quickly) it convers down to cortisol.
So dosing of pregnenolone is better when in early morning hours (hen cortisol should be highest).

...................
Eventually you should not need progesterone script in first half of cycle.
If you do, you may want to increase pregnenolone in third quarter of cycle, say days 16-24

### mafixit — 2014-09-11T03:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-140586>

I have been gone from this forum for a while - busy with other things, but am back to share my experience. I have been working with Dzugan since April and am no better and in fact worse than I was when I started. They are turning out to be not as attentive as promised and I think this is due to some personnel changes. I put in my updates and get messages that they have been received, but don't get my program adjustment they say is ready for release, or they get my update, confirm its been received and then don't even say they have adjusted. My original patient rep is gone, so I have had Linda's help - and she is helpful and caring, but apparently is at the mercy of my local Dzugan physician.

At first they had me on a host of supplements and hormones, then pulled back to just Tri-Est, Progesterone, Testosterone, Pregnenalone, DHEA and 5HTP. I put in two updates without any adjustment.

I just went to have 3 month labs redrawn (Labcorp lost my first set of orders) and those should be back this week. I am not having any confidence in BHRT at all. I did the elimination diet on the advice of my nutritionist - no help for headaches or pain. I am eating a good balance of fruits, veggies, and all that is good for me more than ever, but it seems the more I try to do for myself that is healthy, the worse I get. I can say that I do have more physical energy, but my brain hurts - literally. I had a Lyme panel done at Igenex - I should have the results in a couple of days.

The headaches are daily - they never let up. Migraine meds don't work and I don't have typical migraine symptoms - no nausea, vomiting, sensitivities for the most part. I just get dizzy and get these all over tension headaches that start in the left side of my neck and shoulder and work their way up until they take over my whole head. The more the left side hurts, the more my head hurts or the more my head hurts, the more my left side hurts.

I have a bulging disk with mild flattening of the left anterior cord, compressed nerve (moderate to severe foraminal inlet stenosis on the left) and bone spur at C5-C6. One spine doc says yes, that is causing the constant pain on the left side in my neck, shoulder and upper back and could be contributing to my headaches; one spine doc says its all in my head and go read a book on the mind/body connection and bring out my repressed emotions; one doc (from the Dzugan team) says yes, there could be a correlation, which could be why I am not responding to BHRT. I am scheduled for a some testing and a diagnostic nerve block with a laser spine surgical team. Pain relief from the block= surgery.

All I know is, it sucks. Anyone else have C5-C6 issues that contributed to headaches?

Karen from Arizona

### nonchalant — 2014-09-11T04:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-140611>

Hi Karen. I had issues somewhere in my neck and concurrent headaches (not migraine). I got relief from one chiropractor who was very good with necks. It really felt good when he would finish up by pulling on my head (often popping some joints). After a while, I discovered simply lifting my head (pushing up on the jaws while a pillow or something behind the neck kept the spine in alignment) would bring quick relief from a new headache. I got quite good at it, but now I seldom do it because my neck is much better now. I did not have a bone spur, though.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-22T15:09:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bhrt-advice-suggestions-and.1633/post-153595>

Hi everyone. This thread is how I found Jack's forum/website/book/Jack himself. Now that I've been here a month or more I figured it was time to post. I just saw my compounding pharmacist yesterday for my BHRT consult (my gyn refers people to him when they complain of issues that she feels BHRT /Adrenal Fatigue treatment can help with). He pushes AF supplements and diet really hard - pretty sure he makes most of his money that way based on how he ignored everything I came in for and just told me he diagnoses everyone with AF and puts them all on \*his\* diet he invented (it's called Paleo, gee how smart of him) and his special supplements. \*eyeroll\*

Unfortunately this is my only shot at BHRT if I need something prescribed because it's who my gyn works with. So, ignoring all his AF schpiel - which was about 90 minutes while BHRT was about 5 - here's his bottom line: he is recommending my gyn prescribe me progesterone lozenges (some people call them by their french name of "troche") which I should take half of on day 14 and the other half on day 15 of my cycle. Or maybe he said to do that twice a day, I forget.

No mention of strength.
He says if a woman has ever been on synthetic progresterone like with the pill or depo-provera, he goes straight to lozenge because cream isn't appropriate/effective for those people. (My skepticism and pessimism tells me that this is untrue and that he simply knows that he won't make any money off people who buy creams online, and he will make money off people who have him compound lozenges. Is this unfair?)

I was wondering what people here think of this suggestion. My only other recourse is to order something online and self-medicate. I'd really appreciate some advice.. I've been trying for two years now to get a doctor to help me out with this and it's become apparent that if I want something done right I have to do it myself. But I can't without some help and advice... so, thanks for any input on this subject that anyone can offer!

# Thread: thyrotoxicosis, selenium analogues lithium, candida peliliculosa menengitus
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx

### chocolate — 2012-05-25T04:10:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-27236>

Selenium analogues of antithyroid drugs--recent developments.

Roy G, Mugesh G.

Source

Department of Inorganic & Physical Chemistry, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India.

Abstract

> Thyroxine (T4), the main secretory hormone of the thyroid gland, is produced on thyroglobulin by thyroid peroxidase (TPO)/H(2)O(2)/iodide system and deiodinated to its active form (T3) by a selenocysteine-containing enzyme, iodothyronine deiodinase (ID). The activation of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) receptor by auto-antibodies leads to 'hyperthyroidism', a life-threatening disease which is treated by antithyroid drugs such as 6-propyl-2-thiouracil (PTU) and methimazole (MMI). The present review describes the biological activities of a number of S/Se derivatives bearing the methimazole pharmacophore. **It is shown that the isosteric substitutions in the existing drugs lead to compounds that can effectively and reversibly inhibit the heme-containing lactoperoxidase (LPO). In contrast to methimazole, the selenium analogue, MSeI, does not interfere with the enzyme directly, but it inhibits LPO by reducing the H(2)O(2) that is required **for the oxidation of the Fe-center in LPO. These studies reveal that the degradation of the intracellular H(2)O(2) by the Se analogues of antithyroid drugs may be beneficial to the thyroid gland, as these compounds may act as antioxidants and protect thyroid cells from oxidative damage. Because the drugs with an action essentially on H(2)O(2) can reversibly inhibit the thyroid peroxidase, such drugs could be of great importance in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21364070](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21364070)
>
>
> > Int J STD AIDS. 2011 Jan;22(1):54-6.
> >
> > Candida pelliculosa meningitis as an opportunistic infection in HIV: the first reported case.
> >
> > Ratcliffe L, Davies J, Anson J, Hales S, Beeching NJ, Beadsworth MB.
> >
> > Source
> >
> >
> >
> > Tropical and Infectious Disease Unit, Royal Liverpool University Hospital, Liverpool, UK.
> >
> > Abstract
> >
> >
> >
> > We describe the first reported case of Candida pelliculosa meningitis in HIV infection. This case highlights the risk of uncommon opportunistic infections in association with the immunocompromised and the importance of autopsy as a diagnostic tool in HIV infection. We discuss the epidemiology, diagnosis and management of C. pelliculosa, a non-albicans species which is rarely associated with disease, and review current literature.
>
>
>
> [http://repository.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/2433/77068/1/chd062\_2\_050.pdf](http://http://repository.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/2433/77068/1/chd062_2_050.pdf)
>
> Selenium is a homologue of sulfur and tellurium, and has both metallic and nonmetallic
>
> properties. It is recognized as an essential micronutrient for mammals and
>
> some other organisms, though it is toxic at high concentrations.' The selenium amino
>
> acids occur in nature, and most are physiologically active. Several microbial and
>
> mammalian enzymes contain a selenocysteine (2-amino-3-hydroselenopropionic acid)
>
> residue as a catalytically essential moiety.2)
>
> Some of amino acid analogues have been reported to act as a false corepressor or
>
> a false feedback inhibitor of a key enzyme participating in biosynthesis of the
>
> corresponding amino acids, and also inhibit incorporation of the amino acids
>
> into protein.3) Se-(R-Aminoethyl)-L-selenocysteine (L-selenalysine), in which the r—
>
> methylene group of lysine is replaced by a selenium atom, functions as a competitive
>
> inhibitor of L-lysine in protein synthesizing systems from Escherichia coli, rat liver and**
>
> rabbit reticulocyte". Recently, we found that this selenium analogue inhibits growth
>
> of Candida pelliculosa5' and acts as a false feedback inhibitor of homocitrate synthase**
>
> (EC 4. 1. 3. 21) of the yeast5). This paper describes effect of L-selenalysine on
>
> L-lysine transport in C. pelliculosa compared with L-thialysine (5-(13-aminoethyl)—
>
> L-cysteine), in which a sulfur atom was substituted for the selenium atom.

### chocolate — 2012-05-25T04:30:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-27242>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21586647](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21586647)

The fungal pathogen Candida albicans autoinduces hyphal morphogenesis by raising extracellular pH.

Vylkova S, Carman AJ, Danhof HA, Collette JR, Zhou H, Lorenz MC.

Source

Department of Microbiology and Molecular Genetics, The University of Texas Health Science Center, Houston, Texas, USA.

Abstract

pH homeostasis is critical for all organisms; in the fungal pathogen Candida albicans, pH adaptation is critical for virulence in distinct host niches. **We demonstrate that beyond adaptation, C. albicans actively **neutralizes the environment from either acidic or alkaline pHs. Under acidic conditions, this species can raise the pH from 4 to >7 in less than 12 h, resulting in autoinduction of the yeast-hyphal transition, a critical virulence trait. Extracellular alkalinization has been reported to occur in several fungal species, but under the specific conditions that we describe, the phenomenon is more rapid than previously observed. Alkalinization is linked to carbon deprivation, as it occurs in glucose-poor media and requires exogenous amino acids. These conditions are similar to those predicted to exist inside phagocytic cells, and we find a strong correlation between the use of amino acids as a cellular carbon source and the degree of alkalinization. Genetic and genomic approaches indicate an emphasis on amino acid uptake and catabolism in alkalinizing cells. Mutations in four genes, STP2, a transcription factor regulating amino acid permeases, ACH1 (acetyl-coenzyme A \[acetyl-CoA\] hydrolase), DUR1,2 (urea amidolyase), and ATO5, a putative ammonia transporter, abolish or delay neutralization.** The pH changes are the result of the extrusion of ammonia, as observed in other fungi. We propose that nutrient-deprived C. albicans cells catabolize amino acids as a carbon source, excreting the amino nitrogen as ammonia to raise environmental pH and stimulate morphogenesis, thus directly contributing to pathogenesis.

IMPORTANCE: Candida albicans is the most important fungal pathogen of humans, causing disease at multiple body sites. The ability to switch between multiple morphologies, including a rounded yeast cell and an elongated hyphal cell, is a key virulence trait in this species, as this reversible switch is thought to promote dissemination and tissue invasion in the host. We report here that C. albicans can actively alter the pH of its environment and induce its switch to the hyphal form. The change in pH is caused by the release of ammonia from the cells produced during the breakdown of amino acids. This phenomenon is unprecedented in a human pathogen and may substantially impact host physiology by linking morphogenesis, pH adaptation, carbon metabolism, and interactions with host cells, all of which are critical for the ability of C. albicans to cause disease.**

If lithium is a yeast infection treatment and a thyroid quieter, what would bind to the ammonia when you feel a headache coming on to stop the ph change? I see the selenium analogue inhibits the growth of another yeast and quiets thyroid... but no mention of this yeast. Selenium stops topical yeast. Why would it need to be an analogue?

The pH changes are the result of the extrusion of ammonia, as observed in other fungi. ### chocolate — 2012-05-27T03:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-27785>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19120662](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19120662)

Inhibition of hyphae formation and SIR2 expression in Candida albicans treated with fresh Allium sativum (garlic) extract.

Low CF, Chong PP, Yong PV, Lim CS, Ahmad Z, Othman F.

Source

Department of Biomedical Science, Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, University Putra Malaysia, Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

Abstract

AIMS:

The aims of the present study were to determine whether Allium sativum (garlic) extract has any effect on the morphology transformation of Candida albicans, and to investigate whether it could alter the gene expression level of SIR2, a morphogenetic control gene and SAP4, a gene encoding secreted aspartyl proteinase.

METHODS AND RESULTS:

Candida albicans cells were incubated with a range of concentrations of fresh garlic extract, and the morphology was monitored via light microscopy. Garlic extract treatment caused the transition of yeast form to hyphal form to be obviated. The expression of SIR2 was down-regulated from 1.2- to 2.5-fold with increasing concentration of the garlic extract, as determined from relative quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction. There was no difference in the SAP4 expression in control vs treated cultures.

CONCLUSIONS:

Garlic and its bioactive components have the ability to suppress hyphae production and to affect the expression level of SIR2 gene.

SIGNIFICANCE AND IMPACT OF THE STUDY:

**Hyphal production is an essential virulence determinant of C. albicans for invasive infections, therefore garlic and its constituents can be effective not only against colonizing C. albicans strains present in mucosal infections, but also virulent strains causing systemic or invasive candidiasis.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T00:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-28084>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6151602](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6151602)

L-Leucine prevents ammonia-induced changes in glutamate receptors in the brain and in visual evoked potentials in the rabbit.

Ferenci P, Pappas CS, Jones EA.

Abstract

The effect of L-leucine on glutamate receptors in the brain and on visual evoked potentials was studied in hyperammonemic rabbits. Hyperammonemia was induced by the iv infusion of 2.1 mmol NH4Cl/h over 3 hr. Hyperammonemia was followed by a 116% increase in the specific binding of 3H-glutamate to synaptic membranes prepared from the hippocampus. This increase was due to both an increase in the affinity and in the density of the glutamate receptor. T**he simultaneous infusion of L-leucine (6.7 mmol/hr) completely prevented the ammonia-induced increase in the specific glutamate binding, whereas L-valine and D-leucine had no effect. Hyperammonemia was also associated with typical, reproducible, and reversible changes in visual evoked potentials. The amplitudes of the first negative and the second positive peak decreased, whereas the latencies of these peaks remained unchanged. The simultaneous infusion of L-leucine completely prevented these changes. These findings indicate (1) that L-leucine prevents ammonia-induced changes in the glutamatergic excitatory neurotransmitter system and (2) that pharmacologic doses of L-leucine modulate the effects of hyperammonemia on central neurotransmission as assessed by visual evoked potentials. _A causal relationship between the effects of L-leucine on ammonia-induced changes in glutamate receptors and in visual evoked potentials cannot be inferred with confidence._** These findings provide a potential alternative explanation for the apparent beneficial effects of infusions of branched-chain amino acids on hepatic encephalography in patients with chronic liver disease.

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T00:38:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-28085>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1686641](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1686641)

Ammonia-induced alterations in glutamate and muscimol binding to cerebellar synaptic membranes.

Rao VL, Agrawal AK, Murthy CR.

Source

School of Life Sciences, University of Hyderabad, India.

Abstract

Binding of glutamate and muscimol (an agonist for GABAA receptors) to their respective receptors has been studied in the cerebellum of normal and hyperammonemic rats. **There was a decrease in both high- and low-affinity binding of glutamate in the cerebellum during hyperammonemia. **Kinetic studies revealed that the decrease is due to a reduction in the number of binding sites, but not due to changes in the binding affinities**. Further studies also revealed that the decrease was only in the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA)-specific binding sites without any alterations in the binding to non-NMDA sites represented by kianic acid (KA)- and quisqualic acid (QQ)-sensitive receptor sites. These effects were also mimicked when the membrane preparations from the cerebellum of normal animals were incubated with ammonium acetate. Enhancement of muscimol binding was observed in animals injected with ammonium acetate. _It is concluded that hyperammonemic states, even in the presence of a functional liver, are capable of altering amino acid neurotransmission and this might play an important role in cerebral dysfunction under these conditions._**

### chocolate — 2012-06-03T08:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29187>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/4629910](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/4629910)

Accelerated cellular uptake and metabolism of L-thyroxine during acute Salmonella typhimurium sepsis.

DeRubertis FR, Woeber KA.

Abstract

The effects of acute Salmonella typhimurium sepsis on the kinetics of peripheral L-thyroxine (T(4)) distribution and metabolism and on serum total and free T(4) concentrations were studied in rhesus monkeys inoculated i.v. with either heat-killed or viable organisms. The rate of disappearance of labeled T(4) from serum was increased within 8 h after inoculation of monkeys with either heat-killed or viable Salmonella. The effects of the heat-killed organisms were transient and no longer evident by 16 h postinoculation. The monkeys inoculated with the viable Salmonella experienced a 2-3 day febrile, septic illness that was accompanied by an increase in the absolute rate of T(4) disposal. **In the infected monkeys, serum total T(4) and endogenously labeled protein-bound iodine concentrations fell significantly during the period of acute sepsis and then rose during convalescence to values that exceeded the preinoculation values, suggesting that thyroidal secretion of hormone had increased in response to a primary depletion of the peripheral hormonal pool. Total cellular and hepatic uptakes of T(4) were enhanced by 4 h after inoculation of monkeys with either heat-killed or viable Salmonella, but the increase in total cellular uptake persisted for 24 h only in the monkeys inoculated with the viable organisms. These alterations in T(4) kinetics could neither be correlated with changes in the binding of T(4) in plasma nor attributed to an increase in vascular permeability. Moreover, they could not be ascribed to an in vitro product of bacterial growth, suggesting that the presence of the organisms themselves was required. An acceleration of T(4) disappearance was also observed during Escherichia coli and Diplococcus pucumoniae bacteremias. Our findings are consistent with a primary increase in the cellular uptake and metabolism of T(4) during bacterial sepsis, possibly related to phagocytic cell function in the host.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-03T18:36:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29227>

### chocolate — 2012-06-06T22:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29767>

[http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm](http://http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm)

Inhibitory effect of beta-thujaplicin on ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in mouse keratinocytes.

Baba T, Nakano H, Tamai K, Sawamura D, Hanada K, Hashimoto I, Arima Y

Department of Dermatology, Hirosaki University School of Medicine, Japan.

**Sunburn cells are thought to represent ultraviolet B-induced apoptotic keratinocytes. It has been demonstrated that enzymatic and nonenzymatic antioxidants effectively suppress sunburn cell formation, indicating that reactive oxygen species may play a role in the progression of ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis. Metallothionein, a cytosol protein, has antioxidant activity, and overexpression of metallothionein has been reported to reduce the number of sunburn cells in mouse skin. We have also demonstrated that overexpression of metallothionein inhibits ultraviolet B-induced DNA ladder formation in mouse keratinocytes.** These findings support the hypothesis that cellular metallothionein may play an important role in the inhibition of ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes through its antioxidant activity. In the present study, we investigated the effects of beta-thujaplicin, an extract from the woods of Thuja plicata D. Don. and Chamaecyparis obtuse, Sieb. et Zucc., on ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes and on metallothionein induction. Topical application of beta-thujaplicin decreased the number of ultraviolet B-mediated sunburn cells and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT)-mediated dUTP-biotin nick end labeling-positive cells in mouse ear skin. Incubation with beta-thujaplicin suppressed ultraviolet B-induced DNA ladder formation in cultured mouse keratinocytes. Histochemical analysis showed that topical application of beta-thujaplicin induced metallothionein protein in mouse skin. Northern analysis and western blotting revealed significant induction of metallothionein mRNA and metallothionein protein, respectively, in beta-thujaplicin-treated cultured mouse keratinocytes. These findings indicate that beta-thujaplicin inhibits ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes and strongly suggest that the inhibitory mechanism is due to the antioxidant activity of metallothionein induced by the agent.

### chocolate — 2012-06-06T22:08:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29770>

[http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm](http://http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm)

Cell Mol Biol (Noisy-le-grand) 2000 Mar;46(2):465-88

Metallothionein in human disease.

Simpkins CO

Hamot Research Center and Tri-State Emergency System, Erie, PA, USA. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

\[Medline record in process\]

Evidence concerning a role for metallothionein (MT) in human disease is reviewed. Current knowledge of MT is juxtaposed with our understanding of the pathogenesis of disease. MT is known to modulate three fundamental processes: 1) the release of gaseous mediators such as hydroxyl radical or nitric oxide; 2) apoptosis, and 3) the binding and exchange of heavy metals such as zinc, cadmium or copper. The capability to specifically manipulate MT levels in cells and in mice is beginning to provide answers regarding how MT could impact complex disease scenarios. **Associations among MT and several diseases, including cancer, circulatory and septic shock, coronary artery disease, and Alzheimer's disease have been made. Strong evidence exists that MT modulates the immune system. The primary function of MT remains unknown.**

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T00:29:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29793>

[http://www.pnas.org/content/81/17/5494.full.pdf](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/81/17/5494.full.pdf)

> The metallothioneins (MTs) are a group of low molecular
>
> weight proteins that bind heavy metal ions belonging to
>
> groups Ib and IIbj such as Cd2+, Zn2+, and Hg2' and Cu',
>
> Ag+, and Au+, respectively. They are characterized by their
>
> high cysteine content and capacity for metal binding; they
>
> exist in several molecular forms and are classified by their
>
> electrophoretic behavior as either MT-I or MT-Il (1). Yet,
>
> each class might represent more than a single protein species.
>
> Their major role is in the regulation of trace metal
>
> (Zn2+, Cu+) metabolism, acting in the storage of these ions
>
> in the liver. However, since the transcription of MT genes
>
> increases dramatically after exposure to heavy metals (2),
>
> they also, can serve as a protective system against heavy
>
> metal toxicity. The control ofMTgene expression by glucocorticoid
>
> hormones (3, 4) is probably involved in the regulation
>
> of plasma Zn2+ concentration (5). Defects in MT gene
>
> expression have been suggested to be the cause for several
>
> disorders of trace metal metabolism in man-i.e., Menkes
>
> kinky hair syndrome (6, 7), Wilson disease (8), and acrodermatitis
>
> enteropathica (9). Yet, no direct involvement of the
>
> MTgenes in any one of these diseases has been demonstrated.
>
> MTgenes also have been suggested to be involved in the
>
> genetic predisposition of mice to the toxic effects of Cd,
>
> such as testicular necrosis (10), and Cd-induced hypertension
>
> (11) in rats.

# Thread: How Long on LR?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NickieG — 2012-05-26T21:39:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-27692>

How long have you folks stayed on the LR diet? How do you know when it is time to do something different?

I have been on the LRx for 3 months now. While I have not lost weight in terms of numbers on the scale, my weight is stable. My clothes fit differently i.e. looser and I need to buy new jeans as all the ones I own are now big on me.

I have been doing CT for about a month. I started with face dunks which I love. Moved to cold showers and have recently begun to fill the tub with cold water and sit then lay in it and read a book. I am not loving this yet and am doing it.

I do love the results. My skin is softer. The skin on my feet has changed tremendously as evidenced by going to get a pedicure. Little dead skin to be cut off. I am sleeping well. No more waking up at 3 or 4 am. However, up evening I got extremely angry at myself about something and didn't sleep well at all. I was able to see a clear correlation between my perceived stress and poor sleep. CTed the next night and slept well.

Not sure how long to keep doing the LRx and would love to hear how others knew it was time to do something different. Thanks you guys!

Nickie

### David — 2012-06-02T14:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-29027>

Nickie,

I have continued the Leptin Rx for about 16 weeks and have been progressing with CT for about half that time. I have lost weight, and I have also experienced many of the changes you have noted. Several times I have experienced a net weight gain for a week or so, always followed by a loss to a lower net weight, so the program has not been a smooth transition to a lower weight for me by any means.

During his first webinar, Dr Kruse noted in passing that one cannot really be Leptin sensitive if still obese, which I interpret as having a BMI over 30, so I will ignore the 6-8 week Rx estimate and stay on LR reset for now. By the way, I changed my entire lifestyle to conform to the Paleo Diet (ala Robb Wolf) last November and have lost about 50 pounds.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-02T15:36:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-29031>

I have been doing the LRx since I started last Nov. except for the supplements. This is how I live now and it's great. BAB totally paleo. I haven't lost weight but have lost body fat and gained muscle. Stick with it it gets better everyday.

i did 6 months on the LRx without a missed day. during that time i tried an hcg round and gave it up at day 11 because i was craving more protein/fat then i could get in on the Simeons protoocl. very poor weight loss but very easy to do. then i over ate like crazy for a week and picked up 7 lbs. about 3000 cals protein/fat instead of the 1650 i usually ate. did JUDDD for 6 weeks and dropped 10 lbs. then a 5 week break still doing the LRx. then ended the LRx at 6 months and 1 week. because i had all the signs that were spelled out in the FAQ. i am doing JUDDD again down 2.5 in my first week. still paleo and my first meal is very high protein but not always within 30 mins or even 60 mins of awakening.
