### janagram — 2012-06-13T18:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30912>

I need to tighten up the protocols! I am known to eat rice crackers at a party, drink too much wine....but that's about the extent of my evildoing....

My bp was high at the doc yesterday....140/90, its usually great, so....? Could it be the coffee?

My sleep has slipped a bit and I end up dreaming too much and not getting into deep sleep...also waking repeatedly thru the night...I call it surf-sleeping.

### ealachan — 2012-06-13T18:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30917>

> **janagram;31016 wrote:** I need to tighten up the protocols! I am known to eat rice crackers at a party, drink too much wine....but that's about the extent of my evildoing....
>
> My bp was high at the doc yesterday....140/90, its usually great, so....? Could it be the coffee?
>
> My sleep has slipped a bit and I end up dreaming too much and not getting into deep sleep...also waking repeatedly thru the night...I call it surf-sleeping.
>
> Folks, this site keeps me keeping on, so, thank you all, I really want to perfect my lifestyle to the point where I am healthy and the right weight.

re: blood pressure - mine always reads about 20 points higher in the doctor's office than it does at home, sometimes even higher if they use the wrong size cuff for my arm. Plus, one blood pressure reading is just a snapshot of that particular second. It doesn't mean your BP is running high all the time. If you're concerned about it and have a cuff at home (or have the means to buy one...they're not that expensive), you can try taking it every day at varying times for a few weeks to get an idea of what it's doing on the longer term. If you DO buy a cuff to monitor your BP at home, make sure you measure your arm and get the correct size of cuff, otherwise your readings will be inaccurate.

### janagram — 2012-06-13T19:34:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30932>

yeah, good point. gotta keep it in perspective. thanks.

David....thanks for this...I have noticed over the past week that my weight has gone up three to four pounds.

I have also noticed headaches every day. I rarely have headaches so I am assuming I am detoxing something. I have continued to spend some of my shower time in cold water and also spot CTing. I have also been experiencing stress at work with another individual. My strategy has been to say what is on my mind, then be polite when I see her and otherwise steer clear of her. Luckily she is temporary at work.

I will continue to stay with the Leptin RX way of eating. I find it very easy overall. I did go to a wedding last week and the meal was not food I would eat and the timing was off. I chose not to have anything. The most uncomfortable thing was the multiple times I no thank you to someone offering me food, then offering me something else, then something else. I have finally learned to joke about my "weird" way of eating. Seems to work better than simply saying "no thank you".

I appreciate everyones responses....I am learning so much.

Nickie

# Thread: The embarrassment of cold adapting!

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 55

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T20:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27888>

I live in a beach community, really small lots of landâ€¦ for 20somehting years we had essentially no neighbor behind usâ€¦there was an old woman but she never showed herself, she had lots of family in the area but she liked to stay inside, so we had a lot of privacyâ€¦weâ€™re hardly in our yard, just always at soccer games etc., last year we decide to get a pool, letâ€™s be home more with the kids etc., enjoy our privacy â€“ _the old woman dies! One of her grandkids takes the house, cuts down a bunch of the trees, big holesâ€¦their house now has clear views because of their deck that you could never even see before, who knew? And to boot, second floor windowsâ€¦so Iâ€™m letting my dog out, I open the door like I always do, half paying attentionâ€¦still thinking Iâ€™m in privacy landâ€¦forgetting that I have a pair of undies on over my yoga pants to hold in my ice packs so they stay in place, bright white ice packs hanging out of my underwear that is now overwear!!! I look up and lock eyes with the new young couple as they sip their coffee on their deck facing my back door, I have yet to meet them holy \*\*\*\*_â€¦I die because I canâ€™t just take the ice packs out of my underwear in front of themâ€¦I slink down, as if Iâ€™m picking something up and crawl awayâ€¦

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-28T02:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27943>

You pick your house. God picks your neighbors!

Don't you just wish people would just mind their own business?

MJ, instead of fight or flight, you could have asked them to join the ice party.

On nudity. Isn't clothing a neolithic thought?

Happy Memorial Day Weekend to you all. Yesterday I did my first three-bagger (48 lbs of ice) in the bathtub. Used my IR gun to see how cold. 30.2degrees F. Got in, total shocker. Went numb within a minute. Stayed in three minutes and had totally bright red skin. Quite a burning experience. Got out, sunbathed for 30 minutes per side to warm up. Got back into the tub again, lasted 5 minutes--water temp was still brutal. Waited about two more hours, got in again for 40 min this time with water tmp about 43. Best CT day ever. Until today. Only a two-bagger (32lbs of ice) and was quite relaxed for 30 minutes, then started mild shivering tremors. Got out at 40 min. Water temp was 40 F. Sunbathed immediately for 25 min each side. Never felt better!

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-28T05:42:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27975>

> **Jack Kruse;28047 wrote:** someday soon I am going to talk about nudity because it plays into this because we are the only great ape without any substantial fur and that fits into the story of Optimal ironically.......we just are not at the point yet.

Ohh, now I want to know! I hatehatehate most clothing  I must have missed out somehow on the epigenetic change that makes you think restrictive rubber bands, padding/wires (!!), and belts around your torso must be a good thing. They drive me insane.

Lots of cultures have some sort of tradition around nudity, some separate between genders, some do not. It's often part of grooming/wellness behaviors. My family did when I was little - it included sweating in saunas, ice-water plunges  and some swimming, performed at least once a week for increased wellness and health.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-28T05:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27976>

Looks like the NY times has a suggestion on what's going on with the missing fur...

[http://www.nytimes.com/2003/08/19/science/why-humans-and-their-fur-parted-ways.html?pagewanted=all&src=pm](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2003/08/19/science/why-humans-and-their-fur-parted-ways.html?pagewanted=all&src=pm)

> It implies we were naked for more than a million years before we started wearing clothes....
>
>
>
> One theory holds that the hominid line went through a semi-aquatic phase -- witness the slight webbing on our hands....
>
>
>
> Humans lost their body hair, they say, to free themselves of external parasites that infest fur -- blood-sucking lice, fleas and ticks and the diseases they spread....
>
>
>
> Among the newly furless humans, bare skin would have served, like the peacock's tail, as a signal of fitness.

I'm sure Dr JK has a different explanation entirely  It certainly would explain why some of the young girls these days appear to think the more skin exposed the better...

### Inger — 2012-05-28T11:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-28002>

hahaha.. this is a fun thread..

I have to tell what happened to me the other day...

I do my CT in the river behind my parents house where I live these days cause me and hubby are separated.

I always do it naked cause there are no one (usually... I always check out before if anyone is close).

So, this morning I sit there under the small bridge, singing looooud.. I was so happy and all was so beautiful and the river ends right there in a small waterfall that makes noice so I thought no one will hear me sing that loud anyway. Great.. all of a sudden there comes a man (a worker with his working-clothes on) to check out something on the bridge / waterfall (it is a factory far away that takes their water from it).. and he saw me sitting there naked in the river singing loud.. Oh my.. I stopped singing for sure.. I was feeling so ashamed I do not knew where to look honestly. Thanks god he did not stay for long...Things happens in CT life.. haha..

Inger

LoL oh my gawd Inger thanks for sharing...first I want to say, I am really happy for you that you could find such a great sense of joy at as I like to say, "being one with nature". The fact that you want to be singing out loud like that and enjoying nature so much that you get lost in it, that is so cool. I think our little Long Island swim group felt some of that joy yesterday! You probably made his day LoL LoL LoL (I can only imagine the story he had to tell from his view point), and at least it's unlikely you'll ever see him again to find out - that or he'll be fixing that bridge a whole lot more now.

Maybe one day everyone will learn to be ok with all of it...for now, that was so funny, I just loved your story!

Lengthening your drawers might be faster ...... then, they could assume you were really fat ..... LOL, funny thread.

Reminds me of a TV documentary about some group in a very tropical climate. They only wore a little belt. Their word for "naked" was "beltless".

Dr. Kruse, you appear to have reached the enviable position in your neighborhood of owning what Bertrand Russell called a "lunatic's license," where people expect you to do unusual things --- and they understand and don't mind. About as close to true social freedom as one might get.

# Thread: short chain fatty acids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-28T03:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/post-27952>

[http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/61/2/364](http://http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/61/2/364)

Short-Chain Fatty Acids Stimulate Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Secretion via the G-Proteinâ€“Coupled Receptor FFAR2

Abstract

Interest in how the gut microbiome can influence the metabolic state of the host has recently heightened. One postulated link is bacterial fermentation of â€œindigestibleâ€￾ prebiotics to short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), which in turn modulate the release of gut hormones controlling insulin release and appetite. We show here that SCFAs trigger secretion of the incretin hormone glucagon-like peptide (GLP)-1 from mixed colonic cultures in vitro. Quantitative PCR revealed enriched expression of the SCFA receptors ffar2 (grp43) and ffar3 (gpr41) in GLP-1â€“secreting L cells, and consistent with the reported coupling of GPR43 to Gq signaling pathways, SCFAs raised cytosolic Ca2+ in L cells in primary culture. Mice lacking ffar2 or ffar3 exhibited reduced SCFA-triggered GLP-1 secretion in vitro and in vivo and a parallel impairment of glucose tolerance. These results highlight SCFAs and their receptors as potential targets for the treatment of diabetes.

### chocolate — 2012-05-28T03:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/post-27954>

[http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Short\_chain\_fatty\_acids](http://http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Short_chain_fatty_acids)

DESCRIPTION

Short chain fatty acids are produced in the large bowel as a result of bacterial fermentation of soluble fibre. They can provide over 500 kcals a day of energy which is especially useful when blood sugar levels fall low because mitochondria like to use SCFAs as an energy source. At night we all switch to SCFAs as blood sugar levels fall and this prevents hypoglycaemia at night. Night-time hypoglycaemia is a common cause of disturbed sleep.

This test looks for a hypoglycaemic tendency by measuring levels of short chain fatty acids first thing in the morning before breakfast has been taken. The logic is that when there is hypoglycaemia, the body switches early to using short chain fatty acids for energy supply and these will then deplete. Therefore, low levels of short chain fatty acids indicate a hypoglycaemic tendency. Hypoglycaemia is a common problem for people with a fermenting gut, so the test may indirectly be pointing to a problem with yeast or bacterial overgrowth in the bowel.

Low levels of SCFAs may also be indicative of a low fibre diet. This can be helped by eating foods rich in fibre such as nuts, seeds and vegetables.

# Thread: what is the medical definition of fat adapted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-medical-definition-of-fat-adapted.1699/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-05-29T22:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-medical-definition-of-fat-adapted.1699/post-28228>

I am a bit miffed that I can't find this by googling. The term fat adapted is used a lot... even in a lot of studies.. but each study doesn't contain a definition. Seems like a gray area that I would like cleared up, similar to cold adapted.

Here are some things I know about fat adaptation:

\- more tissues up-regulate fat burning

\- FFA concentration in blood goes up

\- blood glucose consumption goes down

\- respiratory exchange rate goes down

I'd like to know the medical definition of fat adapted as well as any other things associated with it.

thanks,

jesse

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-29T22:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-medical-definition-of-fat-adapted.1699/post-28236>

There is none.......you wont find it. It basically is a function of how fast you use fats in muscles, mitochondria, and the brain for energy instead of glucose. Those enzymes in humans do not ramp up until glycogen stores are depleted.

### hellojtm — 2012-05-29T23:16:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-medical-definition-of-fat-adapted.1699/post-28240>

Thanks Jack. Saved me some time!

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-30T02:21:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28261>

I'm buying another freezer!

It has to be small due to space limitations, so thinking around 5 cubic feet, 7 if I put it in my garage. I'd prefer stainless but will do with any color if necessary. How much am I going to be able to fit in there?

I absolutely cannot decide between upright or chest style. What do you own and why do you or don't you like what you have?

I imagine things might tumble out of the upright kind - have given myself several black'n blue toes with falling ice packs ... but then the chest kind might be hard to get to stuff on the bottom.

 Opinions, please

### angieonthegogo — 2012-05-30T02:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28265>

> **Souldanzer;28360 wrote:** I'm buying another freezer!
>
>
>
> It has to be small due to space limitations, so thinking around 5 cubic feet, 7 if I put it in my garage. I'd prefer stainless but will do with any color if necessary. How much am I going to be able to fit in there?
>
>
>
> I absolutely cannot decide between upright or chest style. What do you own and why do you or don't you like what you have?
>
>
>
> I imagine things might tumble out of the upright kind - have given myself several black'n blue toes with falling ice packs ... but then the chest kind might be hard to get to stuff on the bottom.
>
>
>
>  Opinions, please

[http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/GE/h\_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40Zlo/R-100598963/h\_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/GE/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40Zlo/R-100598963/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051)

I bought this one a little over a year ago. I live in a small apartment (this actually sits in my dinning room - eek!) but I like it since I have no garage or anything. I can fit a quarter of beef in there and have some room to spare. When I get my meat deliveries I portion the different meat cuts into leftover paper WF or TJ paper bags so that I can easily life them out of the freezer if I'm looking for something. That way I'm not hanging over the edge into the abyss of the freezer searching for something. If I had the space I would have bought a bigger one (economies of scale, yo!) so I could get more meat at a time but it has been a great little freezer. I just scraped it really for the first time a week ago and it wasn't that much ice buildup, only around the edges of the lid opening. I'm constantly in and out of it and from what I can tell it keeps the temp at a good even and cold temp. I'd buy it again in a heartbeat. I think when I got it, it was also free shipping. I researched for months and months for a small one and went to several stores to compare them in person. In the end, this is what I chose for maximum room/quality for minimum $$. I would have preferred a S/S one since I knew this would be sitting in full view all of the time but they are hard to find in the small freezer sizes and the quality I found just wasn't there in addition to the noticable price difference. Happy hunting!

### JC2K — 2012-05-30T06:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28324>

I believe chest styles get colder faster. But I don't think I've ever seen chest styles with ice makers. So you'd have to put it in containers and hope they don't burst in freezing. Place the sheets in first, ice on top of it, and use the ends of the sheets to pull out the ice from the depths.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-30T20:12:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28384>

Great ideas. I wanted a freezer even before needing to have ice on hand for CT. I like the form factor of uprights, seems like they make better use of floor space. Plus, I know given the temptation of all that flat surface with a chest style, I would pile stuff on top and then have to move it all to get to the goods.

Is there any problem keeping one in an unheated garage? Our garage generally stays 10-15 degrees warmer than outside in winter but it does get and stay below freezing for days at a time (assuming we ever get winter again).

### JC2K — 2012-05-30T20:17:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28386>

Not that I noticed. My mom has a stand up one in the garage and it gets 100+ in there during the summer. Food stays frozen ok. Not sure on the power useage though (it might suck up more power).

### Lyndra — 2012-05-30T20:17:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28387>

> **Souldanzer;28455 wrote:** tried that... fail. The cover works most of the time. Sometimes it comes off and I don't notice and that's not good.

When I first started, I used blue painters tape to seal off the overflow holes. I have a reconditioned tub and didn't want to hurt the paint and that worked pretty well. There was some leakage during the bath but not a lot.

### JC2K — 2012-05-30T20:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28393>

That sounds like a cheap way to do it. In reality, you don't need it to be perfect, just not drain as fast.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-30T21:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28397>

naaa, I need it to be perfect. It bugs me when it drains slowly!

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-30T21:13:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28399>

> **Lyndra;28483 wrote:** Great ideas. I wanted a freezer even before needing to have ice on hand for CT. I like the form factor of uprights, seems like they make better use of floor space. Plus, I know given the temptation of all that flat surface with a chest style, I would pile stuff on top and then have to move it all to get to the goods.

a bit of LHT isn't so bad.... I know I will end up having to sit on the lid to get it to shut  I've been contemplating getting a chain and lock for the other freezer so the door will stay shut!

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-30T22:47:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28412>

> **Souldanzer;28449 wrote:** Do you know if I can plug my bath tub overflow that way? I'm having trouble with the overflow cover. But I do have some silicon stuff laying around... Can I get it back off if I needed to?

I did the blue painters tape after trying that silly plug thing that didn't work at all. I have two layers of painters tape - and it works great. No leakage.

Also, Our freezer stays in the garage - no issues and our electric bills are in line.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-31T23:47:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28744>

Geez, was thinking I am the only one who's too incapable to get it to work.... just won't stick. I have two if anybody needs one

### angieonthegogo — 2012-06-01T01:15:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezers-whats-your-favorite.1702/post-28765>

> **angieonthegogo;28364 wrote:** When I get my meat deliveries I portion the different meat cuts into leftover paper WF or TJ paper bags so that I can easily life them out of the freezer if I'm looking for something. That way I'm not hanging over the edge into the abyss of the freezer searching for something.

Could work for ice too if you want to partition it out. The reusable grocery bags would probably work best in that case though over the paper.

# Thread: Telomeres on Mark's Daily Apple
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-05-30T21:53:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28406>

[Article](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/the-tall-tail-of-telomeres/) from today's MDA discussing telomeres. Interesting stuff.

### Jim — 2012-05-30T22:28:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28410>

Yes I wondered about that. I guess that when you are pushing the envelope, relying on the latest research of others, you are bound to travel down a few dead ends. Not to say that telomeres are a dead end for me just yet although Rosedale surely believes it.

### ealachan — 2012-05-30T22:35:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28411>

> **Jim;28509 wrote:** Yes I wondered about that. I guess that when you are pushing the envelope, relying on the latest research of others, you are bound to travel down a few dead ends. Not to say that telomeres are a dead end for me just yet although Rosedale surely believes it.

I didn't read it so much as him saying it was a "dead end"...just more that it's still very much a new science and as such they're not entirely sure WHAT it all means. I think I'd much rather have a doctor or researcher say "Look, here's a whole new thing to look at, even though we don't quite know what it all means yet", rather than someone saying "Yes, this is clearly the answer to life, the universe and everything", or even worse, someone saying "Meh...so what? Not worth studying."

### JC2K — 2012-05-30T23:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28416>

I always saw telomere length as more a portion of than the sole indicator of overall longevity. Even if your cells have the ability to reproduce without error indefinitely (without causing cancer, etc), that doesn't mean you won't get a heart attack or aneurysm or drive your car off a cliff after drinking too much... Long telomere's also don't mean you'll have a **healthy** long life...

### ealachan — 2012-05-30T23:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28418>

> **JC2K;28515 wrote: I always saw telomere length as more a portion of than the sole indicator of overall longevity. Even if your cells have the ability to reproduce without error indefinitely (without causing cancer, etc), that doesn't mean you won't get a heart attack or aneurysm or drive your car off a cliff after drinking too much... Long telomere's also don't mean you'll have a healthy** long life...

Exactly what I was thinking.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-30T23:56:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28426>

I dont think you can listen to anyone right now but Elizabeth Blackburn.......and her opinion is a lot different.....and very much in line with my thoughts on telomerase. What happens to our telomeres due to lifestyle is very important. I am sure the science will evolve as time does but if you read what he is saying and implying it does not jive one bit with what many of the leaders in the anti aging field are doing and reporting. Someone mentioned TA-65 in the thread comments. TA 65 was old news 4 yrs ago. There are now newer treatments. I have several patients on TA 65 now and I am just following them.

Nobel Prize winning Dr. Blackburn is involved with the telomere testing company in Barcelona who is doing the most advanced testing on humans today. Right now telomere testing is used primarily by anti aging docs and by patients who are interesting in the cutting edge to see what if what they believe and use in treatments is really working or not.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-31T00:45:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28440>

> **Jack Kruse;28525 wrote:** I dont think you can listen to anyone right now but Elizabeth Blackburn.......and her opinion is a lot different.....and very much in line with my thoughts on telomerase. What happens to our telomeres due to lifestyle is very important. I am sure the science will evolve as time does but if you read what he is saying and implying it does not jive one bit with what many of the leaders in the anti aging field are doing and reporting. Someone mentioned TA-65 in the thread comments. TA 65 was old news 4 yrs ago. There are now newer treatments. I have several patients on TA 65 now and I am just following them.
>
>
>
> Nobel Prize winning Dr. Blackburn is involved with the telomere testing company in Barcelona who is doing the most advanced testing on humans today. Right now telomere testing is used primarily by anti aging docs and by patients who are interesting in the cutting edge to see what if what they believe and use in treatments is really working or not.

So what's your explanation for why so many of us are testing way above average in telomere lengths while we don't feel healthy at all and our hormone panels are in the toilet?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-31T01:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28455>

It says that most of your problems are likely epigenetic and not genomic.....that is good because it means they are fixable. Good news......not bad news.

### Jim — 2012-05-31T06:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28518>

> **JC2K;28515 wrote: I always saw telomere length as more a portion of than the sole indicator of overall longevity. Even if your cells have the ability to reproduce without error indefinitely (without causing cancer, etc), that doesn't mean you won't get a heart attack or aneurysm or drive your car off a cliff after drinking too much... Long telomere's also don't mean you'll have a healthy** long life...

Dang... I thought if my teleomeres were long enough I would be able to walk in front of a hail of bullets.

Seriously though, this is still bleeding edge stuff and it is possible (likely?) that we'll be making a course correction or two. Still longer is better I suppose. At any rate I am trusting in Jack's interpretation of the studies since I haven't read them nor do I have the capability to assess them.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-31T15:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28545>

People with diet books to sell want you to think that is all you need to do.......its not. Look at internet forums. People fail on diet alone. When I talk telomeres I am telling people who are struggling with the diet and exercise those 'experts' say is best to test that on themselves. It shows you if what you currently believe is hurting you or not......I do not use it for anything more than that. It is a dogma check. If you are not getting results but your telomere length is OK......then you know you are fixable because you are not actively depleting your stem cells........This is a big issue for endurance athletes of the crazy cross fitter who go pedal to the metal with WOD. This is a test that should have a purpose.........its not a panacea.

### ealachan — 2012-05-31T17:20:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28592>

> **Jack Kruse;28644 wrote:** People with diet books to sell want you to think that is all you need to do.......its not. Look at internet forums. People fail on diet alone. When I talk telomeres I am telling people who are struggling with the diet and exercise those 'experts' say is best to test that on themselves. It shows you if what you currently believe is hurting you or not......I do not use it for anything more than that. It is a dogma check. If you are not getting results but your telomere length is OK......then you know you are fixable because you are not actively depleting your stem cells........This is a big issue for endurance athletes of the crazy cross fitter who go pedal to the metal with WOD. This is a test that should have a purpose.........its not a panacea.

I think this is a great explanation.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-31T18:01:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-28611>

> **Jack Kruse;28554 wrote:** It says that most of your problems are likely epigenetic and not genomic.....that is good because it means they are fixable. Good news......not bad news.

Like it!! B/c I'm with the "above average" TL folks when I expected to turn out like... 90 years old, lol.

So could you have the same disease in two people but in one case it is entirely genomic (not fixable) and in another case it is epigenetic and fixable given the right lifestyle changes? Like one's got the off-switch but the other one is missing it?

I was part of a huge genetic AN study years ago, they never cared to tell me any results, though. I think it did find that AN has a strong genetic component, though. They might not have been clued in about epigenetics and thrown it all into one. My sis is now showing initial symptoms of the AN monster  Her kids don't eat normally, either. My niece eats just like I did at 4 years old... not good. My nephew (2) will keep eating and is gaining too much weight. They both were born like this. It is very sad to witness what is happening in my family with food, ever since my mother's AN it all went downhill from there on.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T03:48:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-29306>

> **Anna K.;29399 wrote:** that's exactly what made me doubt the validity of telomeres test as a unifying theory of longevity.
>
> something just didn't add up
>
> and this article just clarified some things.

Well, according to Dr K's answer it just means that my problems are caused by epigenetics rather than genetics. Which actually thrills me b/c that means I can feel better given the correct lifestyle changes. It means I can still have a healthy life b/c I wasn't depleting my stem cells. That would have been much harder to fix if at all.

Personally, I'm glad I did this test. It's a huge weight of my shoulders to be pretty sure I can fix stuff b/c everybody has been telling me I can't. Unless you need a situation assessment of your health overall, it's probably not necessary. Or if someone is in need of checking dogma... but then, if their problems turn out to be of epigenetic nature they might just get their dogma reinforced, lol.

### JC2K — 2012-06-04T03:54:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-29307>

> **Anna K.;29403 wrote: If your results are already as 7 years old while you are NOT healthy?
>
> what does it prove?**

**

My take on it is you can't really take those numbers, you have to compare to yourself (so multiple tests). The 7 year old type thing is in comparison to the general population (much like blood lab test ranges). My problem with that is that it's measuring you in comparison to the general (unhealthy) population on a SAD. So great, I'm a 7 year old in comparison to** the general population, but I'm still a mess. You really need to test against yourself and ignore the years pretty much. So first test, you're at X, you make dietary changes, etc. and second test you're at X-10 or so, so you can see an improvement. Or X+10, so you can see a deterioration, and know you need to make changes.

### Birdy — 2012-06-05T02:05:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomeres-on-marks-daily-apple.1711/post-29455>

> **Souldanzer;28539 wrote:** So what's your explanation for why so many of us are testing way above average in telomere lengths while we don't feel healthy at all and our hormone panels are in the toilet?

well, at least if your hormones are screwed up, you still aren't crossfitting yourself to death.

# Thread: Eating Disorder Tendency...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-05-31T03:36:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-28471>

I am finding myself becoming very hardwired into the "rules" of this WOE. I have to eat as soon as I get up. I canNOT eat in between meals. I found I have made a rule for myself that if my breakfast is so big, I should NOT be eating lunch. There's a rule about eating early enough before bed. I can't allow myself to splurge, because I am afraid of what might happen if I do.

I have been following the Leptin Rx for about six weeks now, and have, for the most part, been very happily eating two meals a day. Some days I make it easily until 5:30 or so, others I am hungry by 3. I am eating a very big breakfast, so full I can't eat anymore. I am not craving sweets or carbs. But I have a hard time stopping eating when it is meal time. And then because I am so full, I feel like I must immediately exercise to undo the damage I have just done.

But if I am being completely honest with myself, I feel guilty for eating. I felt guilty eating lunch today (small lunch at that), and I felt guilty eating dinner (it was late today, because of above mentioned lunch). I find myself walking alot these days, because I am not supposed to work out. So I walk every morning for about 45 minutes. Then I find additional reasons to walk throughout the day (take the kids to the park, walk to the library, post office... whatever.) And today after I ate dinner, I felt like I should walk on the treadmill (I didn't).

I did the Reset last summer, lost weight, was feeling great... no problems. But then I jumped the gun on the post Leptin RX, and my hormones went crazy, I have gained weight... now problems. So I am attempting Leptin Rx for the second time but find I am struggling with these eating disorder behaviors.

How do I get past these feelings? How have some of you recovered and moved on from eating disorders? I've never been diagnosed anorexic or bulemic, but I have a large tendency to create rules with food. An obsessive relationship with food, too. I want very much to succeed here, I've seen others overcome it. Just not sure what to make of this.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-31T03:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-28474>

Tweety...hugs...look at MamaGrok's journal...I still struggle with emotional overeating.

What do you mean you jumped the gun on the post LRx and your hormones went crazy?

### tweety — 2012-05-31T03:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-28480>

thank you for the support. I have read Mamagroks posts... I have been around since the beginning on MDA- I am just slow to realize I was evenhaving this problem of disordered eating!

To answer your question, I switched to post leptin WOE because I wanted to, not because I saw enough signs that I should. At the same time, I started on BHRT which was essentially only progesterone. It has helped in some ways, like mood stabilization, but it has been a nightmare for weight.

I just realized today though that the rules of leptin Rx are feeding directly in to my need to create rules around food. I have always done this. It was just that when the leptin Rx was working well for me last summer, I didn't notice. Now that its not helping me get the weight off, I find it is increasing my need to follow the rules. Its feeding my need to control food, no pun intended.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-31T05:17:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-28502>

I wish I knew what to tell you. Disordered eating is so complicated.

Is progesterone known to put on weight? If so I didn't know this.

Why no estrogen too?

### tweety — 2012-05-31T15:28:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-28548>

I think improper use/dose of progesterone can lead to weight gain. No estrogen bcs the dr said I was estrogen dominant, but looking at my numbers I am low in that too. I have an appt with a new person today for BHRT.

Thanks again for your support. I just didn't realize I was still dealing with problems because, until now, I wasn't unhappy with my weight.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-31T16:36:57+0300

may I take exception from that.....  You get what you believe...

I'm not entirely sure Dr K believes I can get well from my AN considering my trashed hypothalamus and that after 20+ years my brain is plain wired differently... I don't care  I observe the litte changes every day and I know I am getting well.

### tweety — 2012-06-04T16:45:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29354>

Thats a good point Souldanzer. And Mamagrok does this too- you both are just not letting things stop you, and you are celebrating all the big and little progress along the way. I don't know what my journey will look like up ahead, but I have come a long way and I will keep working on it. Little by little.

I want to believe its physiological too, because that helps me feel like it will heal as my body does. But, I also know emotional baggage can't be ignored. I have to deal with it. I just wanted to be one of the ones who made a few changes and voila! All is good!  Oh well, working hard for something makes it feel that much better when its achieved. Positive thinking!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T18:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29375>

> **tweety;29454 wrote: **I don't know what my journey will look like up ahead**, but I have come a long way and I will keep working on it. Little by little.
>
>
> I recommend you do. In as much detail as possible. Spend time thinking about this. What DO you want your life to look like?
>
> I love collages for this....
>
>
>
> I want to believe its physiological too, because that helps me feel like it will heal as my body does. But, I also know emotional baggage can't be ignored. I have to deal with it.** I just wanted to be one of the ones who made a few changes and voila! All is good!  Oh well, working hard for something makes it feel that much better when its achieved. Positive thinking!

I've even changed my mind about this part. A lot of the "emotional baggage" I thought I had was just stuff therapists told me HAD to have an impact on my life. BS. Everybody has baggage from childhood but not everybody gets entirely screwed up by it. Conclusion: Another factor has to be involved and baggage might not matter at all.

I don't give \*\*\*\* about my baggage any longer. The less time I spend thinking about my baggage the better. I don't want to reinforce those connections in my brain... the less I use them the less often those pathways will be activated. Use it or lose it, I'm choosing lose it for this one.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T18:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29379>

I actually recently had a funny experience with this. I sought help from a therapist (what was I thinking ) b/c I was dealing with significant grief and loss all of the sudden. I came in, told my story and said I wanted to talk about how to make things better. This woman wasn't able to follow me. She kept bringing up all the emotional stuff (to make me cry and "process"), even said we \*really\* had to talk about the things that happened in my childhood (though I kept saying I was done with all of that and wanted to focus on the future). She was entirely unable to simply assist me with problem solving the \*now\* as I needed help \*creating\* my future in my mind.

Needless to say, I was glad when it was time to write my check and get the heck outta there.

The whole idea of "processing" your emotions is a bit suspect to me these days. I know sometimes we need to get some feelings out.... but so many people (and particularly the psychotherapeutic profession) are getting stuck in this. They keep reinforcing the upsetting things, clients keep raising their stress hormones, leading to more illness/problems.

I do not understand any longer why people like me go to therapy to rehash what the hell happened 20/30 years ago. I understand it was all very upsetting - SO WHY DO I NEED TO KEEP TALKING AND THINKING ABOUT IT??? It's not like it's any less upsetting now?

Sometimes all this makes me want to go back to my "real" job - and show them how to do it differently

Jill Bolte Taylor is a neuroscientist who had the opportunity to lose all her baggage... you might want to read her story if "baggage" is what is holding you back.

### tweety — 2012-06-04T19:24:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29383>

Interesting. I can see your point, and agree with you too. Some things I think do hold us back, and I feel personally I would benefit from learning to let go. I am not there to dwell on what happened, but I need to learn that what happened happened, and its ok for me to forgive and move on. I want to be different from my past, and right now its harder than I thought!

I will check out Jill Bolte Taylor though. I am always up for reading other people's successes.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T19:43:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29385>

Your brain works with patterns. It wires the way things used to be. You can counter this. Think, dream, imagine, create, build, choose, craft, collage, write, talk, play the future you want. You can create the patterns you prefer. The more you do it, the stronger the new choices will be.

Think about what happens if you move your fridge from one side of the kitchen to the other. How often will you still reach for the old location habitually while cooking? The reason your brain learns quickly is b/c the old way doesn't work any longer (there is no fridge and the automatic reach doesn't get reinforced), so you have to stop, think, and then consciously reach for the correct location... success! Reinforcement! The old pattern dies off, the new one gets used more and more... but you have to consciously think about it for a while.

### tweety — 2012-06-04T19:56:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29391>

that I understand and can agree with for sure. I am realizing that I don't do alot of envisioning what I want my future to look like. So I am adding that to my "to do" list right now. I have to be the change that I want to be for myself- its not just going to happen for me on its own.

it sounds like you have probably already processed alot of your own baggage at this point. I think those of us who tend to think about emotions and acknowledge they are weighing us down can move on. I know people in my life (as we all do) who tend to just suppress and repress, and for them I imagine they need to do a little processing and exploring of the emotions. I am thinking of someone in my life in particular. I feel like it would be hard to "lose" the baggage that you haven't allowed yourself to acknowledge you are carrying.

so what you are saying makes absolute sense, and I think thats a good direction for me to point myself in. it's time for me to move on, thats for sure. thanks for the nudge!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T20:13:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29393>

> **Jack Kruse;29462 wrote:** Im with you on this.....you are what you believe and think. Thinking is an endocrine secretion and is physiologic.....your just conditioned to believe it is not and is some how different and it is not.

uh, wow, okay, that means it's not restricted to my brain/nervous system... it's not just hardwired patterns in the brain that can be changed using imagination. Does this mean a thought might affect might little toe , as the endocrine system really communicates with the entire body - as in every cell that might be sensitive to that particular chemical released?

I guess the answer is yes, i.e. if we think about stressful stuff, stress hormones are released, which then affect overall inflammation in the body, so if the little toe happens to be injured - a stressful thought could make the inflammation worse... I bet cortisol isn't the only thing at work this way.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T20:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29402>

> **tweety;29491 wrote: that I understand and can agree with for sure. I am realizing that I don't do alot of envisioning what I want my future to look like. So I am adding that to my "to do" list right now. I have to be the change that I want to be for myself- its not just going to happen for me on its own.
>
>
>
> it sounds like you have probably already processed alot of your own baggage at this point.** I think those of us who tend to think about emotions and acknowledge they are weighing us down can move on. I know people in my life (as we all do) who tend to just suppress and repress, and for them I imagine they need to do a little processing and exploring of the emotions. I am thinking of someone in my life in particular. I feel like it would be hard to "lose" the baggage that you haven't allowed yourself to acknowledge you are carrying.
>
>
>
> so what you are saying makes absolute sense, and I think thats a good direction for me to point myself in. it's time for me to move on, thats for sure. thanks for the nudge!

No. It has nothing to do with being done with processing. If you buy into the processing theory you will be processing for the rest of your life. I've spent the last 25 years processing \*\*\*\* and it didn't get me anywhere than deeper into the black hole. My entire being became what I was constantly processing  My life evolved around what happened to me and I see this in many people who are in long-term psychotherapy.

Psychotherapy is no different than conventional medicine. They have you stuck in the revolving door. They have no interest in getting you well in a short period of time. They want to see you coming back for sessions year after year....

The moment things changed was when I decided to identify with something different.... a part of myself that I liked. This included letting go of my profession (psychotherapy!!) When I decided time was running out and the past wasn't going to change, I could as well make the best out of the Now. I went a bit to an extreme with this.... I got rid of everything I owned pretty much... I kept my car and some clothes, my laptop. That was pretty much it! I even got rid of most people in my life... b/c they weren't serving my new purpose. I let my life go blank for a couple of years, spent most of my time in nature until I felt I was free of everything and could start over... and then I went for it

I still own pretty much nothing  I like it this way. Life's lighter if you can fit your belongings into your car and take off whenever. No baggage is weighing me down any longer, not emotional, not physical.

IIRC, they have found that people who are best at "suppressing/repressing" their emotions are the happiest....the one's who spend the least amount of time on dwelling on negative things.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-05T00:02:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29427>

Souldanzer....I completely agree with your thoughts about baggage and old stuff...what once served us as survival mechanisms can keep us from thriving in the present...emotionally, in our own lives and evolutionarily, in our species.

I work on the fringe of psychotherapy and while articulating the past can work for someone who has never been "heard," it is also a slippery slope for many people who simply buy into the idea that telling their story over and over will change it. It won't. As you said, it actually just re-traumatizes the mind and body again and again.

The work I do is with horses...the horses keep everybody in the present tense-- literally. They have no use for the past or the future and they can sense if someone is not fully present, or doesn't really know what they are feeling, or is incongruent. The horses are absolute masters of responding to inner clarity...they don't even care what the emotion is...it's just information, but they will only feel safe with somebody who is fully aware of their feelings and fully engaged in the present moment. That is their survival mechanism as prey animals. They are hard wired to notice their environment, adjust to any changes, find their balance/safety and then go back to grazing.

Change is available at any moment. Once that is experienced, the world becomes a different place and we truly step into ourselves and the lives we image we can have. The false self beliefs, the "buts" the "I have to's" ....all that stuff can just be treated for what it is...old noise that gets in the way. I think of my false self as a fly that sometimes makes a lot of annoying noises and needs to be swatted....nothing more than that.

All the best on your journey tweety....

### indigogirl — 2012-06-05T00:11:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29432>

Hi Souldanzer,

I don't believe that people who suppress/repress their emotions are the happiest.

When you suppress or repress something you don't acknowledge it. I think we need to acknowledge our feelings and then move on...not to attach such importance to something that may have happened that it becomes our story of who we are, but to say yes this happened and now I can choose how I want to react to it, or what I will create in my life.

I like the way you think!

And I do think our cells have memory and everything is connected.

Do I always live consciously believing in what I just wrote? No! But I would like to...

I like what you do with your false self... I had to do that with AN. I split off that part of myself b/c it wasn't myself. It had a name (Ed) and I'd treat it like an entirely different person. I learned to be assertive with that false self and eventually it lost its power.

One of my friends from grad school went on to work with Medicine Horse locally. I think this stuff is great. I'm technically a dance/movement therapist or body-centered psychotherapist. It forces you to be in the present, to enact stuff, not to go down the path we already know goes nowhere. I'm sure CBT can be used in positive ways... it's just that most therapists don't.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-05T00:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29443>

> **indigogirl;29532 wrote:** Hi Souldanzer,
>
> I don't believe that people who suppress/repress their emotions are the happiest.
>
> When you suppress or repress something you don't acknowledge it. I think we need to acknowledge our feelings and then move on...not to attach such importance to something that may have happened that it becomes our story of who we are, but to say yes this happened and now I can choose how I want to react to it, or what I will create in my life.
>
>
>
> I like the way you think!
>
>
>
> And I do think our cells have memory and everything is connected.
>
>
>
> Do I always live consciously believing in what I just wrote? No! But I would like to...

There is two distinct ways people deal with things gone bad.... one person will ascribe the reason to something on the outside, something they didn't have control over, something environmental or another person. These tend to be the happy people. Something bad happens, they look for the reason outside of themselves and they move on. The other person is the kind that looks for the reason within. Those are the depressed people. They keep searching and searching and are getting nowhere, therefore, it must be all their fault.

There is a distinct gender difference present as well. Men think everybody else is responsible, women think it must be themselves.

I \*very\* consciously choose to spend as little time of my life with the negative emotions.... the more time I have for happiness. Yes, I do cut off my anger, sadness, all of that... I choose to. I give myself a time limit, and when that is up, I pick myself up, make a plan, and go do it.

People who suppress their negative emotions are the happiest b/c they don't find it rewarding to spend time in that place. They've learned means to get themselves out of that sad spot. It takes about 2 seconds to acknowledge you're angry/sad, whatever. After that, you're choosing to spend time unhappy.

Stuff flows over me; I focus on the positive; I don't dwell on the past; I try to forgive; I try to assume the best of people. I'm very realistic, too (not a blind optimist).
>
>
>
> \*except for one 24hr period of depression after a birth, a two-hour period after giving up dairy & potatoes the first time, and one other time not coming to mind right now. This proves even more to me that our outlook is affected by our chemistry SIGNIFICANTLY. Temperament, yes, but I think temperament is largely chemistry (directed by genetics & epigenetics), too. That's why it's so easy for me to believe it when I see folks here have all kinds of mental illness / neurological / emotional challenges clear up for them -I think it's ALL chemical at its root. I've seen these things turn on and off in my own brain like a switch, unmistakably clearly. I think it's a switch for everyone; just some have it stuck on "DEPRESS" or "BAGGAGE" or whatever, and need some help switching it off.
>
>
>
> Souldanzer;29485 wrote:**
>
> Think about what happens if you move your fridge from one side of the kitchen to the other. How often will you still reach for the old location habitually while cooking? The reason your brain learns quickly is b/c the old way doesn't work any longer (there is no fridge and the automatic reach doesn't get reinforced), so you have to stop, think, and then consciously reach for the correct location... success! Reinforcement! The old pattern dies off, the new one gets used more and more... but you have to consciously think about it for a while.

NICE.

### tweety — 2012-06-05T14:56:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29517>

> **MamaGrok;29575 wrote:** -I think it's ALL chemical at its root. I've seen these things turn on and off in my own brain like a switch, unmistakably clearly. I think it's a switch for everyone; just some have it stuck on "DEPRESS" or "BAGGAGE" or whatever, and need some help switching it off.

This makes alot of sense. I see what Souldanzer is saying, and it makes sense to me too. Flipping that switch is what I aim to do, what I WILL do. Just like anything else, finding what flips that switch is so individual.

I like that you cleared your life and started fresh! I can see what that must have taken to do such a thing. I think about that alot, but I have a few people in my life I would pack up and take with me.  Instead, I am starting fresh with what I have. I tell myself that all the time. I don't want to dwell on the negativity from the past, but I definitely want to tell it goodbye.

I appreciate everyone's perspective by the way. Pyscotherapy can be CW too, thats true. No different than this Leptin Rx, sort of. Some of us can flip our switch just with the changes we make at home, and small guidance from a dr. But some of us take alot more time and tinkering and need more help from a dr.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-05T18:27:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-29561>

Such great points in this thread.

I guess I fall in the middle. I don't think it is good to dwell on past and constantly rehash and "process" it - it can get like picking a scab off just before the wound is about to heal. It will bleed again and eventually it will scar. But, if you are unconscious of your patterns, you can ignore them but continue to play them out. Sometimes, without knowing. I think you gotta get conscious and present. There is a difference between stuffing something down/repressing it and letting something go because it's not helpful. One is an unconscious process and the other is a conscious choice. For me, I think the key is stopping my analysis of what it means (as in, it’s all my fault and means I am a terrible person, etc). In the past, I have had the tendency to make a lot of assumptions about events that just are not true. Just this weekend I was telling myself that I was acting like something that happened was "life and death" and it really wasn't. My spiritual teacher once told me that everything has a beginning, a middle and an end. Sometimes I have to tell myself this because I have had a tendency to think "things will never change." I am a TRUE believer that being happy and feeling good is priority number one. Feeling bad has never brought about a positive outcome. Of course, if your body chemistry is all out of whack, good luck finding the happy feelings. It can very much be a switch. I know in the past that i have just changed my mind about something and it is as simple as that. For example, I used to keep myself up with worrying about things and had terrible insomnia. One day, I decided that it sucked and I wasn't gonna do it any more and bam, gone. I love it when that happens. I am super focused on making internal shifts in thinking and watching the outer world change. That’s why it is best not to “know” anything – because then everything is possible.

Thanks for sharing everyone!

XOXO

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T07:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-30079>

This is a great thread. I totally relate to Tweety's first post and my awareness that following rules starts to become essential to me being 'good'. The tension between finding good information and guidance and then merging that with my own experience and needs is an important one.

I've 'confessed' on another thread that I am a psychotherapist by training and profession (though with long periods of time not working in the field when my children were younger). I agree with what you've noticed, Souldanzer. In fact, I do a lot of training of therapists and I have said almost the same words. Far too many therapists think that activating emotion, especially emotion tied to the past, equates with healing. While I do not believe it is always re-traumatising (sometimes it's just downright boring for the client!), there isn't a shred of evidence that it's helpful. A healing process must flow from the person who is trying to heal. Whether you are the therapist, the listening friend, the hairdresser, it's not your journey, it's theirs. "Processing" or being able to talk about something painful,whether past or present, can be a critically important stage for many in that process. But how long that needs to carry on OR if it needs to happen at all is a decision that only the person going through it can make.

When I work with clients (and myself, though I'm a real pain in the ass when trying to deal with myself), I take the stance that the task at hand is to become familiar with ourselves and to understand who we are, what we want, what creates a life-spark in us, what gives us meaning. Becoming familiar with ourselves - or befriending ourselves as Pema Chodron and others call it - is about not pushing any of us away. But it is also not about identifying with any part of ourselves over any other. Someone once asked Thich Nhat Hahn to help him heal his wife because she was suffering from anxiety and depression. His answer? I can't help her until I know her favourite colour and what she loves. I think the point is that we tend to divide against ourselves (or maybe that's just me), labeling parts of ourselves good and part bad.

For many many people, I think feelings of depression are completely healthy. There are plenty of events in life that warrant sadness or upset or anxiety, etc. To feel otherwise is a form of craziness. (And bad therapists, in my experience, are ones who want you to be different than who you are). But at the same time, feelings of happiness are also completely healthy. And, because life is how it is, it is quite common to experience them both at the same time. But we tend to pathologize one as bad and one as good.

Now, I'm not saying that feeling depressed all the time is acceptable nor am I saying that physiological things aren't part of the underlying picture for everyone. Because I believe it is. I also think we are extremely individual and complex and our relationships and external circumstances also can create/eliminate inflammation, upset, stress etc. So you can have a perfect diet, be cold adapted and tied to light cycles and if you're married to a total jerk, you're still not going to feel good. (Note, that's about what's happening NOW... as Thich Nhat Hahn also likes to say, you can heal the past in the present moment.)

One last point, in attachment theory, there are the two positions you named, Souldanzer - the one who distances/represses and the one who internalises/becomes overwhelmed - but there is also a third position and it is the one associated with security. It is the one that does not react against the circumstance but is able to be in it - to 'be with' it - without losing the core sense of self. If you can do this (which may be akin to what you were describing with being able to 'let go'), you don't have to repress or over-focus... things can be and you can be OK in the midst of them. (And 60 years of research show that this position of security - flexible ability to both have needs (and ask for them to be met) and feel confident to explore the world and your place in it - is most highly correlated to both mental and physical health).

But as I said from the start, I totally relate to this journey. It requires a lot of my energy to learn new ways and new options and to take new information on board and not turn it into something I use against myself when I 'mess up.' I have learned that there are so many things out of my control (which is where my spiritual life comes in and rescues me), but that which is in my control I need to use both conscientiously AND kindly toward myself. Baby steps...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:17:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-30086>

The key is knowing there is not a blueprint for the population for you to follow and wind up optimal.......each one of us has the same DNA plan but how that plan is executed and expressed determines our blueprint and how to tweek it........that is the key understanding point. Until you get that point you will never feel like you have control over things even when you do. When you realize how much you really do and can control.......you have found your inner guru. The more you teach and feed that inner guru the better your DNA expression becomes and the more you change without thinking about it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-09T00:04:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-30202>

I think I've said this somewhere else but whenever you have an experience that activates your fight/flight/freeze system, your body does all sorts of other things that prepare you for fight and flight. It doesn't matter if the experience is physical or emotional. Your body thinks you just got attacked by a tiger and it will get you ready to save your life by activating energy stores etc.

If you don't go and run or fight this energy off, nobody really knows what happens with it. Animals tremble/shiver to use it up, after running or fighting. Animals don't get PTSD.

Somatic Experiencing is one way to deal with this but there's other body-centered therapies out there. The worst thing in my experience is to sit glued to your chair/couch in CBT while reactivating the memory. Find a therapist who will allow you to get this energy out physically, may it be dance, running, hitting pillows, it really doesn't matter. Somatic Experiencing will encourage the natural trembling response.

You can do this on your own... you have an intrusive memory - run for your life! As hard as you can for as long as you can. It helped me. And is totally evolutionary, lol.

Long ago, Art Therapy was my focus in college. After thinking about what you read, it was encouraging to look back to see that I do this naturally. When I am creating (art, gardening, etc) I am always thinking. Even walking has been helpful for thinking through feelings. What you said just reinforces this.

In fact, after my consult with Dr. Kruse, and even other professionals, I realized that without being aware of this I am actually moving pretty instinctively through life. I find myself pursuing certain things (drs, activities, etc) without knowing why, and then later come to realize that is exactly what I should have been doing. Eating habits too. I crave certain things at certain times of the year, and when I think about it they have been seasonal choices. I love seafood, can't get enough, and Dr. Kruse told me that should be my staple over red meat. (whats nice is now I am comfortable giving in to my chocolate craving- 100% cocoa is the best!)

So thanks Dr. Kruse and everyone because I realize now I just need to let my body lead me. I can trust it, because its telling me what to do to heal. I didn't think it was trustworthy, but apparently it is!

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-06-09T17:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-disorder-tendency.1718/post-30283>

> **Souldanzer;29502 wrote: No. It has nothing to do with being done with processing. If you buy into the processing theory you will be processing for the rest of your life. I've spent the last 25 years processing \*\*\*\* and it didn't get me anywhere than deeper into the black hole. My entire being became what I was constantly processing  My life evolved around what happened to me and I see this in many people who are in long-term psychotherapy.
>
>
>
> Psychotherapy is no different than conventional medicine. They have you stuck in the revolving door. They have no interest in getting you well in a short period of time. They want to see you coming back for sessions year after year....
>
>
>
> The moment things changed was when I decided to identify with something different.... a part of myself that I liked. This included letting go of my profession (psychotherapy!!) When I decided time was running out and the past wasn't going to change, I could as well make the best out of the Now. I went a bit to an extreme with this.... I got rid of everything I owned pretty much... I kept my car and some clothes, my laptop. That was pretty much it! I even got rid of most people in my life... b/c they weren't serving my new purpose. I let my life go blank for a couple of years, spent most of my time in nature until I felt I was free of everything and could start over... and then I went for it
>
>
>
> I still own pretty much nothing  I like it this way. Life's lighter if you can fit your belongings into your car and take off whenever. No baggage is weighing me down any longer, not emotional, not physical.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> IIRC, they have found that people who are best at "suppressing/repressing" their emotions are the happiest....the one's who spend the least amount of time on dwelling on negative things.
>
>
> Souldanzer;29538 wrote:** I like what you do with your false self... I had to do that with AN. I split off that part of myself b/c it wasn't myself. It had a name (Ed) and I'd treat it like an entirely different person. I learned to be assertive with that false self and eventually it lost its power.
>
>
>
> One of my friends from grad school went on to work with Medicine Horse locally. I think this stuff is great. I'm technically a dance/movement therapist or body-centered psychotherapist. It forces you to be in the present, to enact stuff, not to go down the path we already know goes nowhere. I'm sure CBT can be used in positive ways... it's just that most therapists don't.

what a great thread!

I agree - once you have the "aha" moment about what might have driven you to a particular outcome, you should let it go and move on...rehashing and dredging up the past doesn't help. it keeps you stuck and unable to live in the present. i could spend all day ticking off things that I did wrong or how I was wronged, but it would require a very negative state of mind...no thanks.

I worked with horses in high school to overcome on ED and like Souldancer named it "ed" and was able to finally seperate myself from it. Therapy was helpful then bc at the time, all i knew was that i was depressed, did not want to live anymore, and unable to make myself eat (event hough I wanted to terribly). Therapy was helpful in allowing me to connect the dots (realized, that my soccer coach was in fact molesting me and it was not ok! that i was not eating bc i was depressed and also prob to get rid of my boobs so he'd have nothing to grab...) say "aha!" and get me angry enough to want my normal life again. i recovered in about 6 months after that! got back to my old foodie self. however, do i want to sit around with someone all day talking about how f-ed up that time was and how the school did nothing to stop the abuse? nah. its over. it cant be changed...I learned a ton and am stronger for it, so moving on.

Equine therapy as well as art therapy was used and they were amazingly helpful...as was naming the disorder and treating it like a seperate entity. it allowed me to disengage from disordered thinking and move on. the interesting thing is, to this day, there's only a handful of people that know i ever had an ED. not bc i'm embarrassed by it, but bc i dont want it to define me. i'll talk about therapy or how its helped me to my closest friends, but all i do is refer to the molestation part - not the ED.

# Thread: What is the antidote to the mercury in fish?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vwaggs — 2012-05-31T15:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28541>

I remember reading somewhere that Dr. K said not to worry about the mercury levels in fish because they contain the natural antidote to this issue. I can't find it again. Is this correct and can anyone explain? Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-31T15:04:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28542>

Selenoproteins.......

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-05-31T17:04:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28584>

Very readable article on mercury, selenoproteins, and omega 3's.

[http://www.undeerc.org/fish/pdfs/Selenium-Mercury.pdf](http://http://www.undeerc.org/fish/pdfs/Selenium-Mercury.pdf)

### vwaggs — 2012-06-01T06:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28819>

That was so helpful! Thanks!

### MamaGrok — 2012-06-01T16:15:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28846>

That was very interesting, but what's with their assertion that most Americans' selenium status is good? Nonsense. Our soils are totally deprived, thanks to monocropping, so their idea that foods made from grains grown in good soil are Se-rich is off, too. Makes it hard to trust.

I just have a sense that yes, Se binds with Hg, but what happens as that Se:Hg ratio drops? It can't be good, even if the Se:Hg molar ratio is still "high enough."

Interesting take-home is the caution that freshwater fish will depend on local levels of Se in the soil.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-01T21:20:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-28912>

> **MamaGrok;28945 wrote:** That was very interesting, but what's with their assertion that most Americans' selenium status is good? Nonsense. Our soils are totally deprived, thanks to monocropping, so their idea that foods made from grains grown in good soil are Se-rich is off, too. Makes it hard to trust.
>
>
>
> I just have a sense that yes, Se binds with Hg, but what happens as that Se:Hg ratio drops? It can't be good, even if the Se:Hg molar ratio is still "high enough."
>
>
>
> Interesting take-home is the caution that freshwater fish will depend on local levels of Se in the soil.

The Willamette Valley, while fertile and beautiful, is notoriously deficient in selenium. Hence, lifelong supplementation is in order for anyone who lives here and wants to "eat local." Ditto D3 -- far too dark all winter, and often lightly clouded in the summer.

Cilantro is supposed to be able to chelate mercury and take it out of the body.

### hazyjane — 2012-06-03T00:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-antidote-to-the-mercury-in-fish.1719/post-29131>

I take selenium for my autoimmune thyroid disease, but when I eat high mercury fish, I just take some HMD heavy metal formula after eating it. It has cilantro but is more effective than just cilantro or chlorella (good studies behind it). I've done courses of HMD throughout the years to make sure my levels stayed low.
