### Birdy — 2012-06-01T19:53:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28900>

I have noticed that throughout the past year, Sisson tries to keep right alongside Kruse without endorsing or trashing him. A bit behind the curve, I think, but still always good summaries. He gave a nice explanation on why now that I do CT, I heat up after a meal, heat up after a dab of coconut oil, heat up after taking my evening magnesium supplement? I used to have to eat quite a bit of coconut oil or carbs in order to get the same response.

### AKMan — 2012-06-01T21:26:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28913>

> **Birdy;28999 wrote:** I have noticed that throughout the past year, Sisson tries to keep right alongside Kruse without endorsing or trashing him. A bit behind the curve, I think, but still always good summaries. He gave a nice explanation on why now that I do CT, I heat up after a meal, heat up after a dab of coconut oil, heat up after taking my evening magnesium supplement? I used to have to eat quite a bit of coconut oil or carbs in order to get the same response.

I think what may have gotten Dr. Kruse into trouble is the word 'thermogenesis'. People took that to mean "magical transformation". Thermogenesis is defined as the body's way of creating heat. Thermogenesis is regulated by the sympathetic nervous system and hypothalamus gland. The word 'Genesis' conjures up images in the mind related to religion, magic, and miracles.

There are many types of thermogenesis: Non-shivering; exercise induced; overeating; cold; dietary induced; and probably more.

Another thing that has bugged me about all this is that Jack mentioned the cold-induced apoptosis of fat cells early on in his CT series, but nobody ever talks about that. Zeltiq is doing it for huge profits and even patented a device for home use. Jack showed us how to do it with ice packs, but not many are talking about it.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-01T21:34:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28917>

I used cold myself first to get rid of fat before Zeltiq was ever invented. It worked even using my rudimentary ice water techniques because the biology is built into to us.......The Mass General Plastic surgeons now use 45 minute treatments using cold metal plates to do the same thing for 700-1500 dollars a treatment.

It is thermogenesis because the entire purpose of it is to turn fat into free heat using the cold.......once the cold is used chronically it will also induce fat cell apoptosis......take a while but it works. The Zeltiq measure induces apoptosis quicker because it is using metal which is 75 times more conductive of cold to fat cells to kill the fat cells quickly.......my cheap technique takes longer. That is the only difference.

### AKMan — 2012-06-01T23:36:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28937>

> **Jack Kruse;29016 wrote:** I used cold myself first to get rid of fat before Zeltiq was ever invented. It worked even using my rudimentary ice water techniques because the biology is built into to us.......The Mass General Plastic surgeons now use 45 minute treatments using cold metal plates to do the same thing for 700-1500 dollars a treatment.
>
>
>
> It is thermogenesis because the entire purpose of it is to turn fat into free heat using the cold.......once the cold is used chronically it will also induce fat cell apoptosis......take a while but it works. The Zeltiq measure induces apoptosis quicker because it is using metal which is 75 times more conductive of cold to fat cells to kill the fat cells quickly.......my cheap technique takes longer. That is the only difference.

I can definitely attest that ice on the love handles reduces the fat contained in them. I religiously iced my belly for about 3 weeks. Starting out my waist (at the belly button) measured 35". After 3 weeks, it was still 35". 10 weeks later it is 32".

Here is something else I have noticed. Last year, if I would over eat, I would gain 5-10 lbs in just a few days and my waist measurement would increase several inches. I attribute this to 'water weight' where the fat cells in my belly area become super hydrated. Now that these cells are gone, not just shrunk, this doesn't happen. If I over eat, I may fluctuate 1-2lbs, but I don't see an increase in waist measurement.

It seems normal dieting just shrinks the fat cells, and they will haunt you forever. Cold apoptosis kills them.

### youthfuliz — 2012-06-01T23:42:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28940>

> **AKMan;29036 wrote:** I can definitely attest that ice on the love handles reduces the fat contained in them. I religiously iced my belly for about 3 weeks. Starting out my waist (at the belly button) measured 35". After 3 weeks, it was still 35". 10 weeks later it is 32".
>
>
>
> Here is something else I have noticed. Last year, if I would over eat, I would gain 5-10 lbs in just a few days and my waist measurement would increase several inches. I attribute this to 'water weight' where the fat cells in my belly area become super hydrated. Now that these cells are gone, not just shrunk, this doesn't happen. If I over eat, I may fluctuate 1-2lbs, but I don't see an increase in waist measurement.
>
>
>
> It seems normal dieting just shrinks the fat cells, and they will haunt you forever. Cold apoptosis kills them.

This is toooooooo tempting! I'm going into a cold tub right now!

A question though: were you eating differently last year? maybe it was the carbs that you were eating that caused the water weight gain?

### youthfuliz — 2012-06-01T23:56:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28945>

> **AKMan;29036 wrote: I can definitely attest that ice on the love handles reduces the fat contained in them. I religiously iced my belly for about 3 weeks. Starting out my waist (at the belly button) measured 35". After 3 weeks, it was still 35". 10 weeks later it is 32".
>
> **Here is something else I have noticed. Last year, if I would over eat, I would gain 5-10 lbs in just a few days and my waist measurement would increase several inches. I attribute this to 'water weight' where the fat cells in my belly area become super hydrated. Now that these cells are gone, not just shrunk, this doesn't happen. If I over eat, I may fluctuate 1-2lbs, but I don't see an increase in waist measurement.
>
>
>
> It seems normal dieting just shrinks the fat cells, and they will haunt you forever. Cold apoptosis kills them.

Also, can you share what kind of method you use to keep ice around your belly/waist? Do you use a ice belt that can be purchased online? Thanks.

### AKMan — 2012-06-01T23:58:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28946>

> **youthfuliz;29039 wrote:** This is toooooooo tempting! I'm going into a cold tub right now!
>
> A question though: were you eating differently last year? maybe it was the carbs that you were eating that caused the water weight gain?

Here's an example. I had been eating very strict low carb paleo for about a year when I went out for sushi with friends and had rice, eggrolls,and rice noodles. I probably only consumed 1500 calories in that extravagent (for me) meal, with probably 300-400g carbs. The next morning I was up 5lbs and in two days was up 9 lbs. and 2 full inches on my waist measurement. It took 2 weeks to get back where I was before this minor cheat.

Last week, at a friends barbeque, I had a big heap of brown rice, a big scoop of potato salad, and a huge chunk of watermelon along with bbq chicken, and a couple beers. Probably 500g carbs/2000 calories for the meal. That night I weighed before bed, 169lbs. Next morning I was back down to 167lbs. Here a week later, I haven't seen any adverse effects of this one-time carb frenzy.

The drawback to fat apoptosis with ice packs is that it is slow and you can't really see it happening, so it's easy to get discouraged. Keep that ice on your fatty areas for 1-2 hours in the evening before bed. I used ice cubes and water in a 1 gallon ziplock bag. The water helps distribute the cold and conforms to your body. No danger of frostbite this way. As long as there is water, it won't be colder than 32 degrees.

### youthfuliz — 2012-06-02T00:21:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28948>

Thanks for sharing. This is very interesting and I'm already having my first ice/water pad on!

### angieonthegogo — 2012-06-02T01:35:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28961>

> **youthfuliz;29047 wrote:** Thanks for sharing. This is very interesting and I'm already having my first ice/water pad on!

I did something similar in my CT bath a couple nights ago and then the next day I felt chilly/had goosebumps all day. But the point is in my bath I left the ice on my right side only almost exclusively the entire time and the next morning you could see the slight indentation. I mean, I have plenty to lose and it was pretty even on each side but now there is no mistaking that it works because that indentation is there. I wish I would have done before/after photos but I was blown away. I've done ice packs for months but this was a block of melting ice in my 67F water that did it with visible results the next day. All day yesterday I kepy lifting up my shirt when I would go to the restroom at work to make sure I wasn't seeing things. I will try this again tonight because I'm still so amazed by it. No bones about it...thermogenesis is miraculous...just not instantaneous.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-02T02:06:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-28967>

OK, I'm going to be more constistant on spot ct'ing. Thanks for the inspiration!

Awesome observations...I am totally into spot icing...I'm getting great results and really look forward to what you are describing...if I cheat it is so very rare, especially because I hate the setback. ### youthfuliz — 2012-06-02T05:32:37+0300

### AKMan — 2012-06-04T05:25:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-something-good-from-sisson.1726/post-29321>

> **Anna K.;29409 wrote:** Akman, do you put the bag with ice water directly on your skin or over a t-shirt?
>
>
>
> thanks!

Directly on the skin. As long as there's water sloshing around, the temp is right at 33 degrees, won't cause frostbite and will kill fat. I keep it in one spot for 5-10 minutes then adjust slightly to a new spot. A gallon size ziplock about half full with ice and some water will last about 45 minutes, maybe a little longer, but long enough until I'm bored with it anyway!

# Thread: Magnesium Threonate
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T02:55:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28783>

Finally got my mag threonate in the mail. I have to get the pure powder b/c of allergies from wholesale places. This particular one has been more than unhelpful. All the bag says is 99 percent mag threonate. No serving size, no mention of actual mag content per gram.

I looked up the lef.org kind and it says 2 grams of magtein mag threonate have 144mg of magnesium. Would that apply? I'm really unsure since it says 99 percent... when I get my PS the bag says 50 percent and that means 100mg of product have 50mg of phosphatidyl serine. So I'm guessing if I take 100mg of 99 percent mag threonate, I'm getting 99mg of mag threonate. Now how much mag is that really?

### colleencoble — 2012-06-01T03:13:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28788>

Gosh, I don't know. I got the Life Extension capsules since I've never had any trouble with their fillers. And I LOVE the stuff. Amazing, amazing difference!

### JC2K — 2012-06-01T03:15:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28789>

Your logic sounds correct to me, but don't hold me to it. I use the LEF stuff, a scoop a day. It's a different type of magnesium that goes to the brain more, so it's less likely to help with cramps and the like. However, since it is more targetted, you need less of it for that purpose. You also want to be careful supplementing the other magnesium because it has the potential to crowd out the threonate.

What's your source of the stuff. Maybe a quick googling will help track down a source.

incidentally for the LEF stuff, 144mg/2000mg = roughly 7% Magnesium content. It's a branded component, so you're probably not going to see much variance in that. Don't know if that applies to your bulk stuff though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T03:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28791>

> **colleencoble;28887 wrote:** Gosh, I don't know. I got the Life Extension capsules since I've never had any trouble with their fillers. And I LOVE the stuff. Amazing, amazing difference!

Problem is, right now I'm looking at the bag sitting on my counter, imagining all the wonderful things it will do to me  If I could just figure out what my dose is!!

### colleencoble — 2012-06-01T03:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28792>

Oh it TOTALLY demolishes cramps! I had eyelid twitches and leg cramps for decades. I consumed magnesium like it was candy but could never get enough to stay in. 3 days on this stuff and all those problems are gone. I am a huge fan of the stuff!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T03:41:50+0300

I googled and googled  Nada. This stuff is from Cerebral Health/Bioscience Neutraceuticals which I CAN NOT RECOMMEND I only ordered from them b/c it's the only place that sells this stuff as pure powder. They have some pretty super cool stuff though... it's just the customer service s\*cks.

Is the worst thing that can happen the runs or can I accidentally kill myself with this stuff? Do I have to take it away from other mag or should I stop the other mag entirely?

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T03:42:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28794>

> **colleencoble;28891 wrote:** Oh it TOTALLY demolishes cramps! I had eyelid twitches and leg cramps for decades. I consumed magnesium like it was candy but could never get enough to stay in. 3 days on this stuff and all those problems are gone. I am a huge fan of the stuff!

### colleencoble — 2012-06-01T03:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28796>

i hope so. This form of magnesium is downright miraculous.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T03:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28797>

Now if I could stop looking at the bag of miraculous stuff and eat some already

### JC2K — 2012-06-01T04:52:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28802>

From the FAQ at Magtein.com, they say it's GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) so it's not going to suddenly kill you, and the animal studies indicate 1gram twice a day (which roughly corresponds to the 2 grams/dose that LEF gives). They're doing human studies to narrow down that number. But if it's giving you 144mg of Magnesium, you've still got a ways to go for the recommended 400 or so mg of Magnesium/day.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T05:33:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28815>

I think I might take 1gram tomorrow AM and see how that goes... and then take the regular mag at night maybe.

> **colleencoble;28891 wrote:** Oh it TOTALLY demolishes cramps! I had eyelid twitches and leg cramps for decades. I consumed magnesium like it was candy but could never get enough to stay in. 3 days on this stuff and all those problems are gone. I am a huge fan of the stuff!

3 a day of the Life Extension capsules got rid of my eyelid twitching in short order, as well. Plus I don't seem to get palpitations when tired the way I did. I'm a happy camper at this point. I seem able to take it by mouth, unlike the other varieties.

### ealachan — 2012-06-01T14:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28834>

> **Souldanzer;28914 wrote:** I think I might take 1gram tomorrow AM and see how that goes... and then take the regular mag at night maybe.

I had to go back to taking regular magnesium at night in combination with the NeuroMag, because when I stopped taking the regular magnesium I got really constipated. Now I split my NeuroMag dose - two pills in the morning, and then the third one before bed with my regular magnesium. I haven't noticed any of the "miracle" effects of the NeuroMag, at least not yet.

### colleencoble — 2012-06-01T16:17:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28847>

ealachan, if you get really constipated without citrate, I'd guess your thyroid is low. I take 2 capsules in the morning and 2 at night. I haven't taken citrate since.

### ealachan — 2012-06-01T17:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28868>

> **colleencoble;28946 wrote:** ealachan, if you get really constipated without citrate, I'd guess your thyroid is low. I take 2 capsules in the morning and 2 at night. I haven't taken citrate since.

I've never taken citrate. The "regular magnesium" I have is mag oxide.

I'm sure my thyroid probably IS out of whack. The doctor's office I go to only ever tests TSH, none of the other stuff (unless asked, I presume. I HOPE they'll do the other stuff if I ask!). I've always been on the low end on TSH, but within the "acceptable" range, so my questions about thyroid issues in the past were always pooh-pooh'ed.

Can't afford to have the test done until my FSA account refills on 7/1. Ahh, the joys of catastrophic insurance. :

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-01T17:37:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28870>

I took 500mg last night before bed and I am still kicking  So I upped it to 1000mg this AM. I'm pretty sure it's 144mg of mag per 2grams of product, as in the lef.org products.

I slept despite eating a bunch of chocolate last night which usually keeps me up with heart palps. I did have bit of trouble falling asleep but then slept through the night. Let's see how today goes. I will keep with my other mag for now probably at a reduced dose and away from the mag threonate.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-01T20:57:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28908>

> **ealachan;28967 wrote:** I've never taken citrate. The "regular magnesium" I have is mag oxide.

Ealachan, mag oxide is so laxative that it has been known to draw magnesium out of the body.

While thyroid no doubt deserves consideration, I think you're up against a problem which may be unrelated to thyroid: you've been depending on an osmolar laxative, magnesium oxide. You might as well be eating ExLax.

If you give up taking magnesium oxide as a laxative, you'll probably rebound and have severe constipation, but really, it's a crutch.

Bowel movements should be moist and soft and full of beneficial bacteria. If you have "dysbiosis" (not enough gut flora), movements will be dry and hard and difficult to pass. So, eating more fermented food like sauerkraut, kim chee, and real (live) pickles may help your problem.

The Amish have a healthy habit: they have a little apple cider vinegar in warm water first thing in the morning. Be sure to rinse out your mouth afterwards, so the acid doesn't etch your tooth enamel.

You may need to phase out the mag oxide gradually, to give your gut time to adjust.

I have the opposite problem: irritable bowel. "Healthy high-fiber vegetables" bloat me and give me the runs. So many people thrive on them ... it's very frustrating.

It is going to take me time to see if mag threonate is truly non-laxative for me, but I'm strongly motivated to keep taking it. I feel so much more cheerful and energetic, and I lost the stubborn eyelid spasms and haven't had a single missed heartbeat since I started the stuff.

I hope you find the right answer for this dilemma.

### ealachan — 2012-06-01T21:15:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28909>

>
>

thanks for the info. I actually didn't start having constipation issues until I started taking the threonate, so go figure. I wouldn't say I use the Mag Oxide as a crutch, per se. I started taking it because it was a recommended supplement before the NeuroMag stuff came along, not because I needed a laxative. I also don't take large amounts of it. I take two pills, where the recommended dose on the bottle is three.

The reason I think my thyroid is screwed up is actually nothing to do with constipation, but rather all the other boxes I can check when I read down through the hypothyroid symptom list. But, then again, they're all pretty vague symptoms, so who really knows. Certainly not me until I get some bloodwork done.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-01T21:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28915>

I recommend Mag malate for leg cramping and threonate just about for everything else.......In my surgical practice I use threonate more than other forms because it penetrates the CSF pathways in the brain and spinal cord.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-01T21:33:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-28916>

> **ealachan;29008 wrote:** thanks for the info. I actually didn't start having constipation issues until I started taking the threonate, so go figure. I wouldn't say I use the Mag Oxide as a crutch, per se. I started taking it because it was a recommended supplement before the NeuroMag stuff came along, not because I needed a laxative. I also don't take large amounts of it. I take two pills, where the recommended dose on the bottle is three.

>

But, then again, they're all pretty vague symptoms, so who really knows. Certainly not me until I get some bloodwork done.

Hi, Ealachan

Glad you don't have chronic constipation. Here's a little article I just searched out about the various forms of magnesium. It doesn't mention mag threonate, which is quite new, but it does explain that one only absorbs 4% of magnesium oxide, and the rest stays in the gut acting as a laxative.

[http://www.medhelp.org/posts/Heart-Rhythm/Magnesium-supplements----to-avoid---how-to-take-it-and--safety/show/1002415](http://http://www.medhelp.org/posts/Heart-Rhythm/Magnesium-supplements----to-avoid---how-to-take-it-and--safety/show/1002415)

Mag malate seems quite popular. I couldn't take it. I couldn't even manage mag glycinate, supposed to be the most gut-friendly.

### ealachan — 2012-06-01T21:42:33+0300

I can help clear up the confusion (I used to work in the supplement field). Here's the deal with magnesium: most brands list the total weight (which is how you dose), not the elemental weight. So, the 144 mg is actually the elemental weight, which is not what you want to base your dose on. Base it on total weight milligrams. I'd just take it to bowel tolerance.

PS- I'm loving mag. chloride! It's so easy on the gut It's the form that all the other forms have to be broken down into.

### MonteD — 2012-06-04T16:29:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-29350>

> **Souldanzer;28882 wrote:** Finally got my mag threonate in the mail. I have to get the pure powder b/c of allergies from wholesale places. This particular one has been more than unhelpful. All the bag says is 99 percent mag threonate. No serving size, no mention of actual mag content per gram.
>
>
>
> I looked up the lef.org kind and it says 2 grams of magtein mag threonate have 144mg of magnesium. Would that apply? I'm really unsure since it says 99 percent... when I get my PS the bag says 50 percent and that means 100mg of product have 50mg of phosphatidyl serine. So I'm guessing if I take 100mg of 99 percent mag threonate, I'm getting 99mg of mag threonate. Now how much mag is that really?

The chemical formula for Magnesium L-threonate is:

C\[sub\]8H\[sub\]14\[/sub\]MgO10\[/sub\]. To find the molecular weight you would multiply the atomic weight of any element by the number of times that elements atoms occur, then add up each elements final weight to get the total weight of the formula. The total weight being 294.5g. Mg occurs once and has an atomic weight of 24.305g-mol. Therefore, every 294.5g of your magnesium l-threonate should yield 24g of elemental Mg. The LEF product states that 2000mg magnesium l-threonate yields 144mg of elemental magnesium, not far off from the 165mg you'd expect given the formula above.

### chocolate — 2012-06-05T03:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate.1733/post-29468>

Well, I have this supposed super absorbing zinc/copper combo. I'm hoping to make [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/)**Metallothionein.

> Metallothionein (MT) is a family of cysteine-rich, low molecular weight (MW ranging from 500 to 14000 Da) proteins. They are localized to the membrane of the Golgi apparatus. **MTs have the capacity to bind both physiological (such as zinc, copper, selenium) and xenobiotic (such as cadmium, mercury, silver, arsenic) heavy metals through the thiol group of its cysteine residues, which represents nearly the 30% of its amino acidic residues.\[2\]**
>
>
>
> MT was discovered in 1957 by Vallee and Margoshe from purification of a Cd-binding protein from horse (equine) renal cortex.\[3\] MTs function is not clear, but experimental data suggest MTs may provide protection against metal toxicity, be involved in regulation of physiological metals (Zn and Cu) and provide protection against oxidative stress. There are four main isoforms expressed in humans (family 1, see chart below): MT1 (subtypes A, B, E, F, G, H, L, M, X), MT2, MT3, MT4. In the human body, large quantities are synthesised primarily in the liver and kidneys. Their production is dependent on availability of the dietary minerals, as zinc, copper and selenium, and the amino acids histidine and cysteine.

**The zinc is the challenge and the iodide is all good. It cuts the fat out of your arteries and dissolves it in your liver. And kills dead anything with a loose appendage.

### BJK77 — 2012-06-01T21:27:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rosacea.1735/post-28914>

I just realized that I've been dealing with rosacea for quite a while now. I really had no idea what it was until I stumbled across an article that mentioned it yesterday. Anyone else have this?

I used to get bloodshot eyes regularly and apparently that's a form of rosacea - "ocular rosacea." Now I'm getting the rashes that look like red pin pricks all over my cheeks and they get totally heated up. I used to think this was a sign of a food intolerance for me, but I'm learning it's a flare of rosacea usually caused by heat or eating liver and sometimes avocado. I eat a ton of sauerkraut and that doesn't seem to have the same effect although I've read it may due to the high histamine content.

Any suggestions on managing this? It seems to be getting worse instead of better, especially when I'm hot, which will likely be often now that summer is here. I'm currently doing my CT 5-6 days a week and have been for the last few months, but it doesn't seem to be helping. I also follow the AI diet - no nightshades, nuts/seeds, dairy, eggs, etc.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T05:04:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rosacea.1735/post-30040>

Rosacea: Alzheimer’s of the Face????????? From cooling inflammation.........

Is Rosacea Caused by Amyloid LL-37, as Alzheimer’s Is Caused by Anti-microbial Abeta?

A recent article in PLoS One suggests that the amyloid beta (Abeta) proteins that aggregate to form fibrous plaques in the brain tissue of Alzheimer victims, function as typical defensive anti-microbial peptides (AMPs), similar to the LL-37 cathelicidin implicated in facial tissue in rosacea. The structural and functional similarities of Abeta and LL-37 suggest to me that Alzheimer’s and rosacea may also be similar in initiation and treatment. Let’s compare amyloids and AMPs.

\[The figure shows a model protein (from ref.) used to examine stain binding to amyloids. The stains appear to bind to aromatic amino acids spaced evenly between adjacent proteins, but adjacent basic amino acids (blue) are spaced the same way and provide sites for heparin binding.\]

Amyloids:

•Amyloid proteins/peptides align into stacks and fibers

•Stacked beta sheets bind amyloid stains: Congo Red, Thioflavin-T

•Fibers form on anionic polymers: heparin, DNA

•Short amyloid stacks are toxic to cells

•Proteases produce multiple sizes of amyloid peptides

Anti-microbial Peptides:

•AMPS typically contain heparin-binding domains -- basic peptides/ plus charge

•Some AMPs, e.g. LL-37, form fibers on DNA, heparin (stain with amyloid stains)

•Toxic to cell membranes

•Kallikrein stimulated by gut flora migrates to face and clips LL-37 to a smaller peptide that binds to host DNA and stimulates the TLR receptor to produce inflammation

•Stomach pepsin hydrolyzes dietary proteins into anti-microbial peptides (heparin is secreted by mast cells onto to the intestinal surface to protect from any amyloid-like effects)

•Defensins, cathelicidins and other AMPs are under transcriptional control of vitamin D receptorAbeta Is Anti-microbial Like LL-37

Amyloid beta is the well-known source of the fibrous plaques forming brain lesions in Alzheimer’s disease. The normal function of Abeta has not been firmly established. The recent article shows data to support Abeta as an anti-microbial peptide comparable to LL-37 against several pathogenic bacteria and yeast. Knock-out mice deprived of a gene corresponding to Abeta are susceptible to bacterial infections. The anti-microbial activity present in extracts from Alzheimer’s disease brains was inactivated by anti-Abeta antibodies.

Implications of Abeta as an AMP Like LL-37

The similarities between and amyloid peptides suggest some implications for both Alzheimer’s disease and rosacea. Vitamin D is a hormone that binds to a cytoplasmic receptor and the vitD/receptor complex then acts as a transcription factor that controls the expression of defensins in the intestines, LL-37 in facial skin and perhaps Abeta in brains.

Amyloids form fibers on a scaffolding of heparan sulfate (HS). There is usually an excess of HS on the surface of cells and the HS is rapidly recycled back into cells. During inflammation, mast cells release heparin, short fragments of HS, that should also inhibit amyloid fiber formation on HS. Chronic inflammation, however, reduces HS production and may set the stage for amyloid fiber formation. HS metabolism of the brain may be vitally important to the development of Alzheimer’s disease, especially since the increasing chronic inflammation of aging people should deplete brain HS.r />

LL-37 forms complexes with DNA from damaged host cells in rosacea skin. The LL-37/DNA complexes trigger TLRs and inflammation. LL-37 may normally bind to cell surface HS and chronic inflammation of the skin may cause the shift to pathogenic autoinflammation. Topical application or perhaps low dose IV heparin may be effective in disrupting the autoinflammation due to LL-37. Part of the toxicity of LL-37 in the skin may be due to amyloid like structures that could form with inadequate HS and overabundant LL-37 production. Vitamin D metabolism should also be very important, since LL-37 synthesis is controlled by vitamin D. This is consistent with the benefits that some rosaceans observe with high doses of vitamin D3 supplements. Vitamin D and Alzheimers patients needs to be checked..........I bet most have levels that are quite low and the Gut flora is likely the smoking gun.........

I want to return to the subject of aging. A year and a half ago I wrote, “You don’t wear out, you flame out.” I still think that is true, but I need to update that idea of inflammation and aging to include diet, gut flora and immune system development. So here is my old article with a new focus on the gut.

I don’t think that aging happens -- most symptoms associated with aging are just medically mismanaged chronic inflammation. The major observations are that older people have more degenerative/autoimmune diseases and they suffer from fewer infectious diseases. The typical explanation is that the bodies of older people have figured out infections with an experienced immune system and that mechanical damage takes its toll over time -- joints wear out. I think that there may be a minor amount of truth in this cultural perspective, but there is something more profound at work, sarcopenia combined with a compromised gut flora.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T05:04:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rosacea.1735/post-30041>

Sarcopenia (muscle loss) is the gradual loss of muscle and replacement by fat. Thus, by age fifty most people are physically less active and even if they appear to have the same weight and shape as in their active youth, the muscle of their abdomens and limbs has been partially replaced with fat. This fat, as in those who are obese, releases inflammatory cytokines into the circulation and the body reacts as if it has a low grade infection.

Senior citizens are constantly expending energy and taxing their immune system by chronic inflammation. As a result they get fewer infections, but the chronic inflammation provides the foundation for cancer and autoimmune diseases. Their bodies aren’t mechanically wearing out, but they are wearing out by over use of the immune system.

Those seniors who are physically active and eat an anti-inflammatory diet, do not appear to age as fast as those who are sedentary, obese and display the typical symptoms of chronic inflammation, the metabolic syndrome. Most of the characteristics associated with advancing years are merely symptoms of poorly managed chronic inflammation that can be reversed by an anti-inflammatory diet and exercise.

Diet also contributes to aging, because diet controls development of gut flora and gut flora control development of the immune system. The gut flora of an individual reflect the bacteria that have entered the GI tract, nutrients available to the bacteria in previous meals, bacterial growth regulators released by the gut, exposure to antibiotics, exposure to phytochemicals and gut transit time.

Gut flora appears to be amazingly diverse from individual to individual with thousands of bacterial species inhabiting humans worldwide and about 150 species in each individual. The same species remain in an individual for long periods of time regardless of diet. The dominance of particular species depends on recent diet. Major changes can result from antibiotics or gut diseases, e.g. Crohn’s.

Bacteria grow quickly and with ample nutrients gut bacteria can double in number in about an hour. Bacterial species are usually defined by the ability to utilize various carbohydrates or polysaccharides as nutrients. Depending on the food eaten, nutrients favor the growth of particular bacterial species and the gut flora population changes dynamically. New species are incorporated into the gut flora only if they find their way into the gut on food, e.g. riding on fresh, uncooked vegetables, and food provides nutrients that can permit the new bacteria to grow. It will take several meals for new bacteria to reach appreciable numbers. In the mean time the new fiber may be partially degraded and produce chemicals that disrupt other gut flora and cause bloating symptoms of food intolerance. This is not an allergic reaction of the immune system. It just takes time and persistence to permit the gut flora to adapt. Most people systematically make themselves intolerant to particular foods by over-reacting to initial maladaption of their gut flora to the new food. If they persisted with progressive exposure to diverse foods, their gut flora would adapt.

People of increasing age who maintain a diverse, anti-inflammatory diet and maintain muscle mass by weight-bearing exercise, avoid age-related inflammation and disease, i.e. they age more slowly. Conversely, those who simplify their diets by eating processed foods high in starch and vegetable oils, show symptoms normally associated with advanced age, even when young. The aging diet is inflammatory and it also produces a gut flora which is different from the youthful.

Constipation is a extreme example of dysfunctional gut flora and since gut bacteria are needed for the normal development of the immune system that is located in the lining of the small intestines, constipation is also an indicator of a compromised immune system. Aging is frequently accompanied by digestive problems with one extreme being constipation. It should not be surprising that individuals with compromised immune systems also develop numerous degenerative diseases indicative of a lack in the immunological tolerance systems that develop in the gut in response to normal gut flora. Constipation and digestive problems are not normal signs of aging.

A healthy diet, healthy gut flora, and a competent immune system are all tightly connected. The typical symptoms of aging merely reflect an unhealthy diet and lifestyle that leads to chronic inflammation, a compromised immune system and disease. The process of aging can be slowed by attention to the next meal. Most people who fail to be healthy and active well into their 80’s are simply victims of bad choices (or of bad medical advice.)

references:

Soscia SJ, Kirby JE, Washicosky KJ, Tucker SM, Ingelsson M, Hyman B, Burton MA, Goldstein LE, Duong S, Tanzi RE, Moir RD. The Alzheimer's disease-associated amyloid beta-protein is an antimicrobial peptide. PLoS One. 2010 Mar 3;5(3):e9505.

Abedini A, Tracz SM, Cho JH, Raleigh DP. Characterization of the heparin binding site in the N-terminus of human pro-islet amyloid polypeptide: implications for amyloid formation. Biochemistry. 2006 Aug 1;45(30):9228-37.

Biancalana M, Makabe K, Koide A, Koide S. Molecular mechanism of thioflavin-T binding to the surface of beta-rich peptide self-assemblies. J Mol Biol. 2009 Jan 30;385(4):1052-63. Epub 2008 Nov 14.

# Thread: Study of Iron on Early Earth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-of-iron-on-early-earth.1736/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-01T21:36:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-of-iron-on-early-earth.1736/post-28919>

I think this fits neatly into factor X:

[http://phys.org/news/2012-05-early-earth-iron-magnesium-rna.html](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-05-early-earth-iron-magnesium-rna.html)

There is considerable evidence that the evolution of life passed through an early stage when RNA played a more central role before DNA and coded proteins appeared. During that time, more than 3 billion years ago, the environment lacked oxygen but had an abundance of soluble iron.

In a new study, researchers from the Georgia Institute of Technology used experiments and numerical calculations to show that iron, in the absence of oxygen, can substitute for magnesium in RNA binding, folding and catalysis. The researchers found that RNA's shape and folding structure remained the same and its functional activity increased when magnesium was replaced by iron in an oxygen-free environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-01T21:42:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-of-iron-on-early-earth.1736/post-28923>

But Akman.....realize before oxygen we had millions of years with the same life forums......when we got the Cambrian explosion due to the appearance of O2, the fossil record shows that we got 32 phyla right after it. This shows you the major effect that the environment has on all life forms.

### Entelechy — 2012-06-01T22:48:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-of-iron-on-early-earth.1736/post-28928>

[**A molecular timescale of eukaryote evolution and the rise of complex multicellular life**

](http://http://www.biomedcentral.com/1471-2148/4/2/abstract)

with all the communication and signalling between cells,you need energy.. lots of it... to build a multicellular organism.

For this level of energy, you need oxygen.. lots of it. ( and mitochondria).


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: The most important thing you need to know about Diet and Exercise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-06-01T23:48:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-28943>

If I had a dollar or a euro (would rather the euro ;-)) for every time I got asked "whats the best" tip/advice/strategy to can give to keto adapt, increase fat burning, improve endurance, etc etc... I would be a very rich man !

The best tip I can give is this... "there ain't no"... by that I mean there is no single thing that will improve your health or fitness. Everything related to how you function optimally is down to the accumulation of marginal gains from every aspect of well-being from food to sleep to thoughts to exercise. Further more, and arguably more important to realise is this - there is no priority list. You are as strong as your weakest link.

Let me put this into perspective. You might be the most paleo individual known to man. You hunt your prey, grow your own vegetables and climb tree's and lift rocks. However, you don't sleep well and work causes a lot of stress. So what happens then is that you have raised cortisol, poor GH secretion and suppressed immunity i.e. your not that healthy. To anyone else though, you are super fit and healthy. You are ticking practically every paleo box. Then you ask someone what the best supplement is for improving ketosis. They tell you to take high dose's of l-carnitine and omega 3's. But nothing happens. The reason nothing happens is that your sleep and stress problems completely override anything you try to improve related to food or exercise.

It might be the most minute detail that is not allowing you to achieve optimal health. It could be as simple as a "thought". It could be eliminating the grass fed unpastuerised yoghurt you are eating that is causing your leaky gut. It could be anything. The only person who is truly able to work that out is you.

People are forgetting the most important thing Jack has done here. Its not the cold. Its not sleep. Its not seafood. Its not PQQ or Krill Oil. The most important thing Jack has done in my opinion is in his words "connect the dots"... thats what its all about. I knew about Leptin, AMPk, Sirt-1, UCP's etc etc... what I didn't know is how all these things are intrinsically linked. Thats the key.

So the best advice I have for anyone trying to improve their health is this: do not look for magic bullets, they don't exist. Rather, look to improve every aspect of your life from nutrition to thoughts no matter how big or small they may seem. Everything counts. Nothing is more important than the other. You are as strong as your weakest link.

Finally, don't try to be perfect. There is no perfect. There is whats perfect for you, the individual. Thats what matters. Do whats right for you and don't listen to what the so called "experts" claim.

"Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined"

Barry

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-02T01:07:41+0300

Excellent advice, Barry. Thank you Dr Kruse for helping us "connect the dots". Your Quilt is an ingenious tapestry that with each thread that you weave gives us the big picture.

### AKMan — 2012-06-02T01:21:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-28956>

I've never been one to chase the magic bullets, but I have come up with a very sustainable way of life involving eating right, exercising, and using cold to my benefit.

I take minimal supplements (green tea extract, resveratrol, Vit D3, krill oil, and kelp). I eat mainly grass-fed or wild animals/fish, lots of veggies, and 100% dark chocolate. I do body weight exercises and sprint on occasion. Since I live in a cold place, I get as cold as I can as often.

I'm a 46yo male, 5'11. 3 years ago I was 250lbs, had gout, high bp, high trigs, high ldl, low hdl, fatty liver disease, pre-diabetes, hypothyroid, sleep apnea, and a ravenous appetite. Now, I'm 164lbs, on no medicine, trigs and hdl both 60. Sleep great, eat great, no hint of gout in 2 years.

I could probably quit any number of the things I do and still be very healthy...but if it works, don't fix it, right?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-02T18:33:08+0300

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-03T07:06:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-29183>

I do have an answer for people when they ask this. I tell them to not eat four hours before going to sleep. I say this because i believe it is very important but also because i think it may be the most accessible change. It costs absolutely zero and initially involves no extreme diet change. You want to eat crap? Fine eat crap, just don't do it for four hours before sleep. You will be better off. Then I tell them to do all of Jack's protocols!

### Barry — 2012-06-03T15:26:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-29193>

> **Dali Dula;29283 wrote:** I do have an answer for people when they ask this. I tell them to not eat four hours before going to sleep. I say this because i believe it is very important but also because i think it may be the most accessible change. It costs absolutely zero and initially involves no extreme diet change. You want to eat crap? Fine eat crap, just don't do it for four hours before sleep. You will be better off. Then I tell them to do all of Jack's protocols!

Dali Dula,

Ironically , that defeats the whole point I am trying to make !

There is no "best" tip. This is the antithesis of what achieving optimal health is all about. Being healthy is about creating a new normal , it's about finding out for yourself what makes you feel good , and ultimately it's about incorporating all the best health practices so that they become 2nd nature and sustainable .

This is where people go wrong. While the "4hr" last feed before sleep has its merits , it's only one of a zillion things that can improve your health . By singling it out and making someone believe that this is the best step for them to take is creating a subconscious distraction. They make the effort to change their eating habits around sleep, yet they neglect other important nutrition principles . Not only that , but the change requires them to make an effort and forced behavioural change because you told them to. This is another counter productive move. People make better changes when they decide for themselves . It means they are making the change based on their own free will. This makes the behavioural change easier and more importantly, it means that they will make it a long term change .

So to echo Jacks comment above, the best advice we can give to people is to point them in the right direction with a really good map . Let them then read the map as much and as quickly as they like. Let them choose their own route and let them enjoy the journey. They might fall off the track now and again, they might find a faster route or they might take the scenic route. But the key to it all is that they are finding their own way and will eventually get to their destination !

Nothing is more important than the other, everything matters.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-03T16:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-29198>

I guess I see the 4 hr thing as a way to take the first of many baby steps towards the challenging practice of changing our lives. You are correct in that we all need to own our bodies and the quest to optimal is not one fix. I believe the 4 hr thing would let people feel better and get their bodies repairing with the least struggle. My motivation toward optimal began with my desire to treat/heal T2D without meds. Now I pursue this because I feel amazing and am in the best shape of my life and I continue to maximize that. So this to me is about accesibilty, simplicity and effectiveness. It introduces people to some circadian correctness while providing a +/- 12 hr fast and an opportunity for the body to autophagy. That's a lot of benefit from a simple change. Peace on. Keep chilling.

Each one of us has a treasure map to optimal in us.......the real problem is optimal for humans is like a finger print or a retinal picture.......unique. Using the concepts will show the user which ones are more important for you and how your epigenetic switches are set.

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-05T06:00:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-29487>

I think that is soooomthe key here, we are all different and will have a different journey depending on the state we begin in. What I have discovered here in the blog and the forum is, there is no easy answer, and my mileage may vary. What I have also discovered is a wealth of knowledge in connecting my own dots.... And why things have not worked in the past for me the same as for others. And now I see things working because of what I have put into action here. And there are so many moving parts!

I see what Dali Dula is saying though... You have to start somewhere. For me it was VLC. Then 'leptin rx' eating rules/timing...then cutting out wine. Then installing flux. Then getting orange glasses. Then WEARING them! Starting CT. Earthing during CT in the ocean. Celaning up my food sources... Finding Grassfed animals. Adding more seafood. Finding the supplements..... And the list goes on.

The more I read the more things I find to change. And inch by inch it's a cinch. If someone told me to do all this on Day 1, I'd have been waaay too daunted. but as I have discovered things....on my own, nobody with a gun to my head..... I have changed. I'm glad I found this blog before the book because honestly the book may overwhelm many!! I hope not. The thing is to start somewhere and keep adding the bricks that pave your personal road to optimal. I'm sure Jack has that message in there.

Discovering at my own pace and the way jack has forced us to process this ourselves, be curious, and ask questions, has given me OWNERSHIP of the process, which I have found extremely gratifying, and endlessly motivating, in a way that being spoon fed might never have done.

Weight loss is a side effect on the road to optimal, and that's the goal for me now. I thank God for Jack and for the community he has created - all of you. I'm learning so much from all of you who take your time to share....so unselfishly....thank you!!

### AKMan — 2012-07-03T21:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-35323>

> **Barry;29042 wrote:** If I had a dollar or a euro (would rather the euro ;-)) for every time I got asked "whats the best" tip/advice/strategy to can give to keto adapt, increase fat burning, improve endurance, etc etc... I would be a very rich man !
>
>
>
> The best tip I can give is this... "there ain't no"... by that I mean there is no single thing that will improve your health or fitness. Everything related to how you function optimally is down to the accumulation of marginal gains from every aspect of well-being from food to sleep to thoughts to exercise. Further more, and arguably more important to realise is this - there is no priority list. You are as strong as your weakest link.
>
> Barry

This thread has haunted me ever since I read it a few weeks ago. While it is very true that there are no 'one-size-fits-all' answers, there are a lot of generalities you can make when people ask how the can get their health back.

Being a retired military man, I have a lot of middle-aged, overweight, male friends. They are all in shock when the see me, especially if it's been a while. First they ask if I am dying of something (since only deathly sick people lose weight anymore!), when I assure them I am not, they start asking what I did and what they can do. For me to tell them 'There ain't no...' would be a huge disservice.

I tell anyone who will listen that they need to do two simple things to get their life on track.

1\. Quit eating crap, especially sugar, vegetable oil, and processed grains.

2\. Start moving, walk every day and lift something heavy a couple times a week.

If they take the bait, it usually results in follow-up conversations where we talk about exercise routines, dietary advice, intermittant fasting, testing labs, great websites, sleep, supplements, leptin reset, cold thermogenesis, eating patterns, little tricks, and all that.

Sadly, most lose interest when you say 'quit eating sugar', but I have enlightened many people and have turned around at least a dozen people who were pretty bad off. In the end, it must be an individual journey to optimal, but a few well-placed words can make a huge difference.

### kathylu — 2012-07-03T21:42:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-35329>

Thanks AkMan. I just had a disappointing exchange with my sister, who is thin but quite ill. I am overweight and much much healthier than she is. However, she thinks I am going to drop dead any day! I have told her the key to eliminating her IBS, gout, Sjogren's and RA may lie in eliminating grains and dairy and I suggested gently that she might want to start by reading "Wheatbelly" by William Davis. My goodness, you would have thought I suggested she drink cyanide! She literally yelled that there was no way she would ever give up oatmeal, healthy whole grains and dairy! How could I even suggest such a thing?

So, I like your gentle approach. Although sadly, I think these changes may never come to the people we love most. They seem to be the ones with the largest blinders on.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-03T21:59:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-35337>

I find the biggest thing we can do is to inspire curiosity. Once someone starts thinking, they research for ideas on how to change. Once they do the research - then we can guide them to the proper path. But without that initial spark, it ain't going nowhere.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-03T22:15:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-35339>

> **kathylu;35490 wrote:** My goodness, you would have thought I suggested she drink cyanide! She literally yelled that there was no way she would ever give up oatmeal, healthy whole grains and dairy! How could I even suggest such a thing?
>
>
>
> So, I like your gentle approach. Although sadly, I think these changes may never come to the people we love most. They seem to be the ones with the largest blinders on.

I remember when I first read The Paleo Diet for Athletes. I threw the book down when I read I should eat 12 ounces of salmon and cantaloupe for breakfast. I was addicted to my healthy whole grain breakfast cereal with skim milk. How could I give that up!?! It took a few weeks for the ideas to sink in and for me to become receptive to making a major change. I admire both of you for bringing up the topic with people who are in need and willing to listen.

### Stephen — 2012-07-26T05:53:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-41158>

> **Lyndra;35500 wrote:** I remember when I first read The Paleo Diet for Athletes. I threw the book down when I read I should eat 12 ounces of salmon and cantaloupe for breakfast. I was addicted to my healthy whole grain breakfast cereal with skim milk. How could I give that up!?! It took a few weeks for the ideas to sink in and for me to become receptive to making a major change. I admire both of you for bringing up the topic with people who are in need and willing to listen.

I've got to wonder how anyone who says "I could never give up eating X" doesn't eventually stop for a minute and think..."hey wait that's not right, it's just food, why would I feel this way about any one particular food." I've seen the reaction kathylu describes and the resistance you're describing Lyndra too many times to count. I've had friends tell me explicitly that they've stopped eating bread and a few minutes later directly contradict themselves in passing or I've later seen stacks of pizza boxes in their house.

A theory that I've been kicking around in my head is this is (at least in part) the effect of grains messing with opiate receptors. If you were a plant (a grass, e.g. wheat) and you convinced a bunch of domesticated pack hunting apes with a penchant for tool making (technological prosthesis) to bulldoze the competition and plant you in their stead you would be self selecting through a symbiotic relationship for your survival over other plant species.

Stephen

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.1737/post-42130>

> **Jack Kruse;42371 wrote:** Stephen its not a stupid theory.......its what happens

Here's a great article on the "cornification" of us....

[http://michaelpollan.com/articles-archive/when-a-crop-becomes-king/](http://http://michaelpollan.com/articles-archive/when-a-crop-becomes-king/)

So it seems corn has indeed become king. We have given it more of our land than any other plant, an area more than twice the size of New York State. To keep it well fed and safe from predators we douse it with chemicals that poison our water and deepen our dependence on foreign oil. And then in order to dispose of all the corn this cracked system has produced, we eat it as fast as we can in as many ways as we can—turning the fat of the land into, well, fat. One has to wonder whether corn hasn’t at last succeeded in domesticating us.

# Thread: Coconut Oil...what's the difference with Extra Virgin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-02T05:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-28998>

I buy a huge plastic container of LouAnn CO at my local grocery store for about $6. Super inexpensive, which is great, because I consume one container a month.

The only ingredient is CO, but it's not Virgin or Extra Virgin. Those are a lot more expensive, but what's the difference? Is there a large benefit to the V or Extra V?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-02T06:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29009>

I have no idea what you get in terms of range in the US, but in NZ the difference is how it's processed. Some is sun-dried the same day it's harvested, left raw and either slightly fermented or totally fermented and then put into jars. Others are only processed centrifugally to get the oil (and low/cool temps) and then jarred. These sorts retain the highest levels of MCT oils and other goodness. Then there are processes that deodorise the oil and others that process it at high/higher heats which denature it to differing degrees. So you need to find out what sort of methods are behind the processing, how they are sourced and treated.

### JC2K — 2012-06-02T07:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29023>

In short, no difference between Virgin and Extra Virgin for coconut oil. I found this page to be helpful in explaining it: [http://www.tropicaltraditions.com/what\_is\_virgin\_coconut\_oil.htm](http://http://www.tropicaltraditions.com/what_is_virgin_coconut_oil.htm)

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-03T17:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29218>

> **PrimalPam;29097 wrote:** I buy a huge plastic container of LouAnn CO at my local grocery store for about $6. Super inexpensive, which is great, because I consume one container a month.
>
>
>
> The only ingredient is CO, but it's not Virgin or Extra Virgin. Those are a lot more expensive, but what's the difference? Is there a large benefit to the V or Extra V?

there are no standards in the US for different grades of co like there are for olive oil. if you want to know about the different kinds then read up them at tropicaltraditions.com . what you pay for would be higher levels of MCTs. that LuAnn brand is really cheap so that means heated during processing; maybe bleached. therefore low levels of MCTs. but there are lots of products between $6 and the $34 or so for highest grade co. i like the house brand from vitacost. i think $20 and shipping but i buy it when i am ordering vits so it defrays the shipping cost.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-03T18:15:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29220>

The most important issue with CO is does it have significant mycotoxins or not......if it does your gut wont dig it. This is why I use Nutiva.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-04T00:14:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29263>

> **Jack Kruse;29320 wrote:** The most important issue with CO is does it have significant mycotoxins or not......if it does your gut wont dig it. This is why I use Nutiva.

Thanks!! I had just ordered two 54-oz. containers of Nutiva from Amazon! I'll be swimming in it!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-06T00:57:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-whats-the-difference-with-extra-virgin.1738/post-29629>

> **Anna K.;29415 wrote:** I like this brand ...

So glad you posted the link - I like that company's products as well and knew I had seen a quality report. Thanks!!

# Thread: Anti depressants

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-06-02T22:23:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29097>

I have been trying to get off anti depressants. I was taking Wellbutrin and another SSRI and I can't remember the name. I decided to go off of them pretty much on my own. But it has been. TERRIBLE. I wake up every day so ANGRY! I scream at my kids at the littlest thing and just feel so much RAGE. I'd like to stay off of them but not at my family's expense. Any advice?

### indigogirl — 2012-06-02T22:41:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29101>

You've got to wean off them slowly!!

Check out Dr. Kalish's website...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T23:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29118>

Agreed...must wean slowly or the result is what you describe. I'd go back on and wean if I were you!

Also adopt a lifestyle low in caffeine, alcohol, keep blood sugar stable (paleo great for that) and mild exercise (walking the dog, not running!!).... Meditating to decrease stress helps too. I did all these things as I was weaning. And got myself outdoors to walk for my brain health, not for weight loss... That kept me doing it more than if it was weight. Walking is a great stress reliever (I go for a slow walk a couple times for 10 min daily when stress builds at work, always makes me feel better!)

### Tinanan — 2012-06-02T23:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29119>

Check out The Mood Cure by Julia Ross. My husband used the recommendations in it to wean off of Paxil very painlessly over about 6 weeks.

Good luck! It is worth it to be free of antidepressants!

### indigogirl — 2012-06-03T00:17:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29126>

Also, taking fish oils supps is good for depression...good quality high dose...about 4,000 mg a day

### ealachan — 2012-06-03T03:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29153>

> **Sem;29197 wrote:** I have been trying to get off anti depressants. I was taking Wellbutrin and another SSRI and I can't remember the name. I decided to go off of them pretty much on my own. But it has been. TERRIBLE. I wake up every day so ANGRY! I scream at my kids at the littlest thing and just feel so much RAGE. I'd like to stay off of them but not at my family's expense. Any advice?

I was on Wellbutrin for years. Weaned off it last summer. The two things that helped me the most were upping my D3 to 10k IU a day, and upping my Omega 3 supplementation. When I finally cut my carbs down below 25g a day I started noticing a big difference, too. It took a few weeks, but it did really help.

### ColdBren — 2012-06-03T05:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29170>

Im so sorry, and i definitely understand. I went off a ssri and dealt with that awful anger. Seratonin is the aggression neurotransmitter. I think (but am not sure) that tryptophan and 5-htp helped my seratonin levels come back. I tried so many different things I dont know what worked or didnt. But it makes sense that natural precursers to seratonin help. Also, phosphatidylserine really is helping my cortisol levels, also something that makes me irritable and angry. Give it time and be nice to yourself in your thoughts....the Monk who sold his ferrari is a great book to have playing on audio all the time....it focuses your mind to positive things.

### indigogirl — 2012-06-03T06:42:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29177>

I think 5htp and antidepressants can be dangerous taken together or even taken too close to one another...please research this!

### Sem — 2012-06-03T06:53:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29180>

I think you have to be off of 5-HTP for at least six months!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-03T17:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29203>

Don't take amino acids (5-htp/tyrosine) if you're on anti-depressants without consulting your doc.

That said, anti-depressants deplete serotonin and dopamine in the brain and therefore \*should\* always be combined with amino acid therapy. It's the reason anti-depressants stop working after a few months and why you feel like cr\*p coming off them. If you're depressed, most likely you already are depleted in neurotransmitters b/c that is a symptom. Anti-depressants will "force" more serotonin/dopamine from the storage areas in your brain to be released... but they do nothing about the depletion, in fact, make the depletion worse by their action. Read Dr. Kalish's website [http://www.drkalish.com/index2.html](http://http://www.drkalish.com/index2.html)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;cVtd85zZyjg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;cVtd85zZyjg)

The weird thing is that I feel especially bad in the morning. I wake up just totally pissed off at the world, especially my poor kids who are usually the ones who woke me up.

### Sem — 2012-06-03T19:23:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29240>

It's really bad, Dr. Kruse. This is the same doctor who gave me Topamax which made me a complete, blubbering wreck and messed up my metabolism. I went off of the Topamax because it would stop working for my migraines and he would just increase the dose! Insane. Same with the anti depressants. I said one wasn't working and the answer was to add a second and to give me Atavan. It's amazing that I am not curled up in a fetal position in some heroin debbie by now.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-03T20:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29243>

I somehow escaped the psychopharmaca in my 30 years of depression and mental illness - always was scared of it and never understood it as a solution. But I do observe the steady decline in people who are on this stuff on a daily basis in my own surroundings.

I have no input on whether or not you can start amino acid therapy at this time. Honestly, if you feel the way you are describing, I think you desperately need to see a doc who knows what he is doing in getting you off them.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-03T21:18:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29247>

> **Jack Kruse;29323 wrote:** This class of drugs has as many side effects as good effects. I am still real surprised there is not a more MDs who really look at the lack of efficacy of these drugs before writing Rx for them.

It's not just the efficacy - they are just plain mis-prescribed by so many physicians. I have lost count of the times a physician prescribed anti-depressants or anti-anxiety meds for what I knew was an entirely physical problem. They never cared to investigate other possible causes.

Let's give you something to stop the crying, let's add this to shut you up and mellow you out, this other med to make you sleep at night and then, to wake you up so you can go to work, add this on top of that. You're experiencing side-effects? I can give you something for that as well. Drugged up world.
