### L8F — 2012-06-04T01:24:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29266>

I believe in SSRIs when one feels better on them. They help many many people. For some they do not work at all, but if they work, they can work very well.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T02:28:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29270>

> **L8F;29366 wrote: I believe in SSRIs when one feels better on them. They help many many people. For some they do not work at all, but if they work, they can work very well.
>
>
> I have no opinion on whether or not anti-depressants should be used in a particular person. I do know the damage they can do if not used appropriately. I also know that these days they are given out like candy by most MD's, instead of investigating the real source of the problem.
>
>
>
> [http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter\_depletion.html](http://http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter_depletion.html)
>
>
>
> ...."NE-selective antidepressant drugs appear to be primarily dependent on the availability of NE for their effects. Likewise, 5-HT-selective antidepressants appear to be primarily dependent on the availability of 5-HT for their effects." NE" is short for norepinephrine and "5-HT" is short for 5-hydroxytryptophan, which is better known as serotonin. This study verified what Dr. Hinz had been saying, **"drugs that work with neurotransmitters do not work if there are not enough neurotransmitters to work with."....
>
>
>
> ....Some medications actually increase the excretion of neurotransmitters. Paxil is among the worst at wasting neurotransmitters...
>
>
>
> ....3. DEPLETION WITH SSRI MEDICATIONS (the theory we are working under now): As the level of intersynaptic neurotransmitters increase, the Monoamine Oxidase (MAO) system is stimulated and increases the breakdown of neurotransmitters. If you do not increase the nutritional intake of serotonin and norepinephrine precursors in patients that are being treated with antidepressants, the net effect is further depletion over time.** More and more medication is required with greater and greater depletion leading to a vicious cycle of progressively more medication need with less and less effect.....
>
>
>
> Every MD has seen numerous patients who were on antidepressants (SSRIs) that seemed to quit working. When the proper amino acids that are the building blocks of neurotransmitters are provided, the benefits of the medications resume within one to two weeks....
>
>
>
> ....It is noteworthy that the largest producer of serotonin in the body is not the brain, but rather the gastrointestinal tract.** A healthy colon does more than provide adequate digestion!....
>
>
>
> ....Depletion of the neurotransmitters may be only part of the story. Some of the medications seem to be causing damage to the nerves as well.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T02:45:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29275>

> **Sem;29340 wrote:** It's really bad, Dr. Kruse. This is the same doctor who gave me Topamax which made me a complete, blubbering wreck and messed up my metabolism. I went off of the Topamax because it would stop working for my migraines and he would just increase the dose! Insane. Same with the anti depressants. I said one wasn't working and the answer was to add a second and to give me Atavan. It's amazing that I am not curled up in a fetal position in some heroin debbie by now.

Topamax probably stopped working for your migraines b/c it works by affecting the serotonin levels in your body, which if you don't have any serotonin any longer won't do much. Most migraine meds will increase serotonin which constricts blood vessels and alleviates migraine headaches that way (most migraines are caused by an unusual dilation of blood vessels). Topamax does the opposite. Now my doc recently wanted to put me on Topamax (thank God I refused), and when I read up on it it actually causes a reduction in serotonin and a dilation in blood vessels - after he just told me my blood vessels are too dilated and that's what's causing my headaches... who knows what his reasoning for this rx was! Most likely none.

Honestly, I am not a medical person, but there appears to be a confounding problem putting someone on SSRI's and Topamax at the same time? One forces more serotonin to be released while the other one opposes that action. I guess SSRI's action is inside the brain while migraine meds work in the blood vessels of the covering of the brain. But still....

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-04T02:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29276>

> **Sem;29321 wrote:** What if I am already off the anti-depressants?? Do you have to be off of them for six months? It's been a couple of weeks for me. Thank you! The weird thing is that I feel especially bad in the morning. I wake up just totally pissed off at the world, especially my poor kids who are usually the ones who woke me up.

Well on one level this doesn't surprise me... For normal folks cortisol is highest in the morning!!! It's what wakes you up in the morning. Its also high when you experience stress. Exactly what you don't need. I feel ya, I went through exactly this....

Think of it this way. Your body was not making the neurotransmitters it needed naturally and you were inducing them to both be made, and to be retained longer before being used up. When you artificially induce them your body becomes dependent on the external stimulus to make them. So when you stop that pill, your body's forgotten how to do it naturally. Thus, you turn into a raging beast. (my dad calls his anti depressants his 'anti-asshole' pills...because if he forgets to take them' well...!!!He is not ready to make the changes he needs to make to wean though)

I experienced this same thing when I quit smoking 12 years ago.....nicotine was my stimulus. Then I took it away. And my body within weeks used up what it had and I slid into a dark and terrible depression where I felt like I was standing there watching myself be a bitch to friends family and coworkers with no way of stopping myself. Horrible. I got on antidepressants and felt better in a few months, then started researching what I needed to do to wean off them.

I wish you luck in your journey...my prayers are with you!! My $0.02 is I felt much more able to cope while ON them, so I could research and make the changes I needed to, and THEN wean off them. If it wasn't 10 years ago for my weaning I'd remember what I did and share (beyond avoiding alcohol, caffeine, maintaining stable blood sugar, and very mild exercise as stress relief...and yes, what you think is VErY important.)

I did buy the infomercial product "overcoming anxiety and depression" and for the mental part, being kind to yourself in your thoughts, realizing any self defeating thoughts you need to axe....I found that very helpful. It's a series of tapes (probably mp3s now!) and a workbook, and I felt like I was making progress working through it and it really helped me. There was one that your spouse could listen to as well, which explains the physiological things going on (I.e. you're not just being an asshole, you literally can't help it at the moment). That helped my saint of a husband know how to deal with me, but I still made a point of, after realizing I'd just snapped his head off, apologizing and rephrasing and acknowledging that I'd just heard myself do it. It helped him know that I was trying very hard to get better and not take it out on him.

Good luck we are all rooting for you and I'm sure others will share their ideas with you too!

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-04T02:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29278>

> **Souldanzer;29370 wrote:** I have no opinion on whether or not anti-depressants should be used in a particular person. I do know the damage they can do if not used appropriately. I also know that these days they are given out like candy by most MD's, instead of investigating the real source of the problem.
>
>
>
> [http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter\_depletion.html](http://http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter_depletion.html)

Thanks for this info. Working with my dad to eventually wean him and this is more incentive.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-04T02:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-29281>

Nicotine works similarly to anti-depressants in that it stimulates your brain to release more serotonin than it naturally would... you deplete. Any stimulant will cause this really... that's why so many people like them b/c they do make you feel better, initially. Especially if you already have low levels of neurotransmitters.

### Josh — 2014-09-22T02:35:06+0300

The presentations are just to get oriented to the ideas behind the therapy, not the reality of these drugs in an N=1 individual. ### mafixit — 2014-10-02T22:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-depressants.1747/post-142610>

I have just started Pamelor to try to get my chronic daily headaches under control. I feel increased heart palpitations, especially when I lay down, for some reason.

I am on a low dose and am told to ramp up slowly. Normally I hate drugs but I am at the point of desperation for the pain that I opted to try this. I will take some anxiety over daily headache pain any day of the week. I have spent 3 years looking for a fundamental reason for my chronic pain, which has gotten progessively worse, and have tried, looked at and researched everything under the sun, but still I have no answer. I feel the drugs are just a cover up, but I have to try to break this cycle. I thought maybe it was working as I felt slightly better on days 2-4, but then yesterday the migraine pain came back and hangs around today. No sense in taking Midren, Fioricet, Meloxicam, etc., and they do nothing for me for the pain.

I am perimenopausal, but hormone therapy has not help in the least. Next week I see a laser spine surgeon for some diagnostic testing to see if my disk herniation and nerve compression in my next is contributing to these damn neck and headaches.

Karen from Arizona

# Thread: It's what they DIDN'T eat.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-06-02T22:55:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/post-29104>

When having a discussion with a doc this week - he was doing a little eye rolling at Jack's sites/ideas . . .going the whole 'it's not proven', and 'what ancient people at is all just speculation' and 'all those tests are just not needed'.

I had a great thought, after I left of course. . when there I sat there mutely.

There are things we CAN know, and a large bit of that is we know what ancient people - who give us our genetic heritage did NOT eat.

They did not eat highly processed this and that. . . they did not eat banannas in winter when glaciers were around. . .they did not have chemical sunscreen and sunglasses and on and on.

Take that!

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T23:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/post-29106>

They know what they ate from artifacts..and from how their teeth looked (grain eaters teeth ground down from the stone dust used in grinding grain).... So there's some more ammo for you!

But yep you know there was no Cheez wiz in paleo times. And we know there was no agriculture in paleo times. We were hunters and gatherers, again from the artifacts....

### indigogirl — 2012-06-02T23:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/post-29107>

It's very frustrating talking to almost everyone in the medical community about this.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-02T23:14:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/post-29108>

They know from coproliths.....we did not eat plants much. We ate protein and fat.

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T23:51:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-what-they-didnt-eat.1748/post-29121>

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-06-08T01:19:18+0300

> **KiwiLauren;30065 wrote:** Are you soaking to reduce phytic acid content or for some other reason? Do you think soaking is important if this is the only nut someone is eating (and just 1 per day)?

for me yes - it helps with digestion. plus I believe it minimizes the phytic acid. If you eat something everyday - which I try to do with brazil nuts for selenium - or often enough, I want to make it as safe as possible. I was just lazy buying them already soaked/dehydrated - totally something I could do myself  I bought mac nuts as well - but i'm going super easy on them

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-08T02:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30013>

> **janagram;29337 wrote: Where do you get your brazilnuts? I'd want to find a source that I could trust would be fresh, etc. plus should they be "organic"?**

**

Hi Janagram,

The best place to buy your Brazil nuts depends on where you live. You should look of nuts and seeds that are from this season's harvest, if at all possible.** You should buy your nuts in the shell if you want to store them at room temperature. If you want to buy shelled nuts and seeds, buy this year's harvest and store in the freezer. I know this sounds like a lot of trouble but nuts oils are very delicate and need careful storage.

Here is where I get my this season nuts. You can only buy once a year in the fall. I live in BC, Canada so if you live far away this source wont be any good for you. Here is a picture of how I store my nuts and seeds:

[http://www.ranchovignola.com/](http://http://www.ranchovignola.com/)

[http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-part-ii/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-part-ii/)

### nonchalant — 2012-06-08T02:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30019>

I store mine in the refrigerator, and they stay pretty fresh. Probably would be better if I put them in a vacuum.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T05:35:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30058>

> **nuttmegs17;30102 wrote:** for me yes - it helps with digestion. plus I believe it minimizes the phytic acid. If you eat something everyday - which I try to do with brazil nuts for selenium - or often enough, I want to make it as safe as possible.

Good point. I do not have a dehydrator. What is your process? Soak overnight and then dehydrate? Could I put them on a tray into a low temp oven for a longish while? Anyone have experience with this?

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T06:08:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30071>

that would work. Might not technically stay "raw" but they probably aren't in the first place due to the shelling process.

thanks, Caroline, glad to know about the nuts.

If you want to buy shelled nuts and seeds, buy this year's harvest and store in the freezer. I know this sounds like a lot of trouble but nuts oils are very delicate and need careful storage.
>

> KiwiLauren;30160 wrote:** Good point. I do not have a dehydrator. What is your process? Soak overnight and then dehydrate? Could I put them on a tray into a low temp oven for a longish while? Anyone have experience with this?

hehe, i dont have a process. i am so new at this. i bought the pre-soaked brazil bc at the time i wasnt even sure i liked brazil nuts (some people hate them) so i bought some already prepped ones to try. pretty tasty. i have to be careful with mac nuts. i literally take out like 5 at a time and close the bag. they.are.too.good.....

i believe the general process is soaking them in water over night and then laying them on a baking sheet in the oven at an extremely low temp for several hours - or using a dehydrater. which at this point i'm seriously considering buying (To make my own jerky - much cheaper right?!) although i'm also saving for a yogurt maker so not sure where i'm going to keep all this stuff in my city condo...

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-08T16:04:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30113>

> **KiwiLauren;30160 wrote:** Good point. I do not have a dehydrator. What is your process? Soak overnight and then dehydrate? Could I put them on a tray into a low temp oven for a longish while? Anyone have experience with this?

Actually, I find nuts and seeds hard to digest, raw or soaked. I do not consume nuts and seeds on a daily basis. (Coconut is okay for me on a daily basis but everyone is different.) I save nuts and seeds for special occasions like birthdays and holidays. This is the way I soak and dry my nuts and seeds:

1\. Put the nuts or seeds in a large bowl and cover with filtered water. Add some sea salt to the water.

2\. Soak overnight or at least 6-8 hours.

3\. In the morning, drain off the water and salt. You can add a bit of sea salt to the wet nuts if you want to eat "salty" nuts but I tend to use nuts in desserts and cakes, as a flour substitute, so I don't add extra salt.

4\. Spread the nuts on the dehydrator sheets and dehydrate for 24 hours or as long as needed. I try to keep the temperature below about 110F. If you do not have a dehydrator just use your oven on its lowest setting and leave the oven door open to reduce the heat. You will need a temperature probe to adjust the temperature. Change the size of the opening of the oven door.

5\. After I dry the nuts and seeds I store them in the freezer for later use.

Here are some nut recipes:

[http://eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-source-of-brazilnuts.1751/post-30199>

Nuts you buy should be refrigerated in the store you get them from already and pre-packaged. Don't ever get nuts from bulk bins. They are always rancid.

Once you clean up your diet for a while you will develop a very accurate taste for rancidity. Nuts from bulk bins are always rancid b/c of air exposure.

# Thread: Should we take a break?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-04T15:49:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29339>

I've been doing intermittant fasting for several years, and one of the foundations is to take a "mini break" one day of the week or to change up the schedule. For instance, if you're eating for 3 hours in the afternoon, one day per week eat all day, another day eat must breakfast, etc etc. The idea is that your body won't get stuck in a pattern.

I have been CTing everyday since March and had some remarkable results (n=1) in terms of how my body looks, weightloss, and energy. However, I have to confess - I took my first warm shower this morning - it has been roughly 3 months since the last one. Whether it is based in logic or not, I decided to take today off from CT and start up tomorrow.

So, using the principles for IF, should this extend to CT? Is it okay and possibly beneficial to take one day of rest?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-04T16:33:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29351>

its perfectly fine to take some time off from CT.....one of the great things is that you can miss some time and jump back on without any loss.

### Birdy — 2012-06-04T17:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29359>

I hadn't been doing as much ct since it is so hot, and this seems to be a season for heat. But I haven't been able to go for more than three days before jumping back in. I miss it! It looks like I will at least be CT-ing in the late afternoons when I can, at the hottest part of the day. The hose still puts out cold water, and it cools me off enough so that I am chilled for a couple of hours afterwards. This will take me through the hottest part of the day with no air conditioning, no problem. So far, a CT tub is the lowest cost, lowest tech way to cool off. It is more efficient than a misting system because I can re-use the water when I am finished.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-04T17:16:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29361>

I swim a lot and this is the easiest summertime way to CT beside the air conditioner in the car and house and the use of fans.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-04T17:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29364>

> **Jack Kruse;29461 wrote:** I swim a lot and this is the easiest summertime way to CT beside the air conditioner in the car and house and the use of fans.

I keep trying to have my parents lower the temp in their pool! But they are determined to keep it 85F or higher : (

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-05T03:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-take-a-break.1753/post-29458>

I wish there was something that would reduce the temp from 88 to 50-55....but I'm guessing if there is its probably extremely expensive...doubt it exists at all oh well. How low do temps have to be to get a benefit from the temp.

# Thread: New Study: 6g/d of Cod Protein (!!!) builds muscle and strips fat!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MonteD — 2012-06-04T20:35:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29400>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22647247](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22647247)

**A randomised study on the effects of fish protein supplement on glucose tolerance, lipids and body composition in overweight adults.

> The popularity of high-protein diets for weight reduction is immense. However, the potential benefits from altering the source of dietary protein rather than the amount is scarcely investigated. In the present study, we examined the effects of fish protein supplement on glucose and lipid metabolism in overweight adults. A total of thirty-four overweight adults were randomised to 8 weeks' supplementation with fish protein or placebo tablets (controls). The intake of fish protein supplement was 3 g/d for the first 4 weeks and 6 g/d for the last 4 weeks. In this study, 8 weeks of fish protein supplementation resulted in lower values of fasting glucose (P < 0Â·05), 2 h postprandial glucose (P < 0Â·05) and glucose-area under the curve (AUC) (five measurements over 2 h, P < 0Â·05) after fish protein supplementation compared to controls. Glucose-AUC was decreased after 8 weeks with fish protein supplement compared to baseline (P < 0Â·05), concomitant with increased 30 min and decreased 90 min and 2 h insulin C-peptide level (P < 0Â·05), and reduced LDL-cholesterol (P < 0Â·05). **Body muscle % was increased (P < 0Â·05) and body fat % was reduced (P < 0Â·05) after 4 weeks' supplementation.** Physical activity and energy and macronutrients intake did not change during the course of the study. In conclusion, short-term daily supplementation with a low dose of fish protein may have beneficial effects on blood levels of glucose and LDL-cholesterol as well as glucose tolerance and body composition in overweight adults. The long-term effects of fish protein supplementation is of interest in the context of using more fish as a protein source in the diet, and the effects of inclusion of fish in the diet of individuals with low glucose tolerance should be evaluated.

In depth review can be found here:

[http://suppversity.blogspot.com/2012/06/fishing-for-better-wheys-to-improve.html](http://http://suppversity.blogspot.com/2012/06/fishing-for-better-wheys-to-improve.html)

P.S. Found some excellent wild caught pink salmon at Krogers for 4 dollars a pound. I'm now eating 1 pound per day! MUCH** cheaper than organic grassfed beef which can easily top 10 dollars a pound (or more) for anything other than ground chuck...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-05T06:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29494>

I just baked 4 fillets!!! What great news!

My dog had major liver problems, her enzymes were like 50x elevated above normal, vet couldn't believe she didn't expire on the spot. She got SAM -e... And prescribed a diet of white ocean fish which mama (me) cooked for her. I ate a lot of cod for a while.... And her liver went back to normal, her fur grew back....she was like a puppy again (she's 10).

So to hear seafood is so important is not surprising when I know from my dog's experience, how to help her liver!! Now that I'm focused on my own, it's seafood for me.

I've also used coconut oil on her hotspots when she gets allergies and wants to lick. No steroids for her (I want her off that train). The coconut oil really soothes her and helps her scratched-all-to-hell spots go from almost bloody to pink and calm in hours. and doesn't hurt her if she licks it.

Between the 2 of us we are going to be optimal!! Cod for breakfast tomorrow then..... Protein is easier with a hunk of fish or meat than with eggs, I've found!!

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-05T07:12:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29504>

Nope didn't pull the query. Put in Cod and there are some great ones there. One grilled with Cajun spice and lemon pepper, then slathered Ina lemon butter sauce (of course we would use pastured butter..) ...... Baking in the oven is dead easy too.

My tip w fish I buy it in Individually sealed filets in a bag, you can just soak them in a bowl of cold water for a half hour to thaw fast while the oven is warming.... Spice EM up with spices of your choice and stick in the oven til white and opaque and flaky....add herbed butter and eat!! I can be eating within an hour of getting home this way. Fish to thaw, oven on, change clothes, read mail, greet family, pop in oven, get veggie going, read blog/forum until the timer goes off.....EAT! ( then ct..)

My foster pup did this once and it disappeared after having her on a grain free diet (I feed raw but she got table scraps and some dog treats as well). ### indigogirl — 2012-06-05T16:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29539>

Really easy cod recipe:

Put a little macadamia nut oil in a baking dish. Place cod in the baking dish and move it around to cover one side with the oil then turn it and cover the other side with oil. Sprinkle lemon pepper seasoning on top. Bake at 350 F for 20 minutes or until done.

The macadamia oil has a light, buttery taste.

### ealachan — 2012-06-05T17:02:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29541>

> **August;29597 wrote:** I would not mind having a couple of really good recipes for cooking cod!

Dust filets with a little salt and black pepper. Pan-fry in some coconut oil, about 2 minutes per side (unless the pieces are really thick, then go for three minutes per side). It's done when it flakes easily.

Cod is also good in fish curry.

### janellestg — 2012-06-05T17:37:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-6g-d-of-cod-protein-builds-muscle-and-strips-fat.1756/post-29549>

I pan fry in coconut oil. In a separate pan I sautÃ© green onions in butter add crabmeat and some zatarans liquid crabboil. I put this on top of cod. Delicious!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-04T20:49:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29401>

So I read that Raw Unfiltered Honey can be good for you and was wondering how much I should put if I'm preparing a gallon of white tea. I don't want to put too much but I want to taste it. This is something I would drink a bit of with a meal not just all the time.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-04T21:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29405>

I tried 2 tablespoons Honey and 2 tablespoons Grade B Organic Maple syrup and its not at all sweet. I don't want to make something that's bad though so I'll stick with that for now. Only drinking a cup with a meal. Anymore would probably be too much sugar even if it isn't the same as table sugar still not good.

### janagram — 2012-06-04T21:20:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29407>

I'd count it as a carb...

### Optimalbound — 2012-06-04T23:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29417>

From Wikipedia:

Typical honey analysis:

Fructose: 38.2%

Glucose: 31.3%

Maltose: 7.1%

Sucrose: 1.3%

Water: 17.2%

Higher sugars: 1.5%

Ash: 0.2%

Other/undetermined: 3.2%

Its glycemic index ranges from 31 to 78, depending on the variety.

​

Sounds like honey is something to avoid if one wants to lose weight.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-05T05:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29478>

Jack calls it bee puke!!! Just sayin'

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honey-in-tea.1757/post-29482>

I'm trying to make something that will work for me for some meals while being better for them since they don't follow Paleo/Primal atm.

At some point I want to see them doing more but they aren't at all receptive at the moment so any change I can make is a plus. If I can get them on Tea with a natural sweetner instead of crappy table sugar I'll take it as a step in the right direction.

# Thread: Mark's Daily Apple (MDA) Cold Challenge
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-05T19:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/post-29569>

So Mark Sisson admitted today in his blog [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-conduct-a-personal-experiment-cold-water-plunges/#axzz1wvxd7uAF](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-conduct-a-personal-experiment-cold-water-plunges/#axzz1wvxd7uAF) that he's been using CT for years.

"I’ve been incorporating them into my own routine for a few years now, and I’ve noticed a big difference. I think you guys will, too, and I think a community-wide push to systematically test the effect of cold plunges will give us a lot of data."

He is touting CT for fat loss, exercise recovery, and metabolic boost via BAT.

On the forums over there, I'm Otzi. I started a thread several months ago ([http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread50560.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread50560.html)) that people really got into, but then when the paleo-sphere turned on Dr. Kruse, people started using that forum to bash Jack and there is a spam-bot on MDA that picks little phrases and reposts them, usually the phrase it picked was "Jack Kruse is a crazy person". I thought that thread was awesome. Dr. Kruse and Ray Cronise both joined in and gave some good advice. There were hundreds of great links. But the bashing was terrible of both Kruse and Cronise.

So, I started a new thread [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread56730.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread56730.html) and asked people to leave the big names out of it. Just focus on CT, or CWT (Cold Water Therapy) as Sisson calls it. As soon as the bashing starts, that one will be abandoned as well.

I hope you guys aren't offended by the new thread. Even though Mark Sisson is putting a 'lose fat now!' spin on CT, there are a lot of readers there who do CT and many others who are interested.

Be forewarned: I'm 'stealing' a lot of stuff from Jack without giving credit. Hope you don't mind!

### hellojtm — 2012-06-06T02:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/post-29639>

I'm not a member of MDA forum or what not, but I did notice quite a while back that he tends to piggy back whatever is currently trending for his blog posts and it kind of bothered me. I guess I cynically assess it that he is jumping on the bandwagon to deliver his readers the idea as if he has had it all along. The "we already do that" camp. It comes with the commercial territory I suppose, he can't afford to lose people to other blogs because they perceive him as having holes in his understanding/approach.

I don't think it's necessary, his approach has worked for what he proposes it to work for and he should just stand by it. If he personally does something but doesn't advocate it I think it's a cop out to later say "well you all weren't ready for it" or like Anna posted:

> **MDA wrote:** "cold water immersion is sort of a hot topic these days around the Primal and ancestral health community. It’s on people’s minds, so they’re already primed to consider it. If I had just come out of the blue with a random charge – “try plunging your mostly-naked body into cold water!” – you might write me off. This way, it’s not such a foreign concept."

If he knew it was important then he should have primed it and it wouldn't be random... In fact Art DeVany in his book did mention doing exactly that and it didn't come off as a random charge because he understood some benefits and explained them clearly.

I'd feel better about Mr. Sisson's approach if he had simply said "hey I didn't think it was that big of a deal before but maybe there is more to it than I realized".

my 2c,

jesse

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-06T02:32:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/post-29643>

Mark is a good guy......he really has helped lots of people and at its core. People do not realize that Art DeVany gave Mark his blogging start with some guest posts. Art sticks to the science and stays out of the 'crapola' and drama in the blogosphere. Most of us are trying to help people....that is what we are all trying to do. Some of the people in this community just don't like others and are malicious. That is similar to life. We can choose to worry about them but then we lose focus about those we are trying to help. Some of us make mistakes as we go......and we have to deal with the consequences of it. But at the end of the day there will always be people who criticize what we do and how we say it and how they dont like the things we attempt. Change is part of life and evolution. It is also part of any business.

Change happens and it occurs when things are not working as we think they should for us.

Focus on the prize and help people.......things will fall into place if you do that 80% of the time.

### Qodesh — 2012-06-06T21:00:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/post-29752>

There are so many people sharing this information on different levels and with different interest groups.

The 'ownership rights' and info possessiveness diverts the focus and cause and creates conflicts.

I vote we just share info, ask questions and leave who 'owns" it out of the equation. It messes up the positive direction of help with petty attacks. The generous aren't interested in loyalty rights. I believe, like Jack says, they just want to help.

### JoeBranca — 2012-06-06T21:16:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/marks-daily-apple-mda-cold-challenge.1762/post-29757>

> **Qodesh;29854 wrote:** There are so many people sharing this information on different levels and with different interest groups.
>
> The 'ownership rights' and info possessiveness diverts the focus and cause and creates conflicts.
>
>
>
> I vote we just share info, ask questions and leave who 'owns" it out of the equation. It messes up the positive direction of help with petty attacks. The generous aren't interested in loyalty rights. I believe, like Jack says, they just want to help.

This is something that should be talked about. \*By no means\* am I comparing any particular individual to Bill Gates, but sometimes it's good to heed the warning of what Gates did. Something like an unconscious Bill Gates-syndrome can and does spill out into other sectors. Look at the early days of software development and when new and exciting things were coming about leading to self empowerment through personal computing. The early openness of information sharing was swallowed up by aggressive commerce moves (Steal, claim, protect: the history of Microsoft in a nutshell); which hopefully no one in this community falls into doing.

This blog post about Steve Jobs happens to include the author's thoughts on Gates by contrast (you can skip the messy historical details and go to The Greatness section, to read his explanation of Gates' sins); it's worth considering how much the business of business has changed for the worst insofar as MS' power and influence has been wielded.

[http://blog.tedgoranson.com/personal/steve\_jobs\_and\_bill\_gate.html](http://http://blog.tedgoranson.com/personal/steve_jobs_and_bill_gate.html)

# Thread: Just released BAT article/study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-released-bat-article-study.1763/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-05T21:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-released-bat-article-study.1763/post-29590>

Not that this is news to any of us, but good controlled study.

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-good-fat-cold-ephedrine.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-good-fat-cold-ephedrine.html)

Researchers at Joslin Diabetes Center have shown that while a type of "good" fat found in the body can be activated by cold temperatures, it is not able to be activated by the drug ephedrine.

The finding, published in today's issue of PNAS USA Early Edition, may lead to drugs or other methods aimed at activating the good fat, known as brown fat. When activated, brown fat burns calories and can help in the battle against obesity.

"We propose that agents that work similarly to cold in activating brown fat specifically can provide promising approaches to fighting obesity while minimizing other side effects," said Aaron Cypess, M.D., Ph.D., an assistant investigator and staff physician at Joslin and lead author of the paper.

"At the same time, we now know that ephedrine is not the way to do it," he added.

Brown fat is found in humans naturally and consumes calories to generate heat. Prior studies had shown that brown fat can be activated by cold exposure in a process called non-shivering thermogenesis.

Researchers have been working for years to find ways to activate brown fat.

Ephedrine, a decongestant and bronchodilator, has been used as a weight loss drug because it increases the number of calories burned. However, there are side effects.

In this study, the Joslin team tested 10 study subjects in three ways. They were each separately given injections of ephedrine, given injections of saline as a control, and made to wear "cooling vests" that had water cooled to 57 degrees pumped into them. After each intervention, the brown fat activity was measured using PET/CT scans.

The researchers found that brown fat activity was the same following both the ephedrine and saline injections. However, after the subjects wore the cooling vests for two hours, their brown fat activity was stimulated significantly.

Both interventions â€” ephedrine injections and the wearing of the cooling vests â€” did result in the same number of calories being burned, Dr. Cypess noted.

"But we found that ephedrine was not using brown fat to do it," he said. "This is the first time it has been found that ephedrine does not turn on brown fat."

Both interventions had other effects on the sympathetic nervous system -- which activates the fight or flight response -- such as increased blood pressure, but those associated with brown fat activation were fewer, the study showed.

"Mild cold exposure stimulates (brown fat) energy expenditure with fewer other systemic effects, suggesting that cold activates specific sympathetic pathways," the paper concludes. "Agents that mimic cold activation of (brown fat) could provide a promising approach to treating obesity while minimizing systemic effects."

As a result of the findings, drug companies may find it easier to come up with agents that stimulate brown fat to help people lose weight, Dr. Cypess said.

One method may be simply to design cooling vests that people can wear to help them lose weight. A future study will have subjects wear the vests for several weeks to see what happens, Dr. Cypess said.

"Will they get the same health benefits they would have seen with several weeks of exercise? That's the billion dollar question."

The findings should also be of interest to heart researchers interested in the mechanisms of activation of the sympathetic nervous system, he added.

Provided by Joslin Diabetes Center

### PaleoDentist — 2012-06-06T20:09:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-released-bat-article-study.1763/post-29747>

### AKMan — 2012-06-06T20:24:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-released-bat-article-study.1763/post-29748>

> **PaleoDentist;29849 wrote:** Tim Ferris seem to think direct application to BAT areas is the way to go, but I wonder if cold exposure to other body areas stimulate BAT as well.

I think the placement of the cold is irrelevant as cold receptors are in the skin, not the BAT or WAT. Remember our talks earlier about TRPM8? TRPM8 is an ion channel, upon activation it allows the entry of Na+ (sodium) and Ca2+ (calcium) ions to the cell that leads to depolarization and the generation of an action potential. This eventually leads to the feeling of cold.

TRPM8 is a protein encoded with the TRPM8 gene, much like the uncoupling proteins (UCP). UCP-1 is the protein that is activated in BAT to bypass the kreb's cycle and burn fat as heat.

I have never read anything that implies that BAT has cold sensors that would activate anything, BAT is the end-product.

# Thread: Help me with figure out my blood sugar readings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-06-06T01:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29633>

I'm hoping for some insights into the blood sugar readings I've been taking since getting a glucometer. First the background info:

I am 10 pounds over my "ideal" (college, pre-children) weight. My BMI is in the low 20s range.

I have hashimotos and pretty much no more functioning thyroid gland, and struggle with adrenal issues

I have low B12 (450) and I have done 2 methyl B12 shots this past week. I think my potassium levels are currently low due to the B12 injection.

I eat mostly low carb, pretty ketogenic. No eggs, nightshades, nuts, dairy, gluten, etc - meats, fish and veggies, coconut oil, occasional fruit and lindt 90%

I've been Leptin Resetting for 10 weeks now, and still think I'm resistant (always feel like eating). I've been CTing almost daily (30 minutes, sometimes twice) for 7-8 weeks.

My fasting morning blood sugars have been in the low 90s on the days I've tested. During the day I generally see 100ish, maybe 120ish after a meal.

Today I ate "a bunch" of watermelon, and tested my blood sugar 30 minutes later. I was horrified to see 167. I waited another 15 minutes and got 176!! At 65 minutes out, it was down to 116. And I haven't tested it since then. Is this a "normal" response? That seems like a really, really big spike to me. Am I glucose impaired/insulin resistant? How do I deal with this, as well as get my am blood sugar down?

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-06T01:41:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29635>

You are officially diabetic if you didn't know this already. Do you take synthroid or armour? If not you probably should. Getting thyroid meds right will go a long way to stabilizing blood sugar. This is only my personal experience. You may want to consider Metformin also.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-06T03:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29658>

Thanks for answering...

I just wrote out a whole response, and then accidentally deleted it - grrrr!

In a nutshell:

Yes, I take Armour thyroid 2-3 grains. Trying to get to 3 grains as 2 isn't enough.

Started the methyl B12 shots, which I think reduced serum potassium levels for reasons I don't understand. I have some of the symptoms, including tingly, weird feeling hands the day after the shots, which I didn't experience prior to the shots (so I don't attribute it to low B12)

Does lower potassium result in impaired insulin secretion, and/or absorption and/or glucose uptake?

I was going to supplement potassium, but the quantity I could buy at the store was only minimal amounts, and didn't seem worthwhile. I think i will buy them and take 10 or so at a time.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-06T03:50:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29659>

You can get potassium chloride, also known as table salt substitute, in higher dosages. Now is the brand I use.

### Ray — 2012-06-06T21:16:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29758>

If you have been very low carb for 10 weeks, an exposure to dietary sugar resulting in blood glucose readings of 170 does NOT mean you are diabetic. Your body has shifted to sparing glucose, and you whacked yourself with a huge bolus of sugar, which your body was not immediately ready to deal with. I think your glucose readings are fairly typical for someone who has been low carb for a while. Your A1C would give you a better idea of what is happening.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T01:01:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29805>

> **Dali Dula;29737 wrote:** You are officially diabetic if you didn't know this already. Do you take synthroid or armour? If not you probably should. Getting thyroid meds right will go a long way to stabilizing blood sugar. This is only my personal experience. You may want to consider Metformin also.

Dali good things will come to you for helping others......great thing here

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-07T19:56:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29939>

I've been reading around, trying to figure out what is a "normal" blood sugar response, and what is "optimal."

I figure I probably took in about 60 grams of carbs, without anything else (I got carried away while cutting up the watermelon for my kids). This site ([http://www.phlaunt.com/diabetes/14046889.php](http://http://www.phlaunt.com/diabetes/14046889.php)) says the following:

"If your blood sugar reading remained under 100 mg/dl (5.6 mmol/L) at the one hour test and all the later tests, you have completely normal blood sugar and can stop worrying about it.

If your blood sugar did not reach 140 mg/dl (7.7 mmol/L) an hour after taking a large dose of carbohydrates and if it was below 120 mg/dl (6.7 mmol/L) two hours after you ate the large dose of carbohydrate, most health authorities would also say that you are normal. These numbers, 140 mg/dl at 1 hour and 120 mg/dl at two hours after a meal are what Joslin Diabetes Clinic of Harvard Medical School defines as upward limit of "normal." "

So for me, at the one hour mark, I was down to 116. Plus I have been very low carb for the past several months, which would make the sugar levels go a little higher. I'm not sure if the site I referenced though is giving good information. It seems good. How do I lower my blood sugar levels when I am allready pretty much ketogenic (minus watermelon incident), and doing the CT? I took insinase (by metagenics) a few months ago, and i'm considering going back to it again. However, if it's just a "bandaid" solution, then it's not worth it, given my sugars are usually in the 90s, low 100s briefly after eating.

The thyroid part is a big challenge for me. I think I have insufficient hormone replacement, and now my adrenals are tanking (low blood pressure, high heart rate, orthostatic hypotension, feeling dizzy, etc). I am going to attempt to raise the dose, and see how it goes. This morning I had to take hydrocrotisone though, because the blood pressure/heart rate/bad feeling was too intense. I'd really like to solve this puzzle without the HC...

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-07T20:59:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-29954>

BKW

here's a good place to start [http://blog.trackyourplaque.com/2011/08/one-hour-blood-sugar-key-to-carbohydrate-control-and-reversing-diabetes.html](http://http://blog.trackyourplaque.com/2011/08/one-hour-blood-sugar-key-to-carbohydrate-control-and-reversing-diabetes.html)

when I first started tracking my BG pre/post meal - I try to keep my spike no more than 10 across the hour. so if my pre is 89 - then my post should be 89 at the 1hr point ... on the monster thread - Jack mentioned checking your BG at 15min intervals until it reaches the original #

I usually do this 1-2x/month...but I think I'm going to start doing this weekly, as my FBG is still a bit high, as is my HbA1C, and fasting insulin. I might discover there's something that's spiking me across the day....

as for the Keto and CT combination...depending on how much stuff you need to fix - it may not fix it all at once. The body will take those low hanging fruit, and knock them off 1st...

as for the thyroid, adrenals and hormones. Continue working the LR and CT - some the craziness could be the result of the CT. Additionally, do you have a pcp who can help you w/the thyroid/adrenals/hormones????

### MonteD — 2012-06-14T08:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31089>

> **bigknitwit;29735 wrote:
>
>
>
> My fasting morning blood sugars have been in the low 90s on the days I've tested. During the day I generally see 100ish, maybe 120ish after a meal.
>
>
>
> Today I ate "a bunch" of watermelon, and tested my blood sugar 30 minutes later. I was horrified to see 167. I waited another 15 minutes and got 176!! At 65 minutes out, it was down to 116. And I haven't tested it since then. Is this a "normal" response? That seems like a really, really big spike to me. Am I glucose impaired/insulin resistant? How do I deal with this, as well as get my am blood sugar down?
>
>
> Dali Dula;29737 wrote:** You are officially diabetic if you didn't know this already.

Your numbers could definitely be improved upon that is for sure. But I wouldn't go so far as to say you are "officially" diabetic. "Officially" diabetic would mean your fasting blood sugar levels would need to be over 126. Pre-diabetic would mean fasting between 110 and 126. Below 90 is considered optimal.

The "watermelon test" is less helpful. You should do a home OGTT. Pure glucose gels and tablets are easy to find at any drugstore.

[http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16](http://http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16)

I may be woefully misinformed but you don't appear to be even close to a full blown diabetic....

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-14T14:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31092>

I was incorrect that you are officially diabetic. I guess i wish I had had a better understanding of diabetes when I was prediabetic and taken steps to deal with it. Although you may not be classified as diabetic with your current numbers please remain vigilant so as not to become diabetic. When I was diagnosed I had a BG of 1100. The chart above is interesting and most of the time I am within normal with no meds and paleo. A spike of 176 may not put you in the official diabetic zone but it is not a good thing. I have read that organ damage begins at 140. I still see spikes in this neighborhood and I don't like it Remain vigilant. Keep testing. Keep tweaking. Good luck.

### kathylu — 2012-06-14T17:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31122>

Interesting thread. I am concerned with this issue too. Since going low carb paleo (1 year) and leptin rx since 9/11, my FBG hovers around 110. However, my post prandial BG (after a meal) hardly ever exceeds 130 and my HbA1C is 5.4. I know 5.4 is not ideal, but it's not too bad either. However, no matter what I do, my FBG stubbornly remains around 110. I don't recall ever getting a reading below 98 in the two years I have been tracking blood glucose. So, I don't ever see dips down to 70's and 80's like others have reported (even after CT). Since I am following Jack's recommendations to the letter, the only other thing I could do is take drugs (metformin) I suppose. However, I'm taking a wait and see approach. Maybe it takes more than 9 months on the leptin rx to see the results others are reporting.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T18:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31136>

In my world an A1c of 5.4 with FBG of 110 is pretty bad. A high of 130 isn't something I'm striving for either. Not ever seeing dips under 98.... that would spell a problem for me. I've entirely abandoned CW ranges in this area.... I want it under 85 pretty much all the time.

Dr K somewhere explained what the stubborn high FBG means.... something with the liver iirc. I'd get at least a fasting insulin to get an idea of what's going on with your pancreas.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-14T18:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31141>

Taking HC will raise blood sugar.

Consider taking a 1/4 t of sea salt dissolved in water upon awaking and before bed to help your low blood pressure and adrenal glands. Add vitamin C to support adrenals.

hth

### kathylu — 2012-06-14T22:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31225>

> **Souldanzer;31240 wrote:** In my world an A1c of 5.4 with FBG of 110 is pretty bad. A high of 130 isn't something I'm striving for either. Not ever seeing dips under 98.... that would spell a problem for me. I've entirely abandoned CW ranges in this area.... I want it under 85 pretty much all the time.
>
>
>
> Dr K somewhere explained what the stubborn high FBG means.... something with the liver iirc. I'd get at least a fasting insulin to get an idea of what's going on with your pancreas.

I do consider it a problem, though my doc says it's nothing to worry about. She thinks it is "physiological insulin resistance" induced by ketones (specifically beta hydroxybutarate) from long-time low carbing. Supposedly that's not harmful. I give her some credit for actually understanding that this phenomenon occurs!

My fasting insulin is always less than 2...that indicates that I am not pre-diabetic. I asked Jack about this some time ago and he basically said to be patient, that it takes the liver a long time to "come around" sometimes...

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T22:51:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31234>

I thought that was only true if your A1c is in good shape with only the FBG going high in the AM - not if your average BGs are high and you have your BG spike after a low carb meal? I remember that post from Dr K and that it has something to do with the liver not being up to speed. Still.... it means something isn't right yet.

If a CW doc tells me not to worry about something, I tend to do so.... I guess you'd know if she's correct if this was low-carb induced and your had better BGs before that.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-14T23:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31245>

Chris Kresser had a good series about insulin & diabetes and talks about why HA1c may not be a good indicator, as it depends on how long-lived the red blood cells are. Dr. K said my somewhat elevated FBG is probably due to my poor sleep/cortisol issues.

### OptimalChick — 2012-06-15T16:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31323>

Hi,

I had this concern on another thread and Dr. Jack replied "Liver is not optimal is the short answer....it takes time to control PEPCK." What I have found about PEPCK from Wiki is here [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phosphoenolpyruvate\_carboxykinase](http://https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phosphoenolpyruvate_carboxykinase)

### OptimalChick — 2012-06-15T16:37:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31325>

by the way my glucose is up even more in the morning .. today is was 115 but I do have fat to burn and lose so that is what I hope is also happening during sleep at night

### kathylu — 2012-06-15T17:09:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31335>

> **OptimalChick;31428 wrote:** Hi,
>
> I had this concern on another thread and Dr. Jack replied "Liver is not optimal is the short answer....it takes time to control PEPCK." What I have found about PEPCK from Wiki is here [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phosphoenolpyruvate\_carboxykinase](http://https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phosphoenolpyruvate_carboxykinase)

That's essentially the same thing Jack told me. To stay on the program and be patient because liver PEPCK is not optimal yet. It makes sense, because my morning cortisol is quite high, which would stimulate PEPCK production which will then ramp up gluconeogenesis giving the high FBG numbers.

Oh well, I guess I just want optimal today....

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-16T00:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31430>

My doctor at the Women to Women clinic in Maine had suggested I take a supplement called Insinase, by metagenics, to help control blood sugar. She said it was the herbal equivalent of metformin. At the time, my fasting insulin was elevated even though my fbg was in the low 90s, she said it meant I was insulin resistant and having blood sugar swings. She was right, as I have since confirmed with a glucose meter. I took the insinase for a month, and noticed my fbg down even at 77 (before I had the meter - I happened to have lab work done that included it), so it must have been helping with the spikes. I have been leptin rxing since then, hoping to fix myself. I recently bought another round of insinase (not cheap!), for times when I may want to take the kids strawberry picking, and other slight deviations off the plan still paleo. Not on an empty stomach, and I'll test to see how it affects my blood sugar.

She also said, like Jack has said, that in my case the insulin resistance is likely due to HPA axis dysfunction. I've increased my Armour dose (which improved the low blood pressure, high heart rate), and started taking DHEA, and will likely start pregnenelone in a few weeks after adapting to DHEA. Maybe with these adjustments, the insulin resistance will improve... I have dropped 15 pounds though, in 3.5 months, doing the leptin rx without eggs/nuts/dairy. Just meat/fish/veggies/occasional small fruit serving.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-19T20:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-32198>

I'm wondering if the "normal" range on this chart is optimal.... A spike as high as 150, an hour after eating a high carb meal is what this chart calls the edge of normal, and then peacefully back down to between 80-100. My blood sugar rarely seems to drop below 95ish, even going without eating from 5pm (low carb food) until the following morning. It's like my liver wants to maintain roughly 100, and can't account for incoming sugar very well. I do not see hypoglycemic dips, but I do see dips after CTing, especially about 30 minutes after getting out (still shivering, so still burning through sugar I suppose - down to 90, 80, once 70). I guess, like someone else said, this points to leptin resistance at the liver...?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-06T05:16:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-29675>

I just found out I have H. Pylori from the Metametrix GI Effects Test (stool). My doc wanted to get the breath test to make sure and then she said we can treat with antibiotics. This doc usually tries to treat with supplements and natural ways but not for this. I found out the breath test is over $300 so that is out for now.

Does anyone have any natural way to treat this that will eradicate the bacteria?

Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-06T06:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-29686>

Oh my gosh! I've been researching bismuth and there is so much info. I'm assuming CBS, colloidal bismuth subcitrate, is the best choice for this. Although this seems fairly innocuous (I could be totally wrong on that) I'm guessing this is by script only. Now research on where to find this.

Any ideas?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-06T14:52:11+0300

Thanks for all the help Dr. K!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-12T04:41:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-30646>

just a thought, don't know really, but would iodine be helpful?

From Iodine4health.com Dr. Derry:"Iodine appears to have several more roles in the body. Iodine protects against abnormal growth of bacteria in the stomach (helicobacter pylori is the most clinically significant.) Iodine can coat incoming allergic proteins to make them non-allergic, which likely also applies to the internal equivalent called autoimmune disease. Iodine binds softly to the double and triple bond of lipids to protect these bonds while they are being transported to synaptic sites in the brain and blood vessels of the body. As well iodine in the stomach deactivates all biological and most chemical poisons.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-12T05:42:04+0300

Incorky that is interesting you brought that up. I took large amounts of iodine (Ioderal) a couple of years ago and broke out into a rash after a year of working my way up to the larger amounts (50 mg). Every time I have tried to take the pills I get the rash back even in very small amounts.

I have been rinsing my mouth out with a potent iodine rinse of Nascent Iodine and water (about 50/50) to keep the dental surgery incision area clean since I'm not on antibiotics. I'm quite tempted to try the Nascent I and see what would happen. If iodine protects against H. Pylori I wonder if if would also eradicate????? Thanks for the info.

### Martha Ray — 2022-04-08T20:10:30+0300

### JanSz — 2022-04-08T22:52:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-311906>

> [Martha Ray said:](/goto/post?id=311904)
>
> **Pepto Bismol** after a meal of bratwurst and cabbage produced **very bad acid reflux.
> Is there something that might indicate?** Too much or not enough acid?

Instead of Pepto-Bismol try Potassium bicarbonate.
Try at first one flat teaspoon.
When it helps, but not fully, increase dose, do not use more than 5 teaspoons daily (unless you did the test).
It changed my life.
I use it daily now. One teaspoon in the morning.
I am buying it over internet.

You may want to do the (Potassium RBC) test first, but not really necessary.

When it helps, send good thoughts to [@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/)

Dh has always been a big pasta/rice guy. His grain consumption has dropped off since I went primal and now that I've been pushing 100% paleo he eats bkfst cereals for bkfst every am, he'll eat sandwiches & pizza at lunch & either rice or quinoa at home 3xweek w/dinner

As for labs - dh hasnt been to the doctor since before we got married (~5yr ago) his weigh is about the same ~165/170 he's 5'10" he's very lean/lanky. His back has been bothering him off/on since 2008 but his bitching moaning whining complaining was out of control last fall so I talked the chiro into evaluating him for free, knowing dh wants to avoid surgery dh went to see the chiro the chiro took one look at the X-rays and sent him for an MRI ( this was in dec 2011. He didn't get the MRI until the end of Feb. chiro sent him to a neurosurgeon b/c he didn't like what he saw. Took DH til may to make the apt. He's already got a 2fused vertabare in his neck (this was done right out of college)

Dh also has crappy sleep. On the wkends he stays up and watches the tv shoes he's dvr'd for the week. He comes to bed btwn 3-5am regularly I've actually been up with the boy at 5am when he's coming to bed. He usually falls asleep while reading the kids their bedtime story and I have a hard time getting him up to help w/getting things ready for the next day

Right now I think he's in denial about his health and he's afraid of knowing what's really going on. I'm going to pull out the Dzugan optimal hormone levels for men and have him take a look at them. He's got to want to change but at this rate he's not going to be able to actively participate as the kids get older and become more athletic.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T01:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-dhs-labs.1770/post-30007>

Shijin, I know it's hard to understand how someone would just ignore their health like that. I think you pointed out something very important, and that is that he is afraid of the information a doctor's visit and all these tests might bring.

If the fear is the reason he's choosing denial/avoidance then "kicking his behind into action" and "scaring him into action" is not a good approach in my opinion. I was in exactly that place until very recently and really what happens is that you are frozen into non-action, it's a flight/fight/freeze reaction and somehow he is stuck in freeze. I was stuck in freeze b/c every time I sought help I was told that there was nothing to be done about xxxx. I just had to learn to live with every complaint I had and every breakdown of my body. If there is no help then why know about it? So you can live in fear of what you now know will happen sooner or later?

You and I, and most other people on here, appreciate the power that knowledge gives (thank you Lyndra ). We are learning how to figure out what will help us to get better. That might not be your hubby's perspective at this point in time. I know it wasn't mine and I went to great lengths to avoid being presented with bad news.

I wasn't really able to turn this around until I found Dr K's blog. With renewed hope that there might be something to fix and safe, I got a bunch of things tested. But the only reason I was able to break through the freeze into fight was b/c fight had become attractive with outlook of success. I know I would never have gotten my recent MRI had it not be for Dr K being so available - I was so scared. I would have traveled to TN if needed... and I live right next to Boulder Neurosurgical Associates.

I think what he might need is for you to approach this with gentleness instead of pushing. Lots of affirmation of support no matter what the outcome will be. He might need lots of information right now about all the thing that \*can\* be done to make his life better or prevent it from getting worse. He needs to understand that at this point in time knowledge does happen to be power and not being stuck with what is. He needs to know that you will be by his side no matter if he can ride a bike in a few years or happens to be in a wheelchair. That is the number one thing I need to hear from my loved ones right now.... Every.Single.Day.

Now, if he isn't taking action b/c he thinks things are just dandy, that's a different story.

As always, YMMV

### Lyndra — 2012-06-08T05:18:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-dhs-labs.1770/post-30047>

Only you know what your husband will respond to in terms of information, cajoling, etc. I was recently reluctant to order up a 23andme, not sure I wanted to know what my genes might say about my future. However, with recent tests showing BG creeping up, I decided that knowing is far better than not, because then I can choose to do something (or choose not to do something) - but at least have a choice. I know for myself, I have to be in the right place and time before I can make that decision to get information and act on it and to someone who is highly action-oriented, I'm sure my behavior would be beyond irritating. I know you'll use all your knowledge to help him work through this.

366 means you better retest in the AM and be prepared for some bad news.........while testing mid day is a bit inaccurate for men, 366 is awfully low even at that time for a guy your age.........just realize that testosterone correlates with heart disease risk in men......and if you have a low T and a high E2.......that is the cardiac death march.......that slowly happens to most American men silently. Do not let this stand.

At LabCorp do these blood tests at 7AM (women on day 3-4 of cycle, or anytime when postmenapausal)

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry Test Number: 140707

2 ------- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) − Comprehensive Screen, Test Nr: 500175

3 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

10 ------- Metabolic Panel (14), Comprehensive

11 ------- Complete Blood Count (CBC) With Differential and Platelet

12 ------- NMR LipoProfileÂ®

13 ------- Iron, Total

14 ------- Ferritin

15 ------- Phosphorus, Serum

16 ------- Uric Acid, Serum

17 ------- Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)\*

18 ------- CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

19 ------- Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

20 ------- RDW

21 ------- Homocysteine, cardio

22 ------- Insulin and C-Peptide, Serum

23 ------- HgA1c

24 ------- C-Reactive Protein (CRP), High Sensitivity (Cardiac Risk Assessment)

25 ------- Thyroid-stimulating Hormone (TSH), T4, FT4, T3, FT3, RT3

26 ------- Lab pls calculate ratios, (Free T3 Ã· RT3) and (T3 Ã· RT3)

27 ------- Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies

28 ------- Thyroid Antithyroglobulin Antibody

29 ------- prolactin

30 ------- IGF-1

31 ------- IGFBP3

\-------------------------------

\===========================

contradicts dr Kruse's advice to eat lots of krill oil and fish oil.

# Thread: Leptin, Hunger,  Cold
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-06T23:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29789>

I have been talking about this on Mark's daily Apple and wanted to get ya'alls opinion.

Studies like this one, [http://ukpmc.ac.uk/abstract/MED/10490779/reload=0;jsessionid=nk8EXhvifHEGUw6IXikW.0](http://http://ukpmc.ac.uk/abstract/MED/10490779/reload=0;jsessionid=nk8EXhvifHEGUw6IXikW.0) ,and Jack has been saying all along, that cold kills hunger.

In this study, obese and non-obese women were studied for leptin sensitivity and other autonomic nervous system functions when warm and exposed to cold. The obese women showed less effect from the cold exposure than non-obese women.

My conclusion is that the leptin resistance at the receptor level is interfering with the hunger signals that should be turned off when cold.

Would a good test for LR be that you are hungry when cold?

Another studied showed similar results in mice:

"Leptin receptor gene expression has been measured in arcuate and ventromedial hypothalamic nuclei. Receptor mRNA in both hypothalamic areas was higher in obese mice than in lean littermates. Twice daily leptin administration for 7 days profoundly affected food intake, reduced leptin receptor mRNA in the arcuate nucleus, and had a similar effect on neuropeptide Y gene expression. A single leptin injection was ineffective. Exposure of lean mice to cold for 24 h caused an induction of leptin receptor and NPY mRNA which was normalized when animals were returned to the warm. Regulation of receptor gene expression may be an important component in the reading of the leptin signal."

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T00:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29798>

Can't be.......I'm just a nut job.

### AKMan — 2012-06-07T00:56:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29804>

> **Jack Kruse;29900 wrote:** Can't be.......I'm just a nut job.

Yes you are!

So, I'm thinking when people ask, "Am I Leptin resistant?" In addition to the mirror test, you could ask if they are hungry when cold.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T01:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29815>

Hunger tells you a lot about leptin levels......a ton in fact. I think the people like Robb Wolf who says CT is hormetic will be hard pressed to explain why cold destroys hunger in obese folks when nothing else does. The other thing it does I have not really talked about publicaly......is in surgery patients that I use CT in that they just do not eat......yet they do amazingly well in recovery. It is like a free pass to IF'ing leangains style. With time we will see if the trainer or the surgeon is right.

### AKMan — 2012-06-07T02:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29821>

> **August;29911 wrote:** So, Hungry (hungrier?) when cold indicates leptin resistance . . . and Not Hungry when cold indicates leptin sensitivity, right?
>
>
>
> (and not a nut job. . . .also right?)

I think you are right...about the hunger and cold. The studies, and human experiences, show when one is leptin sensitive, in extreme cold you should have no hunger as the leptin receptors are taken out of the equation. If leptin resistant, you will still be hungry in extreme cold because the leptin receptors are compromised to begin with.

This shouldn't be confused with the propensity to eat more in cold weather, however. Exposure to cooler temperatures long-term lower leptin levels and actually increase hunger. This is to compensate for the increased demand on metabolism in winter. Staying warm takes more calories.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-07T14:52:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29874>

> **AKMan;29906 wrote:** Yes you are!
>
>
>
> So, I'm thinking when people ask, "Am I Leptin resistant?" In addition to the mirror test, you could ask if they are hungry when cold.

Hungry when cold or cold when hungry? I was cold when I would get hungry a while back but not hungry when I was cold. Interesting that you brought this up. I think Jack made a comment about it after reading something I wrote it and said that's not good. I sort of forgot all about it. Now I will have to make sure that isn't the case. Pretty sure not and this change happended after I started CT'ing.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T15:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29879>

When your in cold your hunger and your fat should go down.......that is the normal response in humans......when it does not it means something is out of order in you.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-07T20:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29942>

> **Jack Kruse;29981 wrote:** When your in cold your hunger and your fat should go down.......that is the normal response in humans......when it does not it means something is out of order in you.

You're talking about hormones, right? I'm curious what are the more typical "out of order" hormone profiles, or degenerative conditions, that result in the inverse response to cold exposure (increased hunger/increased fat). And then, will continued cold exposure eventually turn the boat around, or is the cold in this kind of scenario making things "worse?"

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-07T20:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29947>

I believe any type of hormonal imbalance will cause some weird eating habits. I had/have estrogen dominance and for 2-1/2 months the CTing (cold baths) made me hungrier and gain 5#. I was spilling out lots of the excess estro and had symptoms to show for it. But now it seems to have settled down and I actually have lost all the weight I gained. I actually don't think my ED is completely balanced but it is getting closer.

### janagram — 2012-06-09T04:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-30226>

I remember being a kid and after a day in the ocean or pool I would be FAMISHED!!!! dunno if that means anything. My own kids were too!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-07T04:59:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-29841>

Just picking anyone and everyone's brain for opinions on this - my mom has a prolapsed uterus and her gyn is suggesting hysterectomy. She had surgery for a prolapsed bladder last year. My mom has kind of low pain tolerance.

Anyone with suggestions would be appreciated.

thanks!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-07T05:29:52+0300
