### Melinda — 2012-06-07T07:21:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-29862>

I wonder why they didn't address the uterus at the same time as the bladder. I believe there are ways to tighten up all the internal structures somewhat. I hope she can keep her organ...it's the most natural way.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-07T16:02:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-29885>

Hi Melinda! Well, I don't know who did the bladder surgery, prob not gyn...that's the prob with CW med, one body part per doctor!

The doc told her if she does have the hysterectomy, then other organs could start falling out. So, I guess the better question is, why is stuff falling out of her body and what can she do to keep them in????

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-07T19:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-29938>

Looks like low estrogen has a lot to do with prolapsing stuff. Plus I know my mom has osteoporosis. I've asked her to get copies of her bloodwork and bring them to me when she visits. She needs a hack for sure

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-15T21:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31394>

Bumping this up ... Anyone have any alternatives to hysterectomy for prolapsed uterus? My mom is 60. She is very uncomfortable and is probably gonna have surgery and her doc told her that they will remove her remaining ovary because "you don't get your period any more so what do you need it for?" and she will not take bhrt because when she did she got a brown spot on her arm. Yes, I know... And for some crazy reason no pessary seems to fit. She's like goldilocks or something

### indigogirl — 2012-06-15T23:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31407>

At the very least she should keep her ovary and her cervix. They wanted to take mine, too, 11 years ago and I wouldn't let them ("What do you need your ovaries for? You'll never have to get another pap smear if we take them out too."). Jeesh..are you guys idiots?

The cervix is involved in orgasmic function. Studies have shown women who have their cervixes removed don't orgasm as well. I don't have the study...

My uterus wasn't prolapsed...just a fibroid the size of a grapefruit and menopause was not in sight. Wish I knew then more of what I know now about estrogen dominance.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T00:36:19+0300

There are exercise for the pelvic rotators and a program called beyond kegals. It is supposed to stop incontinence and prop things back up.

### janagram — 2012-06-16T01:10:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31438>

if she has her uterus removed, other organs are NOT going to start falling out!! Pleeese!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-16T01:14:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31441>

Im not a fan of organ removal.......exercises and hormone replacement are better. She needs a good OB/GYN and urology team to get all the options before organ removal. Organ remove is the CW advice 95% will give. Search for the others because it is worth it.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T01:23:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31446>

[http://www.modernmedicine.com/modernmedicine/Modern+Medicine+Now/Beyond-Kegels-When-do-gynecologic-problems-call-fo/ArticleStandard/Article/detail/751427](http://http://www.modernmedicine.com/modernmedicine/Modern+Medicine+Now/Beyond-Kegels-When-do-gynecologic-problems-call-fo/ArticleStandard/Article/detail/751427)

Beyond Kegels: When do gynecologic problems call for physical therapy?Many gynecologic problems require multidisciplinary treatment. Physical therapy may play a significant treatment role in achieving successful outcomes and improving patients' quality of life.

> Most gynecologic problems require multidisciplinary treatment. An often-overlooked modality is formalized physical therapy provided by individuals trained in the treatment of gynecologic problems.
>
>
>
> Although physical therapy for gynecologic ailments is not typically taught in medical school, physical therapists are able to offer new approaches to many gynecologic problems that are traditionally difficult to treat, including pelvic pain and disorders of the pelvic floor. This article reviews common gynecologic disorders for which referral for physical therapy should be considered and can make a difference.
>
>
>
> The role of physical therapy
>
>
>
> Physical therapy is commonly employed for rehabilitation after acute or chronic musculoskeletal injury. Pelvic muscles can require the same type of rehabilitation. The techniques employed by physical therapists for pelvic pain or dysfunction include treatment modalities similar to those for back pain or joint rehabilitation, including taking a detailed history, evaluating posture, stretching the muscles, applying manual massage, electrical stimulation, and exercises to increase control and strength of the pelvic floor, pelvic stability training, having patients keep diaries of pain or incontinence, and instructing the patient in behavior or voiding modifications. This list is not all-inclusive, but specialized physical therapy can contribute much toward improving the function and condition of a patient's pelvic floor.
>
>
> Disorders of pelvic function
>
>
>
> Pelvic organ prolapse (POP)
>
>
>
> The cross-sectional, national health survey conducted by the National Centers for Health Statistics of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention surveyed 1,961 women about symptomatic POP and reported a prevalence rate of 2.9%.11 The effectiveness of pelvic floor muscle training in reversing POP and alleviating symptoms was studied in a randomized trial of 109 women with prolapse ranging from mild to severe. The group randomized to pelvic floor training showed statistically significant elevation of the bladder (3.0 mm) and the rectum (5.5 mm) after treatment and demonstrated reduced frequency and bother of symptoms compared with controls.12 Because of the statistically significant but small measured change in reduction of the prolapse, symptomatic stage 1, 2, and early stage 3 prolapse patients are most likely to benefit from pelvic floor strengthening.

I hope to never need depends.....it could happen though....My kids have already advised I'll be going to a home the minute I become annoying. I'm still here. They have a high tolerance.

nice article.....

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-16T06:54:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31504>

Thank you so much! I agree, I plan on keeping my organs!! And chocolate, I'm glad your kids are keeping u around! Lol

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-16T18:55:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31566>

LOL...the reason I'm here on these boards, came to PaleoFX, eat CO oil daily, do the CT baths....about 3 years ago, my OB/GYN recommended "immediate total hysterectomy" after I complained of painful periods and a sonogram showed fibroids, I ran out of there so fast and started doing my own research. Today, I have all my parts, and a Paleo diet, CT, etc., to thank for it. Oh, and the krill oil has done wonders.

I, too, learned that the cervix lessens or obliterates orgasm. Ain't nobody doing that to me.

The fact that these doctors think we have these removeable parts just astounds me. About 30 years ago, they wanted to perform a total hysterctomy on my mother. She asked for a 2nd opinion. No joke...the doctor picked up the phone, called another doctor, told him her symptoms, and hung up and said, "OK I just got a 2nd opinion. He agrees". She went back to her favorite doctor, who told her she was fine. 30 years later, and she still has all her parts.

Please help your mother by doing a lot of your own research. There have got to be other things they can do. My research found that there are thousands of unnecessary hysterectomies performed.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-19T17:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-32141>

Thanks PrimalPam! Unfortunately I have not been able to convince my mom not to go ahead w the surgery. Although she will talk with the foctir about kerping her ovary. She is very uncomfortable and she has a low tolerance. She hasn't followed thru w the exercises and getting her to make dietary changes is hard. I had such hopes for her trying some healthy and gluten free Sugar free foods while at my house but she wouldn't even taste them! The only thing she tried was the Zevia pop. Its sad because I think she thinks it's "too late" for her. Her mom had osteoporosis and Alzheimer's. My mom already has osteoporosis and migraines. I've been begging her to at least give up gluten. She is trying. Now I know why Dr K says adapt or die. She isn't willing to adapt in many ways. I made some strawberry vinaigrette salad dressing but she wouldn't even try it. Insists on her Newmans Own olive oil dressing. Which would be great if it really was only olive oil but it has canola oil! But I digress. We were working on my yard and she said she'd get preen. I said no chemicals. I don't want them in my yard and told her she shouldn't go near them either and she said "it's too late for me." So sad. I really need to work on my dad. If he did it, she wOuld follow.

# Thread: Anyone in Korea:  Try Cold Thermogenesis in Public Bathouses
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mambo — 2012-06-07T05:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/post-29855>

I was in Korea for 2 weeks and I tried Dr. K's CT in Korea's many public bathouses. There are some excellent cold temperature pools there, with temperatures ranging from 39-41F and 55-63F. Some places with 3 or more cold bathtubs with different temperatures: cold, colder, coldest.

The one in Pusan (Haundae Spa) was the best at around 39F. It seemed to be just above freezing, the coldest bathtub I ever experienced. No one could stay in for more than 30 seconds.

After about 2 weeks of CT, I could feel my temperature rising. Also, I began to sweat profusely (I'm sublicinically hypothyroid and have had problems sweating for the last 3 years). Another wild thing: my metabolism speeded up and I seemed to be burning fat real quickly. When I weighed myself, I was close to 153. I left the country at 161. I seemed to have acquired the metabolism of those legendary sherpas of the Himalayas!

My conclusion: there is definitely something to Dr. K's CT.

### mambo — 2012-06-12T03:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/post-30633>

I tried to stay for at least 30 minutes each time. For every visit, I used the cold pool twice, beginning and end. So that's about 60 minutes total. I could do 30 minutes no problem without prep in 55-63F. When it gets to 39F, I couldn't obviously.

In Korea, you can also stay overnight in such places, called Zzimjilbangs. The charge is for 12 hours at around 10,000W, equivalent to about $8.50. For my 11 day stay, I stayed 8 nights in Korean spas in 5 different cities and had my fill of Cold Thermogenesis. These spas also have "ice rooms" but I mainly stuck to cold pools. I mainly followed the guide provided by this blog:

[http://grrrltraveler.com/countries/asia/korea/fave-tips-stays-for-korea/siloam-spa/](http://http://grrrltraveler.com/countries/asia/korea/fave-tips-stays-for-korea/siloam-spa/)

I agree with Grrrltraveler: Siloam has the best sleeping rooms in Seoul; but in Pusan, you have to try [Haundae Spa Center](http://http://grrrltraveler.com/the-grrr/traveling-solo/night-jjimjilbang/) with its ice cold pool. In Daegu, you have to try [Goongjeon Lavender](http://http://www.goongjeon.com/sub2-8.html) with its cavern-like cold pool.

In the U.S. you can also visit Korean spas but Russian spas also have good cold pools, from what I hear. You can use this search site to look for such places.

[http://saunascape.com/find-sauna/brc-delphi-sauna](http://http://saunascape.com/find-sauna/brc-delphi-sauna)

If you live in the NYC area, the place to visit is [King Sauna](http://http://kingsaunanj.com/) near Fort Lee, N.J. and [Spa Castle](http://http://www.nyspacastle.com/eng/info/info01.php) near Flushing, Queens. If you're near Philly, [Southhampton Spa](http://http://www.southamptonspa.com/) gets high marks. I don't know too much about Onsens or Japanese spas.

> **MJ\*;29962 wrote:** Great testimonial...how long were you able to stay in the cold water? If you continue to adapt to the cold you could go back and shock them all!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: 4:16 AM sunrise, 10 pm sunset--evolutionary coping in the Northern latitudes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thyme — 2012-06-07T13:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/post-29869>

The local mammals seem to go with it--they're up and eating with sunrise and down and sleeping at sunset. How do I know?

Because I'm waking with the bloody sunrise. And while naturally awakening with the sun at a slightly earlier time every day was a lovely example of circadian rhythms in springtime, it's proving to be a royal pain in the arse now that we're in summer. I live on the sea, in the country, and the only light that enters my bedroom is moonlight and sunlight peeping through slight cracks in the heavy curtains.

I wear a silk eyemask.

I'm eating seasonally.

I have no artificial lights at night.

I am falling asleep at 11 (no lights--that's when it finally gets dark outside, a good hour after sunset). and waking at 4:30, when the sun rises, and we aren't yet to the solstice.

How does the nightly sleep hormonal process adapt to 5.5 hours of darkness--and, presumably, sleep?

And other than grumbling at the sun and world in the morning before pulling a pillow over your head and going back to sleep for a couple more hours, how do you cope?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T14:46:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/post-29873>

I go to bed a lot earlier than 11.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-07T16:32:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-29896>

I began LR on 02/25/12, Friday. The day after I had my annual biometrics done at work and found out that my healthy juicing had caused me to gain 12.5 lbs in 7 months. I began snorkeling on 03/26/12. I tried to go too fast w/ the CT & wound up staring at an empty tub for 3(?) weeks. I'm back to spot icing: 6 frozen liter bottles (clavicle to pubis) and baggies of ice on supraclavicular, interscapular & L5/S1 (disc bulge that aches unless iced).

I've lost 15 lbs as of this morning. I slept 8 hours last night for the first time in a very long time.

I would love to say everything's rosy on my wee journey to Optimal, but this week it's not. My tinnitus has been worse for the past week plus I've been looking at [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) to get a handle on my thyroid lab results. As it turns out I have several of the symptoms listed. My PCP emailed me that thyroid is normal so we're going to have to have a conversation about symptoms when I go for my follow-up visit this coming Monday.

What's been keeping me going is, I remember some smart person posting that when sleep kicks in, hormones aren't far behind. I know that CT will help thyroid once the sex hormones are under control. So I'm going to see what my PCP wants to do about the sex hormones. For the record, I subscribe to Dr. John Lee (physiologic doses) not TS Wylie (pharmacologic doses).

If anyone chooses to comment on my labs, I'd be grateful for any assistance. While I can read the results, I have no idea of what to do with the information.

Elin

PS: After reading others' posts about eggs I cut them out last Sunday, and my wheezing has almost completely disappeared. That may be why my hsCRP was so high when labs were done. Thanks to all who weighed in on eggs!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-08T12:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30081>

Night #2 sleeping 8 hours without waking. I feel like Wonder Woman!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30085>

The CRP was a HS CRP? If so that is why your hormone panel is a mess........Free T3 is real low so dont be fooled by a decent TSH......most people use TSH to trend thyroid recs and that is a bad method in my view.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-08T15:36:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30108>

> **Jack Kruse;30187 wrote:** The CRP was a HS CRP? If so that is why your hormone panel is a mess........Free T3 is real low so dont be fooled by a decent TSH......most people use TSH to trend thyroid recs and that is a bad method in my view.

Yes, it was hsCRP. That result is from before I quit eating eggs & cheese, which were giving me a huge histamine reaction. I'm going to talk w/ my PCP on Monday about desiccated thyroid and topical sex hormones. Then I plan to re-test in October or November.

Slept through the night again last night. Hoping that it gets to be a habit.

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-09T15:34:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30260>

Ah well... last night I woke up once about 3:15am to void my bladder. Even though I didn't sleep all the way through, I feel good. I go to bed @ 9pm and have an alarm set for 5am. This morning being Saturday my alarm was off and I slept until 6:15.

Good sleep is better than any drug I've ever heard of.

In my world Saturdays are for errands. I'm off and running...

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-10T14:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30393>

Went to bed @ 10 last night; woke up @ 440 this morning. It's not 8 hours but I'll take it.

I ice every evening from 7pm-8pm. Last night I noticed 2 things:

1- Whereas I haven't been able to feel any cold in my lower abdomen at all since I began this CT journey, I was very aware for the first time that my uterus was cold - not uncomfortable, just an awareness of cold.

2- I put a sandwich-size baggie of ice on either side of my neck when icing to hit the BAT that lives there and for the past 3 nights it's been more water than ice when I'm done. Also, it's no longer painful at the 15 minute mark. I'm really not aware of it's being cold and have to re-adjust the baggies' positions several times to make sure they're where they should be.

Today is my cook-for-the-week, do laundry, get ice day. Git 'er done!

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-11T22:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30591>

Saw the PCP today re thyroid/adrenal issues. She's decided that she wants to fix one at a time so we know what's working. We agreed on estrogen/progesterone via sublingual liquid or troche. We'll re-assess in 3 months. So, I'll also be going to the store for Rhodiola, lubricarion and Eleuthrococcus ginseng (not panax though).

My "homework" is to bring my stress levels down. Gonna have to put on my thinking cap on that one.

Slept 8 hours last night. Still icing for an hour a night.

e

PS: Almost forgot. I lost another 3 pounds! Yay!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-14T15:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31100>

Picked up my hormone Rx from the compounding pharmacy yesterday after work. It's in troche form. Each troche contains Estriol 0.2mg, Estradiol 0.5mg, Progesterone 40mg & DHEA 5mg. I am dosing w/ half a troche twice a day. Pharmacy says it should take 6 - 8 weeks to reach optimal blood levels.

Fingers crossed.

e

### indigogirl — 2012-06-14T16:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31108>

Hi Elin,

You seem to be chugging along! Good work!

I use troches, too. Seems most women on here doing BHRT are using creams or lotions. I wasn't absorbing the lotion so we went to a troche and now I'm testing better levels. I'm not familiar with Lee's method...have heard of him...will have to look into it.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-15T02:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31273>

LOL, chugging along... well put, indigogirl! That's exactly what I'm doing: BAB, work, dinner, ice, sleep, start all over again the next morning.

This is my first experience with troches. (Used OTC cream for a few years but the new PCP doesn't like 'em.) I'm concerned that the estrogen is too high, but I'm willing to be patient while my body adjusts. Are you having good results? Did you have to wait long to feel any change(s)?

Dr. John Lee began treating women with BHRT in his clinical practice in the mid-70's before anyone knew what it was. He wrote "What Your Doctor May _Not Tell You About Menopause" and several other books all about balancing hormones. He advocated physiologic doses (equivalent to normal body function) instead of pharmacologic doses ("considerably greater" than what the body produces). His books contain almost as many biochem lessons as Dr. Kruse' blogs! One of his books, "What Your Doctor May Not_ Tell You About Breast Cancer" was co-written with Dr. David Zava, owner of ZRT Lab, the lab Dr. Kruse recommends for saliva testing.

e

### indigogirl — 2012-06-15T03:00:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31274>

Learning about hormones is so confusing...thankfully there is info out there, but who to trust? I started to look at Dr Uzzi Reiss' info online, have looked at Dzugan (makes sense to me, too pricey), am now reading an e book by Dr. Daniel Kalish.

I'm on Biest and progesterone. For the first 4-6 weeks on the troches I had breast tenderness, which I didn't have on the lotion, so I felt that it was working (meaning I was absorbing it) and lab worked showed I am. My GYN thinks my levels are good. Colleen Coble (posts here) thought they were in the toilet...and I trust her knowledge. Trying to figure out where I go from here!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-15T04:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31284>

Perhaps, like your tenderness, my swollen fingers are a sign that my body is doing something. I hope so. Don't want to blow up like a balloon.

e

### Tizawee — 2012-06-21T02:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-32558>

Apparently I'm not as far along with CT as I thought. Grrr. Now that my female hormones are getting some help, I'm down to 20 - 30 minutes of icing before shivering starts. Most times I can make it to 45 minutes before I have to stop but it really depends on how tired I am.

Tinnitus hasn't stopped and now my hands get cold in the afternoons at work. Both are thyroid signs, I believe. My PCP is going to check my labs again on Sept 21st... so I only have 13 weeks to wait. Woo Hoo.

I had a bad week last week. On Friday, June 8th, a friend died very suddenly. His memorial was Saturday, the 16th then Father's Day was the 17th. My second father's day without a father. No easy feat for a Daddy's girl. I found out that I'm an emotional eater: I just had to have some chips so I got a bag of sweet potato chips and ate the whole thing. I don't feel physically bad but my weight loss has stalled. And there's no telling what kind of damage that canola oil did. To repeat myself: Grrr.

Okay, enough with the poor me's. Back in the saddle, Elin.

Off to CT.

e

### Tizawee — 2012-07-02T13:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-34973>

I "borrowed" this format from two ladies who frequently post in the forums. Thank you both!**Week 18, Day 1 (Sunday, 07/01/12)

**Waking Temperature: 97.3F

**FBG: 93 (high; maybe from too much coffee yesterday?)

**Weight: 152

12 oz Coffee

**Breakfast: ~ 8 oz cod left over from last night (BG 1 hour postprandial: 111)

**Within 30 minutes of waking? Yes

**Lunch: 12 oz bag o’ broccoli (heated up, of course) w/ 3T CO + salt/onion powder

Dinner:** Cod baked w/ CO, salt, turmeric

Within 4 hours of bedtime**?** yes

Cravings?** No

Snacking?** No

Morning Musings:** Today is day 125 on LR. It just looks easier to track doing week/day so I’m going to be using that format from now on.

This is the summer I’ve been waiting for for two years: oral surgery is scheduled for Saturday, 07/14/12. I’m finally going to get my snaggle-teeth taken care of and get a partial denture so I have a full mouth of straight teeth again.

(I have what’s commonly referred to as Meth Mouth: I was on OMG doses of pain management meds for 7 years. I got myself off the meds in May ’09 and have finally saved enough $ for oral surgery + partial dentures.)

Tomorrow @ 0630 I turn 55. I woke myself up @ 0600 on my 13\[sup\]th\[/sup\] birthday just to see what it would feel like to become a teenager… No change. I still had mild muscle/joint aches all over and felt a certain amount of hopelessness about the world. I could never understand how, if life was full of pain and hopelessness, people could manage to be so happy and successful. It would take me 28 more years to discover I have celiac disease and another 13 years to find and eliminate almost all allergens from my diet. It wasn’t until my friend (Bugsy in this forum) showed me this web site that I figured out I have a leaky gut. It was just a few weeks ago I discovered I have a histamine reaction to eggs(!). I know I won’t feel much different at 0630 tomorrow morning but it will undoubtedly be the best birthday I’ve ever had.

Earlier this week Dr. Kruse offered Optimal Living logo shirts on his FB page. In celebration of this most excellent birthday, I ordered one. Mrs. Kruse had it made and in the mail within 2 days. I chose a polo shirt so I can wear it to work, and it’s one of the nicest I’ve ever seen. The logo looks great! I’m waiting to wear it on Wednesday, my birthday party, and will post pics. Once I get down to goal, I’ll order another, more flattering shirt.

I work in an incoming call center for one of the largest insurance carriers in the nation quoting claim status and benefits to doctors and hospitals. We bid for shifts on a quarterly basis. We just did shift bid, and beginning a week from tomorrow I’ll be starting an earlier shift. It’s important to me because it means I’ll be able to eat dinner before I leave the office, which means 4 hours before bed. (I’ve lost over 20 lbs on the Reset + CT even though I’ve been eating as late as 1900 most evenings. My bed time is 2100 so, not good.) Now I’ll be able to eat and CT according to protocol. I’m hoping that’ll make a difference in my results.

Evening Observations:** I made an appointment w/ Dr. Kruse for Wed a.m. It has to be by phone because I don’t skype. I hope I’ll be able to take notes fast enough! LOL! So excited to be hacked by the Master Dot Connector himself and to have stumbled into a bargain to boot! C’mon Wednesday!

That’s enough for now.

Excess Fat around middle is usually a sign of insulin resistance (too much sugar and refined carbs, too little exercise). A poochy tummy (which is different from weight gain around middle and different from saggy tummy caused by pregnancy) can be caused by excess cortisol (stres) and/or estrogen deficiency and/or constipation.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-29T18:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-41801>

Quote taken from Ask Jack re: IGF-1 deficiency. I asked about testing IGF-1 directly. Also, it was because of this thread that Jack mentioned a Kardashian behind is from IGF-1 deficiency.

IGF1 is the best way to test or you can use that is safe in my opinion. The DEA is forcing manufactures to push physicians to get patients to do Stimulation testing but those test can carry some risk. Moreover, they are difficult to find a place who is willing to do them.

GH stimulation tests help to diagnose GH deficiency and hypopituitarism. For a stimulation test, a sample of blood is drawn after 10-12 hours of fasting. Then, under close medical supervision, a person is given an intravenous solution of insulin or arginine. Blood samples are then drawn at timed intervals, and GH levels are tested in each to see if the pituitary gland was stimulated by the insulin (or arginine) to produce expected levels of GH. Other GH stimulants include clonidine and glucagon.

GH suppression tests help to diagnose GH excess, which, in addition to other blood tests and imaging scans, can help identify and locate pituitary tumors. For a suppression test, a sample of blood is drawn after 10-12 hours of fasting. A person is then given a standard glucose solution to drink. Blood samples are drawn at timed intervals, and GH levels are tested in each to see if the pituitary gland is sufficiently suppressed by the dose of glucose.

Often other blood tests that reflect pituitary function, such as free T4, TSH, cortisol, FSH, LH, and testosterone (in men), are also ordered. These tests are usually performed prior to GH testing to make sure that they are normal and/or controlled with medication before GH testing is done. For example, hypothryoidism must be treated prior to testing for GH deficiency in children; otherwise, a falsely low GH result may be seen. Glucose levels are run on the samples collected during the GH suppression test, both to track glucose levels and to make sure that the person's system is sufficiently challenged by the glucose solution.

Since exercise normally causes a temporary increase in GH levels, some doctors may perform an initial evaluation of growth hormone deficiency by measuring GH after someone exercises vigorously for a period of time.

As for Dairy........today's blog post should make my position clear on it.

### Marccus — 2012-09-18T12:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-52313>

Cortisol is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands that helps regulate blood pressure and cardiovascular function, as well as the body's use of proteins, carbohydrates and fats. Cortisol secretion increases in response to physical and psychological stress during the fight or flight response, which is why it's sometimes called "the stress hormone."

# Thread: Why can't I fix this friggin' leaky gut?!?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-06-07T22:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-29962>

I'm having one of those days and am just beyond frustrated. I've been on a low-carb paleo / GAPS diet for 2 years now. I've been doing CT since March. My HDL is good @ 106, so well over the 60 Dr. K says he likes to see. My vitamin D is decent @ 66 though it could obviously be a bit better. It was 90 in February and dropped to 66 in May after I decreased my supplementation, so I'm upping it again to 10,000 IU/day. Trigs are good at 45 and hsCRP is even good at 0.05.

I just cannot figure out why I can't fix this leaky gut. I do have hypothyroidism, but not Hashi's. My hormones are all in the toilet, but I'm supplementing with DHEA and Preg. I feel like I'm just ending up with more and more AI diseases (Rosacea, Raynaud's) as time goes on and I can't for the life of me understand why. I am super clean with my diet - total AI protocol (no nuts, seeds, dairy, eggs, chocolate, etc). I have about 2 cups of bone broth per day and only eat organic, grass fed beef and high quality seafood. I don't do any processed/cured meats or conventional meats at all. I also have organic sauerkraut with every meal.

My BF every morning is a combo of beef and seafood, so I have at least 1 serving of seafood daily - wild Alaskan salmon, sardines, shrimp, scallops, etc. My veggies are all organic, but I'm even super limited in those that I can tolerate - pretty much kale, zucchini, small amounts of mushrooms and maybe a bit of cabbage and broccoli here and there.

I follow the leaky gut protocol as much as I can, but I don't tolerate the l-glutamine and I can't take high dose vitamin C due to oxalate issues. Other than that, I'm doing the best I can with it.

What the heck gives?!?! After 2 years I would think I'd be able to tolerate more foods and be in a much better state of health. Continuing to struggle with constipation that started after I went low-carb isn't exactly helping things either.

Anyone see anything glaringly obvious that I'm missing because I'm really not sure what else to add/take away to make some progress here.

### colleencoble — 2012-06-07T22:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-29966>

### mh586 — 2012-06-18T17:54:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-31934>

Answering the original question: If you see no improvement after 4 months look for other clues in your diet. I am surprised to see the amount of supplements you use trying to cure leaky gut. I have gone supplement free after I found my multivitamin being one of the triggers of my leaky gut symptoms. Do a little research to see how much additives is in each pill - colorants, flavours, stabiliser, filling agents... They did harm me, I am sure they harm others too. Supplement by real food only.

### shilohman — 2012-06-18T18:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-31937>

> **mh586;32041 wrote:** Answering the original question: If you see no improvement after 4 months look for other clues in your diet. I am surprised to see the amount of supplements you use trying to cure leaky gut. I have gone supplement free after I found my multivitamin being one of the triggers of my leaky gut symptoms. Do a little research to see how much additives is in each pill - colorants, flavours, stabiliser, filling agents... They did harm me, I am sure they harm others too. Supplement by real food only.

BJK77, mh586 is spot on with his advice. Check the supplements that you are taking and see how many of them contain magnesium stearate. This is a vegetable based ingredient that is added to supplement ingredients to help them flow in the manufacturing process. The magnesium stearate coats the small intestine so that absorption of nutrients is not possible. Other non-nutritional additives to supplements are corn oil, Titanium Dioxide and others. Do as Souldanzer suggests and get your magnesium from epson salt baths. Your

body may not take internal consumption of magnesium, but it should have no problem absorbing what it needs through the skin.

Also, double up on your use of CO. Dr. K says CO is the best thing for fixing a leaky gut.

Keep on trucking, you will eventually get there.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-19T04:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-32062>

BJK77, I am only just reading this thread and my experience may not be helpful but I'll share it just in case. I was having a similar problem to what you describe... keeping diet squeaky clean, labs showing good things, but food intolerances/allergies increasing. I started to get really worried I was getting worse. Then, one day while walking the dog, I was listening to a random Sean Croxton podcast with Paul Chek. It was a Q&A thing. (I'm not a Paul Chek devotee, but find a good bit of what he says thought-provoking.) In any case, someone called in and basically said what you are saying: their food intolerances are getting worse, not better. He asked about Betaine HCl and the woman said she wasn't taking much/any. He then said "I have seen countless cases of people going from no food intolerances to over 100 food allergies by not taking Betaine HCl when they need it." He said you only need it temporarily, but it gives your gut the rest it needs to start producing sufficient amounts on its own. If you don't have sufficient HCl, then whole food particles get into the blood and food allergies and autoimmune issues start to show.

Like I said, this may not apply to you. Perhaps you're taking heaps of it already. But I wasn't. So I started. And, lo and behold, my allergy symptoms (I was having sneezing allergies as well as swollen fingers) and my inability to eat foods I had eaten successfully before all went away.

> KiwiLauren;32171 wrote:** BJK77, I am only just reading this thread and my experience may not be helpful but I'll share it just in case. I was having a similar problem to what you describe... keeping diet squeaky clean, labs showing good things, but food intolerances/allergies increasing. I started to get really worried I was getting worse. Then, one day while walking the dog, I was listening to a random Sean Croxton podcast with Paul Chek. It was a Q&A thing. (I'm not a Paul Chek devotee, but find a good bit of what he says thought-provoking.) In any case, someone called in and basically said what you are saying: their food intolerances are getting worse, not better. He asked about Betaine HCl and the woman said she wasn't taking much/any. He then said "I have seen countless cases of people going from no food intolerances to over 100 food allergies by not taking Betaine HCl when they need it." He said you only need it temporarily, but it gives your gut the rest it needs to start producing sufficient amounts on its own. If you don't have sufficient HCl, then whole food particles get into the blood and food allergies and autoimmune issues start to show.
>
>
>
> Like I said, this may not apply to you. Perhaps you're taking heaps of it already. But I wasn't. So I started. And, lo and behold, my allergy symptoms (I was having sneezing allergies as well as swollen fingers) and my inability to eat foods I had eaten successfully before all went away.

Hi Lauren,

I had been taking HCL for months and only stopped a few weeks ago when I started having black stools. My doctor thought I might have had an ulcer and the HCL was making things worse. As soon as I stopped it the black stools went away, so I've been hesitant to start it again. I do eat sauerkraut/drink sauerkraut juice before my meals to help stimulate my own stomach acids . . . hopefully that is good enough since I don't think it would be wise to go back on the HCL. Thanks for the suggestion though. I'm grateful to all for your brainstorming and sharing ideas with me.

I had my consult with Dr. Kruse tonight and think I finally understand what's going on with me and why I'm not healing, so I have a path forward and plan to attack this all head on!!

### Lexi — 2012-06-19T06:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-32097>

if your leaky gut is not resolving i suggest you get tested for H. pylori.... this is my issue too and leptin reset did not help me as much as others.... H. pylori is a major cause of chronic fatigue / adrenal fatigue.

### chocolate — 2012-06-29T04:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-34313>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16777920](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16777920)

**Zinc carnosine, a health food supplement that stabilises small bowel integrity and stimulates gut repair processes.**

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Zinc carnosine (ZnC) is a health food product claimed to possess health-promoting and gastrointestinal supportive activity. Scientific evidence underlying these claims is, however, limited.

AIM:

To examine the effect of ZnC on various models of gut injury and repair, and in a clinical trial.

METHODS:

In vitro studies used pro-migratory (wounded monolayer) and proliferation (\[(3)H\]-thymidine incorporation) assays of human colonic (HT29), rat intestinal epithelial (RIE) and canine kidney (MDCK) epithelial cells. In vivo studies used a rat model of gastric damage (indomethacin/restraint) and a mouse model of small-intestinal (indomethacin) damage. Healthy volunteers (n = 10) undertook a randomised crossover trial comparing changes in gut permeability (lactulose:rhamnose ratios) before and after 5 days of indomethacin treatment (50 mg three times a day) with ZnC (37.5 mg twice daily) or placebo coadministration.

RESULTS:

ZnC stimulated migration and proliferation of cells in a dose-dependent manner (maximum effects in both assays at 100 micromol/l using HT29 cells), causing an approximate threefold increase in migration and proliferation (both p

### BrandonMark — 2012-07-02T09:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-34970>

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-08T05:05:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30042>

Alright, I am a Type 1 Diabetic with an A1C of 5.8....I'm 6'1" and about 230lbs...but muscular build, so a little chubby but not really fat per se. Anyway, got some lab work done and I wasn't too happy with my lipid panel AND for some reason I have elevated liver enzymes!?!?! Anyone have any advice/info they can share on this???

Thanks all!

OR = 60</td><td>mL/min/1.73m2</td></tr><tr><td>eGFR African American</td><td>93</td><td>&gt; OR = 60</td><td>mL/min/1.73m2</td></tr><tr><td>BUN/Creatinine Ratio</td><td>NOT APPLICABLE</td><td>6-22</td><td>(calc)</td></tr><tr><td>Sodium</td><td>140</td><td>135-146</td><td>mmol/L</td></tr><tr><td>Potassium</td><td>4.4</td><td>3.5-5.3</td><td>mmol/L</td></tr><tr><td>Chloride</td><td>104</td><td>98-110</td><td>mmol/L</td></tr><tr><td>CO2</td><td>27</td><td>21-33</td><td>mmol/L</td></tr><tr><td>Calcium</td><td>9.4</td><td>8.6-10.3</td><td>mg/dL</td></tr><tr><td>Total Protein</td><td>6.4</td><td>6.2-8.3</td><td>g/dL</td></tr><tr><td>Albumin</td><td>4.2</td><td>3.6-5.1</td><td>g/dL</td></tr><tr><td>Globulin</td><td>2.2</td><td>2.1-3.7</td><td>g/dL (calc)</td></tr><tr><td>Albumin/Globulin Ratio</td><td>1.9</td><td>1.0-2.1</td><td>(calc)</td></tr><tr><td>Total Bilirubin</td><td>0.7</td><td>0.2-1.2</td><td>mg/dL</td></tr><tr><td>Alkaline Phosphatase</td><td>71</td><td>40-115</td><td>U/L</td></tr><tr><td>AST</td><td>73</td><td>10-40</td><td>U/L</td></tr><tr><td>ALT</td><td>86</td><td>9-60</td><td>U/L</td></tr></tbody></table></div><br></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><div class="bbTable"><table style="width: 100%"><tbody><tr><td>Cholesterol</td><td>247</td><td>125-200</td><td>mg/dL</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>HDL</td><td>64</td><td>&gt; OR = 40</td><td>mg/dL</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Triglycerides</td><td>76</td><td>[TD]</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table></div><br><div style="margin-left: 20px"><br><br>​</div><br><div style="margin-left: 20px"><br><br>​</div></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T05:12:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30045>

i love your labs.........but you need to check your hormones.....your LDL is up because I will bet your Free T3 is low and your a glycator due to your disease. But for a T1D youre doing great.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-08T06:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30074>

Jack, thank you. I appreciate you taking the time to read and respond. I also appreciate the praise and feel like I am heading in the right direction here but still have a few things to sort out.

Ok, I was thinking a thyroid test was in order as well and actually already emailed the doctor about it. She agreed so much that she went and ordered a TSH before I could make my case for the Free/Total T3/T4/Rt3 tests....uggghhh. Oh well, I will get it straightened out.

So, I suspect the labs will indicate a deficiency in T4 to T3 conversion... I've been doing high protein breakfasts for probably 3-4 months now and eat no more than prob 30-50g carbs per day (a la Bernstein + Leptin Rx). I kind of feel like I am doing everything right in terms of diet and exercise (heavy weights mostly) so I'm wondering if, as a T1D, the remedy will be simply more time on the Leptin Rx than most OR using medication (ie dessicated thyroid perhaps?).

Dr. Bernstein says that something like +70% of his diabetic patients with elevated LDL end up on thyroid meds and that fixes the LDL problem...but I can't help but wonder if that can be avoided...right now, only meds I take are insulin and I've always been so proud of that...lol.

I know you can't give specific medical advice, but are meds going to be the end of the road for T1Ds with apparent thyroid issues? In other words, am I wasting my time continuing to read the blog posts for "nuggets" to sort this out? I know I am on the right track here, but since it's hard to tell what advice does or does not apply to T1Ds, I can't tell if I've reached the end of the trail or not...

Btw, that question isn't necessarily only directed to Jack, anyone please feel free to chime in.

Thanks again!

M

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:28:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30088>

I think T1D have a real thyroid issue because glycation causes major LDL receptor protein changes.......so keeping your BG in control is vital......as you age as a type 1 it become harder. the other thing to follow is your DHEA level because this is a sign of immuno incompetance from glycation and lipid peroxidation as you age it is behind the poor sleep and poor immune function in diabetics.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-09T09:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30254>

Ok, I am following you. Thanks again for the info. I'll get the thyroid labs and see what they say and take meds if necessary. Still working on getting the A1C down to true normal range...I will get it eventually and am sure it will help with everything

Thanks again!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:56:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30271>

Every diabetic should consider always getting a full thyroid panel anytime their lipids are checked........the linkage is missed by most modern docs.....but if you open internal medicine textbooks form 50-80 yrs ago......this was standard treatment for high LDL in those who glycate.

### JanSz — 2012-06-12T19:47:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30719>

> **Jack Kruse;30373 wrote:** Every diabetic should consider always getting a full thyroid panel anytime their lipids are checked........the linkage is missed by most modern docs.....but if you open internal medicine textbooks form 50-80 yrs ago......this was standard treatment for high LDL in those who glycate.

Thank you for this info.

I context of high LDL dr Patricia Kane worry a lot about Fatty Acids and cell membrane integrity.

Vitamin C

Chromium

Phosphatidylcholine

Evening Primrose Oil

Fish Oil HIGH EPA (EPA/DHA(3:1)

EFA 4.1 Oil (ALA/LA(4:1)

Carnitine

Increase Protein Intake

.

# Thread: Nosebleeds?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T05:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30046>

Why would I get sudden nosebleeds, three in less than 24 hrs, and two more over the week before? I don't usually get nosebleeds... ever.

Anyone? This is weird.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T06:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30069>

Again. Number 4 in less than 24hrs....

### Melinda — 2012-06-08T06:34:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30076>

I'm not sure but when I got my Omega six/three ratio too low, i got lots of unexplained bruising. Taking K2 allows me to eat lots of salmon and still not get bruising. I've also increased the amounts of nuts I eat. For awhile, I was completely adverse to eating any PUFA's and I think I thinned my blood too much. Nose bleeds are like bruises.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T07:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30078>

> **Melinda;30178 wrote:** I'm not sure but when I got my Omega six/three ratio too low, i got lots of unexplained bruising. Taking K2 allows me to eat lots of salmon and still not get bruising. I've also increased the amounts of nuts I eat. For awhile, I was completely adverse to eating any PUFA's and I think I thinned my blood too much. Nose bleeds are like bruises.

That's a really good point. I remember you saying, Souldanzer, that you were eating so much fish you were growing gills. And I saw a comment on one of the blogs (sorry can't remember which) that Dr K had to put some O6's into his diet, too, to balance out his ratio.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:25:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30087>

It could be too high an 03 level......too much vit E too......or it could be related to higher BP, or a bleeding dyscrasia. If it continues please get it looked at. It is not normal.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-08T15:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30099>

> **Souldanzer;30148 wrote: Why would I get sudden nosebleeds, three in less than 24 hrs, and two more over the week before? I don't usually get nosebleeds... ever.
>
>
>
> Anyone? This is weird.
>
>
> I get them around seasonal changes. particularly as fall turns to winter and winter to summer - doesn't matter where I live - for me it has something to do with the allergies, and change in moisture in the air. I never got them when I lived in Colorado or Idaho, but here in NoVa and Okinawa I'd get them... no issues in NJ or Guam.
>
>
>
> Jack Kruse;30189 wrote:** It could be too high an 03 level......too much vit E too......or it could be related to higher BP, or a bleeding dyscrasia. If it continues please get it looked at. It is not normal.

I wonder if my O3 during those time periods is closer to 1:1 considering I'm already at a 2:1????? interesting...

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-08T17:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30128>

I agree - get it checked. Didn't you recently have head trauma or concussion??

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30193>

Thanks, guys. Your input is so appreciated. This AM the other nostril started to bleed. I don't even blow my nose, just use a tissue to gently clean it.

@ Jennifer: Yep, I hit my head pretty badly a couple months ago. I'm not sure this would still be an issue but that thought did cross my mind...

@ Shijin: We actually are having some really humid days in CO right now, which really should help prevent nosebleeds - I thought.

@ Melinda/Kiwi/Dr. K: Score on O3 and vitamin E....

Can high O3 create cravings for O6.... I've got such a hard time leaving the nuts alone? Maybe I'll go pick up some bacon later and see if that helps.

I really don't feel like going in to see someone about it today. I'm done with strangers questioning and touching me for this week

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:20:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30194>

BP is 104/66. Doesn't appear to be the issue.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-08T23:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30195>

> **Souldanzer;30295 wrote:**
>
> @ Shijin: We actually are having some really humid days in CO right now, which really should help prevent nosebleeds - I thought.
>
> (

I only get them at sea level as we transition to a warmer humid climate...don't get them at altitude or in dry environments...

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-09T21:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30307>

I think it's improving. Still a little bit of bleeding whenever I clean my nose but not the soaking tissues with blood kind anymore. Dropped the vitamin E supplement and am balancing out my O3's a bit more. Hopefully this will fix the problem. If it doesn't, I'll address it next week with my doc.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-11T03:30:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30475>

I've been to the ER with a nosebleed that wouldn't stop. I saw an ENT who cauterized some blood vessels, which has mostly done the trick. I had to go back for an additional cauterization, and even now, I can't actually blow my nose. You may have ruptured a blood vessel, and each time you even barely blow your nose, it bleeds again.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-11T20:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30556>

Thanks, PrimalPam. My nosebleeds stop... but they were starting again all day long. This appears to be improving significantly. Still a little bit of bleeding when I clean my nose but nothing major any longer. I think this was an o3/vitamin E/K2 problem.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-12T04:16:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30641>

Weak collagen. You need vitamin c, K1 (for liver to make clotting factors) and dietary collagen from skin, bone broths, chicken feet and/or gelatin. Great for skin, hair and nails too. I get nose bleeds too if I don't include k1 with my high dose cod liver oil supplement from green pastures.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-24T22:50:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-33346>

Ever since I started "absolute Paleo" (i.e. no cheating!), and CT, my nosebleeds have completely stopped. I was still getting minor ones from nose-blowing; now, I no longer have those.

### IceBarbie — 2012-06-25T22:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-33512>

You can quickly stop most nosebleeds by applying ice to the back of your neck. I can't remember if the ice should be closer to the base of the skull or the down a little further so just use an icepack that covers that 3-4" area.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-34096>

Probably has got to do with the changing weather?

# Thread: A Salmon Question for the Aussie's or Kiwi's.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sums-sums — 2012-06-08T05:28:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30054>

I am an American expat living in Sydney and went to buy my weekly Salmon fillets from the fish market. I asked the owner, ' is this wild caught or farm raised.' He informed me that 90% of the Salmon sold in the Southern Hemisphere is farm raised. Oh no, now what do I do? I love Salmon and I don't know if I should now not eat it due to it being farm raised.

Do you eat the Salmon or is there another fish I could substitute for it?

Thanks in advance for your help.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T05:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30060>

Yep, we get almost all farm-raised too in NZ. I don't tend to eat/buy it. Our local fish shop occasionally gets wild caught (it is labelled as such) and when she gets it I always buy it! If it isn't in, I buy other wild caught fish which is usually in pretty good variety. Not sure what you get in Oz, but in NZ we get a pretty good range and we regularly get things like deep sea cod, too. I basically just turn up, ask what was caught this morning and double check it's wild. Also, when Bluff oysters are in season, eat em up!! And if you can get whitebait (only get the NZ stuff) at certain times of the year, buy it...it's super good for you.

### sums-sums — 2012-06-08T06:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30067>

> **KiwiLauren;30162 wrote:** Yep, we get almost all farm-raised too in NZ. I don't tend to eat/buy it. Our local fish shop occasionally gets wild caught (it is labelled as such) and when she gets it I always buy it! If it isn't in, I buy other wild caught fish which is usually in pretty good variety. Not sure what you get in Oz, but in NZ we get a pretty good range and we regularly get things like deep sea cod, too. I basically just turn up, ask what was caught this morning and double check it's wild. Also, when Bluff oysters are in season, eat em up!! And if you can get whitebait (only get the NZ stuff) at certain times of the year, buy it...it's super good for you.

Thanks KiwiLauren. I had a feeling that I have to say goodbye to the farm raised Salmon...so sad. I love Salmon not only for the taste, but all the healthy fats it contains as well. I will start asking for fresh wild caught fish, I appreciate the advice about whitebait.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30090>

Eat the fattiest southern polar fish you can find......it will make up for it.

### primat — 2012-06-12T12:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30671>

Wild caught is hard to get in Norway too, I have take time off work if I want to get to the fish market - which is insanely expensive too.

I always get wild cod etc when it shows up in my local store, otherwise I tell myself that farmed fish is better than no fish :

/rant

### sums-sums — 2012-06-18T12:16:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-31901>

Thanks Dr. Kruse for your response. It prompted me to have a talk with our local fish guy. I basically asked him what are some of the fattiest southern polar fish that are wild caught and not farm raised. He basically told me, 'none.' He said even a fatty fish he would recommend as a substitute is also farm raised. He then tried to explain to me how big the farms are out in the middle of the ocean, that it issn't a big deal and that in a year or so all the fish sold in the world will be farm raised....(i can see your eyes rolling in the back of your head). He then proceded to tell me that everything is farmed raised, chicken, cattle, ect....

So I am stuck eating low fat wild caught white fish for now.

Primat -- I started thinking like you...maybe farm raised fish is better than no fish.

### primat — 2012-06-19T14:34:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-32111>

> **sums-sums;32007 wrote:**
>
> Primat -- I started thinking like you...maybe farm raised fish is better than no fish.

Guess we have to go fish our own dinners...  To bad the fish farming industry is severely polluting the local environments for wild fish too. The amount of food pellets and antibiotics (and fish feces) below the farms are a big problem to local faunas.

As far as I know, tiny fish like [bristlings](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sprattus_sprattus) aren't farmed, and while I do enjoy a [100g box of them canned in ev olive oil](http://http://www.kingoscar.com/products-by-market/usa/usa-sardines/brisling-sardines-in-olive-oil-5.html) a few times a week it's not suitable for dinner. # Thread: Zeliq article
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-09T07:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30250>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-procedure-fat-cells-woman-surgery.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-procedure-fat-cells-woman-surgery.html)

The instrument clamps to the body and gets cold. It doesn't freeze but gets cold enough that it fatally damages fat cells just beneath the skin.

"It doesn't have to be very cold; the skin doesn't freeze or frostbite," Ridenour said. "Except for the tugging on the skin, it's painless."

He couldn't say what the effective temperature was. The manufacturer, Zeltiq Aesthetics Inc., won't reveal that information.

I'll bet i know the effective temperature--it's the same as a baggie full of ice and water...$1500, please.

### Owl — 2012-06-09T08:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30253>

You've made a typo in the name, it's Zeltiq.

From [http://inyourface.ocregister.com/2009/12/17/lipo-rival-zeltiq-aims-to-freeze-away-fat/13699/](http://http://inyourface.ocregister.com/2009/12/17/lipo-rival-zeltiq-aims-to-freeze-away-fat/13699/)

â€œIt chills the fat to about 5 degrees Centigrade \[41 degrees Fahrenheit\]. This is enough to cause frozen crystalline formation within the fat cells,â€￾ said Dr. Christopher Zachary, chairman of the UCI Department of Dermatology. â€œKeep them like this for 30-60 minutes and this induces â€˜programmed cell deathâ€™ a gradual process whereby the fat cells just quietly disappear.â€￾

From [http://www.americanhealthandbeauty.com/articles/?article=3138](http://http://www.americanhealthandbeauty.com/articles/?article=3138)

"It's very controlled freezing of fat cells and then the fat cells don't rupture, there's no immediate death to the fat cells, it triggers apoptosis which is a programmed cell death so they die gradually over a period of three or four months. There is a specific process that the cell undergoes as it dies away." Dr. Fitzpatrick explains, "Initially it sets up an inflammatory reaction so if you biopsy the skin â€“ the fat cell is surrounded by an inflammatory infiltrate so there are white blood cells that move into the area and actually the area feels tender, it feels kind of like a bruise if you push on it but as that resolves, the fat cells then are just gradually reabsorbed. So they go into a programmed cellular death where that's how your body gets rid of cells that are no longer useful so the cold doesn't immediately kill the cell it goes into a stage where it dies gradually."

The treatment is completely painless and is pretty simple. As Dr. Fitpatrick describes, "We have an applicator cup... with a vacuum and it pulls the skin and fat up into the cup and there are cooling plates on both sides of the cup that drop the temperature down and that controlled drop in temperature freezes the fat cells and actually once we finish we keep them frozen for about an hour â€“ when we take the applicator cup off there's actually like a frozen stick of butter there, the skin is very firm like a little frozen brick and it is red at that point. Now it thaws over period of 5 or 10 minutes or so and then there's some redness that the skin has afterwards which is just related to increased blood flow immediately post treatment. Occasionally the patient will get a bruise much like hickey I mean so that's kind of secondary to the suction of the vacuum. If there is a bruise it will generally fade over a period of about a week or so and as I mentioned if you biopsy the skin a few days after you will see an inflammatory reaction around the fat cells and that is accompanied clinically by just some tenderness in the area. Dr. Fitzpatrick says that it is very easy to predict how the fat will be lost and that after the cells die, they are reabsorbed into the body. "You lose that layer of fat cells that's right underneath the surface of the skin. It's a very predictable process, it's good for spot reduction but right underneath the outer layer of the skin, right underneath the dermis there's a layer that will be lost that's about at the most about a half inch in thickness. That layer will be the same no matter how much fat is underneath it, you cannot make it take out more fat if there's a big thick layer. So, sometimes if you have a thick layer you may want to go back after about three of four months and retreat the same spot but in most patients the single treatment works really well.

Around 25% of the unwanted fat can be removed in a single treatment. Slight contour differences are visible after three weeks but dramatic results may take between 3 to 6 months to be seen. This is the period of time the body needs to naturally eliminate the fat. Some patients may actually need a second treatment, which can be done after two to four months, reducing another 20% of fat."

### Greg rx — 2012-06-11T23:13:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30598>

Ok, I am dying to share this. I have been spot cting my abdomen for the last 8 or 10 weeks along with air adapting by riding my bike in shorts and short sleeves. This started back in March here in Michigan with temps in the thirties. Two relative points 1) I reached my halfway goal this morning and it appears I lost all 30 pounds from my stomach. 2) On my way down the stairs after weighing myself, I caught sight of my calf and ankle muscles in the mirror at the bottom of the stairs . Even though I am still quite obese, this part of my body looks ripped and well defined. Keep in mind the extreme cold (and exercise ) this part of my body was exposed to. Also note worthy is the lack of feeling of lactic acid build up from my statin damaged muscles. The muscle fatigue has returned since the temps have increased, but much improved and getting better all the time.

Now I just have to figure out how to get full body ct.

### ealachan — 2012-06-12T17:42:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30701>

> **AKMan;30720 wrote:** I read that fat has a higher freezing point than other tissues. Zetiq works because it can freeze the fat, but not surrounding skin or muscle.

That would make perfect sense, really. If you put the same size lumps of fat and protein in the freezer, the fat will freeze solid much sooner than the protein.

Interesting.

# Thread: The power of self directed adaptation.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-06-09T16:24:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30264>

It has now been one year since I decided to take full responsibility for my health care. Dropped the diabetes meds and went paleo. I had to adapt to a new diet which I was able to do. I kept tweaking and adapting further adding another baby step each time I became comfortable with the changes. Then I started leptinrx and kept tweaking (adapting) and then the huge adaptation of CT in March 2012. Each of these changes has brought significant improvement in my life.

After working through whatever initial discomfort these changes brought I have come out the other side an improved human. So now I am starting to be in awe the power of self directed adaptation. When you adapt to your environment or situation the rewards of that adaptation far outweigh the effort or fleeting discomfort to achieve that adaptation.

This is changing the way I approach life now . So I would like to offer a thread to discuss this concept and hear if other people are experiencing anything like this. **The less you try to control and the more you adapt the better life is.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:28:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30265>

This is an awesome idea for a thread.......this should become the Monster Thread on here.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30266>

Self Directed Adaptation to me:

If you aren't willing to sacrifice your comfort, you just don't have what it takes to make a difference. This maybe harsh but you never can become optimal if you allow things to always subvert you. you must retrain how you think to become the person you were born to be.

People want to be inspired. Leaders use the language of possibility, while behaving they are on their way to immortality. Greatness has a purpose that few others see initially but all seem to feel right away. Invest in you and become your own guru of inspiration. We need more starfishes these days.

Unconscious incompetence is always the first step on the way to acceptance. Historically, most folks wont be aware of this fault in thinking.

I'm no longer allowing me to be my built in excuse for anything any longer. **Lead yourself to your Optimal self.** When you follow the crowd it directs you to a place called average..........

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-09T17:57:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30285>

One of the tings I am trying to figure out is are there universal principles that can be applied toward any direction of adaptation not just those that directly affect our physical health. Jack developed his protocols so that although there may be some initial discomfort they are not painful or harmful but also accessible. This is truly the genius of Jack to me. So how do we figure out how to design protocols for whatever issues we may face? This is **power**. I want to apply these principles to every aspect of my life.

### johnnyb — 2012-06-09T22:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30313>

This fits better with my road to optimal. Some of the things I want are out do my reach. Today I move towards them so that I can reach them.

### AKMan — 2012-06-09T23:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30328>

Here's one of the many things I've done. I'm a retired military guy and have access to VA and military hospital (tricare). These two, working together, had me on 5 different medicines and headed down a path of constant medication. After going primal and ditching all the meds, I thought I would never go back to these quacks.

After hearing Jack harp on labs and testing, I now use these guys to my advantage. The VA and the military hospital will do bloodwork every 6 mo. I have it timed so I can get bloodwork done every 3 months between them.

They are famous for just testing the minimal amount, but now when I go in, I'll say, "do you suppose you could check my Vit D?, or Testosterone? or hsCRP?" They give me funny looks, but usually will add it to the lab sheet. I also go and get the results and read them myself, as they will only follow-up if something is wrong--in their eyes.

I will never again put my health in the hands of others.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-10T01:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30348>

When my previous doctor would not order an HS-CRP test, I ordered one myself, online. I gave the results to my new, low-carb friendly doctor, and he included the test on the battery of tests he ordered. He didn't include a hormone panel, so I went online again and ordered those tests. I'll give the results to him next visit.

I will obtain optimal.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-10T15:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30399>

Another aspect of implementing these changes is the unexpected. My motivation for going paleo, leptin rx, and ct has been to deal with a disease (t2d), but each of these adaptations has yielded one or more unexpected bonuses. After going paleo besides improving my bg levels I no longer sun burn and I pee like a race horse and i don't need Viagra. After the leptin reset all carb cravings disappeared and body comp improved dramatically along with increased energy. Then there is CT and the circadian cycles which brought about sleeping like a log all night, incredible body comp, almost zero systemic inflammation, increased vo2, better mood and amazing labs. These are only the things I can think of now.

So now when I make changes, no matter what my goal, I have come to expect bonuses. Truly expect the unexpected. Thanks Jack

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-10T16:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30403>

> **Dali Dula;30387 wrote: One of the tings I am trying to figure out is are there universal principles that can be applied toward any direction of adaptation not just those that directly affect our physical health. Jack developed his protocols so that although there may be some initial discomfort they are not painful or harmful but also accessible. This is truly the genius of Jack to me. So how do we figure out how to design protocols for whatever issues we may face? This is **power. I want to apply these principles to every aspect of my life.
> agreed and I think you have summed it up well here:
>
>
>
> Dali Dula;30366 wrote:** The less you try to control and the more you adapt the better life is.**

I spent a lot of time trying to make my body, life, people, situations conform to what I want and all I found was struggle, a constant war. Now, when I see a pattern that I don't want in my life, I start asking different questions. In stead of figuring out how I will change the outside, I look on the inside and see what can I do. I can only control my own actions, thoughts, choices. And to my surprise, I get good results with this. I'm so much happier! There is a saying, if you look in the mirror and the face you see is dirty, you don't wash the mirror, you wash your face! I've stopped trying to change the mirror.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-10T17:00:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30406>

One of the benefits of leaving the middle of the road is that I find I can see what I need to do more clearly. It was only when I allowed myself to consider that only eating meat/fish/fats was ok, that I saw how histamine and oxalates were really an issue for me. This has cut down my veg. intake enormously, and also means I have to be careful with what kinds of meat and fish I eat. So, for now I don't have the huge variety that has been the cornerstone of what I had believed was healthy. But I'm healthier now, so off to the extremes I go...happy to leave the middle and explore the edges.

Thank you for serving my Country.

You can get a boatload of information by doing few basic tests, 3 panels and 5 individual items.

Most doctors start with those tests, so the question is really in how one reads them.

Get copy of your tests from your doc's office. If you have most of the input, post it (on separate thread, Optimal Labs section).

BodyBio Report

STOP DELUDING YOUR SELF WITH SWEETNERS!!!! The effect is cumulative to for the human brain because of how it was constructed by evolution.

### Glamazon — 2012-06-17T19:30:00+0300

I remember Dr. K writing that the women were more thrilled with many of the cosmetic changes that were the result of his protocols and CT. Getting optimal health is important to me but these cosmetic milestones also thrill my little girly girl's heart. ### PrimalPam — 2012-06-17T20:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-menopausal-hitting-plateaus-and-just-making-headway-read-this-old-blog.1804/post-31789>

Thank you Dr. K. for linking this thread here with this great heading! I had read this so very long ago, when I first found your site, but I hardly understood any of it.

Lately, I've been reviewing your archived blogs and having a lot of "aha" moments.

This one particularly interests me, as I'm a 50 year-old perimenopausal female, AND I had a major concussion from a bike-car accident (3 days unconscious, in hospital, intravenous feeding) as a 12 year-old. (Guess who wasn't wearing a helmet AND rode out in a busy street?)

And, I've been an artificial sweetener junkie for oh, about 35 years.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-17T20:39:31+0300

I started at age 18 on my first Cavemam-designed diet of 1 bag of microwave popcorn, a snickers bar, and all the diet cokes I could pound one summer. No need to tell the subsequent stories of how that ended. 8+ cans a day for over 20 years was the norm until about age 42.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-17T21:36:07+0300

It scares me to think of the mountain of AS's & MSG I've eaten in my life.

I just received my Canary Club labs and let me tell you, eye opening. Can't wait to see how my visit w/her goes.
