# Thread: How do you pass the time while CT'ing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-09T22:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30316>

Just started CT'ing. Did the face dunks and after all the resistance I put up, I just...got in. Got the water down to the mid-50's in the tub, and I enjoyed it! I think I stayed in around 20 mins. I'll start using a timer soon.

But...I was bored! What do you all do to pass the time?

### indigogirl — 2012-06-09T22:56:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30320>

I listen to music on my iPhone. I use the songs to push myself to stay in longer...as in can't get out for another song, etc, etc...and I choose long songs!

### AKMan — 2012-06-09T23:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30326>

Music here, too.

I also do something weird called 'oil pulling'. It's where you swish sesame (or coconut or sunflower) oil in your mouth for 20 minutes. It really helps your teeth and has some effect on leptin sensors. When you do it, you need in a quiet place and focus on breathing. I found the CT bath a perfect place to do it.

Otherwise, I find the really cold bath very relaxing, makes me somewhat catatonic. I really look forward to it. Especially if I had a serious workout earlier that day.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-10T00:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30333>

I listen to a podcast. Chris Kresser, Abel James, Jimmy Moore, etc. Listening to a conversation helps the time pass plus I learn a few things. Because I am working on healing arthritis in my thumbs, I need to keep my hands in the water, so reading a book isn't an option. I did try that my first time but I shook so much I could hardly focus on the pages but now I'm much more comfortable.

### David — 2012-06-10T00:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30335>

> **AKMan;30429 wrote:** Music here, too.

I couldn't even get comfortable without music.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-10T00:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30341>

I use a portable DVD player to catch up on all the DVD boxes I've bought over the years, thinking I would want to watch them some day. It's entertaining and makes the time fly by. It's also a good way to keep track of the time. A typical half-hour comedy is 22 minutes. A drama is 45. So depending on my schedule I usually aim for 45 minutes during the week and an hour+ on the weekends.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-10T00:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30344>

Pandora........is how I roll

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-10T01:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30347>

Thanks...you guys are awesome! Firing up my ipod for tomorrow...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-10T03:24:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30363>

I listen to podcasts, read on the kindle or even an audiobook works great. For me the mind has to be going and thinking.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-10T05:29:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30378>

When I started CT the pond was cold enough so I would swim to pass the time. Swimming in >50f is hard. Now I use a cast iron tub in the yard. I make up ipod play lists for my ever increasing time and get out when the music ends. I also read, but I don't retain much. I really can't wait for autumn when the native waters get cold. When you can swim and frolic the time passes quickly. I also do fire breathing in the tub. Firebreathing is a yoga thing where you engage the core muscles and breath in and out rapidly through the nose for like a hundred times.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-06-10T05:57:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30385>

I read Paleo Books and listen to podcasts.

### Nymue — 2012-06-10T16:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30401>

> **indigogirl;30423 wrote:** I listen to music on my iPhone. I use the songs to push myself to stay in longer...as in can't get out for another song, etc, etc...and I choose long songs!

I do this, too. At first I tried reading or listening to podcasts, but would still get distracted by the cold. Now I put on the most hardcore music that I have, a playlist that I would work out to, for instance, and use the intensity to drive myself. And will use "one more song" as a goal, and then will usually say, "ok, one more"!

### Kpcst — 2012-06-10T17:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30411>

I listen to podcasts. Or watch tv.

Sent from my DROID RAZR using Tapatalk

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-10T18:56:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30423>

I just made a couple of 35-minute playlists. Just finished a CT session...surprisingly, the playlist ended before I was ready to get out!

### Lanie — 2012-06-11T07:20:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30503>

I watch back seasons of Grey's Anatomy on Netflix by propping up my iPad outside the tub.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-11T14:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30510>

Lanie, I checked out your blog. It's great. I sometimes have that morning sleepiness you talk about. I have had this happen and have found it accompanied by BG spikes. Next time you feel like that check BG.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-11T20:26:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30561>

I read books - the more gripping the better so that I don't feel the cold!

### Optimalbound — 2012-06-14T21:34:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31213>

I've downloaded Dr. Kruse's entire blog onto my Kindle reading tablet. Reading it makes my head hurt so much that my body feels comfortable by comparison.

### Arthur Morris — 2012-06-15T23:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31415>

Books on tape on my Bose.... I actually look forward to the time.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-06-16T00:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31425>

Summer in Utah = cold streams with fly rod in hand. I don't use waders anymore...ha, ha. Just shorts and a beanie. (Too bad I can't go more often. Beats a cold tub any day.) Helps with stress, too. I'm headed out tonight. Midges are coming off just before sunset. If I'm lucky, I'll have some coconut oil fried trout for dinner. Wahoo!

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-16T14:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31523>

> **Dan in Utah;31531 wrote:** Summer in Utah = cold streams with fly rod in hand. I don't use waders anymore...ha, ha. Just shorts and a beanie. (Too bad I can't go more often. Beats a cold tub any day.) Helps with stress, too. I'm headed out tonight. Midges are coming off just before sunset. If I'm lucky, I'll have some coconut oil fried trout for dinner. Wahoo!

Nice. Wild is good.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-16T19:24:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31570>

> **PrimalPam;30418 wrote:** Just started CT'ing. Did the face dunks and after all the resistance I put up, I just...got in. Got the water down to the mid-50's in the tub, and I enjoyed it! I think I stayed in around 20 mins. I'll start using a timer soon.
>
>
>
> But...I was bored! What do you all do to pass the time?

Hi PrimalPam,

I don't find the cold very comfortable at this point. I am just a beginner but this is how I past the time in the tub:

1\. I face dunk and hold my breath throughout the time in the tub.

2\. I meditate and try to quiet my mind. I find the cold tough to take and work at excepting the cold and not fighting it.

3\. I listen to the aerial sounds around my tub. (I would like to do my tub outside.) I keep the window open and listen to the birds, wind, etc. I find it all very stimulating.

This morning I could hear the crows. The young crows have just left the nest and are harassing their parent's for food because they are not very good at getting food for themselves yet. I could hear the wings of one crow, woosh, woosh, woosh, and land on the roof, his nails scratching around on the metal. The local song birds took to the attack because crows steal their eggs and offspring. It was quite a fight.

### jmathisen — 2012-06-28T05:13:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-34106>

> **August;33718 wrote:** May I ask how you like your kindle? Is it by change one of the black and white ones? I was thinking that might be a \[cough\] better solution for reading after dark . . . I think you can reverse things so that it is white on black, right? Do you know if the B&W kindles also offer rudimentary web browsing/internet . . and if so, if you can do the white on black thing for that too? Is it readable with the blue blocking glasses? (I understand most models will also 'read to you' . . that would be nice)

I put on some opera, and read a book on my nook. The new nook with Glowlight has a nice gentle glow to it, so I can turn off the lights, sit in the near dark, and think cold and dark thoughts while I read. It is very similar to the kindle, although a barnes and noble product instead of amazon, but much similarity in terms of features.

The glowlight makes it awesome. Check it out.

# Thread: excess protein and bone loss
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-10T01:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-30346>

[http://www.betterbones.com/bonenutrition/protein/benefits.aspx](http://http://www.betterbones.com/bonenutrition/protein/benefits.aspx)

> Several worldwide surveys document that the countries with highest animal protein intakes are those with highest hip fracture rates. The proposed explanation of this relationship between animal protein and hip fracture incidence relates to the fact that animal protein is rich in acid-forming, sulfur-containing amino acids and low in base-forming precursors (such as vegetable sources of potassium citrate). Further, the contemporary cultures consuming a high animal protein diet also tend to under-consume vegetables, fruits, nuts and seeds, food high in base-forming precursors. This combination contributes to chronic low-grade metabolic acidosis and subsequent bone weakening. The growing body of literature documenting the association between chronic, low-grade metabolic acidosis and bone loss bolsters the argument that high dietary protein, if not balanced with high base-forming precursor intake, can have a detrimental impact on bone.
>
>
> Dietary protein is best considered in the context of the entire diet of each individual, particularity in terms of the balance between acid-forming and base-forming foodstuffs and overall mineral intake. In simple terms this can be seen as the relationship between protein and potassium intakes, as documented by Dr. Lynda Frassetto at the University of California, San Francisco. In this regard, insights from our evolutionary past might be helpful. **Anthropologists speculate **that our human ancestral diet during the _Paleolithic Period 100,000 years ago_** was more nutrient dense than contemporary diets not only in protein, but also in calcium, potassium, magnesium and zinc, while low in sodium chloride, sugars and void of refined carbohydrates. For example, calcium, potassium and protein intakes were two to three times higher than current intake levels, while sodium intake was seven times lower.** These diets were balanced high protein, high mineral, high phyto-nutrient, alkalizing diets. Overall, in fact, researchers report that 87% of likely prehistoric diets provided an excess of base, while contemporary Westernized diets provide an excess of acid. Our diet-induced low-grade metabolic acidity could well be the most significant, and least well-recognized, of all modern bone-depleting risk factors.

### janagram — 2012-06-10T03:02:39+0300

our westernized diets' excess of acid may be why I crave salt like I do....

### Joann — 2012-06-20T23:21:59+0300

What to do??? keto diet would be only protein, right? ugh

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-20T23:49:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32514>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-20T23:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32515>

### Joann — 2012-06-21T00:11:13+0300

### Michael — 2012-06-21T09:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32634>

> **Joann;32622 wrote:** What to do??? keto diet would be only protein, right?
>
>
> No, a ketogenic diet would be adequate in protein and relatively high in fat.
>
>
>
> maybe a mediteranian diet would be a better choice fro osteo?

Likely a low carb paleo diet would be best for this. Dr. Rosedale, whose regimen for diabetes patients is not unlike low carb paleo, reckons that the protein matrix in bone is the key factor. If you teach yourself to be a sugar burner, then you down regulate your fat burning enzymes. He says this means that when you sleep, and there's no sugar around because you're in the fasted state, your body robs out protein from muscle and bone to convert it to glucose to burn.

That would be consistent with Jack's comment that it's processed carbs that really correlate with osteoporosis.

Not doing any exercise, it may not have to be much, seems also to be a big no-no, since you're sending biological signals to your body that you don't need strong muscles and bones.

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/)

>
>
>
> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/)

I got to the link and found this article at:

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/)

It was too long, so here is a couple of tidbits:

ON KRAUSS

The punchline is that we expected that because these diets have low carbohydrate, when we fed the high saturated fat level along with the red meat, we would see a pretty benign metabolic risk profile. Just as we did with low saturated fat and red meat.

But that’s not what happened!

RON KRAUSS

This is one of the surprises that keep life interesting for us for us as researchers and also for the world out there who happens to be looking over our shoulder. In this case, the surprise was that the combination of the high beef diet and the high saturated fat diet caused very serious increases in all of the cholesterol related risk factors we had been measuring, including total particle numbers, small LDL, total LDL cholesterol, inflammation, whatever we looked at, we saw an adverse effect.

Why would the iron in red meat, when coupled with saturated fat, increase risk factors for inflammation, for small particle LDL, for higher blood sugars, and all the rest?

RON KRAUSS

This is just an idea. This is in our whole discussion the one area where we do’t have data . . . yet. But it’s an intriguing hypothesis. Because it’s known from genetic, metabolic and population studies that the iron content of the liver, which stores most of our iron for our needs for red blood cell production and many other metabolic processes . . . if that amount of iron within the liver is excessive, it can lead to impaired sugar metabolism and predispose even to diabetes in the extreme case. High levels of iron in the liver also can be associated with abnormal lipid profiles, with higher amounts of small particle LDL. So we already have evidence that high iron levels in the liver are a potential determinant of things that can influence risk for diabetes and heart disease. And since iron comes from what we eat, it could be related to the dietary response that we studied. Why beef should have a particularly high effect on hepatic iron is a possibility we’re interested in, along with the question of why beef would create this risk for us, in particular, when we consume saturated fat. So while we don’t have data yet, we do have an intriguing collection of clues that maybe would lead to definitive conclusions from Sherlock Holmes.

### Michael — 2012-06-21T16:06:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32682>

> **Destiny;32769 wrote:** I ... found this article ... :
>
> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/)
>
>
>
>
> I hadn't seen that. It's an interesting read. It seems very much at the hypothesis stage.
>
>
>
> The thought seems to be that:
>
>
>
>
> > saturated fat could potentially increase the amount of iron coming in from beef
>
>
>
> But high levels of iron can be problematic:
>
>
>
> ... if that amount of iron within the liver is excessive, it can lead to impaired sugar metabolism and predispose even to diabetes in the extreme case.

One of the potential adverse effects of high hepatic iron is "impair\[ment of\] sugar metabolism". Ron Krauss's "low carbohydrate diet" is, he says, 26% carbohydrate. So there's still a lot of glucose to be metabolized on that.

As a paranthetic comment, Drs. Phinney and Volek suggest that a diet can't really be characterized as "low carbohydrate" unless it's less than 15% carbohydrate; also, I can't help wondering whether hunter-gatherers in many parts of the world _could get up to that level of carbohydrate for much of the year.

Maybe high saturated fat may not be a problem in itself but could be in the context of high carbohydrate_. I think Jeff Volek has indicated that he thinks that may be true. Here would be a mechanism, the mediating factor being iron, if the hypothesis is correct.

In the meantime, maybe watch those iron levels, and ring the changes in protein choices with some seafood and poultry?

### Michael — 2012-06-21T19:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32758>

As an addendum to this one: If excess iron does turn out to be a problem in this, or other, contexts, then one has to ask whether it was sensible of the U.S. Government to mandate "enrichment" of flour with iron, and then on top of that to tell the population to consume more flour-based products.

In any event, it does rather look as though people who should probably know better have been deciding that some food components are "good" or "bad". The "good" ones are then over-promoted - calcium, anyone? Alternatively, they're added to nutritionally-denuded, and in any case fairly worthless, foods that probably shouldn't be consumed anyway in whole-society non-voluntary dosing experiments - folic acid, anyone? It comes across as a very crude way of thinking.

It's also a strange irony that it's the U.S. that does this. Americans tend to pride themselves on what they believe to be their individualism. Yet the U.S. Government makes decisions for its citizens in ways that would probably not be tolerated in many European countries.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[http://www.healthremedies.com/elemental\_hair\_analysis\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/elemental_hair_analysis_genova_diagnostics.html)

\=====================================

On a 2-4day of cycle do a blood draw in LabCorp, do this tests (add LH & FSH):

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry Test Number: 140707

2 ------- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) − Comprehensive Screen, Test Nr: 500175

3 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

10 ------- Metabolic Panel (14), Comprehensive

11 ------- Complete Blood Count (CBC) With Differential and Platelet

12 ------- NMR LipoProfileÂ®

13 ------- Iron, Total

14 ------- Ferritin

15 ------- Phosphorus, Serum

16 ------- Uric Acid, Serum

17 ------- Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)\*

18 ------- CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

19 ------- Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

20 ------- RDW

21 ------- Homocysteine, cardio

22 ------- Insulin and C-Peptide, Serum

23 ------- HgA1c

24 ------- C-Reactive Protein (CRP), High Sensitivity (Cardiac Risk Assessment)

25 ------- Thyroid-stimulating Hormone (TSH), T4, FT4, T3, FT3, RT3

26 ------- Lab pls calculate ratios, (Free T3 Ã· RT3) and (T3 Ã· RT3)

27 ------- Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies

28 ------- Thyroid Antithyroglobulin Antibody

29 ------- prolactin

30 ------- IGF-1

31 ------- IGFBP3

//////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

On day 19-21 of cycle do first 9 lines of above list (add LH & FSH).

////////

Note:

Correlate, correct, adjust, report from Essential & Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA)

using

Why 4:1 Ratio Oil?

[http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-4to1Oil.pdf](http://http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-4to1Oil.pdf)

Goal is (LA/ALA)=(4:1) O6/O3

or

post #1, here:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane)

alternatively (much better)

instead of (EMFA)

do

BodyBio RBC fatty acid

performed

at Kennedy Krieger Institute - Johns Hopkins University

for information look up post #1 mentioned above.

Note that advice from this approach would prevent you from eating Krill Oil and most of fish oils.

This needs to be discussed with Dr Kruse and followed by his method.

# Thread: Hormones insanity from CT, but I think my NMR looks great - any hacks?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormones-insanity-from-ct-but-i-think-my-nmr-looks-great-any-hacks.1814/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### imdreaming — 2012-06-10T18:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormones-insanity-from-ct-but-i-think-my-nmr-looks-great-any-hacks.1814/post-30421>

Brief recent background: I've been low carbing for about 4 years but still struggling with my weight. I did one round of homeopathic HCG and one round of RX HCG. Both times I struggled with keeping the weight off- it always comes back right before my TOM, and it doesn't like to leave. Started the LR in July 2011, and started getting labs done in August 2011. My doc started me on Armour thyroid and adaptogens for high cortisol. Gained 5-8 pounds after starting the reset, but thrilled to be free from cravings! Weight stabilized, but in December, I started having anxiety and panic attacks and went into a downward spiral. Doc took me off Armour and switched me to GTA (over the counter T3 only). Anxiety was debilitating, heart rate increased from 60 bpm to over 80 bpm, started having sleep problems, losing weight rapidly (down 7 pounds in 4 weeks) and at one point, I did not sleep for 5 consecutive days. Long story short, I realized it was the GTA causing problems and stopped taking it. (oh, also- back on 50 mg Zoloft. I've been on and off it for about 15 years. I would like to get off of it permanently, but the anxiety was threatening my family and job) Took a month break from thyroid meds and felt awful, went back to Armour and worked my way back up to 90 mg. Rapidly regained the 7+ pounds. Started CT in mid-March. I am currently taking a cold shower, 10-15 mins daily, 30 min bath (about 58 degrees currently) 2-3 times per week, plus daily spot treatment, minimum 1 hour each. Every night I fall asleep with a cold pack between my shoulder blades, plus I sit on ice packs at work and do spot icing on my hips. Since starting CT, I am up an additional 7-10 pounds. Dr Kruse told me at one point, back in the monster thread on MDA that my problem was hormones. I'm sleeping good again, feeling good- I feel like my cortisol is pretty much under control, anxiety and panic attacks have ceased. My biggest complaint is the weight gain! I'm eating less than ever before, though I had gotten a little sloppy recently. I have just re-committed to strict LR, no dairy, no fruit and no alcohol.

I've posted my hormone tests: first set of numbers is the range, second set is results from 9/12/2011, third set is my recent test on 5/30/12. If there is only one result number, it is from the September test- no new test result.

Test: Range 9/12/2011 5/30/2012

Estradiol (55.8 - 214.2) 56.3 159.3

FSH ( 1.5 - 9.1) 2.9

Luteinizing Hormone (0.5 - 16.9) 1

Progesterone ( 4.44 - 28.03) 5.32 20.65

Prolactin ( 2.8 - 29.2) 3.5

Testosterone, total ( 14 - 76) 37 37

Dihydrotestosterone ( 5 - 30) 4

Estradiol jumped sky high- I suspected that before the test- I'm sure at least half of my extra weight is in my breasts  I'm taking DIM, Estro Factors and NOW Liver detox/regenerator. Another complication for me- I do not have my gallbladder, and I have read that makes detoxing excess estrogen more difficult. My doc wants me to keep taking the supplements and re-test in 8 weeks.

I'm very pleased with my NMR though:

Range 4/21/2012

LDL (=40) 107

Triglycerides (

Triglycerides ( one point, back in the monster thread on MDA that my problem was hormones.

Good start to do blood tests at day 2-4, at LabCorp

and be ready to make corrections.

progesterone

\-------------------------------------------------------------

I am not a doctor.

**

Better (less $) to order thru your doctor.

For those who cannot:

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

\=========================

The test in question is:

**SpectraCell’s micronutrient testing

[http://www.spectracell.com/mnt/](http://http://www.spectracell.com/mnt/)

It tests for:

**VITAMINS

Vitamin A

Vitamin B1

Vitamin B2

Vitamin B3

Vitamin B6

Vitamin B12

Biotin

Folate

Pantothenate

Vitamin C

Vitamin D

Vitamin K

**MINERALS

Calcium

Magnesium

Manganese

Zinc

Copper

**Amino Acids

Asparagine

Glutamine

Serine

**Antioxidants**

Alpha Lipoic Acid

Coenzyme Q10

Cysteine

Glutathione

Selenium

Vitamin E

Spectrox™**

FOR Total

Antioxidant Function

Carbohydrate Metabolism**

Chromium

Fructose Sensitivity

Glucose-Insulin\* Metabolism

Fatty Acids**

Oleic Acid

Metabolites**

Choline

Inositol

Carnitine

///////////////////////////////////////

Correlating Micronutrient Testing with Common Pathologies**

http://www.spectracell.com/clinical-education-center/education-center-webinar-library/

///////////////////////////////////////////

SpectraCell’s Patented Technology

SpectraCell’s patented, chemically-defined control media contains the minimal amount of each essential micronutrient that is needed to support optimal lymphocyte growth or mitogenic response. The functional intracellular status of micronutrients involved in cell metabolism is evaluated by manipulation of the individual micronutrients in the media followed by mitogenic stimulation and measurement of DNA synthesis.

The same technology also provides a total antioxidant function test (SPECTROX™) which assesses the ability of cells to resist damage caused by free radicals and other forms of oxidative stress. Due to the considerable number of cellular antioxidants with extensive interactions, redundancies, repair and recharging capabilities, measuring total function is the most accurate and clinically useful way to assess your patients’ capacity to resist oxidative damage.

Since lymphocytes are produced in the bone marrow and stored in the peripheral locations for long periods of time (the average life span of a lymphocyte is approximately four to six months), SpectraCell’s measurements provide a powerful portrait of each patients’ long-term nutrient status. This is analogous to the use of a glycosylated hemoglobin test to evaluate blood glucose levels over a one to three month period.

///////////////////////////////////////////

# Thread: Grottenolm's Lab sketchbook
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Grottenolm — 2012-06-11T05:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-30491>

Hi All! Going to start posting my latest lab work here in this thread. I am not specifically asking for any help just yet until I get more comprehensive lab work done. I am off on holiday soon and I will take care of subsequent testing when I get back in about a month.

I recently went to the doctor for a routine cervical smear, and the doctor said "lets throw in a blood test while we're at it!" and I was like "Meh, why not?" As I was put on the spot I didn't ask her for all the specific things that I should be testing for, since becoming enlightened by Dr K, these forums and you fine people.

Also I just popped my multivitamin just before these tests, thats why the b12 is ridiculous

So as I post more and more labs, we will begin to sketch out the whole picture of my health.

**What my main concerns are at this point

\- Low energy and sometimes a blah, unmotivated mood despite life being perfect

\- Cycle is erratic since coming off the pill in February 2010. Sometimes its 27 days, other times its 40 days, sometimes there are a few months between periods!

\- Still have a soft puffy fatty midsection, although spot CT helps a bit. It seems to be comprised of a lot of water retention as some days I can even discern a faint line down my midsection, while other days it is one puffy uni blob, lol!

\- Extremely hard time losing body fat despite strict dairy free paleo and moderate activity (bodyweight stuff + walking)

About me

**

~ Female, will be 30 years old in December. 5'4 and currently 139 pounds. Never been pregnant.

~ Went on the contraceptive pill, Diane 35-ed for acne at age 16. Developed IBS at this time.

~ Went on a crash diet at age 18 - Weight Watchers, 18 points a day. Was stupid low calories and I was dumb and young, lol!

~ Managed to keep my weight down at 128 pounds until I turned 25. Then ballooned up to 165 when I moved in with my fiancee. This was partially due to overeating with him. Energy went in the crapper when I gained this weight and I simply did not have the stamina to do ANYTHING - it was found that I was anemic. Also, I started eating a lot of things that I never did until I moved out - namely pasta, bread and oats. And more dairy products. More IBS symptoms.

~ Went off the Diane 35 pill in Feb 2010, at age 27. I felt that it was screwing with my body and IBS.

~ Went paleo-primal in Feb 2010. Weight gradually came down to 125 pounds over the period of 1.5 years (I lose weight at a glacial pace) Energy was up! It was great! But terrible IBS persisted and subsequently took over my life. Also issues with appetite control. Body composition was also crap, quite flabby, big potbelly still there (can you tell that the potbelly is the bane of my existence? It follows me everywhere, lol! Or should I say it heralds my arrival everywhere I go) Only doing walking and body weight exercises at this point.

~ September 2011 - decided to do Leptin RX, until about mid November. Cured all IBS COMPLETELY, woohoo! Brought my weight down to 118 pounds.

~ January 2012 until Present - have moved onto post RX way of eating. But I have ballooned back up to 139 pounds. But my body composition seems better, ie more muscular. I can still fit into the majority of the clothes I wore when I weighed 125 pounds. I don't really care about scale weight numbers to be honest, just bodyfat percentage.

~ Added spot CT on February 26 2012 - ice packs on the belly. The persistent distended abdomen that I have had my whole life is slowly going down as a result of CT. But I have a lot of mushiness in the midsection that I cannot get rid of for the life of me. Would like to get rid of it at some point in my life, because I am a vain mofo who wants to rock a bikini!

~ Sleep is WONDERFUL - In bed by 10.30 and up by about 8am. Lots of gnarly dreaming and I only wake up in the middle of the night if my stupid neighbour blasts his music downstairs.

~ Currently I eat VLC Keto paleo, about 30 carbs a day, because it's winter here. Lots of lamb, pet cow who was pasture raised, salmon, sardines, mackerel, greens, other non starchy veg, coconut oil, eggs, bone broth and gross organs. I drink wine about every 2 weeks - a glass or 2 at a time. No dairy, as it really makes me retain fluid, especially around my midsection (which I have a lot of trouble with - fluid retention. My rings and shoes are either tight or loose depending on weather Venus is in the 7th house of aries or somesuch variable) Drinks are black coffee, green tea, other herbal teas and coco powder in hot water. I can easily go for months and months eating strict paleo with absolutely no sugar or nasty additives/chemicals. I am fortunate that all my family and friends cook good food with real ingredients. I eat this way because it makes me feel great. I don't have any problem with cravings or "falling off the wagon." My doctor is always surprised that I have the mushy body composition that I do, because she remarks "wow! You eat so strict?" and wonders how come...

I am on a myriad of supplements at the moment - Krill oil, Vitamin C, Vitamin d3, K2, Magnesium Malate, B vitamin complex (will drop this one) and Now foods Liver detoxifier and regenerator. I want to wean off supplements over the next few months and perhaps only keep the D3 and Magnesium

Well done if you read my tale up until this point! You get 3 lamb chops with marrow bone, mmmm....Onto my latest test...

**05 Jun 2012, Iron Studies

Serum Iron: 18 umol/L ( 10 - 30 )

Transferrin: 2.1 g/L ( 1.7 - 3.4 )

Transferrin Saturation: 0.34 ( 0.15 - 0.50 )

Ferritin: 179 ug/L ( 20 - 170 ) H

Comment: Normal transferrin saturation usually excludes causes of a high ferritin such as infection, inflammation and hereditary haemochromatosis. Other causes to consider include excess alcohol, chronic iron ingestion, or rarely, hyperthyroidism.

**05 Jun 2012, Thyroid Function Tests

Free T4: 17.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )

TSH: 2.5 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 )

Free T3: 2.7 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 ) L

Comment: Likely to be euthyroid - as judged by TSH.

05 Jun 2012, Complete Blood Count

**

Haemoglobin: 148 g/L ( 115 - 155 )

RBC: 5.07 x10e12/L ( 3.60 - 5.60 )

HCT: 0.46 L/L ( 0.35 - 0.46 )

MCV: 90 fL ( 80 - 99 )

MCH: 29.2 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )

Platelets: 259 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )

WBC: 5.6 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )

Neutrophils: 2.62 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )

Lymphocytes: 2.19 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )

Monocytes: 0.63 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )

Eosinophils: 0.15 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )

Basophils: 0.02 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

05 Jun 2012, Serum B12 And Folate

**

B12: 1178 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 ) HH

Folate: 42.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )

Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170

Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

Comment: If not on vitamin B12 replacement the high vitamin B12 may be due to use of multivitamins. Otherwise in a healthy person this is not significant. Repeat B12 assay is not necessary.

**05 Jun 2012, Diabetic Profile

**

HbA1c: 36 mmol/mol ( 20 - 40 )

Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result virtually excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.

If diabetic and treated with insulin/sulphonylureas, the risk for hypoglycaemia is increased. Suggest repeat HbA1c in 6 - 12 months.

Will post more when I come back from holiday - Looking forward to visiting the States and Mexico, yay!

### Grottenolm — 2012-11-16T04:19:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64635>

Hi All!

Well I'm continually feeling more like crud every day. Extreme lethargy and I would sleep all day if I could. Also have extreme low moods and terrible brain fog - some days it feels as if I am swimming through mud. My hair continues to get more wiry and my skin is dry, with terribly icy extremities. I suspect thyroid problems. My mother suffers from Thyroid issues.

I had a long chat with my doctor before getting these tests, discussing the worrying symptoms, and she said even if the Thyroid labs came back within range, she will still treat me based on symptoms. The brain fog is so severe that I want to persue my Masters degree, but in this state I don't think I can.

Here are my latest labs (also did an H Pylori test to see if it was the cause of my terrible bloating) -

**13 Nov 2012, Diabetic Profile

Fasting status: Fasting

Glucose: 4.7 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 5.4 )

HbA1c: 36 mmol/mol ( 20 - 40 )

Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result virtually excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.

**13 Nov 2012, Thyroid Function Tests

Free T4: 18.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )

TSH: 1.00 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 )

Free T3: 4.3 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 )

Comment: Consistent with euthyroidism. Suggest repeat TSH in 2 years if clinically indicated. FT3 is not indicated when screening for thyroid disease or monitoring thyroxine replacement.

**13 Nov 2012, Thyroid Antibodies

Thyroglobulin Ab: < 15 U/mL ( < 60 )

Thyroperoxidase Ab: 29 U/mL ( < 60 )

**13 Nov 2012, Esr

ESR: 20 mm/hr ( < 21 )

13 Nov 2012, Serum B12 And Folate

**

B12: 642 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 ) H

Folate: 21.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )

Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170

Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

Comment: If not on vitamin B12 replacement the high vitamin B12 may be due to use of multivitamins. Otherwise in a healthy person this is not significant. Repeat B12 assay is not necessary.

13 Nov 2012, Testosterone

**

Testosterone: 1.4 nmol/L ( 0.5 - 2.6 )

13 Nov 2012, Renal Function Tests

**

Sodium: 137 mmol/L ( 135 - 145 )

Potassium: 5.0 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 5.2 )

Creatinine: 58 umol/L ( 45 - 90 )

eGFR: > 90 mL/min/1.73m2 ( > 90 )

13 Nov 2012, Lipid Tests

**

Fasting status: Fasting

Cholesterol: 8.2 mmol/L ( < 5.0 ) HH

Triglyceride: 0.9 mmol/L ( < 2.0 )

HDL Cholesterol: 2.04 mmol/L ( > 1.00 )

LDL cholesterol: 5.8 mmol/L ( < 3.4 ) H

Chol/HDL Ratio: 4.0 ( < 4.5 )

Comment: For established CHD risk (including diabetes) NZGG optimal levels are Cholesterol < 4.0, LDL < 2.0 and Chol/HDL ratio < 4.0. See [http://tinyurl.com/nzggGuidelines1](http://http://tinyurl.com/nzggGuidelines1)

These results imply a 5-year CVD risk of at least 15%.

Raised cholesterol, secondary causes (hypothyroidism, diabetes, nephrotic syndrome, liver disease) should be excluded at this stage. Suggest repeat to confirm.

**13 Nov 2012, Liver Function Tests

Total Bilirubin: 16 umol/L ( < 25 )

Alk. Phosphatase: 63 U/L ( 40 - 100 )

GGT: 10 U/L ( < 50 )

ALT: 13 U/L ( < 45 )

Total Protein: 77 g/L ( 66 - 84 )

Albumin: 46 g/L ( 38 - 52 )

Globulin: 31 g/L ( 18 - 38 )

**13 Nov 2012, Iron Studies

Ferritin: 143 ug/L ( 20 - 170 )

13 Nov 2012, Complete Blood Count

**

Haemoglobin: 134 g/L ( 115 - 155 )

RBC: 4.62 x10e12/L ( 3.60 - 5.60 )

HCT: 0.42 L/L ( 0.35 - 0.46 )

MCV: 90 fL ( 80 - 99 )

MCH: 29.0 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )

Platelets: 202 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )

WBC: 5.1 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )

Neutrophils: 2.19 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )

Lymphocytes: 2.14 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )

Monocytes: 0.52 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )

Eosinophils: 0.24 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )

Basophils: 0.02 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

13 Nov 2012, H.Pylori Igg (Eia)

**

H.Pylori IgG (EIA): 1.75 U/mL

Helicobacter pylori IgG antibodies below 6.25 are unlikely to indicate active infection. Very high levels (>100) are

likely to indicate active infection. The significance of levels of around 7 are less certain.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-16T04:38:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64639>

Hi Grottenolm, you are female but the only hormone tested was testosterone?

Your high LDL might be because you aren't converting its cholesterol to steriod hormones (progesterone, pregnenolone, DHEA, estrogens, etc.) This would cause lethargy. Your first Free T3 was low, and T3 is needed also to create the steriod hormones.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-16T05:13:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64644>

+1 to what Nonchalant said. I'd also worry about both estrogen levels and E metabolism given the hx of oral contraceptives. You can get saliva hormone testing done through PathLab in Auckland ([www.pharmaceutical.co.nz](http://http://www.pharmaceutical.co.nz)). I'd be wanting to know P, E1, E2, E3, DHEA, and an ASI with melatonin. Hormone imbalances can cause some of the symptoms you're experiencing. Are you eating seaweeds? I'd be thinking about iodine deficiencies, too. Good luck.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-17T04:39:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64891>

Re: the pharmaceutical.co.nz folks... you'll have to pay on your own but you need a MD script/referral. Given that it's just saliva testing and you self-pay, it shouldn't be too hard (esp in Auckland) to find a doc willing to write the script.

Don't forget about pre-loading with Se before taking iodine, esp in our country where soil is Se deficient!

Re: ESR, this is just from google, but maybe it's helpful?

The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is an easy, inexpensive, nonspecific test that has been used for many years to help detect conditions associated with [acute](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/acute) and [chronic](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/chronic) [inflammation](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/inflammation), including infections, cancers, and [autoimmune diseases](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/conditions/autoimmune). ESR is said to be nonspecific because increased results do not tell the doctor exactly where the inflammation is in the body or what is causing it, and also because it can be affected by other conditions besides inflammation. For this reason, the ESR is typically used in conjunction with [other tests](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/esr/tab/faq).

ESR is helpful in diagnosing two specific inflammatory diseases, [temporal arteritis](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/tempart) and [polymyalgia rheumatica](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/polymyalgia). A high ESR is one of the main test results used to support the diagnosis. It is also used to monitor disease activity and response to therapy in both of these diseases.

**

~ Follow everything that is mentioned here - [http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/](http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/)
~ Carry on with my current supplements - Magnesium, Vitamin D, Vitamin K2, Selenium, Kelp,
~ Carry on with amino acids which currently help with energy and sleep - Glutamine powder, Tyrosine, L-Theanine
~ Add a good B-complex + Vitamin E mixed tocopherols, glutathione
~ Dr will start me on DHEA, but I am not sure what dose to start with?

**Questions for anyone reading
**

~ What would be a good starting dose for DHEA? Is lower better at first, and then titrate up? I will be getting all my tests re done in 2 months time.
~ What do you make of the Estrogen/Progesterone situation? I still don't understand that fully.

Thanks for reading!

> \=====================================================
> Always state when blood was drawn in relation to menstrual cycle.
> Now you have to:
> find that out,
> go to this chart
> [http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikiped...terone,\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.svg](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.svg)
> go and see where your estradiol is in relation to average thick blue line for the cycle day that the blood was drawn.
>
> Do the same for progesterone, except that you really want large excess there.
> Progesterone: 56.5 nmol/L= 17.8 ng/dL
> Oestradiol: 392 pmol/L = 106.8 pg/mL
> using this calculator
> P/E=143
> well
> P/E ratio is really molar ratio, and you happen to have E2 and pg in molar units,
> that does not happens often
>
> so 56500/393=144
>
> Dr Lam wants P/E around 200
> Dr Kruse wants P/E around 500
>
>
>

> Comment: Likely to be euthyroid - as judged by TSH.
>
> DHEAS: 0.9 umol/L ( 1.2 - 10.3 ) LL ======= you already know that you are low
>
> DHEAs is very important steroid hormone.
> If you take all major steroid hormones in drop of blood,
> DHEAs participation is 95% by weight
>
> so it is big deal when you are missing on it
>
> and the little DHEAs that you already have made alaedy such a good impression on you.
>
> **19 Apr 2013, Insulin
> **
> Fasting status: Fasting
> Insulin: 9.1 mU/L ( 5.0 - 13.0 )

Insulin is really high, should be at the bottom range

//////////////////////////

### caroline — 2013-05-08T04:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-85612>

I hope you get some answers from your doc. Dr. K. told me ....I have to lower my rT3 \[inflammation\] and increase freeT3.
Active free T3 is what we need - right? so I thought - the higher the better.......I know everyone is different but it seems to me that having higher freeT3 must be a good thing ....what am I missing? Isn't that the problem when our GP just tests our T4 and it seems fine - but that doesn't tell us if it is converting to free T3. Sometimes I feel like I am missing the boat entirely! ### JanSz — 2013-05-08T16:46:04+0300

> Active free T3 is what we need - right? so I thought - the higher the better.......I know everyone is different but it seems to me that having higher freeT3 must be a good thing ....what am I missing? Isn't that the problem when our GP just tests our T4 and it seems fine - but that doesn't tell us if it is converting to free T3. Sometimes I feel like I am missing the boat entirely! I was able to reduce my rT3 and raise my free T3 and I am wondering if iodine might help this further as my thyroid is sluggish?

I went thru your journal, fantastic, but not much details on labs.

Generally speaking.
When working on thyroid (and not only) it is good idea to not use supplemental thyroid hormones until thyroid friendly background is prepared first.

We usually do that by implementing dr K's advice, generously provided by him.
But, dr K reserves very important part of puzzle for dr/patient interaction.

That sounds nice until one realizes that it will take long time until more doctors catch up with this way of looking at health issues, because currently is is super hard to find doctor who would provide satisfactory help.

I was actually hoping for more activity on dedicated section of this forum:

Optimal Labs

Discuss labs and places to have your labs drawn

\-------------------

I am not a doctor, but like to learn about this important health topic.

..

### Grottenolm — 2013-05-09T03:19:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-85687>

I misread JanSz's Progesterone graph - actually my progesterone is really HIGH - the doctor said it is similar to someone that is supplementing it! So I am stopping the Maca supplement.

She is going to put me on a very low dose of dessicated Thyroid. Unfortunately we can't get T3 only here in New Zealand. However am going to be travelling to LA/Mexico in June, and she gave me a script that would enable me to pick up a 3 month supply while I am overseas. I will look into that....

So for now we are carrying on with the 50mg DHEA per day because it does feel good. Another thing - when I took the last blood test, I stopped taking the DHEA 24 hours prior. She said next time try 12 hours instead so there is till some left floating around the blood....

As for fasting insulin - I need to start exercising more in order to rectify this. Once my FT3 is up I can hopefully muster the energy to do some sprints  She is happy with my 75% - 80% diet to manage blood sugar levels and is very supportive of fasting when my body lets me.

### Grottenolm — 2013-05-20T08:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-86520>

Just an update - my dr started me on dessicated thyroid and I have been taking it for about 4 days now. It might be too early to tell but I think I'm feeling a lot better and my mood has been great over the weekend since commencing! She gave me 200x 16.25 mcg pills to play around with. I am charting Basal body temperature and other signs, and will go for blood testing again in a few weeks time.

At the moment I have started on 3x 16.25mcg pills per day and will be titrating it up bit by bit until my waking temperature becomes "normal" (pre ovulation) At the moment my waking temps are between 35~36 degrees, and we are aiming for 36.75.

I am not sure when to stop titrating up the dose of thyroid, perhaps keep an eye out for palpitations, anxiety, loose bowels, and general hyper-ness.

### caroline — 2013-05-20T10:24:43+0300

It might be too early to tell but I think I'm feeling a lot better and my mood has been great over the weekend since commencing! She gave me 200x 16.25 mcg pills to play around with. I am charting Basal body temperature and other signs, and will go for blood testing again in a few weeks time.
>
> At the moment I have started on 3x 16.25mcg pills per day and will be titrating it up bit by bit until my waking temperature becomes "normal" (pre ovulation) At the moment my waking temps are between 35~36 degrees, and we are aiming for 36.75.
>
> I am not sure when to stop titrating up the dose of thyroid, perhaps keep an eye out for palpitations, anxiety, loose bowels, and general hyper-ness.

Consider little different approach.

1\. Work on Circadian Rhythm (Jack Kruse's whole Project) so you get proper cortisol/DHEA/melatonin profile and levels on saliva tests
2\. optimize steroid hormone panel on days 3-4
3\. take as much thyroid hormones as it takes to get to TSH~1.5 (1-2) (and leave the rest for latter
4\. do Spectracell analysis and follow up (plus iodine)
5\. do Fatty Acids Analysis and follow up

Now (about year latter, you may be ready for thyroid tweaking (and benefit from it).

....

### Grottenolm — 2013-10-30T03:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-99398>

Hiya! So I've been on DHEA and Dessicated thyroid for a few months now, and this is my latest bloodwork. (100mg of DHEA and have titrated up to 4 grains of thyroid, per day)

Currently waiting on saliva test results, and will post those as well.

Still Following circadian protocal - meal timing, getting light on the retina first thing, no eating after sunset. Drink alcohol about once a week, couple of shots of the hard stuff mostly. Eating a ketogenic diet and feeling wonderful - the thyroid and DHEA have made me feel normal for the first time in years!

My doc says I need more folate/folic acid - so more liver? Or supplementation....

10 Oct 2013 - fasted AM test

**Thyroid Function Tests
**
Free T4: 18.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )
TSH: < 0.01 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 ) LL
Free T3: 5.5 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 )

**Reproductive Hormones
**
Progesterone: 2.6 nmol/L
Oestradiol: 126 pmol/L

Interpretation: Oestradiol
Follicular 50 - 600
Mid-cycle
Luteal 160 - 770
Post Menopausal < 200

Interpretation: PROGESTERONE
Adult Female <6 nmol/L - Ovulation unlikely
6 - 25 nmol/L - Ovulation possible, depending on
timing of sample
\>25 nmol/L - Ovulation confirmed
Post Menopausal Females  nmol/L

**Serum B12 And Folate
**
B12: 520 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 )
Folate: 13.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )
Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170
Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

**Lipid Tests
**
Fasting status: Fasting
Cholesterol: 7.5 mmol/L ( < 5.0 ) H
Triglyceride: 0.6 mmol/L ( < 2.0 )
HDL Cholesterol: 2.45 mmol/L ( > 1.00 )
LDL cholesterol: 4.8 mmol/L ( < 3.4 ) H
Chol/HDL Ratio: 3.1 ( < 4.5 )

Comment: For most patients non-fasting lipids are acceptable for CVD risk assessment, as Chol/HDL ratio is not affected.
For established CVD risk (including diabetes) NZGG optimal levels are Cholesterol < 4.0, LDL < 2.0 and Chol/HDL ratio < 4.0.

**Renal Function Tests
**
Sodium: 140 mmol/L ( 135 - 145 )
Potassium: 4.8 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 5.2 )
Creatinine: 50 umol/L ( 45 - 90 )
eGFR: > 90 mL/min/1.73m2 ( > 90 )

Iron Studies
Ferritin: 40 ug/L ( 20 - 190 )

**Liver Function Tests
**
Total Bilirubin: 8 umol/L ( < 25 )
Alk. Phosphatase: 60 U/L ( 40 - 100 )
GGT: 14 U/L ( < 50 )
ALT: 45 U/L ( < 45 ) H
Total Protein: 69 g/L ( 66 - 84 )
Albumin: 41 g/L ( 38 - 52 )
Globulin: 28 g/L ( 18 - 38 )

**Dhea Sulphate
**
DHEAS: 6.0 umol/L ( 1.2 - 10.3 )

**Cortisol**

Serum Cortisol: 571 nmol/L

Comment: A serum cortisol higher than 400 nmol/L, in an unstressed person, excludes adrenal insufficiency in the vast majority of cases. If there is clinical concern, contact an endocrinologist.
Cortisol (Morning Peak) 200 - 700 nmol/L

**Testosterone**

Testosterone: 2.0 nmol/L ( 0.5 - 2.6 )

Diabetic Profile

HbA1c: 39 mmol/mol ( < 41 )
Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result likely excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.
If diabetic and treated with insulin/sulphonylureas, the risk for hypoglycaemia is increased. Suggest repeat HbA1c in 6 - 12 months.

**Complete Blood Count
**
Haemoglobin: 145 g/L ( 115 - 155 )
RBC: 5.02 x10e12/L ( 3.60 - 5.60 )
HCT: 0.44 L/L ( 0.35 - 0.46 )
MCV: 88 fL ( 80 - 99 )
MCH: 28.9 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )
Platelets: 235 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )
WBC: 7.0 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )
Neutrophils: 3.64 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )
Lymphocytes: 2.21 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )
Monocytes: 0.89 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )
Eosinophils: 0.20 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )
Basophils: 0.01 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

# Thread: Leptin Rx Journal- Getting Serious
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 92
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### allison09 — 2012-06-12T03:11:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30632>

Hi everyone

Today was my first day of the Leptin Rx. I tried to start yesterday, but snacked after dinner-so today is Day 1! A bit of background:

I'm 27 years old, 5'6", 128 lbs. And I like long walks on the beach. Kidding...writing my "stats" makes me feel silly

I've been primal/paleo for a little more than a year, but before that I did the Specific Carbohydrate Diet for about a year to deal with some intense SIBO. Before THAT I was a vegetarian for years, and vegan for 2 years. Typical story, eh? Be healthy! Go veg! Ruin your health! Everyone always leaves out that last part though....

Moving on....like several others around here, I've struggled with an eating disorder for longer than I care to admit. The past 4 years of my life have been tough-I've been severely stressed out (moving, ending a relationship, grad school) and pretty depressed. My bulimia resurfaced-gradually at first- and it's recently been out of control, even though I've moved to a better place and things are generally better for me. I'm not sure about the relationship between emotions and eating and all of that-I'm not confusing correlation with causation, I'm just saying this all happened around the same time. I'd tried a bunch of things to end this cycle- The Diet Cure, strict primal/paleo, Leangains fasting, prozac and Topamax (not together), and therapy. Nothing has helped. Actually, the Topamax worked brilliantly for 2 weeks-then it was back to binging and purging. I don't like medication and it made me feel crappy, so I'm currently off all meds. So...I've found my way to the Leptin Rx. I am hopeful because I've been lurking around here for the past few days reading as much as I can, and it seems like many have found this to be helpful (Momagrok I'm lookin' at you!). I just want to be free of this, I want my brain back. I want my health back, and I want my life back.

I'm currently following the auto-immune Rx because of the SIBO (hmmm...carb binges don't help with that? SHOCKING!) and a suspected candida issue. Ugh. I LOVE eggs and this kind of makes me want to cry. I'll also miss the dairy, but nuts are HUGE triggers for me, so it's better that they are not in my life at all.

I have a million questions about this whole things too, so hopefully some of you seasoned pros would be so kind as to share your experiences and insights with me

1\. Is it ever a good idea to use whey protein in the BAB? I know Dr. K says certain people should/shouldn't-anyone know of any protein powders that make the cut? I'll even spring for the Bulletproof stuff if I know for sure it won't set me back.

2\. Today I had pork spare ribs, ground beef, and sound sauerkraut for my BAB- I finished by 7:45am and wasn't hungry again till like 6pm. Even then, it was a hint of hunger-did I over do it? Too much fat?

3\. I want to lose the few pounds I've recently gained due to all the bingeing/being lazy and not working out. Is it still recommended to eat a bunch of coconut oil? Even though I've been paleo for a while, the idea of eating oil in order to lose weight (as recommended by Dr. K) still doesn't make sense in my brain.

4\. Would it be a huge setback to workout before 5pm once or twice a week?

I have several other questions, but this is turning into a novel, so I'll just wait on them. Plus I've had a killer headache ALL day and I need to get off the computer

I'm excited to start this program-I hope it's as effective for me as it has been for so many others. Any advice is welcome and appreciated!

### Lanie — 2012-06-12T04:22:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30642>

Welcome, Allison! There are a lot of very knowledgable and supportive people hanging around here. I'm still learning myself. The basics aren't hard. Sounds like you've mastered the BAB! Try not to expect perfection from yourself. If you have a snack, just learn from it. Be kind to yourself. One of the best things I've gotten out of the LRx is I've been able to get out of that awful diet mindset that always resulted in me beating myself up. Many people here are binge-free now...even in the face of stress.

Good luck with your journey.

### PaulaRichards — 2012-06-12T18:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30708>

Welcome Allison! I'll say you're definitely in the right place. I have a long history of sugar addiction and binging. This is the best binge-free journey I've been on. I'm not binge-free yet but managed a 77 day stretch, which ended last Monday for a few hours. I consider the BAB and the leptin reset to be the only way to success for me. In the past a binge would have surely lasted at least a week, if not several! I see the mistakes I made leading up to my setback last week and I'm using that knowledge on my road to success!

I know it's hard to sometimes wrap your head around the amount of protein and fat in our BAB but it's the only way to break the binge cycle. I've learned my BAB's need to be between 65-75 grams of protein (with lots of fat...Yum!) so that I don't have cravings. If I eat this amount I rarely have cravings & if I do they are a fleating thought! It's amazing really!

Welcome, good luck & keep us posted!!!

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T05:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30822>

Thanks Lanie and Paulalynn! It's so nice to be in a place where people understand where I've been and where I want to be. Paulalynn- 77 days!!! That's so amazing!!! I'd love to be able to say that! One day...one day

So Day 2 went well for me. I had a BAB of canned BPA-free salmon, a little tuna to bring up the protein, a few pieces of bacon, and some lacto-fermented sauerkraut to help with digestion. I didn't feel as full as I did yesterday, I think because there was less fat today. It was nice. So, I was full pretty much all day, but I ate around 2 anyway for some reason. I had made plans to go running with a friend, and I knew I was going to an intense HIIT class tonight, so I thought I should eat something before the 7pm deadline. I at a lot of coconut manna. I know Dr. K promotes that stuff, and I freakin love it, but it's been a total trigger for me in the past. I make homemade manna, and put 1 Tbsp in mini muffin cups, then refrigerate it. They are so amazing! Buuuut I eat lots of them. So, after polishing off way to many this afternoon, I got rid of the rest of the manna and I'm not going to get anymore. At least not for a while. I think I need to be further in the Rx before I can have stuff like that around all the time. Sigh. Oh well.

So, I was thinking about the BAB today, and even after only 2 days I'm noticing a difference. I mean, it could be placebo effect because I'm all amped about this right now, but I don't know. I went food shopping today and didn't really feel any temptations. I didin't buy any foods I shouldn't have, and I didn't sample from any bulk bins! Hahah I'm being so serious about the no snacking rule! I think it's key though- the whole eating all day thing is NOT good for me, and it leads to crappy digestion and binges. Also, I like the BAB because I feel like I've been fighting my urge to eat right when I get up for so long! I'd always try to put off breakfast, fast for long periods of time, and it would just lead to food obsessions and binges. No more. Leangains isn't for everyone apparently.

I've also been thinking about the exercise component of this. I've decided that I'm not going to stop exercising. I need to move, I need to be active-it helps with my mental/emotional well being. I've been fighting some depression lately, and I think exercise really helps me with that. I do not want to fall down the rabbit hole of depression again- I'm finally on the way out! If it slows down my Leptin reset progress, then so be it I guess. More time to eat a BAB Seriously though, I'm going to make every effort to workout after 5pm. And if I can't fit it in after 5, I'll at least make sure that I get my BAB first, and wait a bit before exercising after. I don't think it's the worst concession to make-esp since I'm committed to the no snacking and the AI protocol. But ohhh man did I want some eggs and coffee today. It's cool, I got through it

I hope everyone had a good day

PS- I just made a batch of homemade turkey sausage patties for breakfast tomorrow and I didn't eat any of it!!! I'm actually not even tempted! Man I hope this lasts!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-13T16:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30891>

Welcome Allison!

I've dealed with bulimia with a little more than 4 years. Going paleo helped me to lower the frequency of binges, but they were still there. I came here to help improve my sleep, and I discovered that I was in such a mess!! As soon as I started the reset, cravings went away and let me alone for the first time in my life!! I felt of the badwagon twice since Febuary, but now I'm 81 days binge-free. I still have a hard time to believe it!!  Since cravings and binging are under control, now I can work on other stuff to bring myself on optimal level. Not there yet, but I see improvement in myself (and in my thinking) every day.

I also had to cut dairies, egss () and nuts. You feel so much better pretty soon, trust me, its worth it.

To answer your questions, 1) I personnaly don't thing anyone should drink their meal. But the only way to know if its ok for you is to tale blood sugar level after you have any, and to see how it affects you (we want to limit BS swings).

2) You'll have to adjust over time. Dr K says that eventually we should have two meals a day. Try to back off a little on the protein and fat and see if you are hungry at night. And what you are doing one day doesn't mean you'll be doing the same a month later...Its constent adaptation.

3)yes! have plenty of it! you probably have a leaky gut, so its good for you! Don't count calories, the reset is not a diet, its a way to heal your body

4)That, I don't know. I've done anything else but walking is the last 3 months. I wouldn't do it, but once again, it depends of your goal (being optimal or not), and without testing, its hard to answer that for you.

Most important right now: BAB, plenty of fat, no panic if you gain weight, and get tested!!

And if you have any more questions, reach out!!

K

And if there are no binges due to feeling satisfied-then I feel like there will be less caloric intake overall? I don't know. ### [email redacted] — 2012-06-13T22:41:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30972>

Actually, that might be too much for you...The HIIT part I mean...Read this blog: [http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/](http://http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/), it might help you undertstand what I mean. If you have suffered from bulimia for several years, it's most likely that your body/hormones/gut is broken, and it might take a while to fix it all.... It depends what you want: losing a few pounds or becoming healthy? The good news it that you can have both, but health will have to come prior to losing weight. Or maybe not. Some are losing pretty quick here, but generally it takes longer for women (b/c of hormones). How is your sleep? Where do you carry fat? How's your mood, your energy?

Don't get overwhelmed. Master one thing at the time (BAB, no snacking, circadian rythm) and see how you feel. If you do all that and don't lose weight, then you may think about adding CT and/or supplements. CT would be the best thing to do to your body along with a keto paleo diet, its speeds everything up. Get there when you're ready, no stress about it. And keep reading the blog. That's a lot of information, but the more you understand, the easier is it to follow the protocol. And don't hesitate to ask questions if you have any concern!!

### allison09 — 2012-06-14T02:44:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31015>

Thanks AbetterME I appreciate the input. I'm familiar with that post on Oprah, but I just re-read it. I definitely know that my gut is broken, and that my hormones are probably a bit wonky (I went 8 months without a period, but got it last month), but unfortunately I don't have testing to know for sure. I think the loss of my period was due to being constantly stressed/anxious. I had a horrible living situation, was going to grad school, and a bit depressed. I moved back to the town I used to live, and within 5 days I got my period! Hahah I don't know how it's all related, but I got a kick out of it

To answer your questions, my sleep is crappy, and has been for a while. Especially these last few nights. I thought this protocol was supposed to improve sleep!! I carry fat in the love-handle and belly region, but I'm not overweight. I'm just not at the weight I want to be at. My mood and energy is not consistent, one day I'll be happy and energetic, and the next I'll be tired and mopey. There are a lot of variables, but right now I'm just sick of my digestive system having a mind of it's own. For example, (and I'm sorry in advance for the graphic nature of what is about to come next), but for the last two days I've had diarrhea, and I don't know why! I'm not eating anything I'm not supposed to-jeez, I'm pretty much eating perfectly. Meat, greens, and fat. That's it! I'm thinking maybe it's from all the junk that I ate last week when I was still binging? Is it because I've been really trying to drink more water? Is it from the enzymes I've been taking? The Kevita and sauerkraut? UGH! Troubleshooting my uncooperative body is so difficult.

I'm going to start writing my daily food intake on here for a record, and also so more experienced people can offer their insight. Some things are not as obvious to me as they are to others.

B-7:45a- 6oz homemade turkey sausage, 5.5 oz broiled tilapia, 2oz spinach sauteed in 1/2 TB CO, Kevita

L- 2p- zucchini "noodles" from a while zucchini (8oz), 2 large mushrooms (4 oz), onion (1.5oz), 4oz pork spare ribs, 1oz kalamata olives, all sauteed together in 1 TB CO, Kevita

Supplements after lunch- enzyme, multi, B's, 2000iu D

40 min gentle yoga before dinner, I'm sore from my HIIT class yesterday

D-7p- 6oz turkey sausage, 2 oz spinach, 4 olives, 1/4 cup sauerkraut, Kevita

I drank one Kevita over the course of the day.

Totals according to Fitday- Totals 1411cal 94g fat 26g carbs 129g protein

The D happened after lunch-any ideas?

I pretty much had to make myself eat dinner tonight. I was only at 1000 calories for the day, but I felt like I should eat something. Although, yesterday I ate at 2pm, went to the HIIT class, didn't eat again till this morning, and I was only the slightest bit hungry. I thought I'd be starving by breakfast, so I probably could have just skipped dinner tonight. I'm not sure what the best thing to do is in this case.

I'd like to try taking magnesium because I'd like to be able to sleep better. I did some digging today for Dr.K's recommendation, and I came up with either Mag malate, which he recommends for sleep, and Mag glycinate, which he recommends for people with a leaky gut. What do you all take? I just don't want it to lead to more diarrhea!

Alright, so I feel like I have a million more questions to ask about this whole thing, but right now I should probably go do some HW. Stupid summer class

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T14:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31093>

B complex vitamins will make your urine yellow leaky gut or not. 5-htp should never be taken alone. Always combine with tyrosine in 1:10 ratio and use co-factors (b-complex, calcium, vitamin c, cysteine). It takes about 5 days to reach neurotransmitter levels at one dose. Do not take more than 300mg of 5-htp without testing neurotransmitter levels. You have a lot of signs of an NT problem.

Listen to this re: happy chemicals and b/p:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;cVtd85zZyjg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;cVtd85zZyjg)

[https://www.box.com/shared/ky3r0leemy](http://https://www.box.com/shared/ky3r0leemy)

[http://kalishresearch.com/](http://http://kalishresearch.com/)

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-14T22:07:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31218>

Hi Allison!

We sure do have a lot in commun!! I went back to school a couple of years ago and that's when bulimia started.I thought it was a stress related think only. It sure improved when I got my diploma, but I was still binging a lot. And sleep became crappy (and sometimes non-exitent!) whila at school too. That's why I ended up here at first, to get my sleep back. I discovered meanwhile that my entire body is broken. Seriously, I don't dont where I would be now if I didn't start the protocol. I'm really far from beainbg optiomal, but I feel way better!!

I, too, cary fat around my waist. This is pretty new. It is usually a sign that something is wrong with insulin or cortisol. Mood and energy are linked to hormones too, that's why I suggest getting tested. I was sure I was dealing with adrenal fatigue, and it wasn't the case at all!! My sleep problems are linked to low DHEA, and supplementing with it helps me really much. I now sleep all night much of the night with good quality sleep  And just like you, sleep was worst when I started the protocol. Give it a few days before you see any improvement in this area.

Are you having more CO than usual? It can cause diarrhea if it's the case. Too much magnesium can do the same.

I'd say, if you are not hungry for dinner, don't eat...See how you feel in the evening and at night, and if you're fine, just go with it. If you're starving by the time you go to bed, then force some food down at dinner time. It's all individual and essay and error...You'll get to know yourself very well on this protocol, and you have to listen to your body

I know somewhere there's a list of the labs you should get done. I'll find it and get back to you.

K

Here's a list of labs you should get: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1007-Help-Labs-List](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1007-Help-Labs-List)

AbetterME-thanks for the advice! Isn't it crazy how school can totally ruin your health! Ugh, I have a summer class that started the other day and I'm so annoyed about it! So today was a pretty good day. I ate well and I felt better than I have been feeling. I felt less bloated, and more like myself. It's a good feeling! Plus, I actually got hungry for lunch and dinner today, and didn't feel like a stuffed whale after eating. Hopefully I can get some sleep tonight.

Tonight at work I ate about a handful of macadamia nuts. It was kind of impulsive, but I don't feel guilty about it, and it didn't spiral into a binge. I definitely did not have that "crazy brain" feeling that happens before a binge.That is seriously a first! I should mention that I work at a natural foods co-op with a seriously amazing bulk department. There are tons of nuts and dried fruits and amazing chocolate treats literally at my fingertips at all times. Can you understand why this can be difficult for me!? There is also an awesome deli that makes all kinds of treats, and anything that goes out of date or gets "damaged" gets put in the staff lounge for people to eat for free. It's like a food frenzy. I'm so proud that I haven't had anything bad the last two shifts I worked! I've realized that bringing my own food from home is key, and the BAB totally helps with cravings. So yea, tonight after I ate my food from home, I had some mac nuts. Although nuts aren't recommended on the AI protocol, I figured mac's aren't the worst since they have a good O3 ratio. So I indulged. And then I stopped. Hahah what a concept!!

Food today:

BAB-finished by 9:30ish- broiled tilapia (5.5oz), turkey sausage (4oz), broccoli (5oz), 1/2 TB CO (enzymes)

L-2pm- 6oz can of salmon, 1TB olive oil, romaine lettuce (supps- fish oil, D, B's, enzymes)

D-6pm- 5.5 oz beef, 5.5 oz zucchini, 3.5 oz mushrooms, 1TB CO, handful of mac nuts

Totals = 1396cal 89.7g fat 29g carb 134g protein

I'm excited about trying the 5-htp out again with the co-factors. I get a good deal on supplements through my job, so I priced everything out tonight, just have to order them!

I'm super tired tonight, hopefully I can sleep. Gotta be ready for my HIIT class tomorrow, I'm excited!

That is a ton of protein, is this ok?!

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-16T00:32:21+0300

It's not that much, don't worry, it's barely over 1g of protein per body weight.

How was the sleep last last? And how is the sleep after HIIT? Is it worst or restful? I had to stop any cardio for a while because it made matters worst on sleep and hormones  I started lifting a little this week, but I'll keep it to a minimum until I'm LS.

Congrats on the not-binging-on-the-nuts. Indulging a little...stopping...yeah, what a concept, but it seems to be harder to get for some of us  If I have a fews nuts, I'll end up having way too much. My doctor says that food that triger a binge is usually food we have intolerence about... For me it would be wheat, dairies and nuts...It makes sense.

You don't have to feel bad asking for testing you know. And if you meet a functionnal dr, you won't have to justify yourself other than: I can't lose fat, I don't sleep, I'm stressed and I have cravings...I think that's enough to order some labs  But as I said earlier, follow the protocol, and if at one point you stall and you can't lose weight, drop the w/o, and if you stall again, you'll have to get tested to see exactly what's wrong.

Have a nice week-end, hang in there!!

K
